Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) are high — the authors flag the one non-vanishing approximation and the heuristic infinite-horizon bound in Prop 3.3 rather than hiding them, though that bound and the untested tensor/pipeline-parallel integration cap Resilience. Practical Impact (78) reflects a released vLLM extension that is directly actionable on bandwidth-constrained GPUs. Weighted: 0.20*82 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*78 ≈ 80.
empirical Novelty 82 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference is split between prefill (compute-bound: processes all input tokens in one parallel forward pass) and decode (memory-bandwidth-bound: streams the KV cache repeatedly per autoregressive step). Mixed batching, which co-locates both phases via chunked prefill in the same forward pass, became the de facto standard because it keeps GPU compute utilized during decode. However, the observation that large-scale Chinese production systems persistently favor exclusive batching raises a concrete hardware-dependent question. Kernel profiling reveals that mixed-batch attention cost exceeds pure-decode attention cost at a decode ratio of only r≈20% on an RTX PRO 6000 (1.792 TB/s), versus r≈80% on an H200 (4.8 TB/s). This hardware-dependent crossover point has never been analytically characterized, and no scheduler exists that exploits it online. The gap: operators either pay MB's interference penalty on constrained hardware, or they rely on heuristic EB implementations like vLLM v0 (which naively switches at every completion, equivalent to EB(k=1)). Key Idea The core insight is that the per-step marginal cost of a mixed batch is a concave function of the decode ratio r, and the degree of concavity (the interference convexity index κ, empirically κ=-11.6 on RTX PRO 6000 vs. κ=-0.69 on H200) scales with GPU memory bandwidth saturation. This means the EB-vs-MB crossover is not a binary hardware property but a parametric condition: MB outperforms EB if and only if β^e_MB - β^w_EB < (amortized fixed-cost advantage)/N, a condition that can be evaluated online from profiled kernel times and estimated workload statistics. Once this crossover condition is in hand, the optimal EB switching threshold θ = k/N reduces to a scalar fixed-point equation that depends only on the ratio of phase-switching fixed costs (p₀α_p/α_d), enabling a closed-form adaptive controller without parameter search. Methodology The scheduling model assumes a saturated system with N slots, alternating prefill and decode phases. A decode phase ends when k idle slots accumulate (completed requests); the system then prefills k new requests and resumes decoding. The throughput objective TP(k,N) = k / (E[T_d(k;N)] + α_p + β_p·k·μ_L) is maximized subject to a KV-cache memory constraint. The solution proceeds in three stages: (1) Optimal threshold under CFR. Applying the fluid approximation (batch size evolves as dn/dt = -p₀n, valid as N→∞ with O(1/√N) error) and solving the first-order optimality condition yields Proposition 3.1: θ₀ is the unique solution to θ₀/(1-θ₀) + ln(1-θ₀) = p₀α_p/α_d, independent of N, μ_L, β_p, β_d — only fixed-overhead ratios and the hazard rate matter. Three error sources vanish as N→∞; one (discretization/overshoot) produces a bounded non-vanishing bias, explicitly enumerated. (2) IFR correction. Real LLM workloads exhibit increasing failure rate (statistically confirmed via Mann-Kendall, p<10⁻⁵, on all three testable workloads). Theorem 3.2 adds a perturbative correction Δθ via implicit function theorem, proving Δθ>0 for all valid parameters — IFR workloads should wait longer before switching. (3) Memory-safe batch size. Proposition 3.3 models KV-cache occupancy as a random walk with negative drift, bounds the supremum via Feller's infinite-horizon result, and yields N* = ⌊(C - v̄·ln(1/ε)) / (μ_L + (1-θ₀)/θ₀p₀ · ln(1/(1-θ₀)))⌋. The authors note this is a heuristic-conservative approximation; a runtime KV-aware feasibility gate (Appendix D.6) provides defense-in-depth. For EB+, Proposition 3.4 derives the MB-vs-EB crossover condition (Eq. 7): if β^e_MB(r) - β^w_EB exceeds the amortized fixed-cost advantage term (O(1/N), vanishes under saturation), EB is preferred. EB+ evaluates this inequality online using EMA-smoothed occupancy and sliding-window hazard-rate estimation. Key Results Experiments span 4 NVIDIA GPUs (0.864–8.0 TB/s), primary models Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B (MoE), and 7 workloads (3 synthetic + ShareGPT, LongBench, NuminaMath, WildChat). Baselines are vLLM v0 (EB(k=1)) and vLLM v1 (MB default). Key numbers: EB(k̂) achieves +41.9% RPS vs. v1 on L40S (WildChat), +7.9% average on Qwen3-8B real-world workloads on RTX PRO 6000, and reduces TPOT by 65% on ShareGPT (93.68 ms vs. 268.97 ms). On H200, EB(k̂) averages only +1.5%, confirming the bandwidth-driven crossover. For Qwen3-30B-A3B, EB's advantage shrinks to +1.4% on RTX PRO 6000 and reverses to -2.9% on H200, consistent with the analytical prediction that larger fixed costs α inflate the MB-favoring RHS of the crossover condition. The interference convexity index κ=-11.6 on RTX PRO 6000 vs. κ=-0.69 on H200 empirically confirms the bandwidth-gap mechanism. EB+ under non-stationary traffic (distribution shift + concurrency shift) achieves the highest or near-highest throughput in all tested cells, outperforming v1 by up to 36.4%. Against PD-disaggregation on 4×RTX PRO 6000, EB+ is best or near-best in 7 of 9 workload×concurrency cells while maintaining 3–18× lower TTFT and zero OOMs. At c=512 with SLO (TTFT<10s, TPOT<100ms), EB+ achieves 80.3% joint goodput vs. 5.8% for v1. The closed-form controller outperforms the best fixed-k sweep by up to 8.0% on decode-heavy workloads, validating that co-adapting both θ and N yields gains beyond threshold tuning alone. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The primary novel contribution is the closed-form EB-MB crossover condition (Eq. 7) and the analytical framework for optimal EB thresholds under stochastic output-length distributions with IFR correction — neither has appeared in prior scheduling literature. The adaptive hybrid scheduler EB+ and its vLLM extension implementation constitute a deployable systems artifact. The empirical finding that MB does not universally dominate is not surprising in hindsight, but quantifying it via the interference convexity index κ and providing a hardware-agnostic controller is genuinely new versus prior work (Sarathi, vLLM v0), which either committed to one mode or used fixed heuristics. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is released at https://github.com/weifang231/eb-vllm. The paper systematically validates all theoretical claims: three structural properties of θ₀ (N-invariance, μ_L-independence, μ_O-monotonicity) are confirmed via threshold sweeps (Tables 10–11); the IFR assumption is tested via Mann-Kendall and linear regression on all real workloads; the IFR shift Δθ>0 is validated in controlled experiments (CFR θ=0.16 vs. IFR θ=0.36, Figure 12). Approximation errors are enumerated in Table 8 with explicit bounds, distinguishing the one non-vanishing source (discretization/overshoot) from O(1/√N) and O(1/N) terms. Experiments use grid search over (B,N) configurations rather than hand-tuned settings, and sensitivity metrics (CV, range ratio) are reported across all workloads. The memory-safe batch size proof (Prop 3.3) and the IFR threshold proof (Theorem 3.2 under heterogeneous request ages) are both flagged as relying on approximations whose rigorous finite-N generalizations are left to future work — appropriate epistemic disclosure. The paper appeared at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Yes — the scheduler runs on standard vLLM with commercially available GPUs (RTX PRO 6000, L40S, H200, B300) in BF16; a one-shot per-hardware kernel calibration (3 v1 runs at varying decode ratios) is needed to parameterize β^e_MB(r). - Efficient: Online parameter estimation (sliding-window hazard-rate fit + EMA occupancy) adds negligible overhead relative to forward pass cost; the KV-aware gate adds one occupancy check per phase transition; PagedAttention is unchanged. - Effective: Evidence directly confirms the stated goal: throughput improvements are large and consistent on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, and EB+ reliably matches the better of EB and MB across all tested traffic regimes including non-stationary workloads. For large MoE models on high-bandwidth hardware, the method correctly switches to MB. - Scalable: Tested only on single-GPU and data-parallel (up to 4-GPU) setups; integration with tensor parallelism and pipeline parallelism is explicitly listed as future work, and the crossover condition's behavior under those strategies is uncharacterized. Software artifact: vLLM extension at https://github.com/weifang231/eb-vllm. Strengths - The closed-form crossover condition (Eq. 7) is analytically derived, empirically confirmed, and actionable: the interference convexity index κ=-11.6 on RTX PRO 6000 vs. κ=-0.69 on H200 cleanly explains the hardware gap and predicts where EB gains are largest (intermediate decode ratios r≈0.5–0.7), a prediction confirmed by the workload results. - The IFR extension is non-trivial: the proof of Δθ>0 for all valid parameters is complete (both bracketed terms shown positive via the CFR baseline condition), and IFR is formally validated on real workloads rather than assumed. - Competing with PD-disaggregation without extra hardware is a strong result: on 4×RTX PRO 6000, EB+ achieves best/near-best throughput in 7 of 9 cells while maintaining 3–18× lower TTFT, and avoids the structural OOM fragility that kills disaggregation at c=2048. - The authors enumerate all approximation error sources explicitly (Table 8), including the one non-vanishing source and its practical magnitude, which is better practice than presenting the fluid approximation as exact. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The memory-safe batch size proof (Prop 3.3) applies an infinite-horizon random-walk supremum bound described as a "standard heuristic-conservative approximation"; the rigorous finite-horizon martingale analysis is deferred. The IFR threshold proof treats successive cycles as independent under the fluid model, which is exact only under CFR (memoryless); the finite-N stochastic analog under heterogeneous request ages requires mixing-time arguments also left to future work. EB's advantage diminishes for large MoE models on high-bandwidth hardware (Qwen3-30B-A3B, H200: -2.9% average) and nearly vanishes for extreme decode-heavy workloads (NuminaMath). Future work is cited for SLO-aware switching and tensor/pipeline parallel integration. The authors confront these limitations directly, which strengthens credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All GPU platforms are NVIDIA; generalizability to AMD (MI300X, 5.3 TB/s) or Apple Silicon is entirely unstated — the bandwidth-interference hypothesis should transfer, but the calibration procedure (Appendix A.4) would need to be re-run and the assumption of GEMM-dominance may not hold on non-NVIDIA attention kernels. - The sliding-window hazard-rate estimator (Algorithm 1) has a cold-start problem: the paper reports W_min and θ_default exist but does not quantify how many requests are needed before the adaptive controller starts meaningfully outperforming the default threshold. For short bursts or highly variable workloads, this warm-up period could matter. - All experiments are single-model; co-located multi-tenant serving with mixed model sizes (where the interference convexity κ differs across tenants) is unaddressed. - The 4-GPU experiments compare EB+ (DP=4) against disaggregation but not against tensor-parallel configurations; for the 30B+ parameter models that typically require tensor parallelism, this omission means the strongest relevant baseline is missing. - The paper does not report wall-clock scheduling overhead or CPU-side latency from the online parameter update loop (Algorithm 1), which could matter in high-QPS deployments. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying 7B–14B models on bandwidth-constrained GPUs (L40S, RTX-class, export-restricted hardware) will find EB+ directly actionable, analytically grounded, and better than any existing single-mode scheduler — this is worth reading and the released code makes adoption straightforward.
Search Results 2606.00516 PDF
#7 — Highly Innovative (79)

Why Do Time Series Models Need Long Context Windows?

L Butera, G De Felice, A Cini, C Alippi · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proves that global time series forecasting decomposes into two coupled tasks — generative process identification (GPI, inferring which stochastic process generated the observed sequence) and conditional forecasting (CF, predicting given process identity) — and formally establishes that achieving minimum attainable forecast error requires an input window strictly larger than the process memory length P, then demonstrates that explicitly decoupling GPI and CF via a cached context encoder improves compute efficiency without sacrificing accuracy. - Why it's important: Foundation models for time series require very long context windows at substantial compute cost, yet the prevailing explanation (capturing long-range temporal dependencies) is demonstrably incomplete, conflating two distinct functions of the input window and impeding principled architectural design decisions. - How it does it: Applying the law of total conditional expectation to the MSE-optimal predictor under a parametric multi-process generative model reveals the prediction as a Bayesian posterior average over process parameters — decomposing naturally into a GPI posterior term and a CF conditional mean term — which directly implies the necessity of W > P and motivates architecturally separating the two tasks. - The good: Theorem 3.2 is a clean necessity proof backed by controlled NAR experiments: a local GRU achieves near-minimum MSE at W = P = 2, a global GRU requires W ≫ P, and a global GRU with a temporally-gapped context (C = 46, S = 50) recovers near-minimum at W = P — exactly as the theory predicts. - The missing: The paper does not build or evaluate a foundation model embodying these principles; the decoupled architecture is demonstrated only at modest scale (up to 1000 time series, window ≤ 720) on domain-specific models, leaving the central claim about foundation model redesign as stated future work. - Read it or not: Researchers designing time series foundation model architectures or studying in-context learning in sequential data should read this; practitioners seeking a deployable system can defer.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (with a formal theoretical result). Novelty (85) is high — the GPI/CF Bayesian decomposition is a NEW FORMULATION plus a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (gapped-context recovery), not a method-to-dataset application. Non-Obviousness (88) is the standout: reframing long-window need as posterior contraction over process identity rather than long-range dependency is genuinely counter-intuitive and the gap-experiment design is elegant causal evidence. Rigor (75) is solid — thorough ablations, 3 runs ±std, current strong baselines (Moirai2, TimerXL) — but no code release and two unquantified confounds (non-contiguous-input artifact, uncontrolled parameter asymmetry in G(·)) hold it below the top band. Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) are good with honestly stated limitations, though the central foundation-model claim is left as future work. Impact (70) reflects a reusable architectural pattern and theory but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*85+0.25*75+0.15*88+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.10*70 = 79.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 88 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Global deep learning models trained on collections of time series — both transductive models sharing parameters across a fixed corpus and inductive foundation models generalizing to unseen series — use input windows far exceeding the autocorrelation horizon of the data, as shown empirically in Fig. 1 for Moirai, PatchTST, DLinear, ModernTCN, and TSMixer on Electricity and Weather benchmarks. The prevailing attribution to capturing long-range temporal dependencies cannot explain why globally-trained models require substantially longer windows than locally-trained models on the same data, nor why foundation models pretrained on heterogeneous corpora need longer windows than domain-specific global models trained on the target domain alone. No prior work provides a formal explanation linking window length requirements to the statistical properties of the multi-process training distribution. Key Idea: The core insight is that for a model trained on time series generated by multiple distinct stochastic processes, the MSE-optimal prediction is a Bayesian posterior average over generative process parameters: the model implicitly infers which process generated the observed window (GPI) and then forecasts accordingly (CF). For stationary Markov-P processes, the CF task needs only the most recent P observations, but GPI can exploit any observations from the same process — including non-adjacent ones — because the posterior over process parameters is time-shift-invariant. Longer windows improve accuracy primarily by contracting the posterior over process identity, not by capturing additional temporal dependencies. This reframes in-context learning in time series models as Bayesian process identification. Methodology: The generative framework (Eq. 3) models each of N time series as drawn from a parametric family G parameterized by ϕ^n ~ P_ϕ, with time-invariant conditional P_x(x_t | x_{<t}; ϕ^n). The MSE-optimal predictor equals E[x_t | x_{t-W:t}], which by the law of total expectation decomposes as the integral over the process manifold of E[x_t | x_{t-W:t}, ϕ^n] (CF) weighted by p(ϕ^n | x_{t-W:t}) (GPI) — Eq. 5. Theorem 3.2 is proved by contradiction under Assumption 3.1 (processes with different ϕ^n cannot be uniquely identified from P observations with probability one): any predictor with W = P has strictly positive expected GPI uncertainty, exceeding the irreducible noise floor Var(ε_t). Empirical validation uses (a) synthetic nonlinear autoregressive (NAR) processes of known order (N = 1000 series, single-layer GRU) to compare local, global, and global+context models as W varies; (b) multi-domain NAR experiments mixing processes of orders {1,2,4,6} to isolate how distribution breadth increases required window length; (c) probing of pretrained Moirai2 and TimerXL by feeding a temporally-gapped context (S = 336 timesteps, ~2 weeks for hourly data) plus a short window (W = 24), isolating GPI from temporal dependency effects on four real datasets. The decoupled architecture (Sec. 4, Eqs. 8a–8b) uses encoder G(·) processing a historical context x_{t'-C:t'} with C ≫ W and t' < t − W, producing a cacheable embedding e^n; the forecaster F(·) then conditions on both the recent window x_{t-W:t} and e^n. Context timing during training is randomized within a maximum back-shift S to prevent temporal-lag bias. Critical design decisions: (1) gap between context and window isolates GPI from CF; (2) e^n caching reduces sequential inference from O(m·(C+W)) to O(C + m·W); (3) G(·) always implemented as PatchTST for comparability across base model choices. Key Results: Theorem 3.2 (theoretical): for any Markov-P process family satisfying Assumption 3.1, window size W > P is necessary to achieve minimum expected error Var(ε_t) — global models cannot attain the noise floor with W = P regardless of capacity. Synthetic NAR(2) (Fig. 3, 3 runs ±std, N = 1000): local GRU achieves near-minimum MSE at W = P = 2; global GRU approaches minimum only for W ≫ 2; global+context GRU (C = 46, S = 50) achieves near-minimum at W = P = 2 in both transductive and inductive settings. Multi-domain experiments (Fig. 4): models trained jointly on NAR orders {2,4,6} need consistently larger W to match single-domain models on any specific target order; increasing W allows multi-domain models to match or surpass single-domain baselines. Foundation models vs. domain-specific (Fig. 5, 3 runs ±std): on LargeST-SD, CEREn, ClmUS-T, ClmUS-R, Moirai2 and TimerXL require W = 720 to approach the MAE of PatchTST trained from scratch at W ~ 72; the foundation models' pretraining corpora include the target domains. Decoupled architecture (Fig. 6, H = 24): decoupled models at small W achieve MAE comparable to standard models at large W across PatchTST, TSMixer, ModernTCN, LRU base architectures on 4 real datasets. Pareto frontiers (Fig. 7, Tab. 6): at 10% MAE budget, the decoupled approach achieves up to 101.91× CPU speedup (LRU, ClmUS-T), up to 85.41× FLOPs reduction, and consistent CUDA memory savings; the decoupled Pareto frontier dominates the standard one across all cost metrics. Baselines are current and strong (Moirai2, TimerXL as foundation models; PatchTST, TSMixer, ModernTCN, LRU as SOTA domain-specific architectures). Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The GPI/CF decomposition via Bayesian posterior averaging is a novel formalization of the global forecasting objective, not previously stated in this form. Theorem 3.2 is a new formal result establishing necessity of W > P for global models. The empirical finding that temporally-gapped context improves foundation model performance comparably to extending the contiguous window is new. The decoupled architecture is a novel application of the insight but follows naturally from the theory rather than introducing new technical primitives — it applies known ideas (separate encoders, embedding conditioning, KV-caching) motivated by the new theoretical framing. The connection between time series GPI and Bayesian ICL in language models is an insightful synthesis rather than a strictly new result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Profile: EMPIRICAL (plus a formal theoretical result). Ablations are thorough: hidden size, number of seeds, context length, gap size, training domain composition, architecture family, and implementation of G(·) (linear vs. PatchTST) are all varied. Three runs with ±std are reported on all experimental figures. No code repository is mentioned; the ClmUS-T and ClmUS-R datasets are described with access instructions (NASA POWER project). Two methodological concerns: (1) the probing experiment feeding non-contiguous inputs to foundation models trained on contiguous sequences (Fig. 2) introduces artifacts (noted in App. I.1 as upward/downward shifts in forecasts), and these artifacts could partially confound the GPI interpretation — the paper acknowledges but does not quantify the artifact's effect on MAE; (2) the decoupled architecture always uses PatchTST as G(·) regardless of the base model F(·), introducing a parameter-count asymmetry that is not controlled for — the decoupled variant has strictly more parameters, so the Pareto comparison is not parameter-matched. The theoretical proof under Assumption 3.1 is complete and the assumption is explicitly stated and well-motivated. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments run on NVIDIA A5000 and Titan V GPUs (24 GB VRAM); the paper notes that most experiments fit in 12 GB, and reducing batch size allows operation under 8 GB, making the approach accessible on widely available hardware. - Efficient: Decoupled inference reduces sequential forecast cost from O(m·(C+W)) to O(C + m·W) by caching e^n; Tab. 6 reports 2–102× CPU speedup and 2–85× FLOPs reduction at 10% MAE budget across models and datasets; total experiment compute is approximately 1800 GPU-hours on a single A5000. - Effective: The decoupled approach achieves comparable or better MAE than standard models at smaller W across 4 real datasets and 4 model architectures, consistently dominating Pareto frontiers; the foundation model probing experiment shows comparable MAE trends for contiguous-window and gapped-context inputs on 4 datasets. - Scalable: Experiments use up to 1000 time series and window sizes up to 720 for domain-specific models; the paper does not evaluate the decoupled architecture at foundation model scale (millions of series, 230B+ datapoints), and scaling behavior is claimed but not tested. The work delivers a reusable architectural pattern and a formal theoretical framework but no software artifact, trained model, or dataset release. Strengths: - Theorem 3.2 provides a clean, formal necessity proof that W > P is required for global models even when the true process memory is exactly P — this closes an explanatory gap that had been observed empirically (Montero-Manso and Hyndman, 2021) but never rigorously characterized. - The context-gap experiment design is methodologically elegant: inserting a 2-week gap (S = 336) between the historical context and the recent window decouples GPI from temporal dependency effects in pretrained foundation models, providing causal evidence for the hypothesis without retraining these models. - The finding that foundation model generality comes with a concrete quantifiable cost — broader pretraining distribution P_ϕ mandates longer windows to achieve the same GPI quality as a domain-specific model — is non-obvious, empirically demonstrated, and actionable for practitioners choosing between zero-shot foundation models and domain-specific alternatives. - The caching insight (GPI produces a reusable embedding because process identification is time-shift-invariant for stationary processes) is practically valuable for production forecasting systems making repeated predictions on the same time series. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that developing and evaluating a full foundation model based on the proposed decoupled design is left as future work. They identify two additional open directions: (1) dynamic selection of the number of recent observations used for CF (rather than a fixed window W); (2) decoupling GPI in the presence of highly time-varying or non-stationary data-generating processes. These limitations are stated directly, which is appropriate given the scope of the paper. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The probing experiment on pretrained foundation models (Fig. 2) feeds non-contiguous inputs to models trained only on contiguous sequences; any improvement from the gapped context could partly reflect model tolerance to extra input tokens rather than genuine GPI — this confound is noted but unquantified. The decoupled architecture always uses PatchTST as G(·) regardless of the base model, introducing uncontrolled parameter-count asymmetry; equal-parameter or equal-capacity comparisons are absent. The theoretical framework assumes stationarity and i.i.d. noise — the claimed extension to multivariate cases is stated but not demonstrated, and non-stationary real-world processes (concept drift, seasonality change) are not analyzed theoretically or tested empirically. Theorem 3.2 establishes necessity but not how much larger W needs to be or the rate of GPI error decay with W — practical guidance on window sizing is left to the experiments rather than derived from theory. Bottom Line: The GPI/CF decomposition is a genuine conceptual advance that replaces a vague appeal to "long-range dependencies" with a rigorous, testable, and actionable explanation for why global time series models need long windows — anyone designing or deploying time series foundation model architectures should read this paper.
Citations 2606.01999 PDF
#8 — Highly Innovative (79)

TabPrep: Closing the Feature Engineering Gap in Tabular Benchmarks

A Tschalzev, N Erickson, Y Wang, H Rangwala… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TabPrep, a lightweight preprocessing pipeline with three pattern-targeted feature generators (Ordered Arithmetic Feature Expansion, CatPrep, and Random Subset Feature Compression) that consistently improves performance for any tabular model family on the TabArena benchmark. - Why it's important: Tabular benchmarks universally omit feature engineering, systematically underestimating achievable peak performance; the authors quantify this gap and show it exceeds recent model-centric advances on more datasets and by larger margins. - How it does it: Rather than exhaustive expand-and-reduce search, TabPrep generates a bounded budget of features targeting three identified structural patterns that current model classes provably fail to capture, delegating feature selection to the downstream model's existing HPO. - The good: PrepTabPFN-2.5 achieves Elo 1663 (tuned + ensembled) on TabArena, topping all 27 competitors including the newer TabPFN-2.6 and TabICLv2, and TabPrep-augmented models establish new peak performance on 24/51 benchmark datasets. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to IID single-table tasks with four model families; the three structural patterns were selected from prior studies and do not cover the full space of possible feature engineering operations; behavior at scales exceeding 100K samples is untested. - Read it or not: Recommended for tabular ML researchers and practitioners — it directly challenges how model comparisons are conducted, provides an immediately usable open-source tool (github.com/atschalz/tabprep), and forces reconsideration of what "state of the art" means in tabular benchmarking.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (Novelty 75, Rigor 82, Non-Obviousness 78, Completeness 82, Resilience 75, Impact 85). Novelty is high but not top-band: the budgeted pattern-informed generation paradigm is a genuine reframing of expand-and-reduce plus a substantive new empirical finding (a preprocessing pipeline topping TabArena Elo over recent foundation models), though it is a NEW METHOD rather than a new problem class. Rigor is strong — leave-one-out ablations, 5-seed sensitivity, feature-count sensitivity, the full TabArena 8x3 nested CV protocol, and released open-source code integrated into AutoGluon. Non-Obviousness is elevated by the mechanistically grounded inductive-bias analysis showing exactly which structural patterns each model family misses. Completeness and Resilience are pulled down slightly by the acknowledged asymmetric comparison (tuned+ensembled PrepTabPFN vs. default-config TabPFN-2.6/TabICLv2) and the Spearman-pruning step that partially re-enters supervised selection. Practical Impact is the top contributor — an immediately deployable, frictionless tool that resets the benchmarking bar. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*85 = 79.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 82 Resilience 75 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tabular ML research focuses overwhelmingly on model architectures while feature engineering, known from competitive ML (Kaggle) and industry practice to have decisive impact, is entirely absent from modern benchmarks including the industry-standard TabArena. Existing automated feature engineering frameworks (OpenFE, autofeat) fail as benchmarking baselines: autofeat fails due to OOM or timeout on 33 of 51 TabArena datasets, OpenFE on 22, and their median per-model training time increases are 180× and 19× respectively — making HPO-based evaluation across thousands of configurations impractical. No simple, ready-to-use FE baseline for tabular benchmarking existed prior to this work. The gap between model-only peak performance and the achievable ceiling when FE is included is the central research problem. Key Idea: The core technical insight is a paradigm shift from the dominant expand-and-reduce approach (generate many candidate features, prune with a supervised selection model) to budgeted pattern-informed generation: identify which families of structural patterns recur across real-world tabular datasets and which model classes exhibit systematic blind spots to those patterns, then build generators that produce a fixed, predefined budget of candidates targeting exactly those families. Feature selection is not performed as an additional preprocessing step but is absorbed into the downstream model's existing HPO pipeline. Methodology: TabPrep composes three generators: (1) Ordered Arithmetic Feature Expansion (OAFE) generates pairwise and higher-order arithmetic combinations (/, ×, −, +) of numerical features, filtered for low-cardinality and near-constant features, ordered by operator learnability difficulty (division first), up to order r=3 and a budget of M=2000 features using up to 150 base features. (2) CatPrep applies three complementary sub-generators targeting group-conditional categorical structure: Out-of-Fold Target Encoding (5-fold CV with shrinkage α=10 to prevent leakage), Target-Encoded Categorical Combinations (up to M=100, Cartesian products encoded via OOF-TE), and Relative GroupBy interactions (xj/E[xj|ck] and xj−E[xj|ck], up to M=500). (3) Random Subset Feature Compression (RSFC) discretizes numerical features, randomly samples subsets, constructs categorical keys, and applies OOF-TE to capture pseudo-categorical numerical structure (M=50 subsets). All target-informed transformations are nested inside inner training folds. When total generated features exceed 2000, they are pruned to the top-2000 by absolute Spearman correlation with the target (computed only on training data). TabPrep is treated as a hyperparameter applied to 25% of 200 HPO configurations. Evaluation uses the TabArena 8×3 nested CV protocol; primary metric is ELO; base metrics are ROC AUC (binary), log-loss (multiclass), RMSE (regression). Key Results: PrepTabPFN-2.5 achieves Elo 1663 (tuned + ensembled), topping all 27 TabArena models including TabPFN-2.6 (1616) and TabICLv2 (1600). TabPrep-augmented models collectively establish new peak performance on 24/51 datasets versus 19 datasets for the two most recent model additions combined. PrepLightGBM wins 85% of pairwise comparisons against base LightGBM; PrepLinear wins 97% against base Linear. On medium-sized datasets (10K–100K samples), PrepLightGBM outperforms AutoGluon's 4-hour multi-model extreme ensemble. Median training-time increase is 6× for LightGBM (vs. 19× OpenFE, 180× autofeat). OAFE is the single most impactful generator; leave-one-out ablations confirm all four generators contribute. Seed sensitivity across 5 random seeds is negligible on most datasets. TabPrep delivers gains even with as few as 100 generated features. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The budgeted pattern-informed generation paradigm is genuinely novel as a contrast to expand-and-reduce. OAFE and RSFC are new generators; OOF-TE and relative GroupBy are known operations, but their leakage-safe composition for large-scale benchmarking and the specific framing as structural-pattern compensation are new. The empirical finding that a simple preprocessing pipeline beats recent tabular foundation models on more datasets than any model-centric advance — and that even flexible neural architectures and in-context learners benefit from explicit arithmetic feature engineering — is a substantive new empirical finding. Rigor & Reproducibility: Leave-one-out component ablations (Figure 4b, Figure 15) confirm each generator contributes. Seed sensitivity analysis (Appendix B.5) shows low variance across 5 seeds. Feature count sensitivity (Appendix B.4) validates that gains appear at 100 features. Evaluation follows TabArena's 8×3 nested CV protocol; consistency with TabArena-full (30/9 outer folds) is confirmed for LightGBM (Figure 17). Code is released at github.com/atschalz/tabprep. The Spearman-correlation feature pruning step (applied only when >2000 features are generated, only on training data) introduces a mild supervised element to feature selection that partially re-enters the model-dependent selection the paper claims to avoid — though the authors frame it as evaluation-only scaffolding and the practical effect on conclusions appears minor. The comparison with TabPFN-2.6 and TabICLv2 uses only their default configurations, while PrepTabPFN-2.5 is tuned + ensembled. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on standard CPUs (Linear, LightGBM) and NVIDIA H100 GPUs (TabPFN-2.5, TabM); CPU-based PrepLightGBM matches GPU-dependent baselines in several regimes, reducing hardware requirements. - Efficient: Median 6× training-time increase for LightGBM versus 19× (OpenFE) and 180× (autofeat); PrepTabPFN-2.5 achieves ~500 samples/second inference throughput, covering all TabArena dataset sizes within one minute. - Effective: Direct evidence on 51 real-world datasets across 4 model families shows consistent improvement; PrepTabPFN-2.5 achieves top TabArena Elo; 24/51 datasets show new peak performance with error-bar-narrow validation across 3 outer folds. - Scalable: TabPrep shows strongest gains at medium scale (10K–100K samples); behavior beyond 100K is not evaluated; attention-based models like TabPFN-2.5 face architectural scaling constraints at larger sizes. Feature budgets provide a tunable compute knob. Delivered as a software artifact integrated into AutoGluon's AbstractFeatureGenerator framework. Strengths: - The budgeted pattern-informed paradigm is empirically validated as superior to supervised expand-and-reduce (autofeat, OpenFE), despite using far fewer candidate features — overturning the implicit assumption that more exhaustive search is always better. - The inductive bias analysis in Appendix D is mechanistically grounded: synthetic datasets and diagnostic real datasets isolate exactly which structural patterns each model family fails on, motivating generator design with causal evidence rather than heuristics. - The practical contribution is immediate and substantial: TabPrep runs on all 51 benchmark datasets, is open-source, and integrates into AutoGluon, making adoption frictionless for the benchmarking community. - The finding that even TabPFN-2.5 and other tabular foundation models benefit from explicit arithmetic feature engineering challenges the assumption that flexible architectures implicitly learn algebraic structure, with implications for future prior design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Authors are direct and credible about five limitations: (1) only three structural patterns are targeted, selected from prior benchmarking studies, leaving others unaddressed; (2) only four model families are augmented with TabPrep, leaving additional model-specific interactions unexplored; (3) feature expansion increases training and inference cost; (4) the evaluation is restricted to IID single-table settings, excluding non-IID, relational, and text-field scenarios; (5) TabPrep is framed explicitly as a benchmarking baseline, not an optimal FE system, so reported gains are a lower bound on FE potential. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The Spearman-correlation feature pruning (applied to reduce features to 2000 when the budget is exceeded) is a supervised dimensionality reduction step that partially reintroduces model-dependent selection — the paper does not report how often this cap is triggered or how much it affects results, and it partially contradicts the "no supervised selection" claim. - The comparison to TabPFN-2.6 and TabICLv2 is structurally asymmetric: those models are evaluated at default settings only while PrepTabPFN-2.5 benefits from full HPO + ensembling; though the paper notes this and the timing difference, the head-to-head framing in Figure 1 may be misleading for readers focused on single-model comparisons. - TabArena is skewed toward small datasets (<10K samples) where foundation models already dominate and TabPrep's gains are smaller; the most compelling TabPrep results are in the medium-data regime, which represents a minority of TabArena. - RSFC has the smallest individual leave-one-out contribution, is acknowledged as more dataset-sensitive, and can degrade performance on some datasets without HPO — but no per-dataset analysis of when RSFC specifically hurts is provided to guide practitioners on when to disable it. Bottom Line: A practitioner in tabular ML will gain both an immediately deployable tool and a reframing of benchmark methodology that is overdue — worth reading for anyone designing evaluations, building competitive pipelines, or developing future tabular foundation models, as TabPrep sets a concrete new bar that purely model-centric comparisons will need to acknowledge.
Citations 2606.02384 PDF
#9 — Highly Innovative (78)

How Much Parallelism Is" Free"? A Principle of Near-Free Parallelism for Parallel Decoding

M He, L Zhang, Y Liu, X Zhou, A Liu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Near-Free Parallelism (NFP), a formal system-side capacity boundary that predicts the maximum number of decode positions a model-hardware-backend stack can process at near-zero additional latency, and derives a closed-form principle from hardware balance and kernel implementation granularity. - Why it's important: Parallelism-based decoding speedups (speculative decoding, multi-token prediction, diffusion LMs) are routinely over-estimated because the dominant intuition — memory-bound idle compute — ignores implementation-level tiling and padding, leading to misconfigured draft lengths and wasted decode capacity that can over-predict the NFP boundary by up to 23×. - How it does it: The paper decomposes NFP into two interacting slack sources — memory-bound resource slack (governing Dense FFNs) and kernel-granularity slack arising from token padding in fused MoE kernels (BLOCK_SIZE_M) and query tiling in attention backends (kBlockM/CTA_TILE_Q) — then combines them via a first-exiting-module approximation into closed-form predictions. - The good: Full-model validation across four models (WeDLM-8B, Qwen3-8B, LLaDA-2.1-mini, Ling-2.0-mini), two serving stacks (nano-vLLM+FlashAttention, SGLang+FlashInfer), three GPU platforms (H20, A800, H800), and both diffusion and autoregressive paradigms, with no fitted parameters, shows the principle matches measured boundaries throughout. - The missing: The entire study uses single-GPU execution at small batch sizes with controlled routing; multi-GPU tensor- and pipeline-parallel settings — the norm in production serving for larger models — are not analyzed and are left as acknowledged future work. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems engineers tuning speculative decoding verification lengths, MTP prediction horizons, or diffusion block sizes — the paper provides the first calibration-free, formula-based tool to set those parameters correctly for a given backend and GPU.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster. Novelty (82) is high — NFP is a new, formally measurable system-side capacity, and the empirical finding that kernel-granularity slack (padding/tiling), not idle-compute, dominates the near-free boundary for MoE and Attention is a genuinely new result that qualitatively corrects the prevailing roofline intuition. Rigor (78) is strong: Nsight profiling, runtime FLOP counts, source-code grounding across two MoE backends and two attention backends, a threshold sensitivity sweep, and zero fitted parameters — held back only by single-GPU scope, no released artifact, and version-sensitive constants in a preprint. Non-Obviousness (85) is the standout: the up-to-23x over-prediction of the standard intuition is surprising and counter-intuitive. Completeness (75) and Resilience (78) are good — claims are well-bounded and source-verifiable, with honestly acknowledged multi-GPU/continuous-batching gaps. Practical Impact (65) is a calibration-free deployment tool (Table 24) but no software artifact. Weighted: 0.20*82 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*65 = 78.
empirical Novelty 82 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 75 Resilience 78 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound: each decode step moves full model weights and KV cache while computing only a single token, leaving GPU compute underutilized. This bottleneck has motivated a family of parallel decoding methods — speculative decoding, multi-token prediction (MTP), and diffusion language models (DLLMs) — that process multiple decode positions in a single forward pass. Practitioners and researchers select the number of parallel positions (draft length, prediction length, block size) guided by the intuition that idle compute makes additional positions essentially free until the compute-to-memory balance point is reached. This intuition provides no concrete, platform-specific prediction of how many positions are actually near-free, and, as the paper demonstrates, systematically over-predicts the realized boundary by large factors for MoE and Attention modules. The gap matters because over-exposing parallelism beyond the actual near-free budget wastes latency without algorithmic benefit. Key Idea The core insight is that the latency of a multi-position decode forward is not a smooth function of N: it is a staircase. MoE kernels in vLLM and SGLang pad routed expert-token workloads to BLOCK_SIZE_M multiples before dispatching GEMMs, so runtime FLOPs are constant within each block and jump discretely at block boundaries. FlashAttention and FlashInfer execute query positions in tiles of size kBlockM/CTA_TILE_Q, so attention work likewise jumps at tile boundaries. These implementation-induced granularity effects — not resource balance alone — set the effective NFP boundary for MoE FFNs and Attention, while Dense FFNs (unfused torch.matmul) do exhibit smooth AI scaling and are correctly predicted by the idle-compute model. The insight is that "near-free parallelism" should be explained by whichever of these two slack sources is binding, and they combine via a simple minimum across modules. Methodology The paper proceeds in two stages. At the module level, the authors isolate Dense FFNs (two-GEMM, cuBLAS), MoE FFNs (fused vLLM v0.9.1 and SGLang v0.5.9 kernels), and Attention (FlashAttention-2, FlashInfer) on NVIDIA H20, A800, and H800. For each module they: (1) derive the idle-compute boundary Nidle by setting AI(N) = ρ = ϕ/β and solving for N; (2) measure T(N) via 10 rounds × 200 iterations with CUDA event timing, reporting median latency; (3) define Nmax = Nmax(0.2) as the largest N with T(N) ≤ 1.2·T(1); and (4) compare Nmax to Nidle. For Dense FFNs, AIdense(N) ≈ 2bN/s gives Ndense_idle ≈ ρs/(2b), predicting 1/b scaling. For MoE FFNs, additional activation traffic from dispatch/combine yields Nmoe_idle; source inspection reveals BLOCK_SIZE_M=16 in the small-token branch of both backends, giving Mmoe=16 and load-balanced bound min(MmoeE/k, E, Mattn). For Attention, AI(N) = 2NL/((L+N)s) gives Nattn_idle but the measured boundary is L-independent and equals Mattn (kBlockM=64 for FA2 on H20 with head_dim=128). At the model level, the first-exiting-module approximation gives Ndense_max ≈ min(ρs/2b, Mattn) and Nmoe,bal_max ≈ min(MmoeE/k, E, Mattn), with no free parameters. This is validated on WeDLM-8B (36-layer dense DLLM, nano-vLLM+FA2) and LLaDA-2.1-mini (20-layer MoE DLLM, E=256, k=8, SGLang+FlashInfer), plus AR counterparts Qwen3-8B and Ling-2.0-mini. A sensitivity sweep over ε ∈ {0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30} confirms that mechanism identification is threshold-independent for granularity-governed modules. Key Results Dense FFNs: measured Nmax tracks Ndense_idle ≈ ρs/(2b) closely across all three GPUs and batch sizes 1–32; on H20 (ρ=37), b=1 gives Nmax≈32-37 and halves with each batch-size doubling. MoE FFNs (load-balanced, vLLM, H20, E=256): Nmax follows min(16×256/k, 256) — specifically 256, 256, 256, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16 for k=2,4,8,16,32,64,128,256 — matching the granularity-based prediction and diverging sharply from Nmoe_idle (~1453 at k=8 vs. measured ~256, a 5.7× discrepancy just for MoE; the full model adds the Attention constraint, yielding ~64 vs. idle-compute baseline of ~1453, a 23× over-prediction). Attention: Nmax ≈ 64 (FA2, head_dim=128) regardless of L ∈ {1024,…,32768}, whereas Nattn_idle predicts strong L-dependence and correctly converges only at extremely long L. Full-model validation on WeDLM-8B: predicted and measured boundaries agree within the granularity step size across L ∈ {128,256,384,512} on H20, A800, H800. Deployment table (Table 24): on H20 b=1 dense models, principle and idle-compute agree (~37); on A800 b=1, idle-compute over-predicts 2.4× (153 vs. 64, Attention-limited); on H800 b=1, 4.6× (295 vs. 64). For MoE balanced E=256, k=8 on H20: 23× over-prediction. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.30851v1, May 2026); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW CONCEPT (NFP as a formal, measurable system-side capacity) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (kernel-granularity slack dominates over idle-compute slack for MoE FFNs and Attention in production backends) + NEW METHOD (closed-form NFP prediction principle with source-code grounding). The idle-compute intuition for decoding is widespread; the finding that padding and tiling create a qualitatively different, larger-than-predicted near-free region for MoE/Attention is non-obvious and was not previously characterized. The Dense FFN analysis, by contrast, is a competent validation of standard roofline reasoning rather than a new contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are replaced by the stronger mechanism-attribution methodology: latency curves, arithmetic intensity profiles, and runtime FLOP counts are jointly profiled using Nsight Compute, and the staircase behavior is confirmed by inspecting production kernel source code (Tables 3–7 for MoE, Tables 10–13 for Attention). The sensitivity sweep over ε confirms robustness of mechanism identification; boundary values shift by at most one granularity step. Experiments use a fixed random seed (torch.manual_seed(0)) and 50 warm-up + 200 timed iterations × 10 rounds. Cross-platform and cross-backend coverage is thorough: 3 GPUs × 2 backends × controlled routing patterns. No code or model weights are released; the paper-level reproducibility relies on the reader having access to the specific framework versions (vLLM v0.9.1, SGLang v0.5.9) and backend configurations. Granularity parameters are version-sensitive and documented in Tables 8–9 and 14–16. No standard benchmarks (MLPerf, etc.) are used; all baselines are internal to the paper's framework. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: All experiments run on single commercially available NVIDIA GPUs (H20, A800, H800) using production serving frameworks (vLLM, SGLang); the principle itself is a closed-form formula requiring only model config + hardware specs + backend version lookup. - Efficient: The NFP principle requires zero additional runtime cost — it is a pre-deployment lookup; the profiling needed to validate or extend it uses standard Nsight Compute tooling with negligible overhead relative to model serving. - Effective: The principle accurately predicts measured NFP boundaries without fitted parameters across all tested models, platforms, and backends, demonstrating it captures the operative physical mechanisms. - Scalable: Multi-GPU tensor-parallel and pipeline-parallel settings are not studied; the authors explicitly state this introduces communication overlap requiring separate modeling, leaving production-scale serving predictions unaddressed. The work delivers a paper-form analytical principle and deployment lookup table (Table 24); no software artifact, library, or tooling is released. Strengths - The dual-mechanism decomposition (resource slack vs. kernel-granularity slack) is theoretically principled, empirically confirmed with hardware profiling, and grounded in production kernel source code — a rare combination in systems ML papers. - The first-exiting-module approximation achieves accurate full-model prediction from module-level constraints with no fitting, demonstrating that the principle is genuinely compositional rather than empirically tuned. - Source-level evidence for both vLLM and SGLang (MoE) and both FlashAttention and FlashInfer (Attention) makes the mechanism identification verifiable by any reader with access to the open-source backends. - The deployment table (Table 24) quantifies the practical error of the prevailing intuition — up to 23× — with enough specificity (model type, GPU, k value) to directly guide engineering decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations forthrightly. Single-GPU execution with small batches and controlled routing isolates mechanisms but limits direct applicability to production multi-GPU deployments. The 20% latency tolerance is fixed (robustness confirmed in sensitivity sweep) and boundary values are tied to specific framework versions and BF16 precision. The sequential module execution model excludes potential effects of kernel fusion or cross-layer pipelining. The authors note that updating to newer backends requires only re-querying kernel configuration tables. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The load-balanced MoE model validation uses only b=1; real serving batches of size 4–32 would interact with MoE granularity in ways not studied (the τ=E branch-validity bound may be violated differently at larger N·b). - FlashInfer's query-tile selector is workload-adaptive with two distinct formulas for CUDA Graph vs. non-CUDA Graph paths (Section F.3), and the non-CUDA Graph formula is N-dependent — meaning Mattn itself shifts as N grows, potentially invalidating the constant-Mattn assumption in the principle for mid-range N. This interaction is not fully addressed. - Continuous batching, the dominant production serving mode (vLLM PagedAttention with dynamic batch composition), produces non-uniform sequence lengths that will interact with attention query tiling in ways not covered by the fixed-L experiments. - The paper restricts to BF16; for FP8 quantization, Tables 8–9 show BLOCK_SIZE_M changes to 64 (from 16), which would substantially enlarge the MoE NFP boundary — an implication not discussed. - No analysis of how the NFP boundary interacts with acceptance rates in speculative decoding (a joint system+algorithm optimization surface that the paper explicitly declines to address). Bottom Line Any practitioner or researcher tuning draft lengths, prediction horizons, or block sizes for parallel decoding methods should read this paper — it is the first principled, parameter-free tool for that design decision, and it demonstrates that the prevailing roofline intuition is not just imprecise but qualitatively wrong for MoE and Attention modules.
Recommended 2605.30851 PDF
#10 — Highly Innovative (78)

Towards understanding modality interaction in multimodal language models via partial information decomposition

W Fang, T Zhang, W Tao, A Chan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework applied to multimodal large language models, decomposing mutual information between model predictions and input modalities into unique, redundant, and synergistic components, and extends this to a conditional "Sensory PID" formulation for tri-modal (video–audio–text) omni-modal models. - Why it matters: Current MLLM evaluation reveals what models predict but not whether they genuinely fuse modalities or exploit language shortcuts — a distinction critical for understanding failure modes and designing better training curricula. - How it does it: The BATCH estimator learns a Sinkhorn-normalized coupling over mean-pooled modality token representations in the shared multimodal token space, enabling tractable PID estimation from model-induced predictive distributions without retraining the MLLM. - The good: Synergy (Svl) achieves Spearman ρ ≥ 0.798 (p < 0.001) with vision-removal accuracy drop across 20 diverse models on synergy-driven benchmarks, while total mutual information achieves |ρ| ≤ 0.118 for the same metric — demonstrating the decomposition captures functionally distinct signal beyond aggregate information. - The missing: The framework requires a discrete, finite decision space (multiple-choice QA); the unimodal conditionals are approximated via embedding masking rather than native inference, potentially biasing unique-information estimates; and the actionable reweighting demonstration covers only one model at full scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal interpretability, training data curation, or modality-use diagnosis — skip if you need open-ended generation analysis or deployment-ready tools, as this is a framework paper with preliminary actionable results.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (weights 20/25/15/15/15/10). Rigor (80) is a top contributor: peer-reviewed ICML 2026, robustness ablations (pooling, τ), 3 seeds with 95% bootstrap CIs, explicit no-leakage guarantee — docked only because no code/weights are released and the per-sample local PID scores lack a formal pointwise justification. Novelty (80) is high: applying PID to model-induced decision distributions is a NEW FORMULATION, and the sensory-synergy bottleneck and three-phase layer dynamics are genuine new empirical findings. Non-Obviousness (82) is strong — the decision-level vs. embedding-level distinction is a real conceptual insight prior work conflated. Completeness (82) reflects four directly stated, unminimized limitations; Resilience (78) is solid but bounded by the MCQ-only scope and the suggestive-only omni-modal analysis (3 models, 1 benchmark). Practical Impact (55) is the drag — a framework in paper form with only proof-of-concept reweighting and no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*55 ≈ 78.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 82 Resilience 78 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal LLMs increasingly serve as decision-making agents in medicine, science, and embodied AI, yet their evaluation remains outcome-oriented: accuracy tells us whether a model answered correctly but not which modalities drove the answer. Existing interpretability tools (attention visualization, cross-modal alignment metrics, modality ablation) either measure how modalities are encoded in latent space or how predictions change when a modality is removed. Neither approach can distinguish whether a modality's contribution is unique (only that modality carries the information), redundant (both modalities carry the same information), or synergistic (the information is only available through joint observation). A performance drop under vision removal, for instance, could arise from any of these qualitatively different mechanisms, requiring different interventions. The same surface-level label — "hallucination benchmark" — can conceal one modality regime (POPE: grounding-sensitive, synergy-dominant) versus another (Reefknot: text-prior-dominant), which ablation alone cannot distinguish. Key Idea The core insight is to apply Partial Information Decomposition to model-induced predictive distributions — the probability vectors pθ(y | xv, xt) the MLLM outputs — rather than to latent representations or ground-truth labels. PID (Bertschinger et al., 2014) partitions the joint mutual information I(Y; Xv, Xt) into four non-negative terms by optimizing over the set of joint distributions that preserve each source's marginal dependence on the target. Synergy is the gap between the true joint MI and the minimum achievable under this constraint; uniqueness and redundancy follow algebraically. Crucially, this computation characterizes functional information use at the decision level, not geometric proximity in embedding space. For tri-modal omni-models, conditioning on text T and decomposing I(Y; V, A | T) avoids the combinatorial explosion of full three-source PID while separating the directive role of language from sensory evidence. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, source representations: vision and text modality tokens are mean-pooled after projection into the shared multimodal token space, yielding per-sample vectors Xv, Xt ∈ ℝd. Second, PID estimation via BATCH (Liang et al., 2023): two 3-layer MLP encoder networks (hidden dim 32, Xavier init, Adam) learn an unnormalized affinity matrix A(y)_ij = exp(f₁(x_v^i, y)ᵀ f₂(x_t^j, y)); Sinkhorn–Knopp normalization enforces the marginal-matching constraint, yielding a doubly stochastic coupling Q̃ from which PID terms are recovered via the formulas Svl = IP(Y;Xv,Xt) − IQ̃(Y;Xv,Xt) and analogous algebraic expressions. Third, calibrated embedding masking: to obtain unimodal predictive distributions without retraining, the masked modality's projected tokens are replaced with Gaussian noise matching per-dimension statistics estimated from a profiling set D_prof. Three stabilization mechanisms guard estimation quality: valid-option renormalization restricts predictions to the candidate set C, confidence gating replaces low-mass predictions (< τ = 0.3) with uniform distributions, and soft aggregation estimates the marginal label distribution once from D_prof. For per-sample reweighting, local PID scores are averaged over K = 50 random batch draws (batch size 256) to reduce Sinkhorn-coupling variance; a fusion potential score FPi = [min{H(p_v^i), H(p_t^i)} − H(p_vt^i)]₊ identifies samples where joint prediction sharpens over unimodal predictions; GapScore combines fusion potential with low current synergy and low shortcut reliance. LoRA adapters (rank 16, α = 32) target the last 20% of transformer layers — motivated by the layer-wise analysis showing synergy emerges late. Key Results Across 20 vision–language models (2B–78B parameters, 7 families: Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen2-VL, Qwen3-VL, InternVL3, LLaVA-OneVision, Cambrian, Gemma3) and 6 benchmarks, PID clusters benchmarks into two regimes without prior labeling: MMBench, MMStar, and POPE are synergy-dominant (mean Svl > Utxt); MMMU, PMC-VQA, and Reefknot are text-unique-dominant (mean Utxt > 60%). On synergy-driven benchmarks, Svl achieves Spearman ρ(Svl, ∆vision) ≥ 0.798 (p < 0.001) and ρ(Svl, Acc) ≥ 0.718 (p < 0.001) across all 20 models; total I(V,T;Y) achieves ρ ≤ 0.118 for ∆vision. Model families cluster: Qwen, InternVL3, LLaVA-OV show joint-modality-reliant profiles (Svl dominant, ∆vision = 7–15 pp on synergy-driven benchmarks); Gemma3 and Cambrian show language-prior-reliant profiles (Utxt dominant, ∆vision < 2 pp even on synergy tasks), stable from 2B to 78B. Uvis is uniformly negligible (< 0.03 bits across all 20 model × 6 benchmark pairs), indicating vision-unique information plays little role in MCQ. For omni-modal models (Qwen2.5-Omni 3B/7B, VITA-1.5 7B) on MUSIC-AVQA AV-Fusion subset: Sav is a minor component everywhere (≤ 0.32 bits), Uvis dominates (1.25–1.42 bits), revealing a sensory synergy bottleneck. Video shuffling induces accuracy drops correlated with Sav at Spearman ρ = 0.94; audio shuffling at ρ = 0.88, confirming the visual dominance is functional. Layer-wise analysis on LLaVA-1.5 (7B/13B) shows a consistent three-phase pattern: silent encoding (0–20% depth), unimodal accumulation with Utxt rising rapidly (20–80%), and a late-stage synergy spike (80–100%) that is sharp and dominant on MMStar but weak on MMMU. LoRA-PID on Qwen2.5-VL-7B (3 seeds, mean ± std): +2.3 pp on MMStar (64.3 ± 0.3 vs. 62.0 ± 0.4 for uniform LoRA, 95% CI [1.4, 3.2]), +1.3 pp on POPE (88.5 ± 0.2 vs. 87.2 ± 0.3), −0.5 pp on MMMU (deliberate de-emphasis of language shortcuts), post-tuning Svl share rises from 67.5% to 73.9%. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is applying PID to model-induced decision distributions of modern MLLMs — prior PID applications covered neuroscience, small neural systems, and multimodal datasets, not LLM-scale models. The conditional Sensory PID formulation for tri-modal systems is new. The BATCH estimator is adopted from Liang et al. (2023); the authors explicitly state their contribution is the application to MLLM decision distributions, not a new estimator. The sensory synergy bottleneck finding and the three-phase layer-wise fusion dynamics are genuinely new empirical findings. The reweighting application is a methodological extension of standard LoRA fine-tuning using PID-derived signals. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations for pooling strategy (mean, last hidden, max) and confidence threshold τ ∈ {0.3, 0.4, 0.5} show variations < 0.01 bits in Svl/Utxt estimates on MMBench, supporting robustness of the estimation pipeline. The reweighting experiments use 3 random seeds with 95% paired bootstrap CIs (10,000 resamples) and an explicit no-leakage guarantee (evaluation benchmarks excluded from PID profiling and LoRA training). Hyperparameters for BATCH are fully specified (Table 5). No code or trained models are mentioned as publicly released. A methodological concern: the per-sample local PID scores are explicitly described as not axiomatic pointwise PID atoms (formal pointwise PID remains an open problem); they are BATCH-induced additive contributions whose validity as ranking signals rests on empirical consistency (ρ = 0.87 between row- and column-anchored scores) rather than theoretical guarantees. The entire framework is restricted to multiple-choice QA with a discrete candidate set — a scope that may inflate uniqueness of text and suppress visual contributions relative to open-ended settings. This is a peer-reviewed ICML 2026 publication. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The BATCH estimator is a 3-layer MLP with hidden dim 32, running on top of frozen model activations; no GPU-intensive training is required beyond the LoRA fine-tuning step. The pipeline runs on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: Stabilizing per-sample PID scores requires K = 50 batch draws of 256 samples per sample — computationally moderate for 3,000 profiling samples but uncharacterized at the scale of full training corpora (>100K samples). Layer-wise analysis requires storing all intermediate activations, which is memory-intensive for 78B models. - Effective: PID profiles predict intervention sensitivity with Spearman ρ ≥ 0.798 on synergy-driven tasks; reweighting yields +2.3 pp on MMStar vs. uniform LoRA for Qwen2.5-VL-7B. The framework fails to benefit language-prior-reliant models (Gemma3, +0.3 pp within seed variation), limiting applicability. - Scalable: Tested on models up to 78B parameters for profiling and on 3,000 training samples for reweighting. Scaling to open-ended instruction tuning corpora and larger fine-tuning sets is stated as future work with no evidence provided. The work delivers a framework and methodology in paper form; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The decision-level framing is conceptually rigorous: by computing PID over model-induced distributions rather than representations, the paper measures what information is functionally used in predictions, not what is geometrically aligned in embedding space — a clean and important distinction that prior work conflated. - The Sensory PID conditional extension for tri-modal systems is practically motivated and technically principled: treating T as a conditioning variable avoids the 2ⁿ-term expansion of full multi-source PID and preserves the interpretive distinction between task specification and sensory evidence. - The cross-model and cross-scale consistency of findings — model-family profiles stable from 2B to 78B, three-phase layer-wise dynamics across LLaVA architectures — lends empirical weight beyond cherry-picked results. - The LoRA-PID vs. LoRA-Random comparison (+2.8 pp on MMStar, 95% CI [1.6, 4.0]) is the cleanest piece of evidence: same weight distribution, randomly assigned versus PID-guided, isolates the informativeness of the PID signal from the weighting scheme itself. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge four limitations in Appendix C: (1) the framework is restricted to discrete decision spaces (MCQ); extending to open-ended generation requires estimating MI over high-dimensional sequential outputs, which remains unsolved. (2) Calibrated embedding masking is a proxy for native unimodal inference and may bias Uvis and Uaud estimates. (3) PID measures statistical dependence, not causal circuits — high synergy is consistent with but does not demonstrate that specific heads or neurons perform cross-modal integration. (4) PID-guided reweighting is a proof-of-concept and has not been scaled to larger corpora or stronger adaptation methods. These are stated directly and without minimization, which reflects well on the paper's intellectual honesty. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The omni-modal analysis rests on only three models (VITA-1.5 7B, Qwen2.5-Omni 3B/7B) evaluated on a single benchmark (MUSIC-AVQA); the "sensory synergy bottleneck" is a suggestive but not broadly validated finding. - The claim that Uvis is uniformly negligible (< 0.03 bits across all 20 models × 6 benchmarks) may be an artifact of the MCQ format: in multiple-choice settings, a model can often narrow to the correct answer via textual reasoning even when a visual display of the referenced object is present, depressing the measured vision-unique contribution independently of model behavior. - The mechanistic explanation for why Gemma3 fails to benefit from PID reweighting is post-hoc ("PID amplifies existing fusion capacity") and untested; a pre-specified hypothesis and experiment would be needed to distinguish this from a failure of the reweighting policy for that model family. - The local PID scores used for sample selection depend on batch composition and are averaged over 50 draws to stabilize, but their reliability for individual edge cases or highly unusual samples is uncharacterized. The theoretical justification for using these as ranking signals beyond aggregate PID estimation is informal. - The cross-model reweighting experiments report only three conditions (Base, Uniform, PID) for LLaVA-OV-7B and Gemma3-12B, omitting the Random, Acc, and Ablation controls that would confirm the PID signal is specifically responsible for any gains. Bottom Line A practitioner studying how MLLMs use modalities, or seeking to curate fine-tuning data to strengthen cross-modal fusion, will find the PID profiling framework genuinely useful and the key empirical findings credible — but should treat the reweighting application as preliminary and be aware the entire approach is currently limited to multiple-choice evaluation settings.
Recommended 2606.00959 PDF
#11 — Highly Innovative (78)

OpenWebRL: Demystifying Online Multi-turn Reinforcement Learning for Visual Web Agents

R Yang, Q Wu, Y Chen, H Bai, W Yao, H Cheng… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenWebRL, an open framework and trained 4B visual web agent that applies online multi-turn reinforcement learning directly on live websites, achieving 68.4% average success rate across three challenging live-web benchmarks. - Why it's important: Open web agents have lagged proprietary systems because they depend on expensive, static curated trajectory datasets (MolmoWeb uses 278K), creating a scalability bottleneck that online RL from live interaction can bypass at a fraction of the data cost. - How it does it: A 412-trajectory supervised warm-start primes a 4B VLM for productive exploration, then multimodal multi-turn GRPO with trajectory-level LLM-as-judge rewards drives improvement on live websites, with context costs kept tractable by discarding all but the most recent screenshot while preserving all historical reasoning traces as implicit memory. - The good: With only 2.2K RL training tasks and a 4B backbone, OpenWebRL-4B exceeds all prior open-source agents and beats GPT-5 SoM (65.8% avg) and OpenAI CUA (51.3% avg) on average success rate, demonstrating that online RL is substantially more data-efficient than large-scale SFT for this domain. - The missing: Official evaluation requires Browser-Use Stealth Browsers, a paid third-party service for CAPTCHA solving that creates a reproducibility barrier; further, 51% of observed failures are attributable to live-web infrastructure (CAPTCHAs, network failures) rather than model behavior, making it difficult to isolate true model capability from environment quality. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building open-source web or GUI agents should read this—it provides an unusually complete, ablated recipe with promised release of code, data, and models.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (85) drives the score — a fully open pipeline with promised release of training code, 4B/8B model weights, and 412-SFT-trajectory + 2.2K-RL-task data would be the strongest open foundation in this space, and the distilled Judge-8B removes the proprietary-API dependency. Rigor (75) is strong: seven controlled ablations isolate each design choice with several counter-intuitive findings, though error bars are absent and official numbers depend on paid stealth browsers with 51% environment-side failures. Novelty (65) is moderate — MM-GRPO is a straightforward multi-turn adaptation, but the reasoning-as-memory context design and live-web infrastructure are genuine. Non-Obviousness (70) reflects the surprising data-efficiency result (412 trajectories beating 278K) and the heavier-SFT-hurts-RL finding. Completeness (85) and Resilience (75) are high given honest limitation disclosure and the secondary no-stealth metric. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.30*85 = 78.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 85 Resilience 75 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Open-source visual web agents fail to close the gap with proprietary systems on long-horizon browser tasks requiring multi-step reasoning, visual grounding, and dynamic page adaptation. The dominant training paradigm is supervised fine-tuning on large curated trajectory datasets: MolmoWeb requires 278K trajectories and FARA-7B requires 123K+, making data collection prohibitively expensive and limiting coverage of the ever-changing open web. Prior online RL work for web agents either operated on text-only observations in simulated or self-hosted environments (WebRL, WebAgent-R1, RAGEN), or relied on costly proprietary evaluation frameworks (PAE, WebGym) that constrain open-research reproducibility. Three challenges specific to live-web visual RL differentiate this setting from controlled environments: non-stationary page dynamics including pop-ups, bot detection, and redirects; slow and brittle live browser execution; and the absence of reliable rule-based verification for open-ended tasks. Key Idea The central technical insight is that a small but carefully curated SFT warm-start (412 trajectories from a 235B teacher model, selected for brevity and website diversity) places the policy in a productive exploration regime, after which online GRPO with trajectory-level VLM-as-judge rewards handles the heavy lifting. The crucial enabling design is preserving all historical reasoning traces as implicit long-horizon memory while discarding all but the most recent screenshot, keeping multimodal context costs tractable without sacrificing the semantic context needed for multi-step planning. This reasoning-as-memory principle, combined with DOM-extracted per-action textual feedback that makes browser state changes explicit, allows a 4B model to maintain coherent long-horizon execution. Methodology The pipeline has three phases. (1) Data preparation: 292K WebGym task instances are filtered by benchmark overlap, near-duplicate removal via Qwen3-Embedding-8B cosine similarity (threshold 0.99 for SFT, 0.95 for RL), and website stability, yielding 15.6K SFT candidates and 2.2K RL tasks. A Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking teacher generates 4 rollouts per task judged by GPT-4.1; the shortest successful trajectory per task group is retained, capped per website, yielding 412 trajectories across 70 websites. (2) SFT warm-start: Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking undergoes 3-epoch multi-turn behavioral cloning with standard cross-entropy loss on assistant turns only. (3) Online MM-GRPO: 90 iterations with 15-step rollouts followed by 50 iterations with 30-step rollouts, alternating live Kubernetes-sandboxed browser rollouts and policy updates. Context at turn t includes the system prompt, task query, all prior model responses (reasoning + tool calls), all prior environment feedback strings (always preserved), and the K=1 most recent screenshots. The reward is gated: a format reward checks for valid thinking tag and parsed tool call; completed trajectories pass to a VLM judge that receives task instruction, final answer, 3 recent screenshots, and tool-call history. The final reward is +1 (format valid + judge SUCCESS), −1 (repeated format failures), or 0 otherwise. MM-GRPO assigns the same group-relative advantage to all response tokens across all turns of a trajectory, uses asymmetric clipping (ε_low=0.2, ε_high=0.28), trajectory-level dynamic sampling (discard all-identical-reward groups), 2 PPO epochs, and no KL/entropy regularization. Three critical design decisions are well-justified by ablations: preserving historical reasoning (−23.7 pp on OM2W if removed), including DOM-extracted textual feedback (−8.0 pp if removed), and using a 15→30 step rollout curriculum (−7.4 pp on WebVoyager vs. 30-step only). Key Results OpenWebRL-4B achieves 74.1% on WebVoyager (595-task FARA version), 67.0% on Online-Mind2Web (300 tasks, 136 websites), and 64.0% on DeepShop (150 shopping tasks), averaging 68.4%, with a 30-step evaluation budget. All prior open-source agents are substantially outperformed: FARA-7B (44.6% avg, 100-step budget, 123K+ tasks), MolmoWeb-8B (51.9% avg, 100-step, 278K+ tasks), Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking (62.3% avg, 30-step). OpenWebRL-4B surpasses proprietary systems GPT-5 SoM (65.8% avg) and OpenAI CUA (51.3%), while remaining competitive with Gemini CUA (69.3%). The 8B variant achieves 68.7% (30-step) and 69.2% (50-step). MM-GRPO contributes +16.4 pp over SFT alone at 4B scale. Pass@4 exceeds 90% across all three benchmarks for OpenWebRL-4B, versus approximately 60% for MolmoWeb-8B on OM2W. The distilled OpenWebRL-Judge-8B achieves 89.8% accuracy and 92.1% F1 against GPT-4.1 oracle on 500 held-out trajectories, outperforming GPT-4o (88.3% F1), Qwen3-VL-32B (87.8%), and WebJudge-7B (79.8%). A counter-intuitive finding: a heavier SFT initialization (1.9K trajectories, 3 epochs) underperforms the lighter one (0.4K, 3 epochs) after RL, suggesting over-imitation reduces policy plasticity. Average interaction steps decrease from 14.0 to 8.9 across RL training while per-step reasoning quality (history summarization presence rate: 14.5%→21.4%; blocker diagnosis: 14.2%→23.7%) selectively increases. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framework adapts existing components—GRPO (DeepSeekMath), POMDP formulation, ReAct-style tool calling, SFT-then-RL paradigm—to the live-web visual setting.
