The novel contributions are: (1) the first fully open pipeline for online visual web RL including fault-tolerant browser infrastructure on live websites; (2) the specific context management design that uses reasoning traces as memory while discarding historical screenshots; (3) a comprehensive ablation study that quantifies the contribution of each design choice; and (4) the distilled judge model. The MM-GRPO extension to multi-turn trajectories is a straightforward adaptation, not a conceptual departure from prior GRPO work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are extensive and controlled: rollout-length curriculum, screenshot count K, textual environment feedback, historical reasoning, SFT data scale and training epochs, dynamic sampling, PPO epochs, and judge model quality are each varied from the same SFT checkpoint. Error bars and multiple seeds are absent—standard practice for live-web evaluation given the cost and non-stationarity. The judge evaluation uses 500 independent held-out trajectories. One substantive concern: official success rates require Browser-Use Stealth Browsers (paid), meaning published numbers include the benefit of CAPTCHA solving and session stability not available in standard academic setups; the authors report a secondary metric (w/o aborted tasks) to partially address this, but the two metrics use different decoding parameters (stochastic vs. deterministic), complicating comparison. The 51% environment-failure share in the error analysis suggests published success rates substantially conflate model capability with infrastructure quality. Code, training data, and model weights are promised for open release (the paper is an arXiv preprint as of June 2026); the repository URL is listed as https://openwebrl.github.io. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: The production model is a 4B VLM running on commercially available GPU hardware; inference via SGLang at tensor parallel size 2 is feasible on 2× A100s. Training requires Kubernetes sandboxes and ~300 B200 GPU hours, which is accessible to well-resourced academic labs but not to most individual researchers. - Efficient: The K=1 screenshot context management and multi-tool-call interface minimize token overhead and round trips. Training produces 54K trajectories in 140 iterations; the distilled 8B judge eliminates ~$545 per experiment in GPT-4.1 API costs. The 8B variant adds marginal compute overhead for +0.3 pp improvement. - Effective: On all three benchmarks, OpenWebRL-4B substantially advances the open-source frontier and matches or exceeds several proprietary systems; however, 51% of failures are environment-side (CAPTCHAs, network failures), so model-only effectiveness under ideal infrastructure conditions is likely considerably higher than published numbers suggest. - Scalable: Tested at 4B and 8B with 30–50 interaction steps. No experiments beyond 8B parameters or 50 steps. Longer-horizon scalability is claimed but not demonstrated; the step distribution on OM2W (avg 11.5 steps to success) and DeepShop (avg 14.2) suggests the 30-step budget is near-binding for many tasks, and behavior at 100+ steps is unknown. The work delivers training code, model weights, and training data (promised for release), making it a reusable system artifact. Strengths - The ablation study is unusually thorough and honest: it isolates each of seven design choices with controlled comparisons and reports several counter-intuitive findings (heavier SFT hurts RL outcomes; adding a second historical screenshot does not consistently help), which is more informative than most systems papers in this space. - The distilled OpenWebRL-Judge-8B, trained from 12.5K GPT-4.1-labeled trajectories, achieves 92.1% F1 while eliminating API cost—this is a practical contribution that directly enables community replication without proprietary dependencies. - The mechanistic analysis of RL training dynamics (step reduction from 14.0→8.9, selective verbosity increase in semantically important reasoning steps, lexical proxy detection of history summarization and blocker diagnosis) provides genuine insight into what RL does to agent behavior beyond a surface performance improvement. - Data efficiency relative to prior work is striking: 412 SFT trajectories and 2.2K RL tasks versus 278K SFT trajectories for MolmoWeb, achieving substantially higher performance at smaller scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: the paid Browser-Use Stealth Browsers dependency creates a reproducibility and cost barrier; 51% of failures are infrastructure-caused rather than model-caused; RL training requires ~300 B200 GPU hours; and the work deliberately targets compact 4B–8B models rather than frontier-scale systems. These limitations are addressed directly and honestly, including the provision of a secondary evaluation metric and the promise of full open release, which reflects credible self-awareness. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The three benchmarks all use LLM-based success judges (GPT-4o for WebVoyager, o4-mini for OM2W, GPT-4o for DeepShop), and the training judge is GPT-4.1; there is potential circularity between the training signal and evaluation signal that the paper does not fully address, particularly since the distilled judge was trained on GPT-4.1 labels from the same task distribution. - The official evaluation metric (stochastic decoding, stealth browsers) and the ablation metric (deterministic decoding, no stealth browsers) differ in both decoding and infrastructure—this makes it difficult to attribute ablation-study differences cleanly to the ablated factor. - The plasticity hypothesis (heavier SFT → worse RL) is compelling but not directly measured: no ablation of gradient norm, entropy, or policy diversity before RL entry supports the mechanism beyond the outcome-level observation. - All three benchmarks are drawn from general-purpose consumer web navigation; the framework has not been tested on specialized domains (scientific literature access, enterprise web apps) where visual layouts and anti-automation measures differ substantially. - The paper beats Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking with a 4B model, a striking result, but the 235B baseline is a general-purpose model without web-specific post-training—a domain-fine-tuned 235B baseline would provide a stronger upper-bound comparison. Bottom Line A practitioner building open-source visual web agents gains substantial, immediately actionable value from this paper: the recipe is unusually well-documented and ablated, the claimed open release of code, data, and models would make it the strongest open foundation in this space, and the core finding—that 412-trajectory SFT warm-start plus 2.2K-task online RL beats 278K-trajectory SFT at competitive cost—is a practically important result if it holds across broader deployment settings.
Citations 2606.02031 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (78)

Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring

R Bommasani, SH Bana, KA Creel, D Jurafsky, P Liang · arxiv.org · Percy Liang - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper provides the first independent empirical study of deployed algorithmic hiring decisions across multiple employers from a single vendor, demonstrating that pymetrics' game-based screening algorithms produce statistically significant racial adverse impact at the per-position level and systemic rejection rates that exceed what chance would predict under an independence baseline. - Why it's important: Over 90% of U.S. employers use hiring algorithms, with a handful of vendors (e.g., HireVue, used by 60%+ of the Fortune 100) controlling screening across thousands of employers simultaneously, meaning a single vendor's biased or correlated models can block the same individuals and groups from employment economy-wide. - How it does it: The study exploits three properties unique to algorithmic hiring: (1) per-position rather than aggregate EEOC impact ratio computation, which vendor-reported aggregate statistics conceal; (2) a Poisson-Binomial independence baseline to isolate correlated model behavior from base rejection rates; and (3) the deterministic replicability of ML models to simulate counterfactual outcomes for 939 applicants across all 495 pymetrics models. - The good: The comparison against Kline et al.'s (2022) 83,000-resume correspondence study across 108 Fortune 500 firms — which fits the independence baseline (χ² = 20.05, p = 0.69) while pymetrics data does not (χ² = 18,481, p < 0.001) — provides compelling empirical isolation of the monoculture effect. - The missing: The study covers only a single vendor using game-based assessments and lacks ground-truth measures of applicant quality or job fit, so it cannot distinguish discriminatory disparate impact from differential predictive validity across racial groups. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers in algorithmic fairness, AI policy, and labor economics; the empirical contribution is significant enough that anyone working on vendor-concentrated AI systems in high-stakes domains should engage with it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the cluster's strongest entry and the only peer-reviewed one (accepted to FAccT '26). Novelty (85) is driven by an unprecedented first-of-its-kind dataset — 4.2M deployment-time algorithmic hiring decisions with cross-employer applicant tracking — plus the per-position-vs-aggregate disaggregation that falsifies the vendor's own compliance claim. Rigor (78) reflects BH multiple-testing correction and the elegant Kline et al. independence-baseline natural experiment that isolates the monoculture mechanism, tempered by 62% unquantified missing-race data and a DUA that blocks code/data release. Non-Obviousness (80) is high — that aggregate impact ratios >0.8 can conceal widespread per-position adverse impact is genuinely surprising. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) are strong: limitations are confronted directly, though threshold sensitivity is absent. Practical Impact (70) is high for an empirical/policy contribution despite no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*85 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 = 78.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation When multiple employers delegate applicant screening to the same algorithmic vendor, the vendor's models become a shared bottleneck across the labor market: an applicant rejected by one employer's algorithm may be identically rejected by all employers using the same vendor, not because of genuine poor fit but because of shared or correlated model behavior. Prior theoretical work predicted this outcome homogenization under algorithmic monoculture, but no empirical study had observed actual deployed decisions across multiple employers from a single vendor. Single-employer studies cannot measure cross-employer rejection correlation; correspondence studies using synthetic resumes cannot observe the algorithmic layer or measure repeated-applicant outcomes; static audits of algorithms cannot observe deployment-time decisions. The specific gap closed here is: what do real, multi-employer algorithmic outcomes actually look like, and does the vendor's own public compliance analysis (reporting aggregate-level no-adverse-impact findings) accurately characterize the per-position discrimination landscape as required by U.S. employment law? Key Idea The paper's core analytical contribution rests on two moves applied in combination. First, it argues that per-position application of the EEOC four-fifths rule — rather than aggregation across all positions in a vendor's portfolio — is the legally correct and empirically appropriate unit of analysis, and demonstrates that vendor-reported aggregate impact ratios above 0.8 can coexist with widespread per-position adverse impact. Second, it exploits the deterministic replicability of trained ML models: because a pymetrics model always produces the same output for the same stored gameplay features, the authors could have pymetrics re-score 1,000 applicants against all 495 models regardless of what they actually applied to, generating counterfactual full-market outcomes that are impossible to compute for human decision-making. Methodology The dataset spans 4,197,168 applications from 3,372,132 applicants to 1,746 positions at 156 employers (December 2018–December 2022), provided under a Data Use Agreement that grants the researchers full independence. pymetrics trains a binary classifier per position using gameplay features from ≥50 current employees as positives and random database profiles as negatives; a probabilistic score p ∈ [0,1] is thresholded at 0.5 to produce binary "recommend"/"do not recommend" outcomes, with an average 41.8% rejection rate. Three analytical lenses are applied. (1) Adverse impact: The EEOC two-sample pooled-proportion z-test and the four-fifths impact ratio threshold (rg < 0.8 and zg > 1.96) are applied separately to each of the 1,746 positions with ≥30 self-reporting applicants; Benjamini-Hochberg correction bounds false discovery rate at α = 0.05. (2) Systemic rejection: The observed all-rejection rate P_observed(0) is compared against a Poisson-Binomial baseline P_baseline(0) parameterized by per-position selection rates, representing the expected systemic rejection rate if each position's decisions were statistically independent. The baseline's validity is confirmed by showing it matches Kline et al.'s correspondence data. (3) Counterfactual simulation: 939 applicants are scored against 495 models; an intermediate "connected set" regime expands each applicant's opportunity set from 1.23 observed models to a mean of 145, defined by transitive applicant sharing (A'sim = min(1, Asim·Bsim)), and systemic rejection is reported as a function of the number of sampled models. Critical design decisions: treating "do not recommend" as operationally equivalent to rejection (well-justified given 2,404 average applicants per position and prior evidence); using self-reported race with no imputation (conservative, potentially understating harm); applying BH correction rather than per-position uncorrected p-values (appropriate for false discovery control, though uncorrected results are also relevant for EEOC enforcement). Key Results At the aggregate level, impact ratios exceed 0.8 for all racial groups (Asian: 0.870, Black: 0.839, Hispanic: 0.916, White: 0.962), replicating pymetrics' own published finding of no adverse impact. At the per-position level (BH-corrected), 10.6% of positions adversely impact Black applicants and 5.3% adversely impact Asian applicants. 25.87% of applications submitted by Black applicants and 14.74% of Asian applicants are directed to positions with adverse impact. If these positions had recommended at parity with the highest-selected group, 11,513 additional Black applications and 29,320 additional Asian applications would have been advanced. Architecture & Engineering shows the sharpest Black-applicant impact ratio (0.646). Gender shows minimal adverse impact (Male: 0.10% of models biased; Female: 1.75%), but intersectional Female Black (8.48% of models biased) and Male Black (9.67%) analyses reveal substantial disparities. For systemic rejection, the observed rate for applicants submitting 10 applications is 4%, significantly above baseline (χ² = 18,481, p < 0.001). The observed exponential decay rate is 0.729 vs. baseline 0.760 — structurally similar but slower, indicating correlated rejections. In the counterfactual simulation, no applicant is rejected by all 495 models (worst case: 52 recommendations out of 495). Under the connected-set intermediate regime, achieving <0.1% systemic rejection requires 25 applications compared to 10 under the independence baseline — 2.5× more applications needed. The Kline et al. baseline comparison (108 Fortune 500 firms, 83,000 synthetic resumes) fits the independence model perfectly (χ² = 20.05, p = 0.69), providing credible evidence that the excess homogeneity is a feature of centralized algorithmic assessment rather than of high-volume hiring generally. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the dataset itself — the first independent cross-employer deployment-time record of algorithmic hiring decisions — and the counterfactual simulation methodology exploiting deterministic ML replicability. The adverse impact and homogeneity constructs are established; applying them per-position across a vendor's portfolio and demonstrating the gap between aggregate and position-level results is genuinely novel and has direct regulatory implications. The Poisson-Binomial independence baseline is extended from prior work (Toups et al. 2023) to the variable-position case. Rigor & Reproducibility Benjamini-Hochberg multiple testing correction is applied appropriately; the Kline et al. external validation is a genuine strength not commonly seen in fairness audits. However, 62.35% of applicants do not self-report race, creating an uncharacterized selection bias — the direction and magnitude depend on whether non-reporters differ systematically from reporters in ways correlated with model outcomes, and this is unaddressed. The counterfactual simulation covers only 939 of 1,000 sampled applicants due to pymetrics infrastructure changes, with no analysis of whether the 61 excluded applicants are systematically different. The data cannot be redistributed and the DUA restricts independent replication; no code or model weights are released. Sensitivity analysis for the 0.5 thresholding decision (noted to vary in practice) is absent. The study is a preprint posted to arXiv (2605.27371v1, May 2026) and is accepted to FAccT '26 (June 25–28, 2026, Montreal) — peer-reviewed venue, though camera-ready version may differ. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (observational study of a deployed system). - Practical: The per-position adverse impact analysis and Poisson-Binomial systemic rejection framework can be run by auditors or regulators with access to vendor-level applicant data; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Computationally lightweight (standard z-tests, Poisson-Binomial distribution evaluation); the binding constraint is data access, not compute. The counterfactual simulation required pymetrics to re-score 939 × 495 applicant-model pairs, tractable for any vendor with stored features. - Effective: The study credibly demonstrates both adverse impact (at per-position level) and supra-baseline systemic rejection in observed deployment data; it cannot establish causation or rule out that disparities reflect differential predictive validity rather than discrimination. - Scalable: The methods scale to larger datasets and more vendors; the connected-set matrix expansion is O(N²M) in applicants and models. The counterfactual simulation framework is only applicable to vendors that store applicant feature vectors and have deterministic scoring — not universally applicable to resume-screening or video-interview systems. The work delivers a paper-form empirical study with policy recommendations; no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released publicly. Strengths - The dataset is genuinely unprecedented: 4.2 million deployed algorithmic hiring recommendations with cross-employer applicant tracking, obtained from a vendor with full editorial independence — a data access model the authors themselves note is exceptional and advocate for as policy. - The per-position vs. aggregate disaggregation result directly falsifies pymetrics' published compliance claim using the vendor's own data and the vendor's own acknowledged legal standard, which is rare in empirical fairness work. - The Kline et al. independence baseline comparison is a methodologically elegant and credible natural experiment that isolates the monoculture mechanism from alternative explanations (high volume, strict acceptance thresholds, industry mix). - The counterfactual simulation produces an actionable policy-relevant number (25 applications needed vs. 10) that makes the harm concrete and quantifiable for regulators and job-seekers alike. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge directly and credibly: findings may not generalize to other vendors or resume-based approaches; there is no external measure of applicant quality or model validity, so they cannot determine whether systemically rejected applicants would have succeeded; they cannot determine whether a less discriminatory alternative algorithm exists; they do not know how employers use pymetrics recommendations downstream (human override possible); the study may paradoxically discourage future voluntary vendor data sharing; and the 62.35% missing race rate likely causes underestimation of adverse impact. These are confronted with appropriate specificity rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The non-reporter bias from 62.35% missing race data is the primary internal validity threat and is acknowledged but not quantified. If applicants who opt out of racial reporting differ systematically from reporters in their model outcomes, the estimated adverse impact rates could be substantially over- or under-stated, and the paper provides no sensitivity bounds. - The analysis disaggregates adverse impact by occupation but not by employer. If biased positions cluster at a small number of employers with unusual applicant pools (e.g., globally skewed applicant bases), the cross-employer monoculture interpretation weakens considerably. - Threshold sensitivity is absent: pymetrics uses 0.5 as the binary threshold in its published analyses, but the paper acknowledges that clients use ternary (recommend/highly recommend/reject) and quintile systems in practice. Adverse impact results are threshold-dependent, and no sensitivity analysis is provided. - The connected-set behavioral assumption — that applicants who share a position have similar preferences and would apply to overlapping sets of jobs — is unvalidated. The 25-application threshold result is sensitive to this structural assumption. - No temporal analysis: the dataset spans 4 years including pymetrics' August 2022 acquisition, during which modeling practices could have changed. Pre/post-acquisition stratification is absent. - The 61 applicants excluded from counterfactual simulation (infrastructure changes) are treated as ignorable; no characterization of this group or sensitivity analysis is provided. Bottom Line This paper delivers the most important empirical result in algorithmic hiring fairness research to date — vendor-level aggregation conceals legally significant per-position discrimination — and practitioners in fairness auditing, labor regulation, and AI policy governance cannot responsibly ignore it.
Recommended 2605.27371 PDF
#13 — Highly Innovative (78)

ReMoE: Boosting Expert Reuse through Router Fine-Tuning in Memory-Constrained MoE LLM Inference

X Zhu, X Liao, T Jiang, Y Zhang, L Wang, L Xiao · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ReMoE, a router-only fine-tuning framework that reshapes MoE routing traces to be more cache-friendly for memory-constrained edge inference by biasing gate parameters toward recently-selected experts, without modifying model architecture or inference runtime. - Why it's important: Fine-grained MoE models deployed on edge devices must stream expert weights from slow storage (UFS/NVMe SSD) at every decode step, making expert I/O the dominant latency bottleneck — a problem standard load-balancing training objectives actively worsen. - How it does it: ReMoE freezes all non-router parameters and fine-tunes only gate weights using a differentiable reuse-mass surrogate for Top-K overlap plus a Trust-KL anchor to a frozen reference router that prevents semantic drift. - The good: On a Jetson Orin NX with SSD-backed expert storage, ReMoE achieves 43.6–49.8% TPOT reduction (1.77–1.99× decode speedup) across ShareGPT, GSM8K, and HumanEval workloads — verified on real hardware, not simulated. - The missing: All primary experiments use a single model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite, 15.7B total / 2.4B active); no evaluation on larger fine-grained MoE models (DeepSeek-V2 full, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2-MoE), leaving scalability to production-scale models undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or optimizing MoE inference on memory-constrained hardware; the idea is simple, well-executed, and the code is public — but readers targeting large-scale datacenter serving will find the gains modest (8.4% vLLM throughput) and the setting (B=1, edge) narrow.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (82) is the strongest contributor: peer-reviewed ICML 2026, public code and checkpoints, real-hardware evaluation across multiple edge platforms, and a methodologically decisive CE-only control baseline that isolates the locality objective from generic fine-tuning. Non-Obviousness (78) is high — the training/deployment mismatch (load-balancing encourages dispersion while memory-constrained decode needs locality) is real and underappreciated, and the differentiable reuse-mass surrogate is a non-obvious solution. Novelty (72) is a genuine NEW METHOD but not a new formulation. Completeness (82) and Resilience (75) are solid: limitations are scoped honestly (B=1 edge only, EOR bound caveats), though the modest vLLM gains (+8.4%) lack confidence intervals and single-model-family evaluation caps generality. Practical Impact (75) reflects released artifacts and verified ~2× edge speedups. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*75 ≈ 78.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 82 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deploying fine-grained MoE LLMs on edge devices (smartphones, embedded AI accelerators, single-consumer-GPU machines) requires storing the full model on slow local storage (UFS 4.0, NVMe SSD) and loading expert weight tensors on demand during decoding. Because each token may activate a different set of K experts across 26+ MoE layers, decoding produces a stream of irregular I/O requests. Only a small expert cache fits in fast DRAM, so cache misses are frequent and expensive. Standard MoE training objectives include auxiliary load-balancing losses that spread tokens across all experts for expert-parallel training efficiency — a goal that directly conflicts with memory-constrained single-request decoding, where keeping adjacent tokens in the same expert working set would reduce I/O. This training–deployment mismatch is the gap ReMoE targets: no prior lightweight post-training method reshapes the routing trace itself to improve cache locality without architecture changes. Key Idea The single core insight is that the MoE router's gate parameters can be fine-tuned post-hoc — with all expert weights frozen — using a differentiable surrogate for step-to-step Top-K expert overlap, combined with a KL-divergence anchor to a frozen snapshot of the pretrained router. Because the Top-K operator is non-differentiable, ReMoE uses "reuse mass" mt = (1/K) Σ_{k∈E̅_{t-1}} P_t^(k) — the probability mass the current routing distribution assigns to the previously-selected expert set — as a smooth proxy that, when maximized, increases the likelihood the next Top-K selection overlaps with the prior one. The Trust-KL penalty keeps the updated router close to the pretrained distribution, preventing routing collapse and quality degradation. Methodology ReMoE operates as a post-training adaptation on an existing MoE checkpoint. A frozen FP32 snapshot of the gate weights serves as a reference router; only the trainable gate θ_gate receives gradients. The full training objective is L = L_CE + λ_KL L_Trust + α_t L_Loc, where L_Trust is the KL divergence from the trainable distribution P_t to the frozen reference P_t^ref, and L_Loc decomposes into four terms: (1) LReuse = -log(ρ + ε), where ρ is the sequence-averaged reuse mass — the primary locality driver; (2) LSmooth = symmetric KL between adjacent-step distributions, suppressing high-frequency routing jitter; (3) LLag = symmetric KL against distributions at lags D = {1,2,4,8,16} steps back, suppressing slow drift; (4) LWS = entropy of window-averaged routing distributions, encouraging concentration within a compact local working set. The auxiliary load-balancing loss L_aux is disabled during fine-tuning since it encourages expert dispersion. Teacher forcing is used throughout (input sequence fixed), though hidden states h_t are re-computed by the current model. Critical design decisions: (1) anchoring at the distribution level rather than the hidden-state level applies the constraint at the decision boundary and is architecture-agnostic — well-justified; (2) treating the previous Top-K set E̅_{t-1} with stop-gradient provides a one-directional reuse signal matching autoregressive decoding semantics — well-justified; (3) the locality warmup schedule (αt ramps linearly from 0 to 1 over Twarm steps) prevents over-aggressive locality forcing early in training — reasonable but not ablated. Fine-tuning uses 100K samples from OpenHermes-2.5, 2000 steps, AdamW with lr=5e-5, micro-batch 1, gradient accumulation 8, on one 80GB GPU (~7.9 hours for DeepSeek-V2-Lite). Key Results Routing locality: EOR improves from 27.3% to 34.5% (+26.4% relative) on DeepSeek-V2-Lite; from 16.95% to 21.56% (+27.2% relative) on Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B. Load-balance CV rises from 0.04 to 0.16, but unique experts visited per sequence is essentially unchanged (64.000 → 63.997), indicating step-level concentration without global collapse. Cache simulation (128 ShareGPT prompts, LRU, B=1, max_new_tokens=64): at C=6 (Top-K matched), uHR improves from 31.87% to 36.87%, #uMiss drops from 0.871M to 0.807M (-7.3%); improvements hold across LFU, FIFO, and Belady's optimal policy. Real-system vLLM (RTX 3090, moe-expert-cache-size=6): throughput 3.58 → 3.88 tok/s (+8.4%), TPOT 254 → 243 ms (-4.5%). Real-system Jetson Orin NX + llama.cpp (NVMe SSD, tighter memory budget): TPOT reductions of 44.8% (ShareGPT), 43.6% (GSM8K), 49.8% (HumanEval), corresponding to 1.81×, 1.77×, 1.99× decode speedup. Iluvatar BI-V150 GPU: 1.67× end-to-end speedup, 2.47× prefill speedup. Ascend 910B3 (Qwen1.5-MoE): +11.6% generation throughput. Capability: MMLU +0.09 pp, HumanEval +2.44 pp, GSM8K strict -0.76 pp (within noise), IFEval strict -1.11 pp — largely preserved. The CE-only control baseline (same data, same recipe, no locality objective) degrades EOR to 22.9% (below the pretrained baseline) and worsens all serving metrics, demonstrating that the locality gain is specifically attributable to the locality-aware objective. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The core contribution — post-training gate-only fine-tuning with a differentiable reuse-mass surrogate and KL trust anchor to improve cache locality without architecture or inference changes — is genuinely novel. Oracle-MoE (Zhou et al., 2025) also targets routing locality but requires redesigning the routing architecture and pretraining from scratch; ReMoE is a lightweight post-training adaptation on existing checkpoints. Cache-conditional expert rerouting (Skliar et al., 2025) achieves locality at inference time but trades quality against locality dynamically; ReMoE internalizes locality during training and deploys with the standard inference graph. The combination of a differentiable reuse-mass surrogate, a KL trust region anchored to the pretrained router, and the decomposed temporal locality objective is a non-obvious but well-motivated design. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: each of the four locality terms and the Trust-KL anchor are ablated individually. The CE-only control baseline is the most important ablation — it rules out the hypothesis that any continued fine-tuning of the router would yield similar gains. Error bars are absent from most main results (Tables 2, 4, 5) but are provided for the Qwen generalization table (Table 14). The sensitivity appendix (Appendix G) sweeps λ_reuse and λ_KL, showing the method is robust across a moderate hyperparameter range, though only two lag-set configurations are compared. Cache simulation is cold-start per prompt (request-isolated), which is appropriate for B=1 edge inference but may underestimate gains from warm-start scenarios. Evaluation uses max_new_tokens=64 for cache simulation, which may not represent long-generation behavior. Real-system evaluations use commodity hardware (RTX 3090, Jetson Orin NX, Iluvatar BI-V150, Ascend 910B3), not cloud VMs or synthetic benchmarks. Checkpoints and code are publicly released at https://github.com/BUAAOSCAR/ReMoE. This is a peer-reviewed ICML 2026 paper. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and systems paper with real hardware evaluations). - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware at every stage — fine-tuning on a single 80GB GPU, inference evaluation on RTX 3090, Jetson Orin NX, and Ascend 910B3 NPU. - Efficient: Fine-tuning cost is ~7.9 hours on one 80GB GPU (gate parameters only); inference adds zero overhead — no additional routing logic, no per-step rerouting computation, no modified inference graph. - Effective: The 43.6–49.8% TPOT reduction on Jetson Orin NX (SSD-backed, tight memory) directly addresses the stated bottleneck; the more modest vLLM gains (8.4% throughput) are plausibly explained by PCIe partially hiding miss costs, and the paper makes this argument explicitly. - Scalable: Tested on DeepSeek-V2-Lite (15.7B total, 2.4B activated) and Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B; no evaluation on larger models (DeepSeek-V2 full ~236B, DeepSeek-V3 ~671B) or multi-layer batched scenarios — scalability to production-scale MoE models is claimed but not demonstrated. The paper delivers fine-tuned checkpoints (a reusable software artifact). Strengths - The problem framing is precise and the training–deployment mismatch (load-balancing objectives encourage dispersion; memory-constrained inference needs locality) is a real, underappreciated gap that the paper makes quantitatively explicit with Proposition 3.1. - The CE-only control baseline is methodologically strong: it uses identical training conditions, same data, same frozen parameters, same compute budget, and shows CE-only actually hurts routing locality — definitively attributing the gain to the locality objective rather than generic fine-tuning. - Real hardware evaluation on two distinct edge platforms (Jetson Orin NX + NVMe SSD with llama.cpp; Iluvatar GPU with expert-kit) is more credible than trace-driven simulation alone, and the Jetson results show large end-to-end wall-clock improvements under realistic storage constraints. - The Trust-KL anchor is theoretically grounded (Pinsker inequality → L1 bound on distributional drift; sufficient condition for Top-K stability via probability margin lemma) and empirically validated through the w/o Trust ablation showing that removing it increases EOR but degrades perplexity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that the EOR-based fetch bound (Proposition 3.1) breaks under C < K, violated cache isolation (KV cache growth competing for the same memory pool), and aggressive prefetchers that evict previously-loaded experts between steps. They acknowledge that a moderate increase in routing imbalance (CV rises from 0.04 to 0.16) is acceptable only because B=1 with no expert parallelism; the method is explicitly scoped to memory-constrained single-request decoding and is not positioned as suitable for high-throughput batched serving. They note that on out-of-distribution domains, the expected effect is attenuation of cache-efficiency gain rather than quality degradation. They characterize ReMoE as a deployment-aware routing objective rather than a domain adaptation method. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All primary experiments use DeepSeek-V2-Lite (15.7B total / 2.4B active, 64 routed experts per layer, Top-K=6); the Qwen experiments in the appendix use a much smaller model (2.7B active). The key claim — that ReMoE is broadly applicable to fine-grained MoE models — rests on two model families, both relatively small by current standards; extension to DeepSeek-V3 (671B total, 37B active) or Qwen2-MoE remains undemonstrated. - The cache simulation uses max_new_tokens=64, which captures short-generation behavior (chat completions) but not long-context generation (code synthesis, document summarization) where locality dynamics may differ. - Training data is fixed to OpenHermes-2.5; it is unclear whether routing locality is preserved when the fine-tuned router is deployed on significantly different input distributions (e.g., a specialized scientific domain), or whether retraining on domain-specific data is needed. - The vLLM gains (+8.4% throughput, -4.5% TPOT) are modest enough that they could be within measurement variance — no confidence intervals or multiple runs are reported for real-system evaluations, making it difficult to assess significance. - The IFEval strict accuracy drops 1.11 pp (17.19 → 16.08), which is non-negligible for instruction-following tasks; the authors do not discuss this or compare against the CE-only baseline for IFEval. - The composability experiment with cache-aware inference-time rerouting (Table 12) uses a single heuristic implementation at β=0.5 and reports only estimated TPS, not measured end-to-end latency — the composability claim is suggestive but not rigorously established. Bottom Line ReMoE is a clean, well-executed engineering contribution that solves a real and underexplored problem — the routing-locality gap in memory-constrained MoE inference — with a lightweight post-training intervention that adds zero inference overhead and delivers verified hardware speedups of up to 2× on edge devices; practitioners building on-device MoE inference pipelines for DeepSeek or Qwen models should adopt it, though users deploying larger MoE models or batched datacenter serving will need to verify whether the gains transfer.
Search Results 2605.27081 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (77)

Towards Demystifying and Repairing LLM-in-the-Loop Vulnerabilities

Y Ma, J Rong, C Yang, L Quan, X Xie, Y Lyu, Q Hu · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLMCVE, the first benchmark of 205 real-world LLM-in-the-loop vulnerabilities (with 101 reproducible instances including Dockerfiles, PoC exploits, and problem statements), and presents an empirical evaluation of five LLM-based repair agents across ten agent–model combinations on this benchmark. - Why it's important: As LLMs become embedded in production software, a new attack surface emerges where the LLM itself acts as source, actuator, or target in an exploit chain — existing vulnerability databases and repair benchmarks do not distinguish these cases from conventional bugs, creating a blind spot for security tools. - How it does it: The authors manually filtered 2,888 raw vulnerabilities from 230 AI/ML components (sourced via Huntr, NVD, GitHub Advisory, OSV) down to 205 confirmed LLM-in-the-loop cases using a formal three-role definition (LLM as Source / Actuator / Target), then ran five agents (SWE-agent, AutoCodeRover, Agentless, OpenHands, Aider) with two models (GPT-4o and GPT-5.2-codex) against 101 reproducible instances, with correctness validated by five independent human reviewers. - The good: Even the best agent–model combination (OpenHands + GPT-5.2-codex) achieves only 53.47% Pass@1 overall and a ceiling of 28.57% on prompt-injection-specific CVEs, quantifying concretely that the LLM-in-the-loop class is materially harder than conventional benchmarks where the same agent scores 71.8%. - The missing: The paper diagnoses the repair gap but proposes no new repair technique; the prompt-injection subcategory has only 28 instances in the full set and 22 in the Python-only subset, making the headline 28.57% ceiling statistically thin; and no open-source models are evaluated. - Read it or not: Security researchers building repair agents or benchmarks for LLM-integrated systems should read this for the dataset, the formal vulnerability taxonomy, and the detailed root-cause breakdown; practitioners seeking guidance on how to actually fix these vulnerabilities will find only implications, not solutions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (78) is high for a benchmark paper — a formal three-role (Source/Actuator/Target) verifiable predicate plus the LLMCVE dataset, and the striking NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING that 93% of apparent LLM-supply-chain vulnerabilities are not LLM-in-the-loop. Rigor (80) drives the composite: 5-independent-reviewer manual validation of 1000+ patches (~500 person-hours), a credible contamination control via GPT-4o's pre-benchmark knowledge cutoff, and full artifact release at Zenodo — unusually thorough. Non-Obviousness (75) and Completeness (78) are strong; the root-cause breakdown (Incomplete Fix + Functionality Damage at 70%+) characterizes why agents fail, not just that they do. Resilience (75) is good but bounded by the prompt-injection subcategory resting on only 22-28 instances with no confidence intervals, and only two closed models evaluated. Impact (70) reflects a valuable released dataset/eval framework, though it stops at diagnosis with no repair method. Weighted: 0.20*78+0.25*80+0.15*75+0.15*78+0.15*75+0.10*70 ≈ 77.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLMs are embedded in frameworks (LangChain, llama_index, Vanna), orchestration layers, and agentic pipelines, they create a new class of vulnerabilities that do not follow deterministic code execution paths. Prior vulnerability datasets (SWE-Bench, Defects4J, CVE-Bench) and prior LLM supply-chain studies aggregate all vulnerabilities found in LLM-adjacent repositories without verifying whether the LLM actually participates in the exploit. This misclassification means agent repair tools are benchmarked on what are effectively conventional software bugs (buffer overflows, ReDoS, SQL injection) discovered in LLM component code, not on vulnerabilities that exploit the LLM's semantic behavior. The result is a systematic gap: repair tools appear capable but fail when deployed against the semantically driven vulnerabilities (prompt injections, trust-boundary violations, jailbreak-mediated RCE) that actually characterize LLM-integrated software. Key Idea: The core insight is that LLM participation in a vulnerability's lifecycle is a binary, verifiable predicate — the LLM must act as Source (entry point of adversarial input), Actuator (semantic transformer/propagator of a payload), or Target (the entity whose alignment or behavior is subverted) — and applying this predicate to existing vulnerability databases reveals that only 7% of apparent "LLM supply chain" vulnerabilities are genuine LLM-in-the-loop cases. Rigorously curating only those 7% creates a benchmark on which standard repair agents underperform by a wide margin relative to their traditional-benchmark scores, isolating the semantic reasoning gap. Methodology: The study proceeds in three phases. Phase A (benchmark construction): starting from 230 open-source AI/ML components listed on Huntr, the authors query NVD, GitHub Advisory, and OSV using exact component-name matching against designated affected-component fields (not free-text search) to collect 2,888 deduplicated candidate vulnerabilities. Each record is manually reviewed — at 100 cases per person-day (240 total person-hours) — to apply the LLM-in-the-loop definition, yielding 205 confirmed cases. From these, 101 are further prepared with a vulnerable commit, a Dockerfile, a reliable PoC exploit that serves as the evaluation oracle, and a synthesized problem statement. Phase B (automated repair): all 10 agent–model configurations are given the problem statement, vulnerable repository, and Docker environment and asked to generate a patch. Per-agent settings mirror original papers (SWE-agent: 75-step limit, $2 budget; Agentless: 40 candidate patches with ranking; AutoCodeRover: up to 3 retries; Aider and OpenHands: default benchmark configs). Models are GPT-4o (knowledge cutoff October 2023, predating all benchmark vulnerabilities, mitigating leakage) and GPT-5.2-codex (chosen as SOTA). Phase C (validation): PoC execution and test-case results are treated as preliminary references only; five independent researchers manually label all 1,000+ patches as pass/not-pass, resolving disagreements by discussion (~500 person-hours). Key design decision 1: strict component-field matching rather than description-text search avoids noisy candidates — well-justified. Key decision 2: GPT-4o's pre-benchmark knowledge cutoff is a credible contamination control. Key decision 3: manual validation over automated test suites — justified because a PoC covers only one trigger path and a passing PoC block does not imply the patch is correct or non-regressive. Key Results: On the 101-instance benchmark, the best overall Pass@1 is 53.47% (OpenHands + GPT-5.2-codex), versus 71.8% that the same agent achieves on SWE-bench-Verified. AutoCodeRover drops from 38.40% on SWE-bench to 12.68% on LLMCVE overall. For prompt-injection-specific CVEs (28 instances), the best Pass@1 is 28.57% (SWE-agent + GPT-5.2-codex; OpenHands + GPT-5.2-codex); GPT-4o-powered AutoCodeRover and Agentless achieve 0.00% on this subcategory. Upgrading from GPT-4o to GPT-5.2-codex consistently doubles or triples Pass@1 (e.g., Aider: 19.80% → 38.61%; AutoCodeRover: 12.68% → 36.62%), but monetary cost decreases for most agents despite higher token consumption, consistent with API pricing differences. Specialized repair agents (SWE-agent, AutoCodeRover, Agentless) do not outperform general-purpose agents (OpenHands, Aider): OpenHands reaches 53.47% vs. Agentless at 35.21% under the stronger model. Root-cause analysis of 626 failed repair attempts finds that Incomplete Fix and Functionality Damage together account for over 70% of validation failures across all configurations, while Syntax Error and Location Error — previously dominant in traditional repair studies — are much less frequent and diminish further with GPT-5.2-codex. Severity distribution in LLMCVE: 42.8% high (CVSS 7.0–8.9), 25.7% critical (9.0–10.0); top CWEs are CWE-94 (31 cases), CWE-502 (19), CWE-22 (17). LLM-as-Target is the most common role (149/205); LLM-as-Source is least common (39/205). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formal three-role taxonomy and the LLMCVE dataset are genuinely novel — no prior work applied a strict predicate to verify LLM participation in the exploit lifecycle before constructing a repair benchmark. The empirical finding that 93% of apparent LLM supply-chain vulnerabilities are not LLM-in-the-loop is a non-obvious and consequential result. The repair evaluation methodology (PoC oracle + 5-expert manual validation) is rigorous but not novel in itself. The insight that specialized repair agents underperform general-purpose agents on this class, and the 70%-incomplete-fix root-cause finding, are empirical contributions rather than algorithmic ones. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are not applicable (no new method is introduced). Manual validation by five independent researchers with joint discussion for disagreements is a strong process for patch correctness — stronger than automated test suites alone given the oracle limitations. Error bars and significance tests are not reported, which matters because the prompt-injection subcategory has only 28 instances (22 for Python-only agents), making percentages like 28.57% (8/28) statistically fragile. The GPT-4o knowledge-cutoff argument for zero-contamination is explicitly stated and well-motivated. Artifacts (dataset, Dockerfiles, PoCs, code) are publicly released at zenodo.org/records/19249830. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.28893v1, submitted May 27, 2026); findings have not undergone peer review. The choice to evaluate only two closed proprietary models (GPT-4o and GPT-5.2-codex) means reproducibility depends on API access, and GPT-5.2-codex's architecture and training details are not stated. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile — the paper runs agents on a constructed benchmark and measures Pass@1, cost, and time. - Practical: The evaluation runs on standard cloud API endpoints; the Docker environments are designed for reproducibility on any host with Docker and internet access to the model APIs. - Efficient: Agent costs range from $0.26 (Aider + GPT-5.2-codex) to $0.72 (Agentless + GPT-5.2-codex) per vulnerability attempt; time ranges from 80s (AutoCodeRover) to 1921s (Agentless) per attempt; Aider uses under 20,000 tokens while SWE-agent uses over 940,000 under GPT-5.2-codex. - Effective: The benchmark shows that current agents solve at most 53% of LLM-in-the-loop vulnerabilities and at most 28.57% of prompt-injection cases — the evidence establishes that existing tools do not adequately solve the stated problem, not that any new method does. - Scalable: The benchmark covers 101 instances; no evaluation at larger scale is attempted or claimed. Generalization beyond Huntr-sourced components is not tested. The work delivers a dataset and reproducible evaluation framework (software artifact at Zenodo), not a repair method. Strengths: - The formal definition of LLM-in-the-loop vulnerabilities with three verifiable roles (Source/Actuator/Target) is a genuine conceptual contribution that operationalizes a previously fuzzy distinction. - The finding that 93% of supposedly LLM-relevant vulnerabilities in existing databases are not actually LLM-in-the-loop is empirically striking and has direct implications for how prior evaluations should be interpreted. - Manual validation by five independent reviewers for over 1,000 patches (~500 person-hours) is unusually thorough for a benchmark paper and produces more reliable correctness labels than automated test suites alone. - The root-cause taxonomy (Incomplete Fix vs. Functionality Damage dominating at 70%+) precisely characterizes why agents fail, not just that they fail, providing actionable direction for future work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that component sourcing from Huntr may miss vulnerabilities in components from other platforms. They acknowledge that manual identification of LLM-in-the-loop vulnerabilities introduces potential bias, mitigated by multi-researcher cross-validation. They state that PoC execution alone is insufficient as a correctness oracle and therefore rely on manual review. These are stated directly and forthrightly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 101-instance repair subset is filtered from 205 by excluding cases with insufficient descriptions or inaccessible vulnerable versions, but the distribution of excluded cases by vulnerability type is not analyzed — if prompt-injection cases are disproportionately excluded or included, the repair evaluation statistics are skewed. The headline prompt-injection Pass@1 numbers rest on 22–28 instances, which is too small for the strong quantitative framing used; confidence intervals are absent. Only GPT-4o and GPT-5.2-codex are evaluated — no open-source models (Llama, DeepSeek, Qwen) — so the finding that "model capability is the bottleneck" is confounded with closed-model characteristics and API access. The paper evaluates agents operating on Python repositories (AutoCodeRover and Agentless explicitly limited to 71 Python instances); generalization to other languages used in LLM stacks (C++, Rust, Go) is untested. The cost paradox — GPT-5.2-codex costs less despite using more tokens — is attributed to pricing or caching but not investigated, weakening the efficiency analysis. Finally, no baseline non-agent method (e.g., raw LLM prompting without agentic scaffolding) is included, so the added value of agent scaffolding over raw completion cannot be assessed. Bottom Line: Any researcher building repair benchmarks or agents for LLM-integrated software security should read this paper for the LLMCVE dataset and the sobering empirical result that even SOTA agents fail on more than half of LLM-in-the-loop vulnerabilities and on nearly three-quarters of prompt-injection cases — but the paper stops at diagnosis and its quantitative claims in the prompt-injection subcategory rest on a sample too small to support the confidence with which they are stated.
Search Results 2605.28893 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (76)

MobileMoE: Scaling On-Device Mixture of Experts

Y Chen, H Huang, E Chang, J Szwejbka, D Desai… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MobileMoE, a family of sub-billion-active-parameter Mixture-of-Experts language models (0.3–0.9B active, 1.3–5.3B total) derived from a principled on-device MoE scaling law, trained through a four-stage recipe, and deployed on commodity smartphones via a custom fused MoE inference kernel. - Why it's important: All existing on-device LLMs are dense, so raising accuracy requires proportionally more active parameters and inference compute; MoE's decoupling of total capacity from per-token FLOPs offers a path to higher quality at lower latency and battery cost on memory-constrained devices. - How it does it: A generalized scaling law L(N_act, D, Ê, x) is fitted over a controlled three-dimensional ablation sweep (expert count E, granularity g, shared expert s), exploiting structural decoupling of these axes at fixed active parameters to avoid combinatorial search; the identified sweet spot (E=8, g=8, shared expert) yields 60 fine-grained routed experts plus one always-on expert with top-4 routing. - The good: MobileMoE-L (922M active) outperforms OLMoE-1B-7B SFT (1.3B active) by +4.5 points average over 14 benchmarks with 30% fewer active parameters, and MobileMoE-S delivers 1.8–3.8× faster prefill and 2.2–3.4× faster decode than MobileLLM-Pro at comparable INT4 memory on Samsung Galaxy S25 and iPhone 16 Pro. - The missing: The scaling law is fitted on runs up to 500B tokens—a 12× extrapolation to the 6T full-training regime—validated only qualitatively, and no ablation quantifies the individual cost of removing any one of the three sweet-spot decisions (E, g, s) at full scale. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building on-device AI for smartphones or wearables; the combination of principled architecture derivation, full training recipe, and actual hardware deployment data is rare and directly actionable.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (on-device deployment with a custom ExecuTorch kernel is the central artifact). Practical Impact (82) is the dominant contributor: real measured deployment on two silicon families (Snapdragon, Apple) with 1.8–3.8× speedups, a full four-stage training recipe, and a complete sub-billion-active MoE design methodology — the most actionable deployment treatment in the cluster. Rigor (72) is strong via controlled per-axis ablations exploiting structural decoupling, fair cross-model evaluation protocols, and 22 benchmarks, but is held back by no seeds/error bars on 2–5 pt margins, the unvalidated 12× token extrapolation, and no stated code/weights release. Non-Obviousness (72) is earned by the E=32 regression finding and the decoupling argument. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) are high given honest limitations (no NPU, distillation gap). NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. Composite: 0.15*70+0.15*72+0.10*72+0.15*75+0.15*72+0.30*82 ≈ 76.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation On-device LLMs must operate within ~5 GB DRAM and sparse per-token compute budgets to preserve battery and latency on smartphones. Every competitive on-device model (MobileLLM, SmolLM2, Gemma 3, Llama 3.2) is dense: improving quality requires proportionally more active parameters and thus more inference FLOPs. MoE architectures decouple total model capacity from per-token compute, but all existing MoE scaling laws target server-scale deployments (100B+ parameters) where memory is abundant and inference parallelizes across GPUs. The sub-billion active-parameter regime has no established MoE design methodology that jointly accounts for mobile memory constraints (governed by total parameters) and inference cost (governed by active parameters). Key Idea The core insight is that the three MoE design axes—sparsity (E), expert granularity (g), and shared expert (s)—are structurally decoupled at fixed active parameters N_act: changing E alters only total parameters (and thus memory); changing g leaves both N_act and N_total unchanged; adding a shared expert can be configured to preserve both. This decoupling justifies three sequential controlled ablations rather than a combinatorial sweep. A generalized scaling law that subsumes both Chinchilla and joint MoE scaling as special cases is then fitted independently per axis, and constrained optimization over mobile memory (≤5 GB INT4) and compute (F_inf = 2N_act) budgets identifies E=8, g=8, and a shared expert as simultaneously memory-optimal and compute-optimal. Fine-grained experts (g=8) increase routing combinatorics without touching memory, and the shared expert provides a generalist pathway that reduces expert imbalance and improves training throughput. Methodology Architecture derivation: A generalized on-device MoE scaling law L(N_act, D, Ê, x) = A_x Ê^{δ_x} N_act^{α_x + γ_x ln Ê} + B_x Ê^{ω_x} D^{β_x + ζ_x ln Ê} + c_x is fitted using two-stage optimization (scipy curve_fit warm start, then L-BFGS-B on MSE). Three ablations sweep E ∈ {1,2,4,8,16,32}, g ∈ {1,2,4,8,16}, and s ∈ {✓,×}, each training on N_act ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.9}B × D ∈ {100–500}B tokens (64 H100 96GB GPUs, 2–10 days per ablation). The memory constraint M = (b_w/8)N_total + (b_kv/8)·2T·n_l·n_kv·d_h ≤ 5 GB is imposed as a hard bound. Critical design decision #1 (well-justified): sequential ablation exploiting structural decoupling avoids O(E×g×s) full sweeps. Critical design decision #2 (well-justified): auxiliary-loss-free load balancing (bias-update-only, λ_lb=10⁻³) plus z-loss (λ_z=10⁻⁴) avoids auxiliary-loss instability while maintaining routing balance. Critical design decision #3 (well-justified): grouped MLP batching 60 fine-grained expert FFNs (each 768×384 in MobileMoE-S) into a single fused GMM kernel replaces ~200× smaller per-expert GEMMs with one batched operation. Training proceeds in four stages: pre-training (6T tokens, context 2048, cosine LR 4×10⁻⁴), mid-training (~500B tokens, context 8192, linear decay, domain-shifted to knowledge/code/math), instruction SFT (~80M samples, dropless dispatching), and INT4 QAT (symmetric group-wise INT4 group size 32, INT8 dynamic activations, FP32 router throughout). On-device inference uses a custom ExecuTorch operator: counting-sort token reordering by expert ID, single batched torchao INT4 GEMM per expert, and all sub-operations fused into one op call per MoE layer. Key Results All baselines re-evaluated under identical settings (greedy decoding, lm-eval with vLLM backend, bfloat16) on 14 foundational and 8 advanced benchmarks. Baselines include current strong models: Qwen3.5 2B, OLMoE-1B-7B, SmolLM2 1.7B, Gemma 3 1B, Llama 3.2 1B, MobileLLM-Pro. MobileMoE-L BF16 SFT (922M active): overall avg 60.1 vs. OLMoE-1B-7B SFT 55.6 (+4.5) at 30% fewer active and 23% fewer total parameters; vs. Qwen3.5 2B 50.8 (+9.3) at ~2× fewer active parameters; math avg 41.2 vs. OLMoE-1B-7B 18.2 (+23.0) and vs. Qwen3.5 2B 36.7 (+4.5); code avg 58.8 vs. OLMoE-1B-7B 33.1 (+25.7). MobileMoE-M (528M active) surpasses all dense baselines up to 1.9B active parameters including Qwen3.5 2B (+4.5 avg) at 3.6× fewer active parameters. MobileMoE-S (272M active) matches Llama 3.2 1B (46.9) with 4.4× fewer active parameters. INT4 QAT incurs 2–3 point average accuracy drop; MobileMoE-L INT4 (57.8 avg) exceeds OLMoE-1B-7B BF16 SFT (55.6) at ~5× smaller memory (2.75 vs. 13.8 GB). On-device runtime: MobileMoE-S vs. MobileLLM-Pro at comparable INT4 memory—Samsung S25: 1.8–2.2× prefill speedup, 2.2–2.6× decode speedup; iPhone 16 Pro CPU: 2.7–3.1× prefill, 2.8–3.4× decode; iPhone 16 Pro GPU (MLX): 3.6–3.8× prefill, 2.5× decode. Counter-intuitive finding: E=32 causes performance regression at sub-billion active scale despite more total parameters—expert count has a non-monotonic effect in the on-device regime. MoE advantage is strongest on knowledge (MMLU, NQ, TQA) and reading (BoolQ, DROP), reflecting MoE's larger total capacity for fact storage; reasoning is MoE's weakest relative domain. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (on-device constrained MoE scaling law), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (MobileMoE models + ExecuTorch MoE kernel), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (E=8, g=8, shared expert as jointly memory- and compute-optimal sweet spot at sub-billion scale). The individual components—fine-grained experts (DeepSeekMoE), shared expert (DeepSeekMoE/Qwen2MoE), auxiliary-loss-free balancing, z-loss, QAT, grouped MLP—are all prior art; the novelty lies in the principled joint optimization under mobile constraints and the demonstration that MoE is practical on commodity smartphone CPUs. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and controlled: each of the three design axes is isolated with all others held fixed, and results are reported both as compute-optimal loss curves (from the fitted law) and as empirical training loss vs. wall-clock plots. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for any benchmark numbers; given that many advantages are 1–5 points, seed variance is not ruled out. No code or model weights release URL is stated in the paper; ExecuTorch and torchao are referenced but no repository is provided. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2605.27358v1, dated May 27, 2026); no peer-review status is indicated. The evaluation protocol transparency (Appendix C.4) is a credibility positive: the authors show that chat-template protocols disproportionately inflate Qwen3.5 2B scores and choose the protocol that is fairest across models. Methodological concern: the scaling law is fitted on ≤500B-token runs but deployed at 6T tokens (12× extrapolation); the paper validates this qualitatively with monotonic improvement curves but does not provide quantitative prediction error at the 6T regime. Llama 3.2 1B uses knowledge distillation from Llama 3.1 8B, which is noted but not controlled for in the active-parameter efficiency comparisons. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on Samsung Galaxy S25 (Snapdragon 8 Elite) and iPhone 16 Pro (Apple A18 Pro) via ExecuTorch CPU with XNNPACK and Apple GPU with MLX, using commercially available INT4-quantized models with ≤5 GB peak RAM. - Efficient: MobileMoE-S uses 0.68 GB INT4 weight memory, peak RSS 1.49 GB at 8K context (22% less than MobileLLM-Pro's 1.91 GB); MobileMoE-L uses 2.75 GB INT4 weights, peak RSS 4.71 GB at 8K. Active parameters are less than one-third of comparable dense models, driving both prefill (compute-bound) and decode (bandwidth-bound) speedups. - Effective: Strong evidence across 22 benchmarks including real-prompt on-device profiling in code, math, and knowledge domains; instruction-following validated on IFEval and IFBench; multi-step reasoning on GSM8K (MobileMoE-L SFT: 77.6) and MATH-500 (32.2). - Scalable: Tested at three scales (0.3–0.9B active); no experiments beyond 5.3B total parameters or beyond 8K context length. Mobile NPU backends (Apple Neural Engine, Snapdragon NPU) not attempted—only CPU and GPU. The scaling law extrapolation to 6T tokens is validated qualitatively but not stress-tested at larger scale. Software artifact: Custom ExecuTorch MoE operator and INT4 QAT checkpoints; no public release URL stated in the paper. Strengths - The structural-decoupling argument that justifies sequential rather than combinatorial ablation is both theoretically correct and empirically confirmed, making the architecture derivation methodology reusable for other on-device MoE design problems. - Runtime validation is unusually thorough: two distinct mobile silicon families (Qualcomm, Apple), three backends (XNNPACK CPU, XNNPACK CPU, MLX GPU), real rather than dummy prompts, and peak RSS profiling that correctly identifies MoE's input-dependent memory behavior as a systematic difference from dense models. - The evaluation protocol analysis (Appendix C.4) demonstrates genuine methodological care: showing that standard chat-template evaluation collapses most baselines on MMLU-Pro/GPQA and choosing protocols that are fairest across all models, not just MobileMoE. - The finding that E=32 produces performance regression despite more parameters is a concrete, surprising result with direct engineering value, and the fitted scaling law curves (Figure 3) give practitioners a quantitative tool for choosing E under any memory budget. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that MobileMoE trails Qwen3.5 2B on instruction following and knowledge & reasoning, attributing this to the absence of distillation and thinking-enabled post-training in their recipe. Future work is identified as distillation, reasoning-oriented post-training, multimodal extensions, dynamic routing, expert pruning, mixed-precision quantization, and mobile NPU deployment. No claim is made for NPU support; the paper is explicit that the current deployment covers CPU and GPU backends only. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The scaling law is fitted at ≤500B tokens but applied at 6T; the 12× extrapolation is validated by monotonic improvement curves but prediction error at the full-training regime is never quantified—making the "principled" architecture claim partially circular. - Qwen3 MoE (128 experts, top-8, no shared expert) and other fine-grained MoE models at comparable active-parameter scales are cited but not compared against, leaving open whether the specific (E=8, g=8, s=✓) configuration is globally optimal or optimal within the paper's ablation design space. - No ablation in the final 6T-trained model tests what removing any single sweet-spot decision (e.g., g=1 vs. g=8 at full scale) costs; all three ablations are conducted only at ≤500B tokens, so interaction effects at full scale are unstudied. - No confidence intervals or multiple seeds are reported on benchmark numbers; several key advantages over baselines are in the 2–5 point range where seed variance in small models can be substantial. - Llama 3.2 1B uses knowledge distillation from Llama 3.1 8B, making the "4.4× fewer active parameters with comparable accuracy" comparison not apples-to-apples; the paper notes this but does not attempt to quantify its effect. - Real-prompt peak RSS rises to 1.2–2.1× dummy-prompt RSS for MoE models, but the practical worst-case memory for diverse user prompts in deployment is not characterized—the conservative Peak RSS upper bound could still underestimate tail cases. Bottom Line A practitioner building on-device LLM systems for smartphones will find this paper directly actionable: the architectural sweet spot (E=8, g=8, shared expert), four-stage training recipe, and custom ExecuTorch kernel collectively deliver a well-evidenced Pareto improvement over dense baselines on real hardware, making this the most complete treatment of sub-billion-active-parameter MoE deployment available.
Recommended 2605.27358 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (76)

Attention-guided Fine-tuning of Multimodal Large Language Models Improves Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

S Sinha, G Xiong, B Liu, Z He, A Zhang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Attentive-CoT (Att-CoT), an attention-guided fine-tuning objective that augments standard CoT-SFT with two auxiliary loss terms — one penalizing premature answer commitment and one rewarding sustained visual-token attention during rationale generation — improving CoT accuracy across six MLLMs on three visual reasoning benchmarks. - Why it's important: Chain-of-thought prompting, which reliably helps text-only LLMs, consistently degrades accuracy in multimodal LLMs on visually grounded tasks, yet practitioners routinely apply CoT-SFT as a default recipe without understanding why it fails or how to fix the SFT objective itself. - How it does it: Att-CoT adds two differentiable losses to the standard SFT cross-entropy: a softplus penalty that discourages high answer-token probability during the early half of the rationale, and a negative log-attention loss that encourages the model to allocate attention mass to visual tokens at every reasoning step. - The good: Task-relevant visual obfuscation experiments show Att-CoT confidence drops of 59.85% (CLEVR) and 44.23% (ChartQA) versus 28.05% and 15.64% for SFT, providing causal evidence that the method actually grounds reasoning in image-relevant regions rather than merely inflating aggregate visual-token attention. - The missing: The method mandates eager (non-flash) attention during both training and evaluation, which imposes a concrete throughput and memory cost that is acknowledged but not quantified; behavior at 70B+ scale is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers working on MLLM fine-tuning or CoT faithfulness analysis should read it; the diagnostic metrics (EC-AUC, ATR, Vis-Dep) are reusable tools, and the failure-mode characterization is cleaner than prior work, though the method's interaction with RL-based post-training remains open.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Scientific Rigor (84) is the dominant driver and the strongest in the cluster: six MLLM backbones at two scales each, three in-domain plus two OOD benchmarks, 3-seed averaging (std ≤0.02), informative loss-term ablations (Table 6), and a causal task-relevant obfuscation experiment that goes beyond correlational attention analysis. Completeness (82) and Resilience (80) are high — the authors confront three scope boundaries directly (RL post-training, closed-source models, diffusion LMs) and honestly report that LLaVA-CoT test-time scaling yields meager gains on modern backbones. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are solid but not top-tier: the two auxiliary losses are a targeted, well-validated application rather than a new formulation. Practical Impact (60) is held back by no released code/weights and an unquantified eager-attention deployment cost, with no evaluation above 12B. Weighted composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*84 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*60 = 76.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 84 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 82 Resilience 80 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners deploying multimodal large language models on visually intensive tasks — chart question answering, compositional spatial reasoning, fine-grained visual understanding — rely on chain-of-thought prompting and CoT-style supervised fine-tuning as default strategies inherited from text-only LLM practice. The problem is that CoT reliably hurts accuracy on these tasks: across ChartQA, CLEVR, and CV-Bench, both Qwen2.5-VL and InternVL3.5 off-the-shelf models score lower with CoT prompting than with direct answering (e.g., QVL-7B drops from 83.44% to 79.23% on ChartQA, 70.75% to 66.96% on CV-Bench). The gap between text-only CoT success and multimodal CoT failure has been noted anecdotally in prior work but not subjected to a systematic mechanistic account. Prior fixes (LLaVA-CoT, LLaVA-Reasoner-DPO) optimize what rationale the model generates rather than how reasoning dynamically engages with visual evidence, and they improve the SFT data recipe or add a post-SFT DPO stage rather than modifying the SFT loss itself. Key Idea: The central insight is that CoT failures in MLLMs stem from a structural mismatch between when answer confidence peaks and when visual tokens are accessed: models commit to the answer early in the rationale (high answer probability before reasoning is complete) while reasoning tokens attend weakly to visual representations, effectively generating text around a pre-selected answer rather than computing the answer from image evidence. The fix is to directly penalize early answer commitment and directly reward visual-token attention during the think span as auxiliary training objectives — not through data curation or inference-time search, but through differentiable losses computed from quantities already materialized during the forward pass. Methodology: The pipeline begins with CoT data construction: ground-truth rationales are distilled from GPT (the paper references 'gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07') via a two-agent pipeline (generator + oracle verifier), followed by a manual two-pass quality check (doctoral-level researchers filtering <1% of 1,000 sampled examples with 100% inter-annotator agreement). Training then applies the Att-CoT objective on three datasets (15,000 ChartQA train samples, 10,000 CLEVR train samples, 1,500 CV-Bench train samples) across six MLLM backbones. The objective is: L_Att-CoT = L_SFT + L_VG + L_DC where L_SFT is standard cross-entropy over rationale and answer tokens; L_DC = E[1/|R| Σ_{t∈R} log(1 + exp(log π_t − log π_{t_ans}))] penalizes positions in the first half of the rationale (t ≤ T/2) where the probability of the first answer token exceeds its value at the answer start; and L_VG = E[−1/|R| Σ_{t∈R} log A_{t,vis}] penalizes low average attention mass to visual-token indices during rationale generation, where A_{t,vis} is averaged over all L layers and H heads. All three loss coefficients are set to 1.0. The method adds no trainable parameters and reuses attention maps already computed during the forward pass; the asymptotic cost above baseline SFT is O(TLHNv), negligible relative to the O(LT²d) dominant cost. A critical design requirement: eager (non-FlashAttention) attention must be used to obtain deterministic per-head attention weights — a constraint not discussed in terms of its throughput implications. Training uses AdamW, linear LR schedule (max 1e-5, 2e-6 for vision tower), 2 A100 GPUs, batch size 4, FP16, up to 5 epochs with validation-loss early stopping. Key Results: Att-CoT outperforms standard CoT-SFT across all six model families on all three in-domain test sets and both OOD benchmarks (EvoCharts for chart reasoning, Super-CLEVR for spatial reasoning). Specific gains over SFT: ChartQA +2–3% in-domain (e.g., IVL-4B: 86.39%→86.84%, Gemma-12B: 89.45%→90.72%), EvoCharts +2–5% OOD (QVL-3B: 45.53%→50.24%, Gemma-12B: 55.21%→57.22%); CLEVR +3–9% in-domain (IVL-4B: 65.50%→74.50%, QVL-7B: 85.50%→87.40%), Super-CLEVR +3–4%; CV-Bench +2–4% (Gemma-4B: 66.85%→74.24%). Att-CoT consistently outperforms both LLaVA-CoT and LLaVA-Reasoner-DPO, which in several cases underperform even standard SFT. Gains are larger on the 'Human' (complex multi-step) ChartQA split (+4.3 pp on QVL-3B Human vs. +0.2 pp on Machine), showing the method helps where reasoning genuinely matters. The visual obfuscation experiment is the most convincing single result: masking task-relevant image regions causes Att-CoT confidence to drop 59.85% on CLEVR and 44.23% on ChartQA, versus 28.05%/15.64% for SFT, indicating grounding is on content-relevant visual evidence, not arbitrary visual tokens. Baselines are current (Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL3.5, Gemma3) and strong relative to the prior LLaVA-based comparisons in the literature; results are averaged over 3 seeds with std dev ≤0.02. Testing scale is moderate (3B–12B parameters); no 70B+ evaluation is reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The three diagnostic metrics (EC-AUC, ATR, Vis-Dep) and their joint application to characterize CoT failure modes in MLLMs constitute a genuine empirical contribution. Att-CoT's two auxiliary loss terms are novel in the MLLM CoT-SFT context — no prior work has modified the SFT objective using attention-based constraints for visual grounding. The individual components (penalizing early commitment via logit comparisons, attention regularization) are conceptually straightforward; the novelty is in their targeted application and validation across diverse model families. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative (Table 6): L_DC substantially reduces EC-AUC (0.897→0.686), L_VG primarily improves ATR and Vis-Dep, and together they achieve the best results. Results are reported over 3 seeds with std dev ≤0.02, providing minimal but non-zero statistical grounding — no significance tests. The paper acknowledges (citing Jain & Wallace 2019) that attention is not explanation and validates the attention proxy through counterfactual interventions and accuracy — a reasonable safeguard. The obfuscation test (manually curated 500 examples) provides causal support. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released. The CoT data construction relies on 'gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07', a model that external researchers may not have identical access to, limiting full reproducibility of the training data. The baseline replications are documented in detail in the Appendix, and the method's interaction with the LLaVA-CoT test-time scaling (Best-of-4) is honestly reported as yielding meager gains over SFT on modern backbones — a credibility-positive finding. No evidence of Goodhart's Law: OOD benchmarks (EvoCharts, Super-CLEVR) show similar trends to in-domain benchmarks. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Training runs on 2 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with batch size 4; inference requires eager (non-FlashAttention) attention, which is functional on standard hardware but may be prohibitive in production throughput-sensitive deployments. - Efficient: Training overhead above standard CoT-SFT is asymptotically negligible (O(TLHNv) additional operations); however, mandating eager attention roughly doubles memory bandwidth relative to FlashAttention and is not quantified in wall-clock or GB terms. - Effective: Evidence covers accuracy, OOD performance, counterfactual image dependence, and task-relevant obfuscation; the method solves the stated problem on visually grounded benchmarks at 3B–12B scale. - Scalable: Untested beyond 12B parameters; 72B-scale VTA heatmaps are shown qualitatively but no accuracy experiments are reported at that scale; claims of generality to larger closed-source systems are explicitly excluded by the authors. The paper delivers a method description and training recipe, but no released code, model weights, or data. Strengths: - The three diagnostic metrics (EC-AUC, ATR, Vis-Dep) are independently valuable: they give practitioners concrete probes for quantifying CoT visual grounding quality in any MLLM, separate from Att-CoT itself. - The causal obfuscation experiment (masking task-relevant visual regions and measuring confidence drop) goes beyond correlational attention analysis to provide direct evidence that Att-CoT grounds predictions in content-relevant image features, not just any visual tokens. - Broad evaluation across three model families, two scales each, three in-domain benchmarks, and two OOD benchmarks, all with 3-seed averaging — the coverage substantially reduces cherry-picking risk relative to single-model papers in this space. - The method adds no parameters, no inference overhead, and no extra model branches, making it a drop-in replacement for the SFT loss with negligible additional training cost (excluding the eager attention constraint).
