Critical design decisions: (a) the choice of ℓ₁ distance rather than ℓ₂ is justified because ℓ₁ admits an exact linear epigraph form, which is essential for the vertex-exactness proof; (b) the backward pass uses a regularized QP (strongly convex approximation) rather than the LP directly, since ŷ appears on the LP's left-hand side and can cause discontinuous solution changes; (c) a sequential partial-DNF scheme ("basic steps") lets practitioners trade tightness for tractability by expanding only a selected subset of rules in DNF form. The main theoretical result (Theorem 3.6) shows that every optimal extreme-point solution of the DNF LP satisfies all original disjunctive constraints exactly, relying on Corollary 2.2 of Balas (2018): every extreme point of the convex hull of a finite union of polyhedra belongs to at least one constituent polyhedron. Implementation uses Julia, Flux.jl, and DiffOpt.jl. Key Results Synthetic cooling-control task (3-dimensional continuous output, 7 disjunctive rules, oracle QP ground truth, n = 250–1000, IID and OOD evaluation, 3 seeds): DNF achieves CSAT = 1.00 on both IID and OOD sets at all training sizes; CNF achieves near-perfect IID CSAT but lower OOD CSAT. Both projection methods substantially outperform the unconstrained base and soft penalty baselines on MSE, with the gap largest under OOD shift. Sequential convexification (CNF → DNF via basic steps) shows monotonically improving CSAT, with near-DNF-level satisfaction achieved by expanding only a few rules. Inference time: CNF 25.03 ms/sample, DNF 28.62 ms/sample (vs. <1 μs for unconstrained models). Stress tests at k = 10 active rules: DNF requires 7174 LP variables, 25097 constraints, and 0.92 s/sample; CNF stays below 10 ms across all k. scRNA-seq classification (PBMC3k, 2604 cells, 8 cell types, 1838 gene features, marker-gene disjunctive rules, n = 12–469): At n = 12 training samples, DNF achieves macro-F1 = 0.326 and CSAT = 0.908 vs. base macro-F1 = 0.232 and CSAT = 0.105. At n = 469, the unconstrained base achieves the highest macro-F1 (0.709) while DNF (0.666) maintains CSAT = 0.882 vs. 0.742 for the base. A rules-only random baseline attains CSAT = 0.980 but macro-F1 = 0.167, confirming rules alone are insufficient for classification quality. Baselines are current within the neuro-symbolic and differentiable optimization literature; the missing direct comparison is ILP inference (Roth & Yih, 2005). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is: (a) proving that a DNF convex-hull LP in the epigraph-lifted space guarantees exact satisfaction of the original disjunctive constraints at extreme points (Theorem 3.6), and (b) constructing this framework to handle input-dependent MILP-representable constraints end-to-end differentiably. The application of Balas's extended formulation and disjunctive programming techniques is established theory; applying them in the lifted epigraph space to preserve the projection objective and connecting this to the neuro-symbolic constraint literature is the original synthesis. The partial-DNF sequential convexification and its tightness-tractability tradeoff analysis are also novel contributions to practical deployment of the framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Formal proofs are provided for all main propositions and theorems (Appendix B), building rigorously on Balas (2018). Ablations are present: the sequential convexification study systematically varies the number of rules expanded in DNF form; dataset-size scaling is tested across training fractions spanning two orders of magnitude. Results are reported as mean ± std over 3 random seeds. Code is publicly released as DisjunctiveNet.jl (https://github.com/li-group/DisjunctiveNet.jl.git). Methodological concerns: (1) the synthetic cooling task uses oracle QP solutions as ground truth, where the same constraints define both the oracle and the projection layer — this artificially ensures label-constraint consistency and reduces the difficulty of the task relative to real applications; (2) the scRNA-seq CSAT denominators exclude samples with contradictory active rules, but the fraction of such excluded samples is not reported, potentially inflating CSAT; (3) all MLP backbones have width 8 (not representative of production-scale networks); (4) no hyperparameter tuning was performed across methods, which is methodologically fair but may underrepresent each method's peak performance. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds and experimentally evaluates a system. - Practical: Runs on standard CPU hardware; all experiments used a single CPU process per run with no GPU requirement. - Efficient: DNF inference costs 25–29 ms/sample at typical rule counts (≤5 active rules), growing to ~1 s/sample at k = 10. Training uses batch size 1 due to per-sample LP solves, making wall-clock training substantially slower than unconstrained baselines; training-time overhead is not reported quantitatively for comparison. - Effective: DNF achieves CSAT = 1.00 on the synthetic task and near-100% on scRNA-seq (excluding contradictory-rule samples), and improves predictive performance over soft baselines in low-data regimes, demonstrating the method solves the stated problem. - Scalable: DNF LP size grows as ∏ᵣ mᵣ (exponential in active rule count); the empirical setting keeps k ≤ 5, and stress tests show feasibility to k = 10. Scaling to larger neural networks or datasets has not been tested; the training-time LP bottleneck (batch size 1) is a significant barrier at larger scales. The paper delivers an open-source software artifact (DisjunctiveNet.jl). Strengths - The formal exactness guarantee (Theorem 3.6) is a genuine theoretical contribution: it demonstrates that the LP relaxation, operating in a higher-dimensional space, recovers exact original constraint satisfaction at extreme points — a non-trivial result derived from Balas's vertex characterization and the epigraph-first lifting design. - The expressiveness unification (Proposition 3.1 and Theorem 3.2) formally bridges MILP (from operations research) and QF-LRA (from neuro-symbolic AI) under a single framework, with constructive proofs in both directions. - Input-dependent rule enforcement is a genuine differentiator: unlike virtually all prior methods achieving hard guarantees (MultiplexNet, STE, perturbed optimizers), the framework handles constraints whose feasible sets change with each input, which is essential for physical and biological domain knowledge. - The sequential convexification analysis provides a practically useful insight: expanding even 2–3 rules in DNF form (out of 7) recovers most of the constraint satisfaction benefit of full DNF, enabling a principled tractability-tightness tradeoff. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) DNF LP size grows exponentially in active rule count, motivating partial-DNF for large rule sets; (2) exact satisfaction requires simplex-based LP solving returning extreme-point solutions — interior-point methods without crossover do not satisfy this; (3) contradictory or misspecified rules require additional conflict-handling mechanisms; (4) no principled method for choosing which rules to expand in sequential convexification exists. These are confronted honestly and are credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The experiments use MLPs of width 8 throughout; it is unknown whether the KKT-based gradient signal is effective for training larger networks (deeper, wider, or convolutional) where the base model can already achieve partial constraint satisfaction through representation learning. - The cooling task's oracle ground truth is generated by a QP using the same rules as the projection layer, which ensures label-constraint consistency by construction and does not test the harder scenario where training labels violate constraints. - No comparison against ILP post-processing (Roth & Yih, 2005) is included as an experimental baseline, despite being the most direct prior method for achieving hard constraints at inference time — its absence leaves a key comparison missing from Table 2. - The fraction of scRNA-seq samples excluded from CSAT reporting due to contradictory rules is not reported; if it is substantial, the CSAT numbers are not comparable across methods. - Training-time overhead is discussed qualitatively but not benchmarked quantitatively; batch size 1 (due to per-sample LP) may make training on realistic dataset sizes prohibitively slow. Bottom Line Any researcher building ML systems for safety-critical scientific or engineering applications where hard rule satisfaction is non-negotiable should read this paper — it provides the first method with formal hard-constraint guarantees for input-dependent mixed logical/numeric constraints — but should calibrate expectations given the toy-scale evaluations and the exponential DNF scaling in production rule sets.
Search Results 2605.30456 PDF
#23 — Innovative (73)

Learnability-Informed Fine-Tuning of Diffusion Language Models

S Parashar, A Chagi, J Helwig, L Jotsna, S Vemuri… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LIFT, a supervised fine-tuning algorithm for masked diffusion language models that adaptively selects which tokens to train on at each diffusion timestep based on jointly modeled token difficulty and available context. - Why it's important: Vanilla SFT degrades DLM reasoning performance in practice, while the only competitive alternative (RL-based post-training) requires thousands of GPU hours, leaving a large gap between method quality and practical deployability. - How it does it: LIFT partitions diffusion timesteps into three regimes — training on easy (high-confidence) tokens when masking is heavy, hard (low-confidence) tokens when masking is light, and vanilla SFT at intermediate corruption levels — matching training signal to information availability. - The good: LIFT achieves comparable performance to d1 (RL-based, 2303 H100 hours) using only 1.8 H100 hours, and reaches 3× relative improvement over vanilla SFT on AIME'24 pass@16 (6.7% → 16.7% for LLaDA-8B-Instruct). - The missing: Absolute AIME scores remain very low (6.7%–16.7% pass@16), the evaluation is confined to math and code tasks with no test on NLP/instruction-following benchmarks, and no experiments exceed 8B parameter DLMs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on DLM post-training or studying training dynamics of masked diffusion models; the diagnostic analysis alone (token frequency × diffusion time interaction) is a useful mechanistic contribution.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The composite is anchored by Rigor (78): peer-reviewed at ICML 2026, public code, results over 3 seeds with confidence intervals, and unusually thorough ablations (H sweep, ρ strategies, two base models, two datasets), with the Random2/Random3 baselines (0% AIME pass@16) cleanly isolating temporal conditioning as the causal ingredient. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) reflect the genuine frequency×timestep-interaction finding and the time-conditioned selection it motivates, and Practical Impact (75) is high given the released artifact and the 1.8-vs-2303 H100-hour Pareto result. Resilience (68) is pulled down by AIME noise at low absolute accuracy and the asymmetric d1 comparison; Completeness (72) by the unacknowledged narrow eval scope (math/code only, ≤8B).
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs like LLaDA and Dream) are trained by progressively masking tokens and learning to reconstruct them. When practitioners apply standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to improve their reasoning, performance can actually degrade relative to the pretrained base — a phenomenon noted empirically but left unexplained. The only competitive alternative, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), requires intractable sequence-level likelihoods and thousands of GPU hours. The gap is: what breaks vanilla SFT in DLMs, and can a targeted fix recover or exceed RL performance cheaply? Key Idea: Token learnability in masked diffusion is jointly determined by two factors — corpus rarity (rare tokens are intrinsically harder to predict) and diffusion timestep (at high t, heavy masking removes the context needed to predict rare tokens, making them effectively unlearnable). The key trick is to match training difficulty to information availability: at high corruption, focus on easy tokens where learning is feasible; at low corruption, focus on hard tokens where context is sufficient; treat intermediate corruption as vanilla SFT. This time-conditioned token selection is operationalized via a secondary masking ratio ρ that enables confidence estimation without changing the training distribution. Methodology: LIFT operates within the standard MDLM NELBO framework (negative evidence lower bound, Eq. 1). At each training step: (1) sample a diffusion time t ∼ U[0,1] and a secondary ratio ρ ∼ U[0, 1−t]; (2) construct a more corrupted input xt+ρ by masking x0 at rate t+ρ; (3) run a forward pass to compute denoising confidence c^k = pθ(x0^k | xt+ρ) for all masked positions; (4) select a subset St of K = ⌊t·|x0|⌋ tokens according to the diffusion regime: Top-K (highest confidence, easy tokens) when t ∈ [1−1/H, 1], Bottom-K (lowest confidence, hard tokens) when t ∈ [0, 1/H], and the full masked set Mt otherwise; (5) fill non-selected masked positions with ground-truth tokens to yield xt; (6) compute the NELBO restricted to St. Hyperparameter H ∈ {2,3} controls the regime boundaries; H=3 performs best empirically. A lightweight LIFT-A variant avoids the second forward pass by evaluating the loss at t+ρ with a gating mask (biased NELBO). Critical design decisions: (a) using secondary masking ratio ρ for confidence estimation is well-justified as it separates estimation from training; (b) time-conditioned regime switching is empirically justified by the analysis on 0.5B tokens; (c) K = t·|x0| linear scaling is pragmatically chosen but not theoretically derived. Key Results: On LLaDA-8B-Instruct fine-tuned on s1K (1K curated CoT traces): LIFT3 achieves 79.4% GSM8K, 38.4% MATH500, 26.4% Countdown, 17.4% Sudoku, 16.7% AIME'24 pass@16, and 6.7% AIME'25 pass@16 — versus vanilla SFT's 78.7%, 34.1%, 20.7%, 16.8%, 6.7%, and 3.3% respectively. Baselines include Vanilla SFT, GIFT (difficulty-informed, what-only), and CART (context-weighted, when-only); all are outperformed by LIFT on AIME. The ablation most strongly supporting the core claim: Random2/Random3 (time-independent mixtures of top-K and bottom-K) achieve 0% pass@16 on both AIME benchmarks, confirming that temporal conditioning is the critical ingredient. Against d1 (RL-based, 2303 H100 hours, per-task fine-tuning), LIFT (1.8 hours) achieves 79.4% vs. 81.9% GSM8K and 38.4% vs. 39.2% MATH500. Extension to Dream-7B and a 12K heterogeneous dataset confirms robustness. Results are reported as means over 3 seeds with box-plot confidence intervals. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The systematic characterization of the token frequency × diffusion timestep interaction as the governing factor in DLM SFT failure is a genuine empirical finding; prior work addressed the what and when dimensions in isolation. LIFT is a new method that operationalizes their interaction. The individual components — curriculum-style training, importance weighting, confidence-based token selection — are known in other contexts, but their joint formulation for DLM SFT is novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results are reported as means over 3 seeds with box plots showing confidence intervals (Fig. 7, Fig. 8). Ablations cover: the what/when interaction (Table 5, with Random baselines), the H hyperparameter sweep from 2 to 20 (Table 6), alternative ρ sampling strategies (Table 8), generation length sensitivity (Table 4), compute-matched comparisons (Table 10), and extension to a second base model (Dream-7B, Table 7) and a second dataset (LIFT-SFT-12K, Table 2). Code is publicly available at https://github.com/divelab/LIFT. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026). One methodological concern: AIME pass@16 numbers involve only 30 problems × 16 samples, making each percentage point correspond to ~4.8 samples — the confidence intervals in Fig. 8 are correspondingly wide and the reported gains (e.g., 3.3% → 6.7%) are within noise for some conditions. The analysis dataset overlaps with training data, creating mild circularity in the frequency-confidence characterization. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: LIFT uses LoRA (r=128, α=256) on 8B-parameter models trainable in under 2 H100 hours, well within commercially accessible hardware. - Efficient: LIFT requires one extra forward pass per batch for confidence estimation (1.8× wall-clock vs. vanilla); LIFT-A eliminates this at minor accuracy cost; both are dramatically cheaper than RL alternatives (1.8 vs. 2303 H100 hours). - Effective: Improvements on math reasoning are consistent across two DLM base models and two datasets, with ablations isolating the contributing mechanism; GSM8K gains are modest (~1%) while AIME gains are large in relative terms but low in absolute terms. - Scalable: Tested only on 7–8B parameter models with up to 12K training examples; no experiments at larger scale, and the optimal H value and frequency-confidence relationship may shift with scale. Delivers a software artifact (GitHub code repository). Strengths: - The diagnostic analysis (Fig. 2) is a specific, mechanistic finding: the token frequency × diffusion time interaction explains SFT failure without invoking vague notions of difficulty, and directly informs the algorithm design. - The Pareto frontier result (1.8 vs. 2303 H100 hours for comparable GSM8K/MATH performance) is a concrete, practically significant finding for the DLM post-training community. - The Random2/Random3 ablation (achieving 0% AIME pass@16) cleanly and convincingly demonstrates that temporal conditioning is the critical ingredient, not the mixture of loss types alone. - LIFT-A provides a compute-efficient approximation that preserves most gains, widening the practical applicability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Authors explicitly note that LIFT's token selection does not follow conventional curriculum learning (easy-to-hard globally); it adapts based on available context at each timestep. They acknowledge the two-forward-pass overhead and address it with LIFT-A, noting the accuracy–efficiency tradeoff. They describe RL-based post-training for DLMs as an open challenge that LIFT does not fully resolve. Limitations related to narrow evaluation scope (math/code only), low absolute AIME accuracy, and scale limits are not explicitly acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps: AIME statistics are inherently noisy at these accuracy levels: 30 problems × 16 samples yields ~480 total evaluations, and the difference between 3.3% and 6.7% is 1–2 correct solutions. The confidence intervals in Fig. 8 are wide enough to question some pairwise comparisons. No evaluation on non-math NLP tasks (MMLU, HellaSwag, ARC) means it is unknown whether LIFT induces regression on general language understanding. The d1 comparison is structurally asymmetric — d1 fine-tunes separately per task — so comparing at the GSM8K level while LIFT is task-agnostic overstates parity. The compute-matched analysis (Table 10) shows LIFT benefits from 20 epochs while baselines degrade, but 20 epochs of fine-tuning is uncommon practice and may overfit on other tasks. Scale generalization (beyond 8B DLMs) is undemonstrated. Bottom Line: A practitioner post-training DLMs for reasoning gets a cheap, well-motivated, and reproducible SFT improvement with a clear mechanistic story — this paper is worth reading, but the AIME results should be treated with appropriate statistical skepticism at these low absolute accuracy levels.

#24 — Innovative (73)

Proteo-R1: Reasoning Foundation Models for De Novo Antibody Design

F Wu, W Xuan, H Qi, HJ Chang, H Cao, Z Zhou, H Zhao… · openreview.net · Jure Leskovec - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Proteo-R1, a dual-expert framework that couples a multimodal LLM (understanding expert) with an AF3-style diffusion model (generation expert) for antibody CDR co-design by converting LLM chain-of-thought reasoning into explicit residue-level anchor commitments. - Why it's important: Existing protein generative models treat all CDR residues uniformly during diffusion, entangling design decisions with continuous sampling dynamics and producing no interpretable account of why specific residues were chosen — a fundamental barrier to controllability and systematic reuse of biochemical knowledge. - How it does it: The LLM analyzes a CDR-masked antibody-antigen complex and identifies key interaction residues; their predicted identities (hard constraint) and final-layer hidden representations (soft constraint) are injected into the diffusion model's embedding space at those positions, enforcing residue-level commitments while preserving end-to-end gradient flow back to the reasoner. - The good: On the RAbD CDR-H3 benchmark across 60 complexes, Proteo-R1 achieves DockQ 0.801, compared to 0.473 for the next-best baseline (BoltzGen), while generated sequences attain lower IgLM perplexity (3.19 vs. 3.53 native heavy chain) — suggesting structurally grounded, immunologically plausible designs. - The missing: All binding quality metrics are computed with Rosetta InterfaceAnalyzer; no wet-lab experimental validation of binding affinity is reported, leaving the translational claim unverified. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers in computational antibody design or hybrid LLM+diffusion architectures; practitioners needing experimentally validated designs should treat the claims as promising but preliminary.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (80) drive the score — the sparse residue-aligned anchor interface, which operationalizes LLM reasoning as gradient-trainable hard constraints with backprop into the reasoner, is a genuinely new architectural idea, not a recombination. Rigor (72) is strong-but-capped: comprehensive ablations (modality, oracle-anchor, CoT+replay), public code/demos, and a leakage-controlled date-based split are offset by preprint status, computational-only (Rosetta) binding metrics, and absent error bars on small test sets. Resilience (68) and Completeness (70) are pulled down by the unverified in-silico-to-in-vitro gap — the DockQ 0.473→0.801 jump is compelling but would face hard reviewer questions absent wet-lab validation. Impact (65) reflects released code and transferability across diffusion backbones. Composite: 0.20*80 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 = 73. This paper most resembles the positive calibration set in rigor and methodological care.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation De novo protein design with diffusion and flow-matching models has achieved atomic-level structural fidelity, but these models treat all CDR residues uniformly during generation — design intent is implicitly encoded in diffusion parameters rather than through explicit biochemical reasoning. This entanglement of reasoning and generation limits interpretability (no account of why residues were chosen), controllability (hard to fix known-critical interactions), and modularity (design logic cannot be inspected or transferred). Human molecular engineers solve this by a two-stage process: first identifying key interaction residues (charged anchors, hydrophobic hotspots, specificity-determining motifs) and then optimizing geometry subject to those constraints. Existing language-guided protein design methods apply text conditioning as a soft, latent signal that does not persist as enforceable constraints throughout generation, failing to replicate this separation. Key Idea The core insight is to route all information from the reasoning module to the generative module exclusively through a sparse, residue-aligned anchor interface — replacing the unknown-token embedding `e^{<X>}` at identified key CDR positions with `e(k̂_i) + W_proj h_i^LLM`, where `k̂_i` is a hard predicted amino-acid identity and `h_i^LLM` is the LLM's final-layer hidden state at that position. This formulation simultaneously enforces symbolic identity commitments (blocking those positions from being freely sampled) and injects continuous reasoning-derived context, while allowing gradients from the diffusion loss to propagate through `W_proj` back to the LLM — training the reasoner to produce anchors that are causally useful for generation, not merely biochemically plausible in isolation. Methodology Proteo-R1 comprises three components and is trained in three stages: Understanding Expert (E_und): Qwen-3-4B-Instruct augmented with lightweight projection layers. Inputs are: (a) ESM-2 sequence embeddings of the full CDR-masked complex, and (b) truncated AF3-style structural features obtained by CDR-masked refolding followed by a forward pass through the AF3 DiffusionConditioning, AtomAttentionEncoder, and DiffTransformer modules, stopping before coordinate decoding (preventing native CDR geometry leakage). Both modalities are fused per-residue via linear projections and an MLP: `h_i^und = φ(W_seq h_i^seq ⊕ W_struct h_i^struct)`. E_und outputs key residue positions, predicted identities, and LLM hidden states via structured JSON with CoT traces. Cross-Expert Anchor Interface (BUILDANCHORS): Key anchor positions receive `e_i^gen = e(k̂_i) + W_proj h_i^LLM`; non-key CDR positions use `e^{<X>}`; framework and antigen residues use their native embeddings. This enforces commitments at both the symbolic and representational levels. Generation Expert (E_gen): An AF3-style diffusion model (MFDesign) trained with the standard noise-prediction MSE loss conditioned on anchor embeddings. The total training loss is `L_total = L_gen + 0.1 × L_und`. Three-stage curriculum: Stage I (10K steps, batch 256): freeze LLM, train projections only on PDB schema completion and captioning tasks — chain count, secondary-structure bins, length bins. Stage II (20K steps, batch 128): unfreeze LLM on PDB data with a four-phase curriculum progressing from residue grounding (DSSP, RSA, identity retrieval) → pairwise geometry (distance bins, contact classification) → interaction chemistry (salt-bridge counts, chain-pair graphs) → interface localization (top-k hotspot identification). A 5% replay buffer prevents catastrophic forgetting. Stage III (10K steps, batch 16): joint end-to-end optimization on SAbDab antibody-antigen complexes. Critical design decision: date-based train/test split (pre-/post-cutoff) to prevent structure-pretraining leakage; this is well-justified. CDR-masked refolding as leakage control is also well-justified. The choice of λ_und = 0.1 is stated but not ablated as a continuous hyperparameter. Key Results Multi-CDR simultaneous redesign (SAbDab test, Table 1): Proteo-R1 achieves the lowest per-CDR RMSD in 5/6 CDR regions (H1: 1.33 Å, H2: 1.13 Å, H3: 3.81 Å, L1: 1.54 Å, L2: 0.85 Å, L3: 1.51 Å) vs. MFDesign (1.61, 1.44, 3.71, 1.65, 1.15, 1.69). Geometric realism: Proteo-R1 achieves best Clash_out (0.14%) and best JSD_bb (0.2661). IMP 56.58%, comparable to MFDesign (59.16%). The oracle-anchor ablation improves loop-RMSD to 3.34 and IMP to 62.25%, indicating anchor quality is the primary bottleneck. CDR-H3-only design on RAbD (60 complexes, Table 2): Proteo-R1 achieves lDDT 0.9693, TM-score 0.9816, RMSD 2.46 Å, DockQ 0.801 — substantially outperforming all baselines including BoltzGen (DockQ 0.473) and DGENet (DockQ 0.431). However, AAR is only 10.75% versus DGENet (42.67%), which the authors argue reflects generating alternative valid sequences rather than native recovery. Structure-sequence consistency (Table 3): Proteo-R1 achieves positive Δ = IF-AAR − AAR for 5/6 CDRs (H3: +4.21 vs. MFDesign −45.31), demonstrating that its generated geometries are more compatible with coherent sequences under an independent inverse-folding model. Sequence validity via IgLM/AbLang/IgT5 perplexity is below or equal to native antibodies across all three evaluators. Transferability (Table 5, UniMoMo backbone): Proteo-R1 + UniMoMo achieves RMSD 0.83 Å, IMP 67.79%, ΔG 7.35 vs. vanilla UniMoMo all (RMSD 1.04, IMP 65.00%, ΔG 8.46), confirming that gains arise from the reasoning paradigm rather than the specific diffusion model. Baselines are current and strong (2022-2026), including recent AF3-inspired models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novel contribution is the sparse residue-aligned anchor interface: operationalizing LLM reasoning as discrete, gradient-trainable residue-level commitments that persist as hard structural constraints throughout diffusion generation. Prior language-guided protein design methods use text as a soft latent signal; prior constraint-based design methods require heuristic or post-hoc specification of anchors. Combining both identity clamping and representation-level injection within an end-to-end trainable framework where the diffusion objective shapes the reasoner is the specific architectural novelty. Individual components (ESM-2, Qwen, AF3-style diffusion, curriculum training with replay) are existing tools assembled in a novel configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: modality ablation (Table 9) shows sequence + structure fusion outperforms either alone (81.4% vs. 71.7% struct-only, 80.3% seq-only); oracle-anchor ablation provides a meaningful upper bound; CoT reasoning + replay ablation (Tables 14-15) validates both contributions. Code and demos are publicly available at https://smiles724.github.io/r1/. Training hyperparameters are fully specified (Table 6). The date-based data split is a methodological strength. Methodological concerns: (1) No error bars are reported in Tables 1, 2, or 3 — the test sets (SAbDab test partition, RAbD 60 complexes) are small enough that variance could be substantial, and no multiple seeds are reported. (2) All binding quality metrics (IMP, ΔG) use Rosetta InterfaceAnalyzer, a computational proxy known to have imperfect correlation with experimental binding affinity. (3) Stage II hotspot supervision uses geometry-derived proxies (atomic contact count, ΔSASA) rather than experimentally measured alanine-scanning energetics — the fidelity of this proxy is not validated against experimental hotspot data. (4) The training data scale for Stage II (~9M examples over 20K steps at batch 128) is large but runtime/GPU costs are not reported, impeding full reproducibility assessment. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on commercially available GPU hardware; Qwen-3-4B, ESM-2, and AF3-style diffusion models are all available or reproducible. No exotic hardware requirements are stated. - Efficient: No wall-clock or peak-memory benchmarks are reported. Each inference call requires CDR-masked refolding, a truncated AF3 forward pass, LLM generation, and diffusion sampling — substantially more compute than purely generative baselines, but the overhead is not quantified. - Effective: The evidence shows strong improvements on DockQ and structural accuracy metrics on standardized benchmarks; however, effectiveness for actual therapeutic antibody development is unverified — all binding quality assessments use Rosetta computational scoring, not experimental binding or neutralization assays. - Scalable: Evaluated on conventional antibodies from SAbDab and 60 complexes from RAbD. No experiments on nanobodies, bispecifics, antibodies against flat or glycan-shielded epitopes, or longer CDR-H3 loops. Scaling behavior is uncharacterized. The work delivers a software artifact (code + demos at the project page). Strengths - The dual-level anchoring — both symbolic identity clamping and representation-level embedding injection with gradient flow back through W_proj to E_und — is technically clean and directly addresses the problem of training the reasoner to produce commitments that are causally useful for generation rather than merely plausible. - CDR-masked refolding as an explicit leakage-control mechanism is methodologically careful; preventing E_und from observing native CDR coordinates is essential for ensuring the reasoning module is tested under realistic design conditions. - The oracle-anchor ablation is a useful experimental design that isolates the contribution of reasoning quality from generation quality and quantifies the headroom for future improvements in E_und — the gains at H3 (RMSD 3.81 → 3.34, IMP 56.58% → 62.25%) indicate that reasoning quality is the binding constraint. - Demonstrating architectural transferability by swapping E_gen from MFDesign to UniMoMo (Table 5) with consistent improvements validates that the core contribution is the reasoning interface rather than a lucky combination with one specific diffusion backbone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The oracle-anchor ablation implicitly acknowledges that imperfect hotspot identification by E_und is the primary bottleneck — the authors state explicitly that "Proteo-R1 is not limited by the generator, but by the quality of its reasoning-derived anchors." Biosecurity risks (potential misuse for designing harmful biological agents) are acknowledged in the Impact Statement. The authors do not directly confront: (1) the absence of experimental binding validation, (2) the reliance on geometric hotspot proxies rather than energetically validated hotspots, or (3) the narrow scope to conventional antibody CDR redesign. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central performance claim rests on Rosetta ΔG and DockQ — computational metrics. Neither metric has been correlated with experimental binding affinity for the specific designed sequences in this work. The jump from DockQ 0.473 to 0.801 is compelling in silico but unverified in vitro. - AAR of 10.75% on RAbD is dismissed as "alternative valid sequences" but verified only by perplexity and IF-AAR. Without functional assays (e.g., expression, thermal stability, binding affinity), it is unknown what fraction of low-AAR designs are truly functional versus structurally plausible but functionally defective. - No variance estimates for the main geometry tables; the RAbD test set (60 complexes) is small enough that single-digit DockQ ranking differences may not be statistically significant. - Stage II hotspot proxy labels are derived from ΔSASA and atomic contact counts — established structural proxies but known to have only moderate correlation with experimental alanine-scanning hotspot data. If the proxy is systematically wrong, E_und learns to identify geometrically prominent rather than energetically critical residues. - Evaluation is exclusively on conventional IgG antibodies from SAbDab; generalization to nanobodies, Fc-engineered formats, or antibodies targeting challenging epitopes (glycan shielding, highly charged surfaces) is untested and not claimed. Bottom Line Proteo-R1 makes a genuine architectural contribution — sparse residue-aligned anchor injection with gradient flow back to the LLM — and demonstrates persuasive improvements on DockQ and structural metrics over strong baselines; computational antibody design researchers should read it, but the case for practical utility in therapeutic pipelines requires experimental binding validation that is absent from this work.
Recommended
#25 — Innovative (73)

VideoKR: Towards Knowledge- and Reasoning-Intensive Video Understanding (26 ▲ )

Lin Fu, Zheyuan Yang, Yang Wang, Tingyu Song, Arman Cohan, Yilun Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VideoKR, a 315K-example post-training corpus of newly collected CC-licensed expert-domain videos paired with skill-oriented QA examples and CoT rationales, plus VideoKR-Eval, a 2,000-example benchmark filtered to require genuine continuous video understanding. - Why it's important: Existing post-training corpora are saturated for current frontier base models (base models score 49–57% zero-shot on them) and are skewed toward everyday-activity perception, leaving models unable to perform multi-hop inference grounded in domain knowledge — the gap that limits real-world deployability. - How it does it: A four-stage pipeline constructs a 63,745-entry domain knowledge bank, drives YouTube CC-video collection via knowledge-grounded scenario queries, generates examples targeting three progressive skill tiers (basic perception → knowledge-enriched perception → knowledge-intensive reasoning), and applies human-validated model selection across a pool of seven frontier MLLMs to diversify and quality-control synthesis. - The good: Under a standard SFT→GRPO pipeline with no algorithmic novelty, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct post-trained on VideoKR achieves 51.5 knowledge-intensive average — best among all 7/8B-scale models, beating the next competitor (Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking) by 1.5 points — and the training-data difficulty analysis (base-model zero-shot accuracy of 42–43% on VideoKR vs. 49–57% on prior corpora) provides a principled, quantitative explanation for why prior datasets fail. - The missing: All post-training experiments are confined to two base models from the Qwen family (7B and 8B); it is unclear whether the gains transfer to architecturally diverse models or models at 30B+ scale, and SciVideoBench scores for VideoKR-trained models show only marginal improvement (~4 points over base for Qwen2.5-VL-7B at 128 frames) despite the dataset's emphasis on natural science. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on multimodal post-training data curation or benchmark design for expert-domain video reasoning; the data difficulty analysis and multi-model single-frame probing methodology are immediately reusable design tools.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Scientific Rigor (80) and the data-difficulty argument carry the composite: thorough ablations (skill composition, CoT-vs-direct, controlled corpus-swap under matched budgets), three-run evaluation, disclosed error rates, peer review at ICML 2026, and open data. Novelty (68) and Non-Obviousness (70) are moderate — the knowledge-bank-driven pipeline has precedents, though the multi-model single-frame probing filter and the data-not-algorithm finding are fresh. The composite is held below 75 by generalization limited to two Qwen-family 7B/8B models with untested transfer to other architectures or 30B+ scale, weak unexplained SciVideoBench transfer, and an unstratified base-model difficulty comparison. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*72 = 73.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal foundation models for video understanding have strong surface-level perception but struggle with tasks requiring domain knowledge and multi-step inference — e.g., inferring a chemical reaction from observed apparatus, or diagnosing a patient from a clinical procedure video. The bottleneck is not model architecture or RL algorithms but training data: existing corpora (LLaVA-Video, Video-R1, VideoRFT, OneThinker) reuse short-form datasets from general everyday activities, rely on single models for synthesis (introducing systematic bias), and are not CC-licensed. Concurrently, existing knowledge-intensive evaluation benchmarks (MMVU, VideoMMMU, SciVideoBench) are polluted by examples answerable from a single frame (35–50% single-frame answerability under frontier model probing), conflating visual understanding with language prior retrieval. Key Idea: The core insight is that knowledge- and reasoning-intensive video understanding can be decomposed into three nested, learnable skill tiers — basic video reasoning (VIDR), knowledge-enhanced perception (KNOWVID), and knowledge-intensive reasoning (KNOWVIDR) — and that grounding data collection and QA synthesis directly in a structured domain knowledge bank, rather than repurposing existing perception datasets, produces training data that is both harder for base models and more causally responsible for downstream reasoning gains. The secondary insight is that using a heterogeneous pool of frontier models (validated per pipeline stage by human experts) avoids the single-model artifact bias endemic to prior corpora. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Domain Knowledge Bank Construction: authors manually map 82 subjects across four disciplines (Natural Sciences, Engineering, Healthcare, Humanities & Social Sciences) onto a four-level hierarchy (Subject → Course → Lecture → Knowledge Point) and prompt LLMs to populate 63,745 knowledge point definitions. (2) Knowledge-Driven Video Collection: LLMs generate 1–3 real-world scenarios per knowledge point; YouTube Data API retrieves CC-licensed videos (top-10 by metadata), a two-stage MLLM relevance filter (text metadata then visual content) removes off-topic material, and Azure AI content moderation removes harmful content; 146,567 videos are collected. (3) Skill-Oriented Example Generation: For each video, six QA examples (two per skill tier) are generated by frontier MLLMs using the video frames (0.2 fps with timestamps), three human-curated seed examples from the same discipline and skill, and the knowledge point. Three filters enforce quality: self-consistency verification (model re-derives answer independently), video dependency filtering (discard if two strong models solve it from text + 4 frames alone), and CoT rationale validation (independent verifier checks step grounding). (4) Quality Control: a human-validated model selection protocol tests seven frontier models (GPT-5.2, GPT-5-mini, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, Gemini-3-Flash, DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B, GLM-4.6V) on 100 live instances per pipeline step; only models with error rate ≤ 3% are eligible for that step. The resulting 315,537 examples are split into VideoKR-SFT-201K (with CoT rationales) and VideoKR-RL-114K (answer-only, for GRPO). Post-training uses a standard SFT (1 epoch, lr=1e-5) followed by GRPO (1 epoch, lr=5e-6) with reward R = 0.1·R_format + 0.9·R_accuracy, ROUGE for open-ended and Exact Match for multiple-choice. Training hardware: 8× A800 80 GB GPUs. Key Results: VideoKR SFT+RL raises the knowledge-intensive benchmark average of Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 41.9 to 46.6 (+4.7) at 128 frames, with the largest gains on MMVU (+4.8) and VideoKR-Eval (+8.5). Qwen3-VL-8B improves from 48.5 to 51.5 (+3.0), achieving the best result among all 7/8B-scale models (next best: Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking at 50.0). On general video reasoning, post-training is neutral to mildly positive at 128 frames (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 64.1→65.5), with a drop visible at 16 frames. In direct SFT corpus comparison (80K examples, controlled), VideoKR-SFT is the only corpus that improves the knowledge-intensive average over the base model (42.4 vs. base 41.9); Video-R1-CoT drops it to 36.2, VideoRFT to 38.4. In zero-RL comparison (50K examples), VideoKR-RL achieves 43.0 (+1.1), edging VideoAuto-R1 at 42.7. The training-data difficulty analysis confirms VideoKR is harder: base-model zero-shot accuracy is 39.2–43.5% on VideoKR vs. 45.8–59.0% on prior corpora. SciVideoBench remains a weak point for VideoKR-trained models compared to MMVU and VideoKR-Eval gains. Skill ablation confirms that all three tiers are needed: VIDR alone yields 41.4%, adding KNOWVID gives 41.3% (marginal), adding KNOWVIDR gives 42.4%. The paper is published at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (primary) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (secondary). The dataset construction pipeline itself is not a wholly new method — knowledge-bank-driven video collection and multi-model synthesis have precedents — but VideoKR is the first large-scale corpus specifically designed for knowledge- and reasoning-intensive video understanding with CC licensing, newly collected videos, and human-in-the-loop multi-model quality control. VideoKR-Eval's multi-model single-frame probing filter is a novel and reusable benchmark construction technique. The empirical finding that data difficulty (not RL algorithm sophistication) is the primary post-training bottleneck is non-obvious and well-supported. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: skill composition, CoT vs. direct output, and controlled corpus-swap comparisons under matched training budgets (80K SFT, 50K RL, 1 epoch each) isolate individual contributions. Evaluation runs each model three times with independent sampling and reports the mean. Error rates in the final corpus are manually quantified: 52/800 examples flagged as potentially non-visual-solvable (~6.5%) and 32/800 reasoning trace errors (~4%), with 17 affecting the final answer — these rates are disclosed rather than hidden. Code and data are stated as open-sourced (GitHub link on title page). Standardized prompt templates and inference configurations per model are fully documented in Appendix C. A concern: the base-model zero-shot accuracy comparison in Table 5 uses only 3,000 random samples per corpus, and the sampling procedure is not stratified by skill tier or domain, so the difficulty gap could partly reflect domain mismatch rather than genuine reasoning difficulty. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Post-training runs on 8× A800 80 GB GPUs, which is commercially available at cloud scale; inference requires up to 128 frames (4,096 video tokens), within the operating range of current MLLM deployments. - Efficient: The dataset construction cost was approximately $70,400 USD in model inference; post-training cost is not stated in FLOPs or wall-clock time but is single-epoch SFT+RL, consistent with existing community practice. No efficiency advantage is claimed over baselines. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine improvement on knowledge-intensive benchmarks, with a causal ablation (corpus swap under controlled training) rather than confounded end-to-end comparisons; the VideoKR-Eval benchmark's low single-frame answerability rate (~10%) provides stronger evidence of genuine video grounding than prior benchmarks. - Scalable: Scaling to 128 frames consistently improves results; no experiments at >128 frames or on models above 8B parameters. Scalability to 30B+ models or 10× data volume is claimed but not tested. The work delivers an open-source dataset + benchmark (software artifact) and does not stop at paper form. Strengths: - The human-validated multi-model selection protocol (7 models, 100 instances per stage, ≤3% error threshold) is a concrete, replicable mechanism for avoiding single-model synthesis bias — a real methodological advance over prior corpora. - The multi-model single-frame probing filter for benchmark construction (requiring all three frontier models to fail on one random frame) is stricter than standard text-only or single-model filtering and directly quantifies and reduces a specific confound. - The training-data difficulty analysis (Table 5) is a falsifiable, quantitative explanation for why existing corpora stagnate: saturation, not algorithm design, is the bottleneck. This reframes the community's attention correctly. - The domain knowledge bank (63,745 points, 82 subjects) enables systematic cross-domain coverage and provides a reusable resource independent of the video corpus. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that VideoKR excludes videos longer than 30 minutes, explicitly scoping out long-context video understanding. They do not claim that post-training algorithmic advances are unnecessary — only that data quality is a primary bottleneck. The impact statement discloses annotation costs and data provenance transparently. No other limitations are explicitly stated. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) All base models are Qwen-family 7B/8B; it is unknown whether VideoKR generalizes to architecturally different models (InternVL, LLaVA, Gemini-family) or to 30B+ scales where training dynamics differ. (2) SciVideoBench gains are small (+4.4 for Qwen2.5-VL-7B at 128 frames), yet the corpus is heavily weighted toward natural science — the weak transfer suggests domain composition or evaluation-set mismatch that is not diagnosed. (3) The video dependency filter uses only 4 randomly sampled frames; adversarial selection of informative frames or keyframe extraction could make the filter more reliable for videos where a single key frame is highly informative. (4) ROUGE is used as the open-ended accuracy reward in GRPO — this metric has known fragility against paraphrase and is not validated against human judgment for the knowledge-intensive open-ended questions in this corpus. (5) The 800-example quality audit samples from VideoKR-SFT only; VideoKR-RL-114K quality is not separately audited, yet RL training is sensitive to reward-label noise. Bottom Line: VideoKR is a well-executed, peer-reviewed dataset paper with a credible empirical argument that training-data quality, not RL algorithm complexity, is the primary bottleneck for knowledge-intensive video reasoning, and practitioners building or fine-tuning video MLLMs in expert domains should engage with both the dataset and the benchmark construction methodology.
Recommended 2606.05259 PDF
#26 — Innovative (73)

SeedER: Seed-and-Expand Retrieval from Knowledge Graphs

H Shirzad, F Wenkel, D Beaini, DJ Sutherland… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SeedER, a first-stage knowledge-graph retrieval framework that seeds from cheap dense retrieval, extracts a bounded local subgraph via heuristic filtering, then trains a query-conditioned GNN expansion policy with group-centered REINFORCE to iteratively select high-recall frontier nodes under a strict budget. - Why it's important: Dense bi-encoders provably fail on compositional multi-hop KG queries — they require Ω(|V|) embedding dimension — while agent-based LLM traversal (8B+ parameters) is too expensive for first-stage retrieval, leaving a practical gap for a lightweight, structure-aware retriever. - How it does it: A K-hop-with-filtering pre-pass extracts a query-specific subgraph of 100–200 nodes; a sparse graph transformer (Exphormer-style) then scores frontier nodes conditioned on the query embedding, trained via REINFORCE with Recall@Any reward using M=8 sampled trajectories and mean-reward advantage normalization, jointly with a BPR ranking head. - The good: With Qwen3-Embedding-4B as the text encoder, SeedER achieves 0.310 Hit@1 and 0.647 Recall@20 on STARK-PRIME using only 1.1M parameters — competitive with or exceeding GraphFlow (8B parameters, LoRA-finetuned LLaMA3) at roughly 1/8000 the parameter count and 10–100× lower per-query latency. - The missing: No code repository is mentioned; the RL policy is trained entirely within a bounded subgraph pre-extracted by the same heuristic it is designed to improve upon, creating a recall ceiling that is not fully analyzed; and on STARK-AMAZON the method delivers only a 4% Hit@1 gain over dense retrieval, with no explanation of why. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building graph-RAG pipelines or lightweight KG retrievers — the theoretical hardness result and the efficiency-recall trade-off are both genuine contributions — but those who need production code should wait for a public release.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (78) are high — the decomposition of compositional retrieval into local RL decisions, backed by a formal Ω(|V|) hardness separation, is a genuine and non-obvious contribution. Rigor (74) is above norm: 10-seed stability with Pearson r≥0.97, clean isolating ablations (BPR head, trajectory count, RL vs reranking), full hyperparameters — but no code release and evaluation confined to the three STARK benchmarks from one source. Completeness (66) and Resilience (68) are pulled down by the uncharacterized recall ceiling from bounded subgraph pre-extraction, the unexplained near-flat AMAZON gains, and the theory applying to a constructed graph family rather than the real KGs. Impact (68) reflects the striking 1/8000-parameter efficiency and 10–100× latency edge, discounted for no released artifact and first-stage-only scope. Weighted: 0.20*78+0.25*74+0.15*78+0.15*66+0.15*68+0.10*68 = 73.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 74 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 66 Resilience 68 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge graphs encode relational knowledge that requires multi-hop traversal to answer compositional queries: e.g., finding drugs that treat Alzheimer's disease via the cholinergic pathway requires traversing disease→gene→pathway→drug, and no node in this chain is textually similar to the query. Dense bi-encoders score nodes independently and are provably inadequate for such queries. Augmenting dense retrieval with naive k-hop expansion fails for a different reason: even a 2-hop neighborhood around 3 seed nodes reaches tens of thousands of nodes in STARK-PRIME (129K nodes, avg. degree 62.6) and approaches graph scale in STARK-MAG (1.87M nodes). LLM-based agentic traversal (GraphFlow, ToG) handles multi-hop reasoning but at 8B-parameter scale, making it unsuitable as a first-stage retriever that must process large candidate sets cheaply. The target: a compact, trainable retriever that produces high-recall candidate sets for downstream reranking or generation, occupying the gap between cheap global similarity and expensive sequential LLM reasoning. Key Idea The core insight is that compositional multi-hop reasoning decomposes naturally into a sequence of local decisions — selecting which frontier neighbor to expand next — and that this decomposition admits an exponentially more efficient representation than global embedding similarity. Rather than learning a single embedding that encodes all multi-hop reachability (which requires Ω(|V|) bits), SeedER learns a query-conditioned local policy that makes one-hop selection decisions iteratively, needing only O(log|V|) bits per node for a constructive linear classifier. The practical realization pre-extracts a bounded search space (100–200 nodes) via heuristic K-hop-with-filtering, then trains an RL policy within this space to discover paths through semantically opaque intermediate nodes that greedy similarity scoring would skip. Methodology SeedER operates in three phases. Phase 1 (seeding): the top-k₀ nodes by cosine similarity between query and node text embeddings (k₀=3 for PRIME/MAG, 10 for AMAZON) form the initial seed set V₀. Phase 2 (bounded subgraph extraction): K-hop-with-filtering runs from V₀, scoring each candidate frontier node as the mean of query–node, query–source, and query–relation cosine similarities, retaining only the top-b_h candidates at each hop until a budget B of ~100–200 nodes is reached. This bounded subgraph Ĝ_q acts as the search environment. Phase 3 (RL expansion): for T steps, a sparse graph transformer (Exphormer variant with edge-type features but no virtual nodes or expander edges) processes the induced subgraph G_t = Ĝ_q[V_t ∪ U_t], producing query-conditioned node embeddings. A policy head scores each frontier node u ∈ U_t via g_θ(h_u, z_q); at training time, Gumbel-top-k sampling selects c_t nodes; at inference, greedy top-c_t selection is used. After T steps, a BPR ranking head sorts V_T by relevance. Training minimizes L = L_RL + λ_BPR · L_BPR, where L_RL is group-centered REINFORCE (M=8 trajectories per query, advantage = reward minus group mean Recall@Any) and L_BPR is pairwise Bayesian Personalized Ranking on the final node scores. Critical design decisions: (1) pre-extracting the bounded subgraph makes full-KG GNN computation unnecessary during training, at the cost of a hard recall ceiling set by K-hop-with-filtering; (2) group-centered advantage over a learned critic reduces variance without adding parameters, validated in ablation; (3) joint BPR training provides dense supervised signal to the GNN backbone, complementing the sparse RL signal. Key Results On STARK-PRIME with MiniLM-L6-v2: SeedER improves Hit@1 from 0.101 to 0.199 (+97%), Hit@5 from 0.218 to 0.411 (+89%), MRR from 0.161 to 0.293 (+82%), and Recall@20 from 0.259 to 0.461 (+78%) over dense retrieval, outperforming all non-LLM baselines (G-Retriever, SubgraphRAG, Beam Search, A*, PPR, K-hop-with-filtering) by large margins. On STARK-MAG: Hit@1 0.203→0.244 (+20%), Recall@20 0.344→0.449 (+31%). On STARK-AMAZON: Hit@1 0.306→0.319 (+4%), Recall@20 0.417→0.444 (+7%) — substantially smaller gains. With Qwen3-Embedding-4B on STARK-PRIME: SeedER achieves 0.310 Hit@1, 0.582 Hit@5, 0.647 Recall@20, matching or exceeding GraphFlow (reranked) at 0.514 Hit@1 / 0.797 Recall@20 on some metrics while using 1/8000 the parameters. Ablation (STARK-PRIME, Table 2b) shows each component contributes: removing the BPR auxiliary loss drops Hit@1 from 0.199 to 0.059; single-trajectory sampling drops it to 0.146; greedy baseline drops it to 0.114; replacing RL expansion with GNN-only reranking of the K-hop-with-filtering subgraph gives 0.168. Ten-seed stability experiments show high validation–test correlation (Pearson r ≥ 0.966 for all metrics). Baselines include current and competitive methods including GraphFlow (2025), ToG+GPT-4o, and SFT/PRM-based agents. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of bounded-subgraph pre-extraction, query-conditioned GNN expansion policy, group-REINFORCE with Recall@Any reward, and joint BPR ranking is new as a first-stage KG retrieval system. The theoretical Ω(|V|) hardness result for dense retrieval on compositional queries is a non-trivial contribution, though it applies to a specific constructed graph family (relation-tracing graphs with permutation-structured edges) rather than arbitrary KGs. The empirical finding that a 1.1M-parameter RL-trained retriever can match or approach 8B-parameter LLM-based graph traversal on STARK is genuinely surprising and practically valuable. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted, isolating the BPR head, trajectory count, baseline type, and RL vs. reranking-only variants. Ten random seeds with stability curves and correlation statistics (Table 6: Pearson r ≥ 0.97 on R@20) are reported — above the norm for this subfield. Standard deviation across seeds is reported in Table 3. Hyperparameters are fully documented (Table 7). No code repository or model weights are mentioned. A methodological concern: the RL policy is trained within a bounded subgraph constructed by K-hop-with-filtering, which means the method's recall is upper-bounded by the pre-extraction step; this ceiling is not characterized quantitatively (e.g., what fraction of ground-truth answers fall outside the 100–200 node budget). Evaluation uses only the three STARK benchmarks from a single source paper; no cross-benchmark generalization is demonstrated. The theoretical hardness result applies to a carefully constructed adversarial graph family (permutation-defined relation-tracing graphs), and the argument that this applies to real biomedical or academic KGs is informal. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Runs on a single A100 or H100 GPU; embedding preprocessing takes up to one day; training and inference take 1–3 hours per dataset; 1.1M parameters. Commercially feasible. - Efficient: 1.1M parameters vs. 8B for GraphFlow; bounded-subgraph GNN avoids full-KG computation; per-query latency is ~10–100× lower than GraphFlow per Figure 4. Initial dense embedding of all KG nodes remains a one-time preprocessing cost. - Effective: Substantially effective on STARK-PRIME (+97% Hit@1) and STARK-MAG (+20% Hit@1); limited effectiveness on STARK-AMAZON (+4% Hit@1), where dense retrieval is already strong due to the low average degree (3.0) and text-rich node descriptions. Solves the stated first-stage recall problem on compositionally demanding KGs. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 1.87M nodes and 19.9M edges (STARK-MAG). Per-query computation does not grow with graph size due to bounded subgraph extraction, but initial dense embedding and seed retrieval scale linearly with |V|. No experiments at 10×–100× larger graphs; no claim of tested scalability beyond STARK scale. The paper delivers a method and empirical results in paper form; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The formal hardness result (Theorem B.2 + B.5) establishes an exponential separation between dense retrieval and iterative local policies for compositional queries, providing a principled theoretical foundation rather than intuition alone. - The parameter efficiency story is compelling and backed by a direct latency comparison: SeedER achieves competitive Recall@20 against GraphFlow (8B params, LoRA-tuned) at ~1/8000 the parameters and 10–100× lower latency, making it practically deployable as a first-stage component. - The ablation suite is unusually clean: each design choice (BPR head, trajectory count, baseline estimator, RL vs. reranking) is isolated and quantified with consistent metrics, and the results are unambiguous. - SeedER is encoder-agnostic: it consistently adds substantial gains over dense retrieval across three different text encoders (MiniLM, OpenAI-ada-002, Qwen3-4B), indicating the graph expansion policy generalizes beyond any particular embedding model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a dedicated limitations section. In context, they note: (a) gains on STARK-AMAZON are modest because dense retrieval is already strong there; (b) MRR improvements are smaller than recall improvements because expansion-discovered nodes carry lower semantic similarity; (c) SeedER is explicitly positioned as a first-stage retriever, not a complete QA system. These are acknowledged indirectly rather than confronted directly, which is a mild credibility cost. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The bounded subgraph pre-extraction (K-hop-with-filtering to 100–200 nodes) sets a hard recall ceiling: any ground-truth answer excluded from Ĝ_q is unrecoverable by the RL policy. The paper does not report what fraction of answers fall outside this budget, nor how this ceiling compares to the reported Recall@20 numbers — this is the most important uncharacterized failure mode. - The theoretical hardness result applies to relation-tracing graphs defined by uniformly random permutations, which is a highly regular structure unlike real biomedical or academic KGs. The paper makes no argument for why the real STARK KGs are hard for dense retrieval in the same sense, beyond empirical evidence. - STARK-AMAZON performance is near-flat (+4% Hit@1, +7% Recall@20), but the paper provides no analysis of why the method underperforms on this domain. The product graph's low average degree (3.0) and text-richness likely make structure-based expansion less informative, but this is not discussed — limiting confidence that the method generalizes beyond biomedical/academic KGs. - No end-to-end QA evaluation is reported; the claim that better first-stage recall improves downstream generation quality is cited from prior work but not demonstrated in this paper. - No code release is mentioned, despite full hyperparameter documentation. Reproducibility requires reimplementing Exphormer-style sparse attention with edge features, Gumbel-top-k sampling, and the full STARK preprocessing pipeline. Bottom Line A practitioner building graph-RAG pipelines will find SeedER genuinely useful — it delivers substantial recall improvements over dense retrieval on compositionally demanding KGs at 1.1M parameters and is competitive with 8B-parameter LLM-based alternatives — but the uncharacterized recall ceiling from bounded subgraph extraction, the absence of code, and the near-zero gains on STARK-AMAZON mean the method's applicability should be validated per domain before adoption.

#27 — Innovative (73)

Graph Neural Networks Are Not Continuous Across Graph Resolutions

C Koke, Y Shen, A Saroha, M Eisenberger, B Rieck… · arxiv.org · Michael Bronstein - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proves that standard GNNs are not continuous with respect to heat-kernel convergence of graphs, and introduces Laplace-transform propagation matrices as a drop-in modification that yields provably scale-continuous GNNs. - Why it's important: GNNs deployed on multi-resolution data — the same molecule at atomistic vs. coarse-grained scale, or a mesh refined during inference — silently produce incompatible embeddings, causing large generalization failures that cannot be fixed by data augmentation alone. - How it does it: Replacing standard normalized-adjacency or polynomial-Laplacian propagation with filters of the form ψ(L) = ∫₀^∞ e^{−tL} ψ̂(t) dt (Laplace transforms of the graph Laplacian) ensures that as graph sequences converge in the heat-kernel sense, the generated latent embeddings also converge. - The good: On QM7 molecular regression, the proposed MPNN and spectral variants achieve ~17–18 kcal/mol MAE in cross-resolution settings, matching same-resolution performance, while all eight standard baselines degrade by factors of 2–100× (GIN reaches 1835 kcal/mol cross-resolution vs. 38 same-resolution). - The missing: Experiments are limited to a single small-molecule dataset (QM7, up to 7 heavy atoms), two citation networks, and synthetic stochastic block models; no large-scale chemistry, physics simulation, or mesh-processing benchmark is tested, leaving practical impact in production GNN pipelines undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone working on molecular property prediction, multi-scale simulation, or graph transferability, where the same object appears at different resolutions; less urgent for practitioners using GNNs on fixed-resolution datasets.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is the top contributor — a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING that contradicts a decade of GNN stability consensus, paired with a NEW FORMULATION (scale-continuity via heat-kernel convergence) and a NEW METHOD (Laplace-transform propagation). Non-Obviousness (80) is equally high: identifying that standard normalization severs inter-supernode communication is a genuine root-cause insight, not an obvious combination. Rigor (72) is strong — 8 baselines, 5 seeds / 100 runs, complete appendix proofs, and crucially public code at github.com/christiankoke/scale-continuous-gnns — but the preprint status and tiny QM7 dataset (≤7 heavy atoms) hold it back. Completeness (65) is the soft spot: the author limitation section is a generic impact statement, and validation is confined to small graphs and a coordinate-dependent coarsening construction. Impact (60) reflects released code that also reduces compute cost, tempered by no large-scale validation. Composite: 0.20*80 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 = 72.25 ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: A central assumption underlying GNN deployment is that similar input graphs produce similar latent representations — a property established by a substantial body of prior transferability and stability work (graphon, graphop, metric-measure space convergence). However, all prior positive results rely on convergence modes that do not cover the case where the same physical object is described at different resolution scales: e.g., a molecule with explicit hydrogen atoms vs. one where hydrogens are aggregated into heavy-atom supernodes. In computational chemistry, materials science, and mesh-based PDE solving, this mismatch is routine. A model trained at one resolution that is then asked to process the same system at a different resolution silently fails, and — crucially — this failure cannot be remedied by augmenting the training set, since one cannot faithfully upsample lower-resolution data. Key Idea: The root cause of scale-discontinuity is that standard normalized propagation operators (renormalized adjacency Â, symmetric Laplacian L = I − D^{−½}AD^{−½}) destroy the heat-kernel information that encodes coarse-scale structure. When intra-cluster edge weights diverge (as clusters merge into supernodes), the normalized entries between heavy nodes tend to zero instead of preserving inter-supernode communication. The fix is to propagate using the unnormalized graph Laplacian through its matrix exponential or resolvent — i.e., ψ(L) = ∫₀^∞ e^{−tL} ψ̂(t) dt — which commutes properly with projection/interpolation operators J↓/J↑ between resolution scales and therefore preserves heat-kernel convergence in latent space. Methodology: The paper proceeds in three stages. First, it empirically demonstrates the failure mode on QM7 using eight GNN baselines (GCN, GATv2, ChebNet, GIN, SAG, SAG-M, UFGNet, Lanczos) by training at high or low resolution and evaluating cross-resolution, measuring both MAE and ‖F − F̲‖ (embedding distance between original and coarsened graph). Second, it traces the failure theoretically: a graph sequence {Gω} converges to the coarse limit G in the heat-kernel sense (‖e^{−tL_ω} − J↑e^{−tL̲}J↓‖ → 0), but standard normalized propagation produces disconnected effective limit graphs where inter-supernode communication is severed. Third, it proposes Laplace-transform propagation matrices ψ(L) := ∫₀^∞ e^{−tL} ψ̂(t) dt, covering heat kernels (Dirac at s: e^{−sL}) and resolvent powers ((λI + L)^{−k}). Theorem 7.1 proves ‖F − F̲‖ ≤ C_θ · max_i{∫₀^∞ |ψ̂_i(t)| · ‖e^{−tL_ω} − J↑e^{−tL̲}J↓‖ dt}, guaranteeing convergence of embeddings whenever inputs converge in heat-kernel norm. Modifications cover both spectral networks (replacing polynomial T_ℓ(L) with ψ_ℓ(L)) and MPNNs (replacing adjacency A with ψ(L) as pairwise weights). Key design decisions: (1) use of the unnormalized Laplacian L = M^{−1}(D − A) rather than normalized L̲ — well-justified because normalized Laplacians are blind to the relevant convergence; (2) sparse approximation exploiting exponential off-diagonal decay — justified theoretically and empirically (>99.87% of entries negligible at ε = 0.05); (3) an initial Laplace-transform pass on raw features before MPNN layers — necessary to bring the input into the scale-continuous regime. Key Results: On QM7 molecular regression (7,165 molecules, MAE in kcal/mol, 5 seeds): all standard architectures fail cross-resolution. GCN: 63.6 same-resolution vs. 136.7–138.1 cross-resolution. GIN: 17–38 same-resolution vs. 658–1835 cross-resolution. Multi-scale variants (SAG, UFGNet, Lanczos) do not help — Lanczos reaches 938 kcal/mol cross-resolution from a 9.9 same-resolution baseline. Proposed methods: MPNNres achieves 18.0±4.3 in all four train/inference combinations; Spectralexp achieves 15.9±1.1 consistently. Embedding distances: standard methods range from 18 to 2876; proposed methods range from 0.2 to 0.3. On node classification with k-clique expansion (Citeseer, Cora, 100 runs), standard methods degrade significantly with clique size k while LTF methods remain stable. On stochastic block models varying intra-cluster connectivity from p=0 to p=1, LTF methods converge to the coarse-graph embedding (‖F_{p=1} − F̲‖ ≈ 0.12–0.30) while standard methods reach 2.15–24877. Computational cost on large graphs (>18k nodes): resolvent uses 168.9 MiB memory and 35.86 ms/epoch vs. ChebNet's 551 MiB and 107 ms, GAT's 2473 MiB and 271 ms. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The negative result — that standard GNNs violate continuity under the natural heat-kernel convergence mode governing coarse-graining — is genuinely novel and directly contradicts the prevailing view established by prior stability literature; the Laplace-transform propagation fix and its theoretical guarantees constitute a new method, while the formalization of scale-continuity via heat-kernel convergence constitutes a new problem formulation. The use of resolvent and exponential filters is not itself new (Koke 2023 used resolvents), but the scale-continuity analysis, its extension to MPNNs, and the empirical cross-resolution benchmarking framework are new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations across 8 baseline architectures, 4 experimental settings (QM7, citation networks, SBM, continuum limit), with 5 seeds on QM7 and 100 runs on citation networks. Error bars are reported throughout. The formal proofs are complete and provided in the appendix (Appendices D–H), covering spectral and message-passing variants, both same-node-set stability and cross-resolution stability. Code is released at https://github.com/christiankoke/scale-continuous-gnns. The theoretical convergence guarantee (Theorem 7.1) is tight enough to be validated by Fig. 7 and Fig. 20. One mild methodological concern: the evaluation dataset QM7 is small (7,165 molecules, up to 7 heavy atoms), and the cross-resolution construction is bespoke (collapsing H-atoms into supernodes); it is unclear whether the failure modes and fixes generalize uniformly to other graph domains without physical distance-based edge weights. This is a preprint; peer-reviewed status is not confirmed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods evaluated on real datasets with GPU measurements). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA Quadro RTX 8000; the resolvent propagation matrix is computed once per graph via sparse linear solve and used across layers. - Efficient: Resolvent uses 168.9 MiB GPU memory and 35.86 ms/epoch on >18k-node graphs — lower than ChebNet (551 MiB, 107 ms) and dramatically lower than GAT (2473 MiB, 271 ms) — because the propagation matrix is effectively sparse (<0.13% non-negligible entries at ε = 0.01). - Effective: Cross-resolution MAE and embedding distances match same-resolution performance across all four tested train/inference configurations, providing direct evidence of the stated goal. - Scalable: Tested up to ~19k nodes; the paper claims effective sparsity enables scaling further but does not test beyond this scale, leaving behavior on large-scale molecular or mesh datasets (millions of nodes) as a claim rather than a measured result. The work delivers code and trained model configurations, but no standalone library or pretrained weights for deployment. Strengths: - The negative result — proving a failure mode that contradicts the consensus established by a decade of positive stability results — is substantive and practically important; the mechanism (normalization-induced disconnection of effective limit graphs) is clearly explained and provable for all standard GNN types. - Theorem 7.1 gives a quantitative, non-asymptotic bound on embedding differences in terms of measurable heat-kernel distances, enabling direct empirical verification (Fig. 7, Fig. 20). - The proposed fix is architecturally minimal (replace the propagation matrix, keep everything else) and reduces rather than increases computational cost — the resolvent uses less memory and time than ChebNet or GAT. - The evaluation spans four qualitatively different experimental regimes (molecular regression, citation node classification, stochastic block models, continuum-limit torus meshes), all consistent with the theory. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note only a generic impact statement with no specific limitations. The paper does not explicitly address the restriction to small molecular datasets, the dependence of the coarse-graining construction on physical coordinates (available in QM7 but not in most graph benchmarks), or the reliance on unnormalized Laplacians which may introduce numerical instability for graphs with large-degree variation. The paper notes that the initial hyperparameter (Laplacian rescaling factor c_nf) needs tuning for unweighted graphs, which is acknowledged but not analyzed in depth. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The primary evaluation dataset QM7 is tiny (7k molecules, ≤7 heavy atoms, 1500 test); no large-scale molecular benchmark (OC20, QM9 with larger molecules, PCQM4Mv2) is tested, leaving open whether the cross-resolution benefit persists when the model must fit more diverse chemistry. - The coarse-graining procedure is specific to Coulomb-matrix graphs with physical coordinates; the paper does not demonstrate scale continuity in domains where coarsening is defined differently (e.g., hierarchical graph pooling without physical distance, social network community detection). - No comparison to methods that explicitly handle multi-resolution by design (e.g., hierarchical message passing with learned pooling); the baselines compared (SAG, UFGNet, Lanczos) are older multi-scale methods, not recent learned-coarsening approaches. - The stability constant C_θ in Theorem 7.1 depends on learned parameters, so the bound is not directly computable without a trained model; no analysis of how C_θ scales with depth or width is provided. - Task performance in same-resolution settings for the proposed methods (~15–18 kcal/mol MAE on QM7) is comparable to but not better than baselines on their native resolution, suggesting scale-continuity comes at no expressiveness cost — but this should be confirmed on benchmarks where baseline GNNs are known to be highly expressive. Bottom Line: Any practitioner working with GNNs on multi-resolution or scale-variable graph data should read this paper; it identifies a fundamental, previously unacknowledged failure mode, provides a mechanistically clear and theoretically grounded fix, and delivers code — the main caveat is that empirical validation is confined to small graphs and specialized coarsening constructions.

#28 — Innovative (73)

DRIFTBENCH: MEASURING AND PREDICTING INFRASTRUCTURE DRIFT IN LLM SERVING SYSTEMS

G Vitale · openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DriftBench, a measurement and prediction framework for quantifying LLM output consistency changes caused by infrastructure transitions (hardware, precision, serving framework), along with the Portability Risk Index (PRI), an XGBoost predictor of drift magnitude from configuration metadata. - Why it's important: Organizations upgrading GPU hardware or switching serving frameworks to cut costs routinely assume identical model weights plus identical inputs yields functionally equivalent outputs—an assumption this work demonstrates fails at rates as high as 23.85% on safety-critical prompts, invisible to standard throughput/latency/perplexity checks. - How it does it: The framework enforces deterministic decoding (temperature=0, seed=42) across 105 configurations to isolate infrastructure as the sole source of output change, measures flip rates using task-appropriate evaluators (execution pass@1, exact match, LlamaGuard-3, semantic embedding similarity), and trains an interaction-feature XGBoost model on the resulting measurements to predict drift for unseen configurations. - The good: The discovery of a systematic/idiosyncratic dichotomy—hardware/precision changes generalize (held-out R²≥0.76) while framework/model changes do not (R²<0.48)—is a genuine empirical finding with actionable deployment consequences, supported by 236,985 prompt-response pairs and transparent three-level R² reporting that honestly distinguishes training fit from true extrapolation. -
