On dense models: 0.5B +1.02 (24.42→25.44), 2B +1.79 (31.91→33.70), 3B +1.35 (37.18→38.53). At 45B MoE (one data point, trained for 240B tokens): avg 52.36→54.02 (+1.66). On MTEB v2.12 with frozen representations from the 3B MoE: overall +2.33 (39.24→41.56), improving 23/25 tasks. Crucially, validation cross-entropy is essentially unchanged across all scales (e.g., 9B MoE: 1.841 NTP vs. 1.840 NITP), confirming that downstream gains come from improved latent geometry rather than from the output distribution. Wall-clock overhead on the 9B MoE is 17 minutes over a 5k-step run (~1.8%). The NTP baseline is the only comparison; no comparison to MTP or other auxiliary objectives is provided. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel element is the autoregressive latent-space prediction using the model's own shallow layers as dynamic, contextualized, self-supervised targets — combining the temporal structure of NTP with continuous representation-space supervision in a way that requires no external encoders. The projection head + cosine loss + stop-gradient triple is individually standard (from self-supervised learning), but the specific combination of temporal shift, shallow-layer anchoring, and theoretical grounding in the NTP null space is non-obvious. The closest prior work — layer-wise distillation (TinyBERT, MiniLLM) and CPC — differs in motivation (compression vs. pre-training regularization) and formulation (cross-model vs. within-model; static vs. autoregressive). Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and thorough: target layer selection, temporal shift, loss function, loss weight, stop-gradient, start step, and projector presence are all varied, each on the 3B MoE model. The theoretical analysis (Lemma 3.1, Theorem 3.2) is complete and detailed in the appendix, with explicit gradient and Hessian derivations. No error bars or multiple training seeds are reported for any experiment — a notable omission given that the gains on smaller models (0.8 points at 1.9B) could plausibly fall within run-to-run variance. Code is released at https://github.com/aHapBean/NITP. All models are trained from scratch on internal data with internal MoE architectures (DeepSeek-V2 style); no results on publicly available checkpoints or on continued pre-training are provided. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The overhead is one SwiGLU MLP projector and a cosine loss; any training setup running NTP can add NITP with trivial engineering changes on commercially available GPU clusters. - Efficient: ~2.3% additional training FLOPs and ~1.8% wall-clock overhead, zero inference overhead (projector discarded post-training); this is negligible compared to data-efficiency and quality gains. - Effective: NITP consistently improves all tested downstream benchmarks across all scales and architectures while preserving token-level perplexity; the MTEB frozen-representation results provide mechanistic evidence that the gain is in representation quality, not output head calibration. - Scalable: Tested up to 45B MoE (one data point, 240B tokens); a trend of declining optimal λ with scale (1.0→0.8→0.6) is observed but not theoretically explained. Performance at 100B+ parameter scale is untested and undemonstrated. The method delivers a software artifact (open-source code) but no trained model weights. Strengths - The temporal shift insight is the cleanest contribution: simply aligning same-position representations across layers (the obvious baseline) collapses to trivial solutions, while the autoregressive (next-token) formulation provides genuinely informative prediction signal. The ablation makes this concrete rather than merely asserted. - The geometric regularization framing is backed by rigorous Hessian analysis that correctly identifies the NTP null space and shows NITP fills it with strictly positive curvature — this is one of the sharper theoretical contributions in the recent pre-training objective literature. - Architecture-agnostic effectiveness across dense and MoE models at multiple scales, combined with the MTEB frozen-representation evaluation, constitutes unusually strong evidence that the mechanism operates at the representation level and not through artifact of benchmark overfitting. - The training dynamics analysis (three-phase NITP loss curve, bottom-up convergence rationale for shallow target stability) provides interpretable mechanistic understanding that guides practitioner choices (apply from step 0, use ≈20% depth for target). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge two limitations: (1) NITP introduces new hyperparameters (target layer depth, loss weight λ) that may require tuning for new architectures; and (2) the robustness of the hyperparameter regime across more diverse model families needs further validation. They confront these directly and concisely in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive, though the acknowledgment is brief and does not quantify the sensitivity range. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison to multi-token prediction (MTP, Gloeckle et al. 2024), which is the most natural competing auxiliary objective and is cited in the related work; the absence of this baseline is the largest single gap in the evaluation. - No error bars or multiple seeds for any experimental result; at the 1.9B scale the average improvement (0.81 points) is small enough that random seed variance could account for it. - All base architectures are internal variants of DeepSeek-V2; zero results on publicly available model families (LLaMA, Qwen, Mistral), which limits reproducibility verification by independent parties. - The theoretical guarantee requires the projector Jacobian to be full-rank ("locally well-conditioned"); whether this holds in practice throughout training is not verified, and the GGN approximation glosses over the MLP projector's non-linearity by reducing the analysis to the direct cosine loss on h. - The λ scheduling trend (1.0 → 0.8 → 0.6 as model size grows) is empirical and lacks theoretical motivation; how to set λ for a new 70B+ model requires a separate search run. - Continued pre-training on existing open-weight models is not evaluated, which would be the most practically relevant use case for most practitioners. Bottom Line This is a technically sound, ICML-published contribution with a clear mechanism, rigorous ablations, and meaningful empirical gains at the cost of ~2% additional training FLOPs — practitioners training LLMs from scratch below 10B parameters should seriously consider adopting NITP, but the lack of comparison to MTP and the absence of results beyond 45B leave open questions about its relative advantage at frontier scale.
Recommended 2605.24956 PDF
#34 — Innovative (71)

Shared Doubt: Zero-shot Cross-Lingual Confidence Estimation for Language Models

A Kyriakou, D Ulmer, I Titov · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a lightweight linear probe, trained on hidden representations of a single source language, that transfers confidence estimation zero-shot to typologically diverse unseen languages by exploiting language-agnostic representations in LLM middle layers. - Why it's important: Multilingual LLM users—especially in lower-resource language communities where models are already less accurate—are poorly served by CE methods that require target-language retraining or degrade under language shift, leaving hallucination detection weakest where it matters most. - How it does it: The probe learns a softmax-weighted combination of all transformer-layer hidden representations at either the last input token or last answer token, concentrating on upper-middle layers where cross-lingual alignment converges, then maps the pooled representation to a correctness probability via a linear layer and sigmoid. - The good: On MKQA, the probe trained on French achieves AUROC 0.82 on Spanish and 0.80 on Russian zero-shot (Qwen3 8B, last answer token), matching or exceeding sequence likelihood, verbalized confidence, P(True), and mass-mean probe baselines on AUROC across most settings. - The missing: Evaluation covers only six languages—all relatively high-resource—with no testing on truly low-resource or highly script-divergent languages where the zero-shot transfer claim would be most consequential. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in LLM uncertainty quantification or multilingual NLP, as it provides the first empirical demonstration of zero-shot CE transfer and a mechanistic account of which layers carry cross-lingual confidence signals.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (72) is the standout and the highest in the cluster: public code is released, standard deviations are reported over both training and ablation seeds, and the layer-importance claim is validated with a causal intervention framework rather than mere correlation. Non-Obviousness (75) is high — the dissociation that calibration transfers robustly while discrimination degrades with typological distance is a genuinely surprising, practically informative finding, and the mechanistic account of where confidence lives is non-trivial. Novelty (73) reflects the first demonstration of zero-shot cross-lingual CE transfer, building on established probing methodology. Completeness (72) and Resilience (66) are pulled down by the GPT-4.1-mini judge confound (no reliability analysis), parallel-dataset inflation risk, only two 8B instruction-tuned backbones, and the unresolved -0.23 AUROC collapse on Japanese, which is the real failure mode but is under-emphasized relative to the calibration framing. Composite: 0.20*73 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*66 + 0.10*66 = 71.
empirical Novelty 73 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 66 Impact 66
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used across language communities worldwide, yet confidence estimation—obtaining a numeric signal indicating whether an LLM output is correct—has been studied almost exclusively for English. Non-English users face a compounded problem: LLMs are factually less accurate in their languages, and existing CE methods that require supervision (e.g., trained probes) or assume distribution stationarity (e.g., calibration methods) either require expensive target-language annotation or degrade silently when language shifts. The specific gap is the absence of any CE method that transfers zero-shot across languages without target-language supervision, which the authors frame as exploiting the language-agnostic structure already present in multilingual LLM representations. Key Idea: Multilingual LLMs converge toward a shared representational subspace in their upper-middle transformer layers, where language-specific surface features are suppressed. The core insight is that correctness signals encoded in this subspace are themselves language-agnostic: a linear probe trained to predict answer correctness in one language can read off those signals in other languages without any retraining, because the geometric structure of correct vs. incorrect representations is approximately preserved across languages in the shared subspace. Methodology: The approach operates on decoder-only transformer LLMs. For each QA example, hidden states are extracted from all L layers at two token positions: the last token of the input prompt (last query token) and the last token of the generated answer (last answer token). These positions are chosen for distinct mechanistic reasons—query latents capture input-side difficulty, answer latents encode post-generation confidence. The probe computes layer weights α_ℓ = softmax(w)_ℓ over a learnable vector w ∈ ℝ^L, pools representations as h̄ = Σ α_ℓ h^(ℓ), and produces a confidence score p̂ = σ(v⊤h̄ + b) via a linear layer. Training uses binary cross-entropy with L2 regularization on v and positive-class weighting for class imbalance. Per-layer z-score normalization addresses exponentially growing hidden-state variance with depth (a design choice well-justified by prior work on pre-layer normalization architectures). Hyperparameters are tuned via Ray Tune with the ASHA scheduler over 50 trials. Three critical design decisions: (1) softmax-weighted layer mixing rather than fixed-layer selection—justified post-hoc by ablation analysis showing middle layers matter causally; (2) linear probe architecture—justified by the linear representation hypothesis; (3) two token positions evaluated separately—justified by mechanistic reasoning about what each encodes. The probe is evaluated on MKQA (5,200 open-ended QA questions per language, 6 languages) and Global-MMLU (4,215 questions filtered from 14,042 multiple-choice items using GPT-4.1 answerability scoring). Answer correctness is judged by GPT-4.1 mini. Baselines include sequence likelihood, P(True), verbalized confidence, and the mass-mean probe. Evaluation metrics are AUROC and AUPR (discrimination) and ECE and Brier score (calibration). Key Results: For Qwen3 8B trained on French (last answer token, MKQA): AUROC 0.82 on Spanish (oracle delta −0.01), 0.73 on Polish (delta −0.09), 0.80 on Russian (delta +0.01), 0.64 on Japanese (delta −0.23). Calibration metrics (ECE, Brier) on Spanish and Russian frequently match or exceed the oracle, whereas discrimination degrades monotonically with typological distance. Against baselines on Spanish MKQA (Qwen3 8B): cross-lingual probe AUROC 0.82 vs. sequence likelihood 0.81, verbalized confidence 0.75, mass-mean probe 0.65, P(True) 0.30. On Japanese, mass-mean probe (AUROC 0.83) outperforms the cross-lingual probe (0.64), marking the boundary where the approach breaks down. Ablation via the causal intervention framework (Geiger et al., 2025) confirms that ablating middle layers causes the largest drops in zero-shot AUROC, while ablating upper layers sometimes improves zero-shot performance by removing language-specific source information. Spearman ρ between learned layer weights and per-layer ablation impact is consistently positive across metrics and languages, validating that the probe's learned weights reflect genuine causal importance. Results replicate across both Llama 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Probing is an established methodology and linear probes for truthfulness have prior art (Li et al., 2023; Marks and Tegmark, 2023); the closest prior work (Zhou et al., 2025) identifies middle layers as important for multilingual CE but restricts to within-language few-shot settings. The genuinely novel contribution is demonstrating zero-shot cross-lingual transfer of a trained CE probe and providing a causal mechanistic account of why it works. The finding that calibration transfers more robustly than discrimination under language shift is a non-obvious empirical result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and use a causal intervention framework rather than simple correlation, which is a methodological strength. Standard deviations over 10 random seeds are reported for ablation experiments; standard deviations over training seeds are reported for probe results. Code is publicly released (https://github.com/AthinaKyriakou/shared-doubt). Hyperparameter tuning details and data preprocessing procedures are documented in the appendix. Concerns: (1) GPT-4.1 mini is used as the correctness judge with no inter-annotator agreement, error-rate analysis, or comparison to exact match—systematic bias in the judge propagates to all CE evaluations. (2) Only two backbone models are tested, both 8B and both instruction-tuned; no base models, no larger scales, no alternative architectures. (3) The ablation and the probe training share the same model checkpoints and datasets—no held-out model validates that middle-layer importance generalizes across architectures. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Experiments run on a single A100-80G GPU; inference requires only extracting hidden states from an existing LLM, which is feasible on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: The probe computation itself is negligible (a linear layer over pooled representations); the main cost is extracting and storing all-layer hidden states per example (for Qwen3 8B: 36 layers × 4096 dimensions × float16 ≈ 300 KB per example), which is modest but non-trivial for large inference batches. - Effective: For close languages (Spanish, French), the probe achieves AUROC competitive with fully supervised baselines; for distant-script languages (Japanese), discrimination degrades substantially (AUROC 0.64 vs. 0.82 on close languages), and calibration on Russian MMLU (ECE 0.42) is significantly worse than the oracle (0.14). - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 8B parameters or 6 languages; authors acknowledge likely degradation for low-resource or typologically distant languages, but provide no empirical bound. Software artifact: code repository released at https://github.com/AthinaKyriakou/shared-doubt. Strengths: - First empirical demonstration of zero-shot cross-lingual transfer of CE via probing, with a causal account of the mechanism (middle-layer ablations causally degrade performance, not merely correlate with it). - The calibration-vs.-discrimination dissociation under language shift—calibration transfers robustly, discrimination degrades—is non-obvious and practically informative: it suggests that zero-shot probes may be more useful for risk thresholding than for ranking outputs by reliability. - Thoughtful experimental design: French as source (rather than English) enables testing English as a genuine OOD case, parallel datasets avoid confounds from domain shift, stratified splits preserve class ratios, and time-sensitive questions are filtered from MKQA. - Hyperparameter tuning is rigorous (Ray Tune, ASHA scheduler, 50 trials, held-out validation set), and training decisions are well-documented with selected hyperparameters tabulated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) only 8B models tested, though they expect results to generalize to larger models based on cited evidence; (2) language selection is limited to relatively high-resource languages, with expected degradation for truly low-resource or typologically distant languages; (3) focus on short-form QA only, with open questions about long-form generation and reasoning; (4) linear probes may fail to capture complex cross-layer interactions. The authors confront these limitations directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - GPT-4.1 mini as correctness judge is the single most underexamined design choice: any systematic error or bias in the judge directly confounds all CE metrics, yet no reliability analysis is provided. For short-form factual QA, exact-match or token-F1 baselines would cost little to add. - The evaluation is conducted exclusively on parallel datasets (same questions in all languages). Real multilingual deployment involves language-specific content; the parallel setup may artificially inflate transfer performance because the LLM has seen equivalent semantic content across languages. - Discrimination (AUROC, AUPR) is more practically useful than calibration for hallucination detection—if you need to flag unreliable outputs, you need discrimination—yet the paper's framing emphasizes the calibration-transfers result, which is less actionable. The -0.23 AUROC gap on Japanese deserves more emphasis as the primary failure mode. - No evaluation of downstream task utility: whether using the CE probe to filter LLM outputs actually reduces user exposure to hallucinations in a realistic pipeline is not demonstrated. - The probe is compared only to methods available without target-language training. A fine-tuned multilingual CE baseline (e.g., training directly on multi-source languages) would clarify how much supervision is actually needed to close the gap. Bottom Line: A practitioner building multilingual CE systems gains a useful zero-shot baseline and a clear mechanistic story about where in the LLM confidence lives, but the restricted language coverage and unresolved discrimination gap on distant languages mean the central claim of viable zero-shot multilingual CE is only partially supported for the settings where it matters most.
Recommended 2605.31220 PDF
#35 — Innovative (71)

TIGER: Bridging the Multimodal Reasoning-Access Gap via Modality Counterfactuals

GKR Lau, MH Nguyen, BKH Low · openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TIGER, a post-training framework that mines text-success/image-failure instances from text-only math corpora and fine-tunes MLLMs on these gap-targeted examples via image-conditioned GRPO to close the modality reasoning-access gap. - Why it's important: MLLMs built on strong language backbones systematically fail on problems they can solve from text when the identical problem is rendered as an image — a gap that persists even when OCR extraction is near-perfect, meaning the bottleneck is not perception but the routing of visual representations into the language backbone's reasoning mechanisms. - How it does it: TIGER renders text problems as images, queries the model with both modalities, retains only text-correct/image-incorrect pairs (D10), and applies GRPO with image-conditioned rollouts and a correctness + format reward, forcing the policy to discover image-conditioned reasoning pathways rather than imitating fixed trajectories. - The good: On Qwen3-VL-8B, TIGER raises MathVerse from 54.5 to 65.8 (+11.3 points) and the aggregate benchmark average from 60.0 to 66.3 (+6.3), and it consistently improves all four tested RLVR-based checkpoints (+3.4 on average) without requiring manually curated multimodal data. - The missing: The diagnostic paradigm — rendering plain text as images — captures only one specific failure mode of visual input; the method is not evaluated on tasks requiring genuine visual grounding of non-text content (diagrams, figures, charts), so it is unclear how much of real-world visual reasoning gaps it addresses. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on MLLM post-training or multimodal reasoning: the modality counterfactual diagnostic is a clean and replicable technique, and the training recipe is simple enough to adopt immediately; skip it if you need peer-reviewed guarantees, as this is a preliminary workshop paper.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (72) lead: the modality-counterfactual diagnostic is a clean isolation technique, and the finding that 92% of image-conditioned errors persist despite correct OCR directly falsifies the perception-first assumption. Rigor (72) is good — 3-seed results, RSA + activation-patching mechanistic support, gap-targeting and GRPO-vs-DPO/SFT ablations — but tempered by being a workshop preprint with no released code and an unanalyzed DPO collapse on InternVL3.5-1B. Resilience (68) is the main limiter: the diagnostic tests plain text rendered as images rather than genuine visual grounding of diagrams/charts, so the strongest 'visual access to reasoning' claims rest on a narrow construct. Composite lands at 71.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are built by connecting a vision encoder to a pre-trained language backbone. The language backbone already contains strong mathematical and logical reasoning capabilities. A widely held assumption is that if the model can correctly perceive (read/extract) visual content, it will reason over it as effectively as over text. This perception-first view motivates most recent work on improving visual recognition and grounding. The paper challenges this assumption: it argues that even after successful perception, the model may fail to route the extracted representation into the latent reasoning mechanisms that activate for equivalent text inputs. Practically, this gap means users presenting mathematical or logical problems as images — screenshots, scanned documents, rendered problem sets — systematically receive worse answers than users who type the same problem as text, a failure invisible to perception-only diagnostics. Key Idea The core insight is a controlled modality counterfactual construction: for any text-only dataset with verifiable answers, render each problem as a plain-text-on-white-background image, query the model on both the text and the image version, and identify instances where the model succeeds on text but fails on the image (D10 partition). These instances cleanly isolate the modality gap from perception failures (confirmed by running OCR and feeding extracted text back to the model, which largely restores text-level accuracy). The trick is then to use only these gap instances as GRPO training data with image-conditioned rollouts, providing targeted reward signal precisely where the gap exists rather than training on randomly sampled image-math pairs. Methodology Stage 1 (Data Construction): Starting from OpenR1-Math-220 (~45K verified problems), the authors construct text inputs and image-rendered counterparts. Greedy decoding on both modalities partitions examples into four subsets (D00, D01, D10, D11). Only D10 (text-correct, image-incorrect) is retained, yielding 1,586–4,043 model-specific training samples. Stage 2 (Gap-targeted Training): GRPO is applied with K=8 rollouts per image prompt. The reward is a weighted sum of answer correctness (α=0.9, Math-Verify) and format correctness (β=0.1, boxed output). Rewards are normalized within the group; the policy is updated via a clipped surrogate objective with KL penalty removed (δ=0). Critical design decisions: (1) image conditioning during GRPO rollouts (tested against a text-conditioned GRPO baseline, GRPOtext, which provides +3.1 points less improvement on average); (2) the gap-targeted selection of D10 (tested against random same-size subsets, which underperform by 2.1 points); (3) using self-generated incorrect responses as the contrast for preference learning. The paper also evaluates SFT (image+text-answer pairs) and DPO (image with text-answer as chosen, image-answer as rejected) on the same data budget. Key Results On synthesized benchmarks (MATH-500-I, BBH-I), TIGER-GRPO narrows or eliminates the text–image accuracy gap across all four tested models (InternVL3.5-1B, Qwen2.5-VL-3B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B). Representative results: Qwen2.5-VL-7B improves from 58.3 to 64.8 on MATH-500-I and from 47.1 to 56.8 on BBH-I; Qwen3-VL-8B improves from 70.9 to 82.4 on MATH-500-I. On real-world benchmarks, Qwen3-VL-8B gains +11.3 on MathVerse, +4.1 on MathVision, and +5.7 on EMMA. SFT and DPO show inconsistent generalization — on Qwen3-VL-8B, SFT degrades MathVision by 2.7 points and EMMA by 6.5 points, while GRPO improves both. DPO catastrophically collapses InternVL3.5-1B on synthesized benchmarks (13.9 on MATH-500-I vs. 37.3 baseline), a result not analyzed in depth. The method also improves text-only performance (Table 15), with Qwen3-VL-8B text-only MATH-500 rising from 75.5 to 83.4. When added on top of four RLVR baselines (R1-OneVision, Vision-SR1, Perception-R1, R1-Onevision), TIGER consistently adds +1.5 to +5.4 points on average. Training dataset sizes are small (1,586–4,043 samples) and training runs on 4 H200 GPUs. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The modality counterfactual diagnostic protocol is genuinely novel as a controlled isolation technique, cleaner than prior gap analyses that conflate perception and reasoning. The training recipe (gap-targeted GRPO on D10) is a novel combination, though GRPO itself is established. The empirical finding that 92% of image-conditioned errors occur despite correct OCR extraction is the most impactful finding, directly contradicting the perception-first assumption. The paper is not a new model, dataset, or benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: GRPOtext vs. GRPOimage (isolates modality conditioning), random subsets vs. D10 (isolates gap targeting), SFT/DPO comparison (isolates training objective). Experiments are repeated 3 times with different seeds; mean ± std are reported. The reasoning-subspace activation (RSA) analysis provides mechanistic evidence, and activation patching (Table 6, 17) provides intervention-based causal support. Point-biserial correlation between RSA and accuracy is moderate (r ≈ 0.37–0.43), which the authors appropriately acknowledge as not strongly predictive. No code or model weights are released (the paper is under review). The paper relies on the closed-source OpenAI prm800k normalizer for answer evaluation. The DPO collapse on InternVL3.5-1B (dropping to 13.9) lacks analysis. The paper is a preliminary workshop submission, not peer-reviewed at a major venue. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (post-training method for existing models). - Practical: Training requires 4 H200 GPUs for models ranging from 1B to 8B parameters; inference uses the same hardware as the base model, so deployment cost is unchanged. - Efficient: Training data sizes are 1,586–4,043 examples — extremely small — and training uses 15 GRPO epochs with 8 rollouts; wall-clock cost is low relative to the gains reported. - Effective: Evidence on MATH-500-I and real benchmarks (MathVerse, EMMA) is consistent and replicated across four model families; the case studies (Appendix E) provide qualitative confirmation; however, the improvement on EMMA (+5.7 for Qwen3-VL-8B) for non-mathematical domains (chemistry, coding) is encouraging but the mechanism is unclear. - Scalable: Only tested on models up to 8B parameters; no experiments on frontier-scale models (70B+); the training dataset size scales with model capability (larger models have fewer gap instances since they already solve more), which may limit gains for stronger future models. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond training scripts referenced in existing repositories (EasyR1, InternVL). Strengths - The modality counterfactual diagnostic (rendering text as image, using OCR-extracted text as a perception control) is methodologically clean and independently replicable; the OCR contingency tables (Table 1, 7) directly falsify the perception-first explanation. - The finding that image-conditioned GRPO improves text-only reasoning (Table 15) is surprising and practically significant, suggesting the method does not sacrifice text capabilities. - The training recipe is remarkably data-efficient: 1,586–4,043 gap-targeted samples produce consistent gains across 6 benchmarks and 4 model families, suggesting high signal-to-noise in the D10 partition. - The complementarity experiments (Table 4) are well-designed: applying TIGER on top of four published RLVR checkpoints demonstrates the method addresses a residual gap that existing RLVR training leaves open. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that their pipeline relies on existing text math corpora, so resulting models may inherit coverage gaps and biases from that source data. They acknowledge potential misuse of more capable MLLMs (surveillance, sensitive data analysis). The moderate RSA-accuracy correlation is explicitly noted. The authors do not directly confront the scope limitation of the synthetic diagnostic (plain text rendered as images vs. real visual content). Analyst-Identified Gaps - The diagnostic paradigm tests one narrow instantiation of visual input — plain text rendered on a white background — which does not constitute genuine visual reasoning. The gap measured on MATH-500-I is primarily about modality-routing for textual content, not spatial, diagrammatic, or perceptual reasoning. The transfer to MathVerse and EMMA is the real test, and while gains are solid, the authors do not evaluate whether TIGER helps specifically on the vision-heavy subsets of those benchmarks vs. the text-heavy subsets (Table 18 partially addresses this for MathVerse subsets, showing V-only gains, but no analysis is provided for why). - The catastrophic DPO failure on InternVL3.5-1B (13.9 vs. 37.3 baseline on MATH-500-I) is reported but not analyzed; this is a significant reproducibility concern for practitioners considering DPO. - The gap-targeted data D is model-specific (each model generates its own D10 partition). No evaluation of cross-model transfer is provided, and the dataset construction requires running inference on 45K problems for each new model. - The paper does not verify that TIGER does not degrade pure visual perception tasks (e.g., grounding, detection benchmarks), which is a concern given that SFT actively reduces visual attention weights (Figure 12, 15). - All training data is math-only; the generalization to EMMA domains (chemistry, coding) produces gains but no ablation explains whether this is because the method improves general visual-to-reasoning routing or because EMMA contains math-adjacent problems. Bottom Line A practitioner training or fine-tuning MLLMs for reasoning-heavy tasks will find the gap-targeted GRPO recipe immediately useful — the training cost is low, the gains are consistent, and the diagnostic protocol (modality counterfactuals + OCR control) provides a principled way to measure and target this specific failure mode; the main caveat is that this is a workshop paper under review, not a peer-reviewed full contribution, and the strongest claims about "visual access to reasoning" rest on a diagnostic that tests text-in-image rather than genuine visual reasoning.
Citations
#36 — Innovative (71)

Revisiting Observation Reduction for Web Agents: Comprehensive Evaluation with a Lightweight Framework

M Enomoto, R Obara, H Zhang, M Oyamada · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Minimal Failure Set (MFS) — the minimal set of HTML elements whose removal causes task failure — as the basis for a lightweight proxy metric ("coverage") that enables fast evaluation of HTML observation reduction methods for LLM-based web agents without running full agent rollouts. - Why it's important: End-to-end evaluation of HTML reduction methods requires 232+ cumulative hours for 11 methods across 32 configurations on 33 tasks, making comprehensive method comparison infeasible and obscuring whether reduction methods actually reduce total latency when their own inference cost is counted. - How it does it: MFSs are constructed once via intervention experiments on successful trajectories using the ddmin delta-debugging algorithm seeded with agent self-reported elements; coverage (fraction of instances where a method retains the full MFS) is then evaluated offline in seconds per instance. - The good: Coverage achieves Pearson r=0.944, Spearman ρ=0.889 correlation with end-to-end success rate on WorkArena L1 while delivering a 290× cumulative and 1098× per-instance speedup; and the resulting insight — that LLM-inference-based reducers can cost more latency than they save — is practically important and previously unquantified. - The missing: Coverage is undefined for representation-transforming methods (summarization, semantic compression) and the entire MFS dataset spans only 59 and 42 instances respectively, limiting statistical confidence in the correlation estimates. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or benchmarking LLM web agents that use HTML-based observations; less relevant for researchers focused on vision-based or summarization-based observation compression.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) leads — adapting delta-debugging (ddmin) from software fault isolation to web-agent trajectory analysis is a principled, non-obvious move, and the surprising negative result that LLM-inference reducers are net-slower than no reduction is quantified in real wall-clock time. Novelty (72) and Rigor (68) are strong: r=0.944 correlation with 290× speedup, cross-LLM independence checks, and partial-correlation controls, but no confidence intervals on the headline coefficients and a modest 59/42-instance MFS dataset. The composite stays below 75 because coverage is undefined for the increasingly-dominant summarization/hybrid methods and no code is released.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-based web agents receive raw HTML observations ranging from 40K to 500K tokens per page step, creating high inference cost and latency. Many extraction methods have been proposed — program-based heuristics, BM25 and dense retrieval, LLM-guided selection, accessibility trees — but prior work evaluates them only on task success rate, ignoring the latency cost of the reduction step itself. This matters because the reduction step can itself require LLM inference (e.g., FocusAgent averages 105.69s per observation on WorkArena), which may offset any gains from shorter inputs. No prior work comprehensively compares across varying reduction ratios, multiple benchmarks, and the joint success-rate-versus-latency trade-off. The primary obstacle is that end-to-end evaluation requires feeding long HTML to a policy LLM across multiple sequential steps against a live web environment: evaluating 11 methods × 32 configurations × 33 tasks with Qwen3.5-122B took 232.4 cumulative hours. Key Idea The core insight is that evaluating whether a reduction method retains task-critical HTML elements is equivalent — and much cheaper — to measuring task success. By precomputing the Minimal Failure Set (MFS) through intervention experiments on already-successful trajectories, coverage can be computed offline (no LLM, no web access) in 0.7 seconds per instance on average versus 12.8 minutes per instance for end-to-end evaluation. The approach adapts delta debugging (ddmin, Zeller & Hildebrandt 2002) from software testing fault isolation to web agent trajectory analysis. Methodology MFS construction proceeds in two phases. Phase 1: successful trajectories are collected from three policy LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-5.1); at each step, the agent's self-reported element references form a candidate set Cs; all elements in Cs are removed from the HTML and the agent is re-run; steps where removal causes failure (confirmed by two independent runs) advance to Phase 2. Phase 2: the ddmin algorithm is applied to minimize Cs to a 1-minimal failure set X̂. For WorkArena, a proxy oracle (matching the erroneous action produced in Phase 1 in a single inference step) replaces full rollouts in ddmin. DOM-distance-based Farthest Point Sampling partitions ddmin candidates to group structurally proximate elements. The resulting MFS dataset: 59 instances on WorkArena L1, 42 on WebLinx. Coverage is the fraction of dataset instances where a reduction method's output fully contains X̂; reduction ratio is the character-length fraction retained. Eleven reduction methods are evaluated across up to 32 configurations: Original HTML (baseline), Random, Pruned by AXTree, DMR-BM25, DMR-Dense (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B), DMR-QueryGen (LLM-generated query before dense retrieval), FocusAgent (LLM selects k elements from full HTML), Prune4Web (Planner screenshot → Filter keyword weights → scoring function), and MFS-optimized variants: DMR-finetuned (embedding model fine-tuned on MFS positives), GEPA ratio=0.2 and ratio=0.6 (pruning programs evolved by LLM-based evolutionary optimizer subject to reduction ratio constraint). Policy models for end-to-end evaluation: Qwen3.5-122B-A10B and MiniMax-M2.5, served on 8×A100 via vLLM. Key Results Correlation: Pearson r=0.944, Spearman ρ=0.889, Kendall τ=0.742 between coverage and end-to-end success rate on WorkArena L1; after partialling out reduction ratio, r=0.913, ρ=0.875 (partial correlation remains strong, ruling out confounding by trivially retaining more HTML). On WebLinx: r=0.855, ρ=0.707 raw; r=0.848, ρ=0.791 partial. Speedup: 290× cumulative, 1098× per-instance on WorkArena; 246×/71× on WebLinx. Key empirical findings: (1) LLM inference-based methods (FocusAgent: 105.69s/obs on WorkArena) achieve higher coverage than retrieval-based methods at low reduction ratios, but their reduction cost exceeds policy inference savings, making them net-slower than passing raw HTML in several configurations. (2) Critical HTML element types differ by benchmark: removing text content drops coverage 59.5% on WebLinx vs. 30.5% on WorkArena; WorkArena relies more heavily on CSS attributes (id: 16.9% drop, class: 10.2% drop, value: 22.0% drop), explaining why BM25 (text-focused) works on WebLinx but not WorkArena. (3) GEPA (ratio=0.2) achieves 2.2× faster per-step latency on WorkArena (65.7s → 30.2s) retaining 84% of original success rate (0.485 → ~0.40); 3.1× faster on WebLinx retaining 89%. (4) Coverage is remarkably sample-efficient: ρ>0.7 with only 4 MFS instances on WorkArena, ~10 on WebLinx. Absolute success rates are low: 48.5% maximum (full HTML, WorkArena) and ~14.4% (WebLinx), reflecting the difficulty of these benchmarks with current models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (MFS-based lightweight evaluation framework) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (domain-specific critical HTML element profiles; LLM reducers are net-slower than no reduction). The application of delta debugging to web agent trajectory analysis is genuinely novel. The GEPA optimization is application of an existing framework (Agrawal et al. 2025) to a new objective. The benchmark-level finding that extractive reduction methods cannot simultaneously achieve low latency and high performance without domain-specific optimization is a new, practically actionable result. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.29397v1, May 2026); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations present: element-type coverage drop analysis (Table 3), FPS vs. random ddmin partitioning (Table 4), sample efficiency curves (Figure 5), per-LLM trajectory model independence analysis, cross-split robustness (Appendix B.2). End-to-end success rates averaged over two independent runs. No error bars or confidence intervals on the headline correlation coefficients; the correlation is computed over 32 method configurations, not individual task instances, so its precision depends on that small pool. The MFS dataset (59/42 instances) is modest; cross-split robustness on WorkArena tests seed variation only, not unseen task types (acknowledged in a footnote). Code not released; MFS data not redistributable due to ServiceNow TOS and WebLinx third-party HTML origins. Full implementation details, prompts, and learned GEPA programs are published in the appendix, enabling reconstruction. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Yes — GEPA program-based reduction runs in under 0.11s per observation with no GPU requirement; policy model serving requires 8×A100, but that is standard for inference on 100B-class models. - Efficient: GEPA reduction overhead is negligible relative to policy inference; in contrast, FocusAgent adds 105.69s per observation (higher than the baseline 65.7s full-HTML latency), making it net-worse. DMR-Dense adds ~3s per step. - Effective: Evidence is strong within the two tested benchmarks for extractive methods; the framework cannot evaluate semantic compression, summarization, or hybrid (screenshot + HTML) approaches, which are increasingly dominant. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 33 task types (WorkArena) or 300 instances (WebLinx); MFS construction must be rerun for new platforms or domains; the paper makes no scaling claims. No software artifact is released. The paper delivers methodology and learned programs, not a library or deployable tool. Strengths - The MFS concept is principled and well-motivated: it turns evaluation from a full-rollout problem into an offline set-containment check, with the theoretical grounding of delta debugging and empirical validation confirming the proxy's reliability. - The finding that LLM-inference-based reducers are net-slower than no reduction is a genuinely surprising and practically important negative result, quantified with real wall-clock numbers, not just FLOPs. - Cross-LLM model independence analysis (coverage computed from Gemini/Claude/GPT trajectories separately, all correlating 0.82–0.88 with success of held-out policy models) is a strong robustness check that reduces concern about trajectory-specific bias. - The GEPA optimization dual-use — coverage as both evaluation metric and optimization objective — cleanly demonstrates the framework's practical value beyond benchmarking. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) coverage is undefined for representation-transforming methods (summarization, semantic compression); (2) MFS reflects only observed trajectories, so elements required by unobserved trajectories are missed; (3) MFS data cannot be redistributed due to licensing constraints, requiring users to reconstruct from procedure. These are confronted directly and represent credible scope boundaries rather than minimizations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The proxy oracle for WorkArena ddmin (matching the specific erroneous action from Phase 1) assumes that erroneous action is stable across partial HTML modifications; if the action changes non-deterministically under partial removal, Phase 2 may converge on elements that are not the true MFS, biasing coverage. This assumption is not empirically verified. - WorkArena cross-split robustness tests the same 33 task types with different random seeds, not genuinely unseen task categories. Generalization to new platform domains (e.g., WebArena's Reddit or GitLab) is untested and would require new MFS construction from scratch. - The baseline success rates are low (48.5% max on WorkArena, 14.4% on WebLinx); it is unclear whether the critical HTML element profiles and method rankings hold for more capable future models that complete tasks more reliably, since higher-success trajectories may exploit different elements. - The paper does not evaluate hybrid methods that combine HTML with screenshots, which are increasingly common in deployed web agents and fall outside the extractive-only scope without acknowledgment. Bottom Line This paper delivers a practical, well-validated evaluation framework that addresses a genuine bottleneck in web agent research and produces actionable findings about which reduction methods actually improve end-to-end latency — making it worth reading for anyone building or comparing LLM-based web agents that process HTML observations.
Search Results 2605.29397 PDF
#37 — Innovative (71)

A Unified Framework for the Evaluation of LLM Agentic Capabilities

P Zhu, L Li, Y Lyu, Q Luo, J Yang, Y Liu, T Hui, X Yuan… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified evaluation framework that migrates 7 heterogeneous LLM agent benchmarks into standardized instruction–tool–environment triplets, executes them under a fixed ReAct-style scaffold (smolagents), provides optional offline environment snapshots, and reports task completion scores, per-task efficiency metrics (steps, tokens, elapsed time), and six-category failure attribution across 15 models. - Why it's important: Agent benchmark leaderboard scores systematically confound intrinsic model capability with scaffold-level implementation choices—parsers, memory formats, tool-call conventions, user simulators—making cross-benchmark and cross-model comparisons unreliable; one benchmark's reference implementation can inflate or suppress a model's score by 20-35+ points relative to a neutral scaffold. - How it does it: By re-encoding all benchmark tasks into a shared (instruction, tool, environment) triplet schema, routing every agent through the same smolagents ToolCallingAgent with a fixed 20-step budget, and replacing volatile live retrieval environments with pre-curated and manually verified offline snapshots, the framework holds scaffold and environment constant so residual variance is attributable to the model. - The good: At 400K rollouts and 5B tokens, the framework produces rank inversions and score shifts that are directionally diagnostic: on τ-bench Retail the GPT-4o vs. GPT-3.5 ordering flips from 61.2% ≫ 20.0% (original) to 28.7% < 55.7% (unified), attributable to the original benchmark's fixed GPT-4-based user simulator sharing stylistic priors with GPT-4o; and online-to-offline gaps of +34.5 TCS (Qwen3-30B-A3B on BFCL) and +37.7 TCS (GPT-4o-mini on MAB) quantify environment-layer failure, not model failure. - The missing: The framework fixes smolagents as the sole scaffold and never characterizes how a different general-purpose scaffold (e.g., LangGraph, AutoGen) would shift results; without this, the claim that unified execution reveals "intrinsic" capability remains partially unverified since scaffold-induced variance has been controlled but not zeroed. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone who designs agent benchmarks, interprets leaderboard rankings, or needs to compare models fairly; practitioners should read Sections 3.2–3.4 and Appendix E for the mechanistic case studies explaining why scaffolding flips verdicts on identical tasks.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) drives the composite — a released open-source harness plus benchmark data that any team interpreting agent leaderboards can use immediately, with directly actionable findings. Novelty (70) is high: no prior single execution harness migrates 7 benchmarks with offline snapshots and unified failure attribution, and the scaffold-vs-environment-vs-model decomposition is a genuine analytical contribution. Rigor (70) reflects the 400K-rollout scale, judge validation (κ=0.95), and released code, discounted for missing significance tests and an unflagged BrowseComp circularity. Non-Obviousness (70) is strong — rank inversions from scaffold choice and the counter-intuitive 'better scaffold exposes more unsafe behavior' finding are surprising. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are solid given honest scope acknowledgment, though the τ-bench simulator change trades one confound for another. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*70+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.30*75 = 71.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents are increasingly evaluated on multi-step, tool-augmented tasks, but nearly every benchmark ships not just task data but also an agent scaffold: a particular parser, memory format, tool-call schema, user simulator, and evaluation script. These bundled components become hidden variables. A model may fail because it cannot solve a task or because its output format is incompatible with a benchmark-specific parser; conversely, a high score may reflect compatibility with a specialized scaffold rather than stronger agentic capability. Prior work (HAL, Kapoor et al. 2026; Dong et al. 2025) observed that performance varies substantially across frameworks, but the mechanistic decomposition—which scaffold choices drive which score shifts, and how much variance is attributable to environmental volatility—remained unclear. The practical stakes are real: without controlled execution, cross-benchmark model comparisons cannot be interpreted as measurements of the underlying model. Key Idea The core insight is that benchmark scores are joint measurements of (model, scaffold, environment), not model alone. The framework holds scaffold and environment constant across all benchmarks, so that score differences across models reflect model capability differences rather than implementation accidents. For dynamic environments specifically, the offline snapshot design separates the question "can the model reason?" from "can the live web be reliably queried?", enabling each to be measured independently. Methodology The framework operates in three layers. First, each benchmark is re-encoded as a triplet D = ⟨I, T, E⟩: an instruction set (task description, ground-truth label, optional canonical tool trace), a tool set (Python callables decorated with `@tool` following the smolagents interface), and an environment set (static files, SQLite databases, or interactive servers). This encoding is modular: new domains are added by implementing the triplet without modifying the evaluation pipeline. Second, a configuration-driven sandbox (YAML system config + JSONL agent config) instantiates a fixed smolagents ToolCallingAgent with a 20-step budget at temperature 0, spawning an isolated environment replica E₀ per task so mutations are confined and the source data stays pristine. For τ-bench and τ²-bench, a UserAgent/DomainAssistant multi-agent topology is instantiated declaratively; for BrowseComp, a locally curated BM25-indexed corpus (830 BrowseComp-Plus snapshots plus actively recovered pages) replaces live web search. Three critical design decisions: (1) smolagents is chosen as a neutral general-purpose scaffold also used as the HAL generalist baseline, avoiding benchmark-favoring bespoke implementations; (2) the τ-bench user simulator is matched to each agent's own model family rather than using the original fixed GPT-4 simulator, removing asymmetric prior advantages at the cost of eliminating cross-agent simulator uniformity; (3) offline snapshot construction for retrieval tasks is performed manually with verification, not automatically, ensuring ground-truth resolution chains remain intact. Third, evaluation applies benchmark-original task-success criteria (deterministic matching or LLM judge, environment-level hash comparison following τ-bench), plus four unified efficiency metrics (steps, input tokens, output tokens, elapsed time, each averaged over tasks), and a six-category failure taxonomy: decision-level (Parsing Failure, Tool Invocation Error, Reasoning Deficit) and execution-level (Iteration Limit Exceeded, Context Overflow, Timeout), classified by an LLM judge applied at scale. Key Results Across 7 benchmarks and 24 domains: Scaffold effects (Table 1): On AgentBench Database, GPT-3.5-Turbo rises 36.7%→63.3% because the original framework's action-text parser fails to extract correct SQL that the unified tool-call interface handles directly. The GPT-3.5 vs. Llama-2-70B gap shrinks from 23.7 to 7.8 points. On τ²-bench, DeepSeek-V3.2 drops −23.8/−35.5/−11.1 across domains as the tightly-coupled reference scaffold is replaced by the uniform one. On τ-bench Retail, the ranking inverts: GPT-4o falls from 61.2% to 28.7% while GPT-3.5 rises from 20.0% to 55.7%, attributable to the original benchmark's GPT-4 user simulator sharing stylistic priors with GPT-4o. Efficiency exposes deceptive TCS ties: on AgentBench Web Shopping, GPT-3.5 (56.5%) and Llama-2-70B (54.9%) appear comparable, but Llama-2-70B consumes 5× the steps, 15× the input tokens, and 8× the execution time. Environmental volatility (Table 2): Moving from Online to Offline on BFCL Web Search yields +34.5 TCS for Qwen3-30B-A3B and +7.5 for Gemini-2.5-Flash. On MAB Research, GPT-4o-mini gains +35.1 TCS offline. Appendix E traces four distinct environmental failure modes in live-web tasks: stale indexed snippets, 403 anti-bot denials, search-index drift corrupting multi-hop chains, and tool-output bloat exhausting token budgets—none attributable to agent reasoning. Advanced model profiles (Table 3): Under unified scaffold, no model is Pareto-optimal. Gemini-3-flash leads on structured short-horizon tasks (AgentBench DB 74.3%, BFCL Single-Turn 83.1%) but scores 35.1% on τ²-bench Telecom where DeepSeek-V3.2 reaches 85.1%. GPT-5-mini is strongest on stateful dialogue (τ-bench Retail 59.1%) but weakest on non-live single-shot function calling (54.8% vs. Gemini 83.1%). The open-vs-closed weight gap has effectively closed under the unified scaffold—DeepSeek-V3.2 trails GPT-5-mini and Gemini-3-flash on AgentBench Database by less than 3 points. Failure analysis (Figure 3): Reasoning Deficit dominates Gemini-3-flash (52.1%), GPT-5-mini (49.2%), and Qwen3-235B (71.6%). DeepSeek-V3.2 is an outlier with Parsing Failure (37.8%) edging out Reasoning Deficit (31.8%). GPT-5-mini and DeepSeek-V3.2 frequently exhaust the iteration limit (32.4% and 24.5%), indicating weak termination on long-horizon tasks. Context Overflow and Timeout stay below 5% on every model. Safety (Figure 4): Under the unified scaffold, both Llama-3.1-70B (21.0→31.2) and Qwen2.5-72B (22.9→37.3) score substantially lower (more unsafe) on AgentSafetyBench, suggesting that the standardized tool-call interface exposes risky behaviors that the original prompt-based scaffold masked through parsing failures and incomplete executions. Judge validation (Table 6): Gemini-3-flash achieves Cohen's κ = 0.95 against human-gold labels on 300 stratified samples (human inter-annotator κ = 0.97). This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.27898v1, May 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The unified framework is a genuinely novel infrastructure contribution: no prior work provides a single execution harness that migrates 7 established agent benchmarks with offline snapshot support and unified failure attribution at this scale. The empirical findings—that scaffold choice produces rank inversions and that offline-online gaps quantify environmental rather than model failure—are new results, though the general observation that scaffolding matters was previously noted (HAL, Zhu et al. 2026). What is novel versus a standard combination: the systematic mechanistic decomposition (scaffold effect vs. environment effect vs. model capability) and the case-study-level attribution in Appendix E are genuinely new analytical contributions, not just replication at scale. Rigor & Reproducibility The scale (400K rollouts, 5B tokens) is substantial and provides high statistical power on the main directional claims. Cohen's κ is reported for judge-human alignment. Code and benchmark data are released (GitHub + HuggingFace). However, no statistical significance tests or confidence intervals accompany the score differences; given the bidirectional nature of the findings and the claim that direction is diagnostic, this is a methodological gap. No ablation isolates the contribution of individual framework components (e.g., retry channel vs. system prompt length). The offline snapshot construction involves manual curation and LLM-assisted recovery using Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5 for BrowseComp—evaluating those same models on this environment introduces a potential circularity the paper does not flag. The τ-bench user simulator modification (model-family-matched vs. fixed GPT-4) trades one confound for another and is presented as a design choice without a systematic comparison of its effect. Deployability (SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS—a built evaluation harness with documented deployment and empirical validation. - Practical: Runs on standard cloud API endpoints (OpenAI-compatible, Anthropic) or locally via vLLM/Hugging Face Transformers on commercially available GPU hardware; the 400K-rollout study used multi-worker parallelism (up to 8 workers). - Efficient: Total compute at 5B tokens is non-trivial; per-model evaluation varies widely (Gemini-3-flash on MAB Research: 2,310K input tokens averaged over tasks; comparable tasks on Qwen3-235B use 684K). The offline setting eliminates runtime environment variability but requires upfront snapshot curation effort (substantial for BrowseComp, where LLM agents were used to recover missing content). - Effective: The framework demonstrably disentangles scaffold effects from model capability, as confirmed by mechanistically traced case studies (Appendix E) and rank inversions that match the paper's causal hypotheses. The τ-bench simulator choice introduces a new design variable that is controlled within the framework but differs from the original benchmark's protocol. - Scalable: Adding new benchmarks requires implementing the instruction-tool-environment triplet plus evaluation function; the modular design lowers per-benchmark engineering cost, but the appendix reveals substantial per-benchmark migration effort (each benchmark has a dedicated 5-10 page migration description). The framework does not address multimodal or embodied environments, which would require extending the observation encoding and action grounding abstractions. The paper delivers a software artifact (released code + benchmark data), not merely a proof or position. Strengths - The mechanistic case studies in Appendix E are the strongest part of the paper: tracing exactly four framework-level properties (system prompt density, memory/retry structure, forced surrender template, tool termination contract) that together flip Gemini-2.5-flash's verdict from incorrect to correct on the same task with the same model and same live web is a convincing proof of mechanism, not just correlation. - The offline-vs-online decomposition is clean and operationally useful: it lets benchmark designers isolate environment-layer failure modes (stale snippets, 403 denials, search-index drift, tool bloat) from agent reasoning failures, a separation that prior benchmarks collapse into a single score. - The safety finding is counter-intuitive and practically important: a more capable scaffold exposes riskier agent behavior rather than containing it, which has direct implications for safety evaluation methodology. - The efficiency metrics (steps, tokens, time) alongside TCS surface operationally relevant model differences invisible to accuracy-only reporting—models with identical TCS can differ by an order of magnitude in compute cost. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge three scope limitations: (1) single scaffold—only smolagents is tested, leaving cross-scaffold variance uncharacterized; (2) modality and embodiment—the framework covers only text-centric tasks and does not address GUI agents, vision-language navigation, or physically embodied settings; (3) benchmark coverage—7 benchmarks across 24 domains is a small fraction of the rapidly evolving landscape, and results may not generalize to uncovered domains. The authors confront these directly and frame them as open directions rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps The most significant unacknowledged issue is the BrowseComp environment construction: recovering missing snapshot content using Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5 as autonomous browsing agents, then evaluating those same models on the resulting corpus, is a potential Goodhart's Law problem—the environment may inadvertently encode the reasoning patterns of the models used to build it. The paper does not flag this. Second, the τ-bench simulator change (model-family-matched vs. original fixed GPT-4) eliminates the GPT-4 prior advantage but introduces a new asymmetry: different models now interact with different simulators, so cross-model comparisons on τ-bench under the unified framework are not fully apples-to-apples. Third, the failure taxonomy is classified by LLM judge at scale, but inter-rater reliability for the taxonomy itself is not reported—only judge-vs-human agreement for task-success scoring. Fourth, the 20-step fixed budget may structurally disadvantage some models on long-horizon tasks regardless of intrinsic capability; no ablation on step budget is provided. Bottom Line Any practitioner who benchmarks LLM agents or interprets agent leaderboard rankings should read this paper, as it provides the strongest empirical case to date that reported scores are as much a property of the scaffold as of the model, and supplies an open-source harness for doing better.
Recommended 2605.27898 PDF
#38 — Innovative (71)

Diagnosing LLM Arbitration Behavior over Pre-evidence Epistemic States in RAG-based Fact-Checking

Y Sun, W Shang, W Gao, X Huang, J Ma · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PAVE (Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation), a diagnostic testbed that stratifies LLM verifiers into four pre-retrieval epistemic states and measures how they arbitrate between parametric knowledge and retrieved evidence under prior-context discrepancy, plus a JSD-based test-time method that selects between the two sources based on output stability. - Why it's important: Standard fact-checking benchmarks pair claims with clean gold evidence, structurally precluding any conflict, so existing accuracy metrics cannot distinguish a model that arbitrates well from one whose parametric knowledge happens to be correct — a critical blind spot for real deployments where retrieval is noisy or adversarial. - How it does it: The core mechanism is consistency-based epistemic probing: query the model N=10 times without evidence, classify each claim as KNOWN/UNKNOWN (strict output consistency) and KNOWS/UNKNOWS (majority correctness), then measure persistence under counterfactual evidence and correction under gold evidence separately within each quadrant. - The good: The demonstration that Qwen3-32B (87.72% prior accuracy, Margin = −0.115) collapses 43.02 points under PCD while Deepseek-v3 (64.17% prior, Margin = +0.235) drops only 19.11 points is a concrete, surprising result that directly invalidates using conventional accuracy for verifier selection. - The missing: The scaling conclusion — that larger models arbitrate better — rests entirely on the Llama family (1B/3B/8B/70B); Qwen3-32B's poor arbitration at 32B contradicts cross-architecture generalization, and this is not confronted. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone selecting or auditing LLM verifiers in RAG pipelines; the arbitration profiles for seven specific models (Deepseek-v3 through Llama3-8B) are immediately actionable, even if the JSD method itself is a modest improvement.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (78) are the strengths — the four-quadrant epistemic-state stratification for PCD evaluation is a novel benchmark framing, and the finding that high prior accuracy anticorrelates with arbitration robustness (Qwen3-32B vs Deepseek-v3 reversal) is surprising and actionable. Rigor (70) credits the multi-stage counterfactual quality control (Fleiss' κ, human audit) and sensitivity sweeps but is reduced by no code repository, an unreported JSD calibration set, preprint status, and high Qwen3-32B point-estimate instability. Completeness (68) and Resilience (65) are limited by the unconfronted scaling contradiction (claim rests on the Llama family while Qwen3-32B violates it) and the unaddressed GPT-4o/GPT-4o-mini stylistic confound. Impact (62) reflects a released dataset and immediately useful verifier profiles, but a modest, threshold-dependent JSD method (OA only 57–63%). Weighted ≈ 71.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RAG-based fact-checking requires LLM verifiers to adjudicate claims against retrieved evidence, but retrieval is imperfect: evidence may be noisy, temporally outdated, or actively misleading. When a model's parametric beliefs conflict with retrieved context — termed prior-context discrepancy (PCD) — the verifier must choose which signal to trust. Existing evaluation frameworks are blind to this: standard benchmarks (FEVER, Quantemp, PolitiFact, Snopes) provide clean gold evidence that forecloses any discrepancy, and evaluation protocols report only final verdict accuracy, leaving the arbitration mechanism completely unexamined. A second problem is that arbitration behavior is conditioned on the verifier's epistemic state: a confidently wrong model resisting sound evidence is a qualitatively different failure from an uncertain model lacking a stable prior. These cases are collapsed under outcome-level evaluation, making it impossible to diagnose or correct them. Key Idea The key insight is epistemic state-conditioning: evaluate LLM verifiers not on their final accuracy but on how they respond to evidence given what they already believe and how confidently they believe it. By running each model without evidence N times, the framework assigns every (model, claim) pair to one of four Knowledge Boundary quadrants — Known-Knows, Known-Unknowns, Unknown-Knows, Unknown-Unknowns — and then measures persistence (does the model resist counterfactual evidence when it is already correct?) and correction (does it update when it is wrong and accurate evidence is provided?) within each quadrant separately. This conditioned evaluation surfaces failure modes hidden under aggregate accuracy. Methodology The pipeline has three components. (1) Epistemic state characterization: each claim is queried N=10 times at temperature 0.3 without retrieved evidence. Strict consistency (all N outputs identical) defines KNOWN; any variation defines UNKNOWN. Whether the majority verdict matches ground truth defines KNOWS vs. UNKNOWS. This 2×2 cross yields four states per (model, claim). (2) PCD benchmark: two dimensions. Counterfactual: 10,866 claims from Quantemp, PolitiFact, and Snopes augmented with GPT-4o-generated counterfactual evidence via entity substitution (Tentity, replacing key entities with same-type alternatives) and semantic negation (Tsemantic, generating semantically contradicting passages). Quality-controlled via automatic filtering plus human audit of 100 stratified samples (Fleiss' κ=0.76; pass rates 88–95% across four criteria). Temporal: 3,995 true claims crawled from Wikipedia's Current Events portal (2024–2025), retaining only claims each model answers incorrectly without evidence and that post-date its training cutoff. (3) Metrics: Correction Rate (CR) = post-retrieval accuracy on prior-wrong claims given gold evidence; Persistence Rate (PR) = post-retrieval accuracy on prior-correct claims given counterfactual evidence; Margin = CR + PR − 1 (positive = more corrected than misled); Odds of Influence (OI) computed separately for KNOWN and UNKNOWN regimes. The JSD arbitration method runs K=5 samples each with and without evidence, computes JSDext = H(p̄ext) − E[H(pext)] and JSDpar analogously, and uses Δ = JSDpar − JSDext against a held-out-calibrated threshold τ ∈ [−0.08, −0.06] to select the more stable source. A vote-entropy variant handles models without logit access. Critical design decisions: (a) Strict all-or-nothing KNOWN criterion is aggressive and sensitive to N and temperature — the paper validates stability via saturation analysis up to N=40 but does not test softer thresholds; (b) counterfactual evidence generated by GPT-4o creates a potential confound for evaluating GPT-4o-mini (a related model) that may recognize stylistic artifacts, artificially inflating persistence — this is not addressed; (c) the temporal subset includes only claims the specific model already answers incorrectly, biasing temporal correction rates upward by construction. Key Results Across seven LLMs (Deepseek-v3 ~617B, Gemini-2.5-flash, Phi-4, Qwen3-32B, Mistral-7B, GPT-4o-mini, Llama3-8B) on the counterfactual setting: Deepseek-v3 achieves Margin = +0.235 (KK persistence rate 71.70%, KU correction rate 40.75%); Llama3-8B achieves Margin = −0.661 (KK persistence only 29.95%). Qwen3-32B, despite the highest prior accuracy (87.72%), collapses to 44.70% post-intervention vs. Deepseek-v3's 45.06% from 64.17%. On temporal acquisition, models are dramatically more receptive: Llama3-8B temporal KU CR = 94.81% vs. counterfactual 30.80%; GPT-4o-mini temporal OI = 9.23 vs. counterfactual 0.35. Counter-semantic counterfactuals are substantially harder to resist than counter-entity: Mistral-7B PR drops 56 points from entity to semantic type. Within the Llama family, Margin improves monotonically from negative at 1B/3B to +0.50 at 70B. The JSD method achieves best or competitive overall accuracy (OA) in 5 of 6 LLM families, with the largest gain on Mistral-7B: 57.08% vs. second-best 50.50% (TPC). Absolute OA for the JSD method ranges from 57–63%, indicating the overall problem remains far from solved. Baselines (8 methods from Huang et al. 2024) are current but all are from arXiv preprints, not peer-reviewed proceedings. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The four-quadrant KB stratification applied specifically to PCD evaluation in fact-checking is genuinely novel and fills a real gap (no existing benchmark is designed for this setting). The empirical finding that high prior accuracy anticorrelates with PCD robustness is surprising and actionable. The JSD arbitration method is an application of existing consistency-based uncertainty estimation (Chen & Mueller 2024) to source selection — the technique is not new, but its operationalization in this context is validated empirically. The counterfactual construction pipeline combines known techniques (entity substitution, semantic perturbation) in a multi-source, quality-controlled way. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover temperature (0.1–1.0), iteration count (K up to 40), and output token variants (Fleiss' κ=0.8856 across 10 prompt variants on 500-sample subset). Variance statistics for CR and PR across 10 runs are reported per model; Qwen3-32B shows high instability (σ=0.2283 for PR-KK) that raises questions about the reliability of its point estimates. Human validity audit on 100 stratified counterfactual instances (κ=0.76) is reasonable but covers only ~1% of the 10,866-claim counterfactual set. Dataset is released at Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.18151788); no code repository URL is provided. The threshold τ for the JSD method is calibrated per-model on a held-out validation set whose size and composition are not reported, limiting zero-shot deployment to new models. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.01120v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Runs on any commercially available hardware that supports the evaluated LLMs; no model modification, fine-tuning, or internal weight access required — only forward passes. - Efficient: The JSD method requires K=5 forward passes per query (5× inference cost); K=3 retains competitive performance (Llama3-8B IE = 56.20%), and the vote-based variant works for models without logit access, but the overhead is non-trivial at scale. - Effective: Achieves best OA on 4 of 6 evaluated models and second on a fifth; fails to best ExplicitSCR on Gemini-2.5-flash; absolute OA of 57–63% reflects a hard problem, not saturated performance. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the 7 evaluated models; the calibrated threshold τ is model-specific, requiring a held-out validation set for each new model, which limits plug-and-play deployment. The paper delivers a dataset artifact (Zenodo) and algorithmic description but no software library or checkpoint. Strengths - The KB stratification framework is conceptually clean and practically useful: it reveals that the same final accuracy can mask radically different robustness profiles, demonstrated concretely by the Qwen3-32B vs. Deepseek-v3 reversal. - The asymmetry between temporal acquisition and counterfactual correction — models are far more receptive to absorbing new knowledge than to revising confidently wrong prior beliefs — is a non-obvious, replicable finding with direct implications for RAG pipeline design (e.g., preferring retrieval for knowledge gaps over knowledge conflicts). - The multi-stage counterfactual quality control (automatic filters + human audit + Fleiss' κ reporting) is more rigorous than typical adversarial dataset papers. - The multi-label extension to PUBHEALTH (3,000 claims, True/False/Unproven) shows methodological generalizability beyond binary classification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: binary-only scope (free-form explanation evaluation is out of scope); conflicts within retrieved evidence and cases where both parametric and contextual sources are wrong are excluded; claims correctly answered without evidence post-cutoff are excluded from temporal evaluation (hallucination vs. valid inference cannot be distinguished); the JSD method incurs marginal inference overhead; temperature and iteration count introduce metric variability (addressed in appendix sensitivity analyses). The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The strict all-or-nothing KNOWN criterion is not compared against soft thresholds (e.g., ≥80% agreement), and the sensitivity of the KB quadrant distributions to this design choice is unreported. - Counterfactual evidence generated by GPT-4o may carry systematic stylistic signatures recognizable to GPT-4o-mini (a related model), potentially inflating GPT-4o-mini's counter-semantic persistence rate as artifact detection rather than genuine epistemic robustness; this confound is not discussed. - The scaling finding (larger = better arbitration) rests solely on the Llama family and directly conflicts with Qwen3-32B's behavior; the paper does not reconcile this, and the cross-architecture generalization claim is not supported. - No comparison against training-time solutions (RLHF or DPO on PCD scenarios); the baseline pool is exclusively test-time methods, so the headroom left by training approaches is unknown. - Absolute OA values (57–63%) and the fact that some baselines beat the JSD method on Gemini-2.5-flash suggest the method has meaningful failure modes that are not fully characterized. Bottom Line Practitioners selecting LLM verifiers for RAG fact-checking should read this paper for the arbitration profiles of seven current models and the concrete finding that prior accuracy is a misleading selection criterion; the JSD arbitration method offers a simple, model-agnostic improvement but provides only incremental gains over existing test-time baselines.
Citations 2606.01120 PDF
#39 — Innovative (71)

GlucoFM: A Dual-Stream Foundation Model for Continuous Glucose Monitoring

Z Li, K Natarajan, W Zhang, M Zhou, SA Lee, Y Zhang… · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
