Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (58) hold it below 75: the launch-bound observation and CUDA Graphs remedy are acknowledged folk knowledge, so the contribution is the controlled cross-GPU quantification, not a surprising mechanism. Impact (65) and the lack of any public artifact (preprint, Modal scripts only on request) further cap it; the A/B is also run only on the two extreme GPUs, leaving A100/L40S inferred. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 = 69.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 80 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Physical-AI systems — robot policy heads (OpenVLA-7B, π0, RT-2-class), autonomous-driving language copilots, on-device assistants — run a workload that LLM serving research mostly ignores: single-stream, batch-1, autoregressive decode of 7–8B-class transformers, where per-token latency is the user-facing metric and throughput batching is structurally unavailable. The dominant analytical model treats batch-1 decode as HBM-bandwidth-bound: step time ≈ (weights + KV cache) / peak_HBM_bandwidth. This model drives the assumption that the GPU deployment ladder L4 → L40S → A100 → H100 is the cost-per-token ladder, and motivates a large body of KV compression and weight quantization work as the primary optimization levers. The gap: empirically, an H100 achieves only ~27% of its peak HBM bandwidth at batch-1 decode, while an L4 achieves ~81%, so the HBM-bound model overestimates achievable speedup on fast silicon by a factor of three to four — a miscalibration with direct consequences for hardware selection and optimization priority. Key Idea Per-kernel CPU launch overhead is approximately constant per decode step (≈30 µs per kernel × ~280 kernels per Qwen-2.5-7B step ≈ 8.4 ms total on H100) and does not scale with HBM bandwidth. On L4, where each kernel's GPU compute takes ~200 µs, the 30 µs launch gap is negligible (~3% of step time). On H100, where the same kernel's GPU compute takes ~10 µs because HBM bandwidth is 11× higher, the launch gap exceeds GPU compute time (~57% of step time). CUDA Graphs, which replaces per-kernel CPU dispatch with a single DAG replay, removes this overhead and is the single-knob intervention that cleanly isolates the launch tax. For L4-class hardware, the parallel insight is that quantization only lands as a bandwidth saving if the kernel implementation streams int4 weights through HBM on the target micro-architecture (Ada SM89), not if it uses SM80-tuned Marlin kernels. Methodology The measurement protocol is a 3×4×4 factorial sweep: three GQA models (Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), four NVIDIA GPUs (H100 SXM5 at 3350 GB/s, A100-80GB SXM4 at 2039 GB/s, L40S at 864 GB/s, L4 at 300 GB/s), four context lengths (2048, 4096, 8192, 16384), all bf16, batch=1, PyTorch SDPA attention on Modal cloud containers (same Debian slim + PyTorch 2.4 image across all cells). Each cell runs 5 warmup + 30 measured single-token decode steps; tobs is the median. The key metric is Rfloor = tfloor/tobs where tfloor = (W+K)/Bpeak uses spec-sheet peak bandwidth and analytically computed bytes-per-step. Four cells OOM'd on L4 (24 GB), leaving 44 valid cells. The CUDA Graphs A/B is a within-session eager→graphed pair on the same container with the same warmup state, replicated across N=10 fresh Modal containers for the headline H100 ctx=2048 cell. Pre-registered falsification thresholds were set before running: H100 speedup <1.15× or L4 speedup >1.15× would refute the launch-tax interpretation. Three critical design decisions: (1) Within-session speedup (CV 0.9%) is used as the headline rather than cross-session ratios (CV 35.3% at N=3), because the within-session ratio is stable while cross-session eager times vary with cloud host scheduling. (2) Nsight Compute was blocked on Modal containers; torch.profiler with analytic byte counts was substituted, which introduces 3.2× overhead on H100 (vs 1.01× on L4), causing profiler-derived bandwidth utilization numbers to understate H100 efficiency — the paper reports unprofiled medians as the headline. (3) torch.compile and FA2+CUDA Graphs cells failed with CUDA allocator errors and were excluded rather than worked around. The quantization comparison on L4 covers bf16, bnb-nf4, AutoAWQ+Marlin (int4, SM80-tuned), and GPTQ+ExLlamaV2 (EXL2 4.25bpw, SM89-tuned), all at Qwen-2.5-7B ctx=2048 batch=1, with N=3 fresh containers for the ExLlamaV2 cell. The backend-pinned SDPA test uses 50 warmup + 300 measured single-layer attention calls per backend on H100 at the Llama-3.1-8B decode shape (forced via SDPBackend context manager). Key Results Rfloor across 44 cells is monotone in peak HBM bandwidth: H100 (min 0.190, median 0.260, max 0.310), A100-80GB (min 0.192, median 0.275, max 0.415), L40S (min 0.340, median 0.516, max 0.723), L4 (min 0.354, median 0.694, max 0.810). At H100 ctx=2048, the Qwen-2.5-7B cell observes 14.83 ms vs an analytic floor of 3.93 ms (Rfloor=0.27). CUDA Graphs A/B at N=10 (H100 ctx=2048 Qwen-2.5-7B): 14.83 ms eager → 11.78 ms graphed, speedup 1.259×, 95% bootstrap CI [1.253, 1.267], CV 0.9%. Same A/B on L4: 64.48 ms → 62.50 ms, speedup 1.028× — a null result, as predicted. The removed launch overhead on H100 is 3.05 ms, ~20.6% of the eager step. At batch=4 on H100, the graphed speedup falls monotonically from 1.110× (ctx=2048) to 1.036× (ctx=16384). Backend-pinned SDPA on H100 (Llama-3.1-8B shape, ctx=2048): default SDPA 36.05 µs/layer, SDPA FLASH_ATTENTION 44.35 µs (0.81×), FlashInfer 48.20 µs (0.75×), FA-3 79.25 µs (0.45×), SDPA MATH 177.55 µs (0.20×), CUDNN_ATTENTION unsupported for this GQA shape on H100 SXM5 PyTorch 2.8+cu126. Default SDPA wins, and the mechanism is unexplained — the cuDNN path is definitively not responsible. L4 quantization (Qwen-2.5-7B ctx=2048): bf16 62.32 ms (Rfloor=0.821), bnb-nf4 59.36 ms (1.05× over bf16), AutoAWQ+Marlin 45.24 ms (1.38×), GPTQ+ExLlamaV2 EXL2 4.25bpw 17.36 ms (3.59×, Rfloor=0.754 vs a 4×-reduced floor of 13.09 ms). The ExLlamaV2 vs AutoAWQ gap is explained by Ada SM89 vs Ampere SM80 kernel tuning, not bit-width. Cost per million tokens (Modal list rates, May 2026): L4+ExLlamaV2 $1.45, L40S bf16 $8.09, A100 bf16 $10.79, H100+CUDA Graphs $11.45. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The controlled 44-cell cross-GPU documentation of the Rfloor inversion, the N=10 within-session CUDA Graphs falsification test as a measurement instrument for launch overhead on physical-AI workloads, and the L4 kernel-tuning gap (SM89-specific int4 GEMM vs SM80-tuned Marlin) are the genuine contributions. The launch-bound observation and CUDA Graphs as a remedy are acknowledged folk knowledge among kernel engineers; the paper's novelty is the controlled, replicated, cross-GPU quantification applied specifically to the batch-1 physical-AI regime and the resulting deployment-ladder inversion framing. Rigor & Reproducibility The headline CUDA Graphs A/B is well-replicated (N=10, bootstrap CI, CV 0.9%), and the self-correction of the initial 1.72× figure to 1.259× at N=10 is a credibility positive. Pre-registered falsification thresholds are stated, though no external pre-registration timestamp or document is cited — a skeptical reader cannot verify the thresholds were not set post-hoc. Many non-headline cells are single-session, with wider uncertainty that is not fully characterized. The software-stack matrix lost four cells to CUDA allocator crashes (torch.compile + eager, torch.compile + Graphs, FA2 + Graphs, torch.compile + FA2); those configurations are excluded without workarounds. No Nsight Compute access; torch.profiler overhead on H100 is large (3.2×) and is reported but cannot be corrected. Modal scripts are cited (paper_b7/modal_validate/v11/) and results archived to a Modal volume, but no public GitHub URL or DOI for the artifact is provided — reproducibility depends on the author sharing the scripts. This is a preprint; peer-reviewed results and vendor self-reports warrant different confidence levels. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL / SYSTEMS (measurement study with deployment guidance). - Practical: All measurements run on commercially available cloud GPUs accessible via Modal; the recommended interventions (CUDA Graphs, ExLlamaV2 int4) are deployable today on standard NVIDIA hardware without specialized access. - Efficient: CUDA Graphs adds a one-time graph-capture cost at warmup with no steady-state overhead; ExLlamaV2 EXL2 reduces peak GPU memory from ~15 GB (bf16) to ~7 GB on L4 while cutting step time 3.59×, a compounding benefit. - Effective: Evidence directly measures end-to-end single-token decode latency at the targeted batch-1 workload; the gains are not proxy metrics. However, the L4 ExLlamaV2 result rests on N=3 sessions (SD 0.004 ms), and torch.compile+Graphs (the strongest combined lever on H100) failed with CUDA allocator errors and is uncharacterized. - Scalable: The paper explicitly does not generalize beyond 7–8B GQA head_dim=128, batch=1 (with batch=4 as a sensitivity check), four specific NVIDIA GPUs, and Modal cloud hosting. No Blackwell, no AMD, no multi-GPU, no speculative decoding, no models above 8B. The paper delivers no software artifact; it references Modal scripts under paper_b7/ but no public release is provided. Strengths - The within-session CUDA Graphs A/B with pre-registered falsification thresholds and N=10 cross-session replication exceeds the statistical rigor of most systems measurement papers; the tight 95% CI [1.253, 1.267] and CV 0.9% make the H100 launch-tax claim difficult to dismiss. - The ExLlamaV2 vs AutoAWQ+Marlin comparison cleanly attributes the quantization underperformance to SM89 kernel tuning rather than bit-width, directly answering a practically important question that was previously conflated in the literature. - The backend-pinned SDPA experiment definitively refutes a plausible prior belief (PyTorch SDPA winning via cuDNN on Hopper) — cuDNN is unsupported for the GQA single-decode shape, and the paper is honest that the winning mechanism is unexplained. - The cost-per-million-tokens framing converts a performance finding into an economically actionable deployment decision for practitioners choosing between GPU tiers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors devote Section 9 to explicit scope constraints (seven bullet points), which is unusually thorough: GQA head_dim=128 only; no MHA/MQA/MLA; bf16 only on the main matrix; batch-1 primary; NVIDIA only; Modal only; no AMD/Blackwell/Jetson/Apple; no speculative decoding. They flag that ncu was blocked on Modal and torch.profiler introduces a 3.2× inflation on H100 that understates H100 bandwidth utilization. They note that at ctx≥8192 on L4, attention compute becomes nontrivial and tfloor under-counts actual GPU work. Failed configurations (torch.compile, FA2+Graphs) are listed in Appendix C rather than buried. The Qwen L4 OOMs are diagnosed as measurement-protocol artifacts (double-allocation in the A/B rig) and correctly excluded. The authors confront limitations directly; this is a credibility positive in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The falsification thresholds (1.15×) are described as set in advance but no pre-registration record is cited; the threshold also appears conservatively generous — a 1.14× speedup would be killed while a 1.16× would not, and the actual 1.259× on H100 is so far above the threshold that the test provides weak discrimination power against alternatives (e.g., a 1.20× true effect). - The CUDA Graphs A/B is run only for H100 and L4; the A100 and L40S regime is inferred from Rfloor patterns alone. A 2-point A/B (the two extreme GPUs) confirms the endpoints of the Rfloor monotone pattern but leaves the A100 and L40S binding constraints directly untested. - The three measured models are structurally near-identical (all GQA, all head_dim=128, similar layer counts, same general architecture family); the paper cannot substantiate any claim for MoE architectures, models above 8B, or substantially different KV head counts. Vision-language-action models (e.g., OpenVLA-7B, which the paper cites as the target deployment) typically have multimodal encoders and different prefill-decode ratios not evaluated here. - The default SDPA dispatcher winning on H100 by ~20% over explicit FLASH_ATTENTION is a result with no mechanistic explanation; if this is due to kernel-selection heuristics in a specific PyTorch version (2.8+cu126), it may not generalize across PyTorch versions or future CUDA releases, making the recommendation to "use default SDPA" fragile. - No public artifact URL is provided despite the paper being a preprint; reproducibility is contingent on the author sharing Modal scripts on request, which is weaker than a stable GitHub repository with pinned dependencies. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying 7–8B GQA models at batch-1 for physical-AI inference gains immediately actionable and well-quantified guidance — ExLlamaV2 on L4, CUDA Graphs on H100 — that is not consolidated elsewhere; outside this narrow scope, the findings should be treated as directional hypotheses requiring independent verification.
Search Results 2605.30571 PDF
#45 — Innovative (69)

Unveiling the Visual Counting Bottleneck in Vision-Language Models

X Pang, Y Hou, J Wang, M Sachan · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies and causally verifies that the failure of VLMs to count visually beyond their training distribution is localized to the symbolic mapping stage — the final projection from latent magnitude to output token — not to visual perception or abstract numerosity. - Why it's important: VLMs catastrophically fail at visual counting outside their training range despite possessing the algorithmic knowledge to count, which blocks reliable use in scientific analysis, inventory, and any precision-counting application. - How it does it: A controlled synthetic laboratory (Go boards, decoupled two-phase training curriculum, 0–49 visual / 0–99 textual) with linear probing and activation steering isolates each of three cognitive stages, then validates on Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct. - The good: The activation steering intervention (masking k stone embeddings causes predicted count to drop by exactly k, near-100% accuracy for k ∈ [1,3]) provides genuine causal evidence — not just correlation — that the visual encoder features drive counting, ruling out spurious-correlation explanations. - The missing: The synthetic toy VLM has only 2 layers, dmodel=32, and 2 attention heads per module; the 95.7% disjoint-circuit finding is drawn from a 4-head total pool, making circuit-level generalization to billion-parameter architectures unwarranted without further evidence. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in VLM interpretability, multimodal grounding, or systematic generalization — the three-stage diagnostic framework and fractured-magnitude framing are reusable and the causal analysis is cleaner than typical probing work.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) drives the score: localizing the counting failure to the symbolic-mapping stage and the comparative-cardinality task that dissociates magnitude awareness from articulation are clever, surprising design choices. Novelty (75) is strong — the three-stage decomposition and fractured-magnitude framing are a new analytical formulation. Rigor (65) benefits from peer review (ICML 2026), public code, and genuine causal evidence via activation steering (masking k stones drops the count by exactly k), but is capped by reliance solely on synthetic Go-board data, no standard counting benchmarks, and no error bars. Completeness (75) is good — four limitations confronted without minimization. Resilience (65) is limited by the toy model's tiny scale (the 95.7% disjoint-circuit claim rests on only 4 heads) and no demonstrated fix. Practical Impact (50) is a diagnostic methodology with code but no artifact or remedy. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*50 = 69.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Vision-Language Models degrade catastrophically on visual counting tasks when object quantities exceed those observed during visual training, even when the model has correctly learned the recursive counting algorithm in the text domain. The failure manifests across open-source and frontier models. Prior work established the phenomenon empirically but left the mechanism unresolved: the failure could stem from the visual encoder not resolving dense object configurations (perceptual crowding), from the magnitude signal dissipating as it propagates through the language decoder (numerosity drift), or from the final token projection failing to connect latent magnitude to the correct symbol (symbolic mapping failure). Distinguishing these possibilities matters because each implies a different architectural remedy. Key Idea The core insight is that a VLM's counting chain can be cleanly decomposed into three separable stages — visual individuation, magnitude awareness, symbolic mapping — each of which can be independently probed and causally perturbed. By demonstrating that a linear probe trained only on in-distribution data (N ≤ 49) recovers the correct count (the "hidden number" NH) with near-zero error up to N = 120, the authors rule out perceptual failure. By showing the model succeeds at comparative cardinality matching (>90% accuracy, binary True/False) in the extrapolation regime where explicit enumeration collapses to 0%, they rule out magnitude-awareness drift. This leaves symbolic mapping — and the circuit divergence analysis (95.7% non-overlapping attention heads between visual and textual counting) provides mechanistic evidence for why: the model learned two disjoint counting sub-programs with no shared projection pathway. Methodology The experimental design has two components. Study 1 uses a toy VLM (2-layer ViT encoder + 2-layer causal Transformer decoder, dmodel=32, H=2 attention heads, M-RoPE positional embeddings) trained from scratch on synthetic 19×19 Go boards. Phase 1 pretrained the decoder on text sequences up to N=99; Phase 2 fine-tuned the full model on visual counting restricted to N ∈ [0,49] with a reduced decoder learning rate (1e-5 vs. 1e-3 for the encoder). The Go board uses 14×14 pixel stones matching the patch size, with white-stone distractors (±30 cardinality) to prevent global density shortcuts. Three evaluation regimes are defined: ID (0–49), VE (50–99, visual extrapolation), FE (100–120, full extrapolation). Diagnostic: a binary linear probe fprobe : R^d → {0,1} is trained on frozen encoder outputs from ID data only, then used to estimate the hidden number NH = Σ fprobe(zi). Two gap metrics — Vision Gap |NH − NG| and Language Gap |NP − NH| — decompose counting error. Causal verification performs activation steering: masking k stone-corresponding token embeddings in attention and verifying the prediction decrements by exactly k. Circuit divergence uses greedy head-pruning to find minimal critical head sets and computes their intersection across visual and textual counting tasks over N=6400 examples. Study 2 applies the same diagnostic pipeline to Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct on 6×6 Go boards (N ∈ [0,20]), calibrated to the model's effective capacity, with chain-of-thought prompting and PCA-based dimensionality reduction (32-d) before probing. Key Results On the toy VLM: textual counting achieves 100% exact-match across ID and VE (0–99); visual counting achieves 100% ID but collapses to 0% immediately at N=50 (VE and FE). Vision Gap remains zero across the entire 0–120 range; Language Gap explodes at N=50. Comparative counting achieves >90% accuracy in the VE regime despite 0% enumeration accuracy — magnitude awareness survives extrapolation. Error topology shows prediction collapse to attractor tokens (49 = visual boundary, 99/90 = textual boundary, 9 = hallucination), not Gaussian noise. Circuit divergence: in 95.7% of 6400 test cases, the critical attention head sets for visual and textual counting are non-overlapping. On Qwen3-VL-32B: visual counting collapses near-zero by N=15 (subitizing range exceeded at N>5); Vision Gap remains <0.1 averaged over the range; Language Gap diverges rapidly after N=4; layer-wise probing shows >99% probe accuracy through the first 40 of 63 language decoder layers, with degradation only in the final 20 layers before the vocabulary projection. Error topology on Qwen3-VL shows visual counting produces a diffuse high-variance cloud, while textual counting shows tight diagonal clustering with a constant offset consistent with a tokenization artifact (off-by-one). No standard counting benchmarks (e.g., CountBench, FSC-147) are used — evaluation is exclusively on the authors' synthetic Go board dataset. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The three-stage cognitive decomposition of visual counting (individuation / magnitude awareness / symbolic mapping) applied to VLM internals is a new analytical framing. The "fractured magnitude hypothesis" — disjoint modality-specific statistical manifolds preventing cross-modal grounding — is a new empirically supported claim. The specific finding that visual encoder representations remain linearly separable well into extrapolation while symbolic decoding fails catastrophically is genuinely novel; prior work established performance degradation without localizing the failure stage. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Russellpang/semproj. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026, proceedings citation confirmed). The activation steering intervention upgrades the probing evidence from correlational to causal, which is a methodological strength. However, no error bars or confidence intervals are reported on any metric — all figures show point estimates. Evaluation is exclusively on the authors' own synthetic dataset (Go boards); no standard visual counting benchmarks are included, leaving it unknown whether the diagnosis holds for real-world counting failures. The toy VLM's small scale (dmodel=32) may make its circuit-analysis results non-representative of larger architectures. The comparative counting task (magnitude awareness probe) and layer-wise probing results for Qwen3-VL are presented but the corresponding figures show limited N range (0–20), which makes the magnitude-awareness claim for foundation models less compelling than the synthetic result. No trained model weights are released. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The synthetic VLM was trained from scratch on consumer hardware; Qwen3-VL-32B requires high-end multi-GPU infrastructure and is validated at a narrow scale. - Efficient: The diagnostic framework (linear probing + activation steering) is low-cost relative to the VLM itself and is applied post-hoc on frozen representations, imposing negligible overhead. - Effective: The evidence that the symbolic mapping stage is the locus of failure is well-supported by converging evidence (Vision Gap=0, Language Gap explodes, comparative counting succeeds, attractor error topology, disjoint circuits, layer-wise probing decay); the diagnosis is credible. - Scalable: The paper validates the failure mechanism on Qwen3-VL-32B (trillion-token pretraining, RLHF alignment) and argues it is structural rather than scale-limited, but proposes no concrete architectural fix and does not test whether any intervention at symbolic mapping restores extrapolation. The work delivers a diagnostic methodology (paper form) rather than a software artifact or trained model. Strengths - The activation steering experiment cleanly establishes causality: surgically masking k stone embeddings causes the prediction to drop by exactly k for k ≤ 3 (near-100% steering accuracy), which is substantially stronger evidence than a standard linear probe. - The comparative counting experiment (cross-modal True/False matching at >90% accuracy in the VE regime where enumeration is 0%) is a clever task design that dissociates magnitude awareness from symbolic articulation without modifying model weights. - The decoupled training curriculum — language pretrained to N=99, visual only to N=49 — creates a clean experimental control that directly tests whether known linguistic labels transfer to unseen visual quantities. - The convergence of four independent analyses (Vision/Language Gap, error topology, circuit divergence, layer-wise probing) pointing consistently to symbolic mapping as the bottleneck makes the conclusion more robust than any single analysis alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the setting assumes perceptual success and does not capture real-world detection failures from occlusion or intra-class variance; (2) Go boards are simpler than natural images and may represent an upper bound on counting capability; (3) the real-world validation is restricted to a single model, Qwen3-VL-32B, so conclusions formally apply only to this architecture; (4) counting black stones may not reflect performance on high-frequency object categories where memorized density-label correlations exist. The authors confront these limitations directly and without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The circuit divergence finding (95.7% disjoint heads) is derived from a toy VLM with only 4 total attention heads (2 layers × 2 heads each). The "non-overlapping circuits" claim has almost no degrees of freedom at this scale and should not be extrapolated to the Qwen3-VL setting, where an analogous circuit analysis is absent. - The fractured magnitude hypothesis predicts that architectures with explicit shared number-space inductive priors would succeed — but no such positive control is provided. Without showing that a modified architecture breaks the pattern, the claim remains correlational at the architectural level. - The comparative counting and layer-wise probing experiments for Qwen3-VL are reported only partially in the main paper; the VE regime for foundation models is not fully characterized by the same set of diagnostics as the toy VLM. - No ablation tests whether the symbolic mapping failure is resolved or reduced by fine-tuning on even a small number of visual-textual count pairs in the extrapolation regime — which would test the authors' claim that "data scaling alone is insufficient" at smaller scale. - Evaluation uses exact-match accuracy, which penalizes off-by-one errors equally with catastrophic failures; reporting mean absolute error alongside exact match would better characterize the severity and structure of symbolic mapping errors. Bottom Line A practitioner or researcher working on VLM interpretability, multimodal grounding, or systematic generalization gains a clean diagnostic framework and a well-supported causal finding (symbolic mapping, not perception or numerosity, is the bottleneck), but should treat the circuit-level mechanistic claims as preliminary given the toy model's tiny scale and the absence of any fix demonstration.
Search Results 2605.30170 PDF
#46 — Innovative (68)

Lodestar: An Online-Learning LLM Inference Router

G Lim, W Zhao, B Godfrey, J Shan, L Xu, L Xie · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Lodestar, a cloud-native LLM inference router that trains a small MLP reward predictor continuously on live per-request cluster snapshots to assign each inference request to the GPU instance with the lowest predicted time-to-first-token (TTFT). - Why it's important: Static heuristics (prefix-cache-and-load-aware) are brittle across workload shifts and heterogeneous GPU clusters, causing latency spikes that waste expensive GPU time in interactive LLM applications where every second of TTFT degrades user experience. - How it does it: A 3-hidden-layer MLP is retrained asynchronously every 1000 samples on a two-pool data buffer (5000-sample recency FIFO plus 5000-sample gradient-coreset replay), so each model generation trains on the distribution its predecessor induced — directly addressing the circular dependency between routing policy and cluster state. - The good: On heterogeneous GPU clusters (A30+V100), Lodestar reduces mean TTFT by up to 4.38× and P99 TTFT by up to 4.42× over AIBrix's production prefix-cache-and-load-aware heuristic, learning purely from latency signals with no hardcoded GPU-specific rules. - The missing: All experiments use Llama3 8B on vLLM at clusters of 15–16 GPUs; no evaluation at larger model scale (70B+), larger cluster scale (100+ instances), or with SGLang or Dynamo as the serving engine, despite claims of generality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners running LLM inference on heterogeneous GPU clusters or building ML-based infrastructure components; the circular-dependency analysis and two-pool training data design are novel insights absent from prior LLM routing literature.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (75) drive the score — the circular-dependency characterization (offline predictor losing all inter-instance ranking signal at deployment) is a genuine and practically important empirical finding, and online-learned LLM routing is new to the space. Practical Impact (72) is lifted by a publicly released open-source system plus the first public per-request routing dataset. Rigor (62) is held back by single-run results with no error bars on cloud infrastructure where 5-10% noise makes the smaller homogeneous-cluster gains (1.02-1.07×) statistically uncertain, and by the absence of SGLang/Dynamo baselines. Composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*72 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference gateways must assign each incoming request to one of many GPU-backed serving instances. Unlike traditional web services where per-request cost is uniform and bounded, LLM requests vary by orders of magnitude in compute (input length 1K–100K tokens, reasoning vs. chat), and each instance's effective latency at a given moment depends on a non-linear joint function of: GPU memory pressure (KV cache utilization), queue depth, inflight prefill vs. decode token counts, expected KV cache hit ratio for this specific prompt, and GPU hardware generation. Existing approaches divide into two families. Model-based systems (Mooncake/LMDeploy, Preble) approximate latency analytically using queue-length heuristics or offline-profiled per-layer timing — these require re-profiling for every (model, engine, hardware) combination and cannot capture cross-request batching dynamics. Rule-based systems (SGLang cache-aware, Dynamo KV-aware, AIBrix prefix-cache-and-load) threshold or weight a handful of proxy signals — but the TTFT mapping is non-linear and jointly dependent on more factors than any fixed rule can express, as Figure 1 demonstrates: the same prefix-cache-and-load-aware policy that achieves 702ms avg TTFT at RPS=5 / 4K-token inputs degrades 5× to 3468ms at RPS=10 / 1K-token inputs with identical 80% prefix-sharing ratio. Key Idea The single core insight is that a reward predictor for LLM routing must be learned online — not just because workloads shift, but because of a fundamental circular dependency: the routing policy determines which requests go to which instances, which reshapes the KV cache state and queue distribution on each instance, which changes the latency profile, which changes the data the next predictor trains on. An offline-trained predictor deployed live loses all inter-instance ranking signal (Figure 3a), making it useless for argmax routing. Online learning addresses this by construction: each retraining round sees data drawn from the distribution the current policy induces, progressively closing the gap. Methodology Lodestar consists of a Stateful Gateway (Go, 8K LOC) and a Routing Service (Python, 4K LOC), both running as Kubernetes pods. On each request arrival, the gateway assembles a cluster snapshot: per-instance counts of running and queued requests, inflight prefill tokens and inflight decode tokens (separated because prefill is compute-bound and decode is memory-bandwidth-bound), GPU memory utilization, expected KV cache hit ratio (computed via a global radix-tree prefix index), input token length, and GPU model name as a categorical feature. GPU utilization, SM activity, and memory-bandwidth metrics are deliberately excluded — GPU utilization reports 100% whenever any kernel runs on any SM regardless of load, and sampled SM/bandwidth metrics bounce with phase composition, adding noise that slows convergence. The Routing Service runs the reward model fθ([xi ∥ r]) — a 3-hidden-layer MLP (128 units, ReLU, dropout 0.1) — in a single batched forward pass over all N instances. Weights are shared across instances with no instance-identity input, making the model instance-count-independent and preventing herding. The request routes to argmax_i ŷ_i (highest predicted reward = lowest predicted TTFT). Training triggers every θ=1000 new samples; inference and training run as isolated async tasks, and the model pointer is atomically swapped on completion. On timeout or cold-start, the gateway uses a pre-computed heuristic selection with zero added latency. Training data uses a two-pool design. A FIFO buffer of 5000 samples holds recent data; a gradient-coreset replay buffer of 5000 samples admits evicted FIFO samples only if their last-hidden-layer activations (weighted by prediction residual) are maximally diverse relative to what is already retained. Each training round uses both pools, balancing recency and coverage of previously-seen regimes. When cluster GPU memory utilization exceeds 80% and the incoming request has a meaningful shared prefix, consistent hashing maps the prefix group to k=2 candidate instances, and the MLP selects among those — concentrating KV state and reducing eviction-induced recomputation. The k=2 and 80% threshold are fixed hyperparameters acknowledged as requiring tuning. Key Results All experiments: Llama3 8B (float16), vLLM v1 with chunked prefill and prefix caching, public cloud Kubernetes. Homogeneous cluster: 8× NVIDIA A30 (24 GB). Heterogeneous cluster: 7× A30 + 8× V100 (32 GB; prefix caching disabled on V100 due to vLLM Volta limitation); second heterogeneous config: 7× L20 + 8× A30. Homogeneous cluster (Figures 6–8): Against Prefix-cache-and-load-aware, Lodestar achieves 1.02–2.15× lower avg TTFT and 1.07–1.86× lower P99 TTFT across 9 Mooncake and synthetic workload/RPS combinations. Gains are larger at higher RPS (near saturation) and lower prefix-sharing ratios. Offline-only Lodestar consistently falls between the heuristic and online Lodestar, directly quantifying the circular dependency gap. Heterogeneous cluster (Figure 9–10): Mean TTFT 1.25–4.38× lower and P99 1.32–4.42× lower. The largest gains occur on a dynamic workload (RPS 10→22 mid-experiment), where Lodestar achieves 0.23× relative TTFT vs. baselines' 0.47–0.80×. Lodestar discovers without explicit rules that V100s cannot use prefix caching and routes prefix-reusable requests to A30s, and that A30s saturate faster in the L20+A30 cluster and should receive less load despite higher prefix-hit potential. Overhead (Figure 12): Mean 3ms, P99 4.5ms, flat from 10–80 RPS. All TTFT results include this overhead. Data selection ablation (Figure 13): Two-pool design achieves 2.39×/2.50× lower avg/P99 TTFT than FIFO-only, and 1.21×/1.11× lower than full-history retention, under a 5%-to-50% prefix-sharing shift. Consistent hashing ablation (Figure 14): Removing it raises avg TTFT 1.72× and P99 1.78× on ToolAgent RPS 12. No error bars or significance tests are reported in any result; experiments are single-run on shared cloud infrastructure. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The online-learning router with circular-dependency-aware training is a novel system contribution in the LLM routing space — no prior work learns routing policies online for LLM inference. The circular dependency characterization and its empirical demonstration (offline model collapsing all inter-instance ranking signal at deployment) is a new empirical finding with direct practical implications. Underlying components — MLP regression, gradient coresets (GCR, CVPR 2022), consistent hashing, radix-tree prefix tracking — are individually standard; their combination and deployment in a production-style LLM gateway is the genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the primary design decisions: offline vs. online training (validates circular dependency claim), three data selection strategies (FIFO, full history, two-pool), consistent hashing on/off, and heuristic threshold sensitivity. The offline vs. online ablation is particularly well-designed. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported for the main TTFT comparisons — cloud infrastructure noise of 5–10% is plausible, making the 1.02–1.07× improvements in some homogeneous scenarios statistically uncertain. Code is publicly available (https://github.com/gangmuk/Lodestar), and the authors release the first public per-request LLM routing dataset pairing cluster snapshots with observed TTFT. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.00946v1, 31 May 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs on standard cloud GPUs (A30, V100, L20) in Kubernetes with one A30 dedicated to the Routing Service; no specialized hardware or model-specific offline profiling required. - Efficient: Routing adds 3ms mean / 4.5ms P99 overhead flat from 10–80 RPS; the MLP is tiny (3×128); training is async and bounded at 10K total samples; total memory footprint is modest and not reported explicitly. - Effective: Consistently reduces TTFT over all tested baselines in all configurations; the heterogeneous cluster gains are large and mechanistically explained by what the model learns. TTFT improvements are convincing; whether GPU utilization or cluster throughput improves is not evaluated. - Scalable: Tested at 15–16 instance clusters only; the paper proposes power-of-d-choices for large N but does not test it; the single Routing Service pod is an unvalidated potential bottleneck at 100+ instances. Delivers a working open-source software system deployable via AIBrix on Kubernetes, alongside a public routing dataset. Strengths - The circular dependency analysis is the paper's most important intellectual contribution: it precisely characterizes why offline ML fails for routing and provides a concrete empirical demonstration (Figure 3a) showing the offline model loses all inter-instance ranking signal at deployment — a result that should be known by anyone building learned infrastructure policies. - The two-pool training data design directly addresses the recency-diversity tension with a principled mechanism (gradient-coreset selection) that has a clear ablation showing both simpler alternatives are worse. - Heterogeneous cluster results demonstrate that the system learns GPU-capability differences (KV cache support, memory budget) purely from latency signals without any hardcoded rules — a strong validation of the approach's generality within the tested scope. - The feature exclusion analysis (§4.1, Exclusions) is unusually rigorous: the paper provides a specific mechanistic argument for why GPU utilization and SM activity would hurt rather than help the predictor, which is counter-intuitive and practically useful. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The consistent hashing parameters (80% saturation threshold, k=2 candidates) require manual tuning per cluster and workload, and the authors explicitly defer this to future work. The greedy argmax routing cannot account for long-term global effects on KV cache state beyond what consistent hashing partially addresses. The guardrail mechanism (OOD detection, fallback) is noted as a research problem in its own right. TTFT is used as the reward because TPOT is too delayed and noisy, but the authors acknowledge this may not align with all operator objectives. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation is limited to Llama3 8B with vLLM v1; the paper claims generality to any model, engine, and cluster configuration, but no experiments use models larger than 8B, multi-GPU tensor-parallel instances, or SGLang/Dynamo as the serving engine. The performance gap at 70B+ models with different prefill/decode compute ratios is unknown. - The primary LLM-specific baseline (Prefix-cache-and-load-aware) is AIBrix's heuristic. SGLang's cache-aware routing and Dynamo's KV-aware routing are cited but not experimentally compared; this omits two of the most widely deployed alternatives. - No throughput or GPU utilization metrics are reported alongside TTFT. A router that concentrates traffic on faster instances to minimize TTFT could reduce cluster-wide throughput or increase token-generation latency (TPOT) — this tradeoff is unexamined. - Single-run results without statistical tests make the 1.02–1.07× improvements in some homogeneous scenarios indistinguishable from cloud noise. - The replay buffer admits samples by gradient-coreset diversity regardless of age; stale-but-diverse samples from a prior cluster state could be admitted indefinitely, potentially hurting accuracy after large configuration changes. The paper does not analyze staleness effects. - The Routing Service single-pod design is discussed conceptually for failure modes but not stress-tested; HA or multi-replica Routing Service deployment is not addressed. Bottom Line Lodestar makes a genuine and practically motivated contribution for LLM infrastructure operators — especially on heterogeneous clusters where the gains are large and the mechanism is sound — but the single-model, small-cluster, single-run evaluation leaves meaningful uncertainty about whether these results transfer to production-scale deployments.
Search Results 2606.00946 PDF
#47 — Innovative (68)

Flashagents: Accelerating multi-agent llm systems via streaming prefill overlap

T Fang, Z Zheng, Z Ma, Y Lu, H Lin, X Han, L Sun · openreview.net · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FlashAgents, a serving system that overlaps downstream agent prefill with upstream agent decode via token-level streaming and an intra-turn radix-tree prefix cache, reducing inter-agent transition latency in Multi-Agent Systems. - Why it's important: Sequential agent pipelines leave GPUs idle during handoffs — each downstream agent waits for complete upstream generation before it can begin prefill — and this idle time accumulates multiplicatively across long agent chains in production systems like DeepResearch and coding assistants. - How it does it: As an upstream agent (Agent A) decodes tokens, it streams them to a buffer; the downstream agent (Agent B) incrementally processes each arriving chunk via causal attention updates to its KV cache, hiding prefill latency behind Agent A's decode; an auxiliary intra-turn radix tree deduplicates shared prefixes across concurrent downstream requests within the same scheduling trigger. - The good: FlashAgents achieves 40.3% end-to-end latency reduction on the MapReduce workflow at N=1 and 3.52× speedup in controlled microbenchmarks at concurrency=2, with consistent improvements across all 240 tested configurations spanning three model sizes (7B, 32B, 235B). - The missing: All experiments are co-located on the same server or a two-GPU node; the paper does not evaluate distributed deployments where network latency between agents would change the prefill-decode overlap calculus, nor does it test against non-SGLang baselines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and engineers building or optimizing multi-agent LLM infrastructure — the mechanism is clearly specified and the implementation is practical; less useful for those focused on agent reasoning or LLM training.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (68) benefits from MLSys 2026 peer review, a clean IASIP-vs-cache ablation (Table 1), and honest overhead characterization, but is capped by absent error bars and no public code link. Novelty (75) is solid — token-level streaming prefill overlap across agent boundaries plus an intra-turn radix tree is a real new mechanism, not a recombination. Resilience (62) is the main drag: the headline 40.3% MapReduce result conflates parallelizing reviewers with streaming overlap, and all experiments are co-located, leaving distributed deployment (where network latency narrows the overlap window) untested. Composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*68 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In Multi-Agent Systems, sequential agent chains create a structural bottleneck: before a downstream agent can generate output, it must first run a full prefill over the upstream agent's complete output. Traditional LLM serving systems (vLLM, SGLang) treat each agent invocation as an independent request and do not begin downstream prefill until upstream generation finishes, leaving the downstream GPU idle for the entire upstream decode duration. This idle time compounds across agent chains and is exacerbated in heterogeneous deployments (e.g., a fast 7B upstream feeding a slow 32B downstream) where upstream decode finishes quickly but downstream prefill is heavy. Existing optimizations — chunked prefill, prefix caching, prefill-decode disaggregation — all operate within a single request boundary and do not address this inter-agent coordination gap. Key Idea: The central insight is that causal attention allows incremental KV cache construction: because each new token in a sequence only attends to prior tokens, a downstream agent can process upstream tokens one chunk at a time as they arrive, building its KV cache progressively rather than waiting for the full sequence. This transforms a sequential dependency (finish A, then start B's prefill) into a pipeline (B's prefill runs concurrently with A's decode). A secondary insight is that multiple concurrent downstream agents within the same scheduling event often share instruction template prefixes, which a temporary intra-turn radix tree can deduplicate within a single prefill trigger, eliminating redundant computation that SGLang's persistent inter-turn cache cannot address. Methodology: FlashAgents comprises two orthogonal mechanisms. The first, Inter-Agent Streaming and Incremental Prefill (IASIP), operates as follows: Agent A's LLM engine streams generated tokens to a dedicated per-request buffer for Agent B; when the buffer exceeds a configurable chunk threshold or Agent B's engine is idle, Agent B executes an incremental prefill over the buffered tokens using the attention operation Attention(Qi, K:i, V:i) = softmax(QiK^T:i / sqrt(d)) * V:i, where K:i grows with accumulated history but the query Qi covers only the current chunk; after Agent A finishes, any remaining buffered tokens are prefilled and Agent B immediately starts decode. The overlap time is bounded by min(T_decode_A, T_prefill_B). The second mechanism, the intra-turn prefix cache, inserts all concurrently buffered token sequences into a temporary radix tree at each prefill trigger; newly created nodes (unique prefix extensions) are materialized once per node in depth-first order, and each request assembles its KV cache by collecting deltas along its path. This cache is distinct from SGLang's persistent RadixAttention tree and operates only within a turn. Both mechanisms are implemented as a modular extension layer on SGLang without modifying its core RadixAttention tree, and integrated with AutoGen via an asynchronous client. A key design decision is the chunk threshold hyperparameter: small chunks (128 tokens) incur up to 3× compute overhead versus single-pass prefill due to memory-bound execution and repeated weight loading, while chunks of 512+ tokens approach 1.2× overhead; the paper validates this empirically but leaves adaptive chunk sizing to future work. Key Results: Microbenchmarks across 240 configurations (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-235B high/low throughput; concurrency 1/2/4/8; varying upstream TPS, shared prefix length 1000–2000, upstream token count 100–800) show FlashAgents outperforms sequential baseline in every configuration, with speedups of 1.05× to 3.52×. Peak 3.52× is at concurrency=2 with the 7B model, driven primarily by GPU utilization improvement and prefix cache reuse rather than single-pipeline overlap. At concurrency=1 with 7B, speedups are modest (1.05–1.2×) because the downstream prefill completes before much overlap is possible. The 235B model shows higher single-request speedup due to slower prefill providing more overlap opportunity, but benefit plateaus at higher concurrency as aggregate downstream prefill exceeds upstream decode capacity. End-to-end real workflows on AutoGen: Chain (4-agent sequential pipeline, 7B→32B heterogeneous) achieves 1.8% reduction at N=1 and 11.9% at N=8; MapReduce (3 parallel reviewers + 1 aggregator, 7B→32B) achieves 40.3% at N=1 and 14.7% at N=8. PPTAgent (12-way fan-out, VLM 7B→14B) achieves 24.1% reduction (3.41s vs. 4.49s). Ablation (Table 1, Qwen3-32B) isolates contributions: at concurrency=8 with prefix=2000, IASIP-only reduces baseline from 10.10s to 5.32s, while full FlashAgents further reduces to 4.20s (2.40× total speedup), confirming the intra-turn cache contributes 21% additional reduction beyond IASIP alone. Orthogonality with SJF scheduling is demonstrated: combined FlashAgents+SJF achieves 56% latency reduction at concurrency=1 and 37% at concurrency=10. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD. The specific contribution — streaming token-level prefill overlap across sequentially dependent agent boundaries with an intra-turn prefix cache — is genuinely novel relative to prior work. IASIP applies the well-understood idea of pipeline parallelism (overlap producer and consumer) to LLM inference at the inter-agent token level, which has not been done before; the intra-turn prefix cache addresses a specific gap in existing prefix caching systems (SGLang/vLLM only update their persistent trees after a complete prefill, missing within-turn sharing). This is not a straightforward combination of known techniques applied to a new setting — the incremental causal KV construction across agent boundaries requires a new scheduling mechanism and correctness argument. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and meaningful: Table 1 isolates IASIP from the intra-turn cache across concurrency and prefix length conditions. The overhead analysis (Figure 8) directly characterizes the computational cost introduced, with 5 repeated trials and median reported. Error bars or confidence intervals are absent throughout — all speedup figures are point estimates. The paper is published at MLSys 2026 (peer-reviewed). Code and model weights are not mentioned as publicly released; no repository URL is provided. The workload parameterization (TPS/Prefix/Upstream labels) is well-defined and the experimental setup (NVIDIA A100-80GB, CUDA 12.6, specific model versions) is reproducible in principle. One concern: the performance metric (downstream response latency = t_B_first - t_A_first) measures time-to-first-token for Agent B starting from Agent A's first token, which captures inter-agent transition well but does not directly measure full end-to-end workflow completion latency for all but the PPTAgent experiment; the real-workflow normalized-time results in Figure 7 address this but lack absolute numbers. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — FlashAgents is a built serving infrastructure extension, evaluated by deployment on real workflows. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs using standard CUDA 12.6; requires SGLang as the underlying serving engine and AutoGen for the multi-agent framework integration, both commercially available. - Efficient: Incremental prefill incurs up to 3× compute overhead at chunk size 128 relative to single-pass prefill, but this drops to ~1.2× at chunk size 512; end-to-end latency still improves because the overhead is hidden behind upstream decode, but GPU compute utilization increases, which is a real cost at scale. - Effective: Evidence is strong for the specific bottleneck targeted (inter-agent transition latency) across diverse model sizes and workflow patterns; the 40.3% MapReduce result is the most impressive but reflects a case where the baseline runs reviewers sequentially — FlashAgents also parallelizes them, which is a conflated benefit not purely from streaming overlap. - Scalable: Tested only on single-node deployments (1–8 A100 GPUs via tensor parallelism for 235B); distributed multi-node deployments with network latency between agents are not evaluated and could significantly alter the overlap opportunity, since network transfer latency would compete with the prefill-decode overlap window. The work delivers a software artifact (extension to SGLang + AutoGen integration), though the repository is not publicly linked in the paper. Strengths: - The mechanistic decomposition is clean and the latency model (Equations 2–5) gives practitioners a concrete tool to predict when FlashAgents will and will not help, with empirical validation confirming the model's predictions about the concurrency-driven transition from overlap-dominated to prefix-cache-dominated speedup. - The ablation in Table 1 cleanly separates the two complementary regimes: IASIP removes the handoff barrier (dominant at low concurrency), the intra-turn cache eliminates redundant computation (dominant at high concurrency), and together they provide consistent coverage across the operating range. - Correctly identifies and handles the position-stability constraint in fan-in topologies (Section 4.2): because RoPE positional encoding is applied before attention, only segments with finalized absolute positions can be incrementally prefilled, preventing silent correctness bugs in merge patterns. - Overhead analysis (Figure 8) honestly quantifies the computational cost introduced, showing it is manageable (4.6–7.9% additional KV access overhead for the worst case per-chunk), which is a credibility-positive level of transparency. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that managing synchronization and dependencies grows in complexity for topologies beyond two agents (N > 2), particularly fan-in patterns where positional stability constraints limit overlap. They note future work on adaptive streaming policies and advanced scheduling strategies. The conclusion flags the need for deeper algorithm-system co-design to enable non-generative operations during overlapped prefill. The authors do not explicitly acknowledge the absence of error bars, the co-location assumption, or the conflation of parallelization and streaming overlap benefits in the MapReduce result. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The MapReduce 40.3% result conflates two distinct optimizations: FlashAgents runs the three Reviewer agents in parallel (fan-out), whereas the AutoGen baseline runs them sequentially by default. A cleaner baseline would parallelize the reviewers without streaming overlap, isolating the contribution of incremental prefill from the simpler benefit of enabling concurrency. - All experiments are on co-located agents (single node or two GPUs connected locally); real production MAS deployments often route agents across different servers or cloud instances, where network round-trip latency (10s–100s ms) would narrow or eliminate the overlap window available for incremental prefill. - No throughput evaluation: the paper focuses entirely on latency; whether FlashAgents's incremental prefill operations (which increase per-token compute) reduce overall serving throughput under high load is not measured. - Chunk threshold is a fixed hyperparameter; the paper notes the overhead-latency trade-off but does not provide an adaptive policy or sensitivity analysis across workflow types, leaving practitioners to tune this themselves. - Only Qwen-family models are tested; whether the results generalize to models with different decode speeds (e.g., models using speculative decoding or MTP, which the paper notes in Figure 1 but does not integrate) is not demonstrated. Bottom Line: FlashAgents addresses a real and previously unaddressed bottleneck in multi-agent LLM serving with a clean mechanism, honest overhead characterization, and consistent empirical results across 240+ configurations — practitioners building production multi-agent pipelines on SGLang should adopt it, with the caveat that distributed (multi-node) deployments require further validation before the claimed gains translate.
Recommended
#48 — Innovative (68)

UniScale: Adaptive Unified Inference Scaling via Online Joint Optimization of Model Routing and Test-Time Scaling

K Huang, X Wang, M Kong, Z Shi, Y Hou, H Xu, Z Dai… · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UNISCALE, an online contextual bandit framework that jointly optimizes model routing and test-time scaling (TTS) configurations in a single unified decision space to navigate LLM inference quality-cost trade-offs. - Why it's important: In production LLM serving, routing and TTS are optimized independently, leaving a richer Pareto frontier unexploited: routing's discrete model-scale jumps can be smoothed by TTS, and TTS's per-model capacity ceilings can be escaped by routing to a larger model. - How it does it: UNISCALE formulates the joint selection of (model M, question parallelism QP, candidate parallelism CP, beam size BS) as a contextual multi-armed bandit solved with LinUCB over Transformer-generated semantic embeddings of both queries and configurations. - The good: Path-aware early exiting reduces TTS inference cost from 4851.4 to 1002.9 eFLOPs (−79.33%) with only −0.91pp accuracy loss, confirmed by clean ablation. - The missing: The entire evaluation rests on 210 instances from math/competition benchmarks and one coding add-on; open-ended or generation tasks where process reward models (PRMs) cannot be applied are completely absent, leaving the claimed task-agnostic generalizability largely unverified. - Read it or not: ML engineers building multi-model LLM serving infrastructure who need fine-grained quality-cost control will find this directly actionable; those outside inference optimization for reasoning tasks can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is the lead strength — the Unified Inference Scaling formulation that fuses model routing and test-time scaling into one bandit action space is a genuinely new problem framing, not just a method swap, and the dense-PRM-as-reward signal is non-obvious (72). Rigor (70) is solid for the cluster: three component ablations, 5 seeds with mean±std, α and verifier-scale sensitivity sweeps, drift-robustness tests, and ICML 2026 peer review — but it is pulled down by a tiny 210-instance corpus, no released code, and an unacknowledged circular dependency where the PRM both selects TTS paths and feeds the bandit reward. Completeness (65) and Resilience (62) reflect that the broad task-agnostic claims rest almost entirely on math benchmarks with one small coding add-on, leaving generalization unverified. Impact (55) is real as an integrated OpenR+vLLM framework but undercut by no public checkpoint and heavy multi-model GPU requirements. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*55 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Production LLM deployments span a wide spectrum of query difficulty, and existing strategies for managing the quality-cost trade-off operate along two independent dimensions. Model routing selects among models of different scales, covering a broad range but only at the coarse granularity of discrete model-size steps — switching models causes discontinuous jumps in both accuracy and compute. Test-time scaling dynamically adjusts inference-time compute within a fixed model (via sampling strategies, beam search, DVTS), offering fine-grained control but hitting hard capacity ceilings bounded by the base model's reasoning capability. Because these two mechanisms are designed and deployed in isolation, they cannot compensate for each other's structural weaknesses. A third compounding problem is environmental drift: real deployments face shifting query distributions, user objectives, and model availability, which static routing schemes cannot handle. The paper targets developers and service operators who run high-throughput LLM inference and must adaptively balance response quality and compute cost without offline retraining. Key Idea The core insight is that model routing and TTS inhabit complementary failure modes that can be resolved by a single unified action space: TTS can bridge the discrete quality gap between adjacent model sizes, while routing to a larger model can escape the diminishing-return plateau of aggressive TTS on a smaller model. Encoding each joint configuration (M, QP, CP, BS) as a structured natural-language description — embedding model capability profiles (benchmark scores on GPQA, AIME, LiveBench, ZebraLogic, LiveCodeBench) and TTS semantics (derived strategy mode, amplification factor) — then projecting both query and configuration into a shared 1024-dim embedding space via a pretrained Transformer, enables a linear bandit (LinUCB) to transfer reward knowledge across semantically similar but never-tried configurations, making online exploration of a high-dimensional action space tractable. Methodology UNISCALE operates as a four-phase closed-loop online system. (1) Context extraction: A pretrained text embedding model (qwen-text-embedding-v4) encodes the incoming query into 1024 dims at runtime; action embeddings are pre-computed offline from structured descriptions and concatenated with the query embedding to form a 2048-dim joint feature vector x_{t,a}. (2) LinUCB action selection: a_t = argmax_a [θ̂_t^T x_{t,a} + α√(x_{t,a}^T A_t^{-1} x_{t,a})], where A_t is updated via Sherman-Morrison rank-1 formula (O(d²) per round vs O(d³) for full inversion). The reward model is linear: r̂ = θ^T x_{t,a}; this is justified on the grounds that the Transformer encoder handles non-linear feature extraction, leaving a linear mapping sufficient. (3) TTS execution with path-aware early exiting: a search forest of QP subtrees is expanded iteratively, with CP parallel samples per step and BS top paths retained. During execution, any incomplete path p_{i,j} is pruned if its theoretical maximum potential score — mixing its current mean path score with the verifier's maximum possible future score — cannot beat the current best completed answer (Vmax). Maximum search depth is adaptively set as H_max = ⌈η·D_best⌉ with η=1.2. (4) Composite reward computation: r_t = w1·Correct(a_t) + w2·Score(a_t) + w3·(1 − C̃_UIS(a_t)), where Score(a_t) is the continuous PRM verifier score providing dense feedback, and C̃_UIS is log-transformed and min-max normalized eFLOPs cost. Four reward mode presets are defined by weight vectors ranging from Cost-Sensitive (w1=w2=0.1, w3=0.8) to Quality-Priority (w1=w2=0.4, w3=0.2). Design decision assessment: the semantic embedding choice over one-hot encoding is well-justified and ablated; the eFLOPs cost model unifying compute- and memory-bound operations via hardware arithmetic intensity (I=156 for A800) is carefully derived with formal propositions; the linear reward assumption is argued but not formally validated across the extreme model scale range (0.6B to 32B). Key Results All experiments use 210 total instances: AIME'24, AIME'25, and 30 instances per difficulty level from MATH-500 (Levels 1–5), plus a coding experiment on LiveCodeBench (210 instances, 91 easy + 119 medium). The protocol is 50-step random warm-up followed by 160 policy-driven iterations, averaged over 5 seeds (3, 23, 42, 50, 57). The model pool is Qwen3 (0.6B–32B) with Skywork-o1-Open-PRM-Qwen-2.5-1.5B as verifier. In the full UIS space under Cost-Sensitive mode, UNISCALE achieves reward 0.7079 (±0.0110), accuracy 46.88pp (±1.72), cost 49.4 eFLOPs (±11.6), compared to the strongest baseline k-NN at 0.6590, 41.38pp, 326.0 eFLOPs — a +5.5pp accuracy gain at 85% lower cost. Under Quality-Priority mode, UNISCALE achieves 57.37pp accuracy, besting Thompson Sampling at 54.00pp and k-NN at 46.75pp. The Oracle bound (omniscient configuration selection) reaches 68.12pp in Quality-Priority UIS, leaving a 10.75pp gap above UNISCALE. The BEST-Route restricted comparison confirms the UIS action space's higher ceiling: full UIS at Quality-Priority achieves +8.12pp accuracy at only 3.6% of BEST-Route's cost. Path-aware early exiting ablation (η=1.2): accuracy drops from 65.43 to 64.52pp (−0.91pp) while eFLOPs fall from 4851.4 to 1002.9 (−79.33%) and TFLOPs from 667.4 to 85.0 (−87.26%). Dense feedback ablation: +5.62pp accuracy and −51.3% cost in Quality-Priority mode over binary-only reward. The coding task shows UNISCALE reward 0.8125 vs k-NN 0.7943 at −59.97% cost and −0.63pp accuracy. Baselines include Random, Greedy, Thompson Sampling, NeuralUCB, MLP router, k-NN router, BEST-Route, and Oracle — a strong and contemporary set. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM (Unified Inference Scaling as a joint optimization space), NEW METHOD (semantic LinUCB bandit for online joint routing+TTS), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (UNISCALE as an inference serving framework). The UIS problem formulation combining routing and TTS in a single bandit action space is genuinely novel; the individual components — LinUCB, Transformer embeddings, PRM-guided search, early exiting — are existing techniques competently assembled into a well-motivated new architecture. The dense verification feedback mechanism, reinterpreting the PRM score as a bandit reward signal, is a non-obvious and validated contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for three key components (semantic representations, path-aware early exiting, dense verification feedback), each with quantitative comparisons. Results are reported as mean ± standard deviation across 5 seeds. Sensitivity analysis of exploration factor α (0, 1, 10) and verifier scale (1.5B vs 7B PRM) are provided. Robustness to four non-stationary drift scenarios is tested. The verifier-score/correctness alignment is empirically validated on 105,840 samples with point-biserial correlation 0.5061 and AUROC 0.8067 — these are disclosed honestly and do not overstate the PRM's reliability. The paper is published at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). No code repository URL is mentioned or released. A concern: the composite reward used for bandit learning includes the PRM score, which is also used to select and execute TTS — creating a circular dependency where the policy is optimized toward configurations that maximize PRM agreement rather than ground-truth correctness. With PRM AUROC only 0.8067, a nontrivial fraction of policy optimization is toward PRM noise. This is not acknowledged as a limitation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper implements and benchmarks a concrete serving framework. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA A800 80GB SXM GPU cluster; simultaneously hosting Qwen3 models from 0.6B to 32B plus a verifier demands substantial multi-GPU infrastructure, and the paper does not report total GPU count, making minimum hardware requirements opaque. - Efficient: UNISCALE's orchestration overhead (query embedding + bandit update) is 0.91% of total wall time; path-aware early exiting reduces TTS cost by 79.33%, making the per-query overhead competitive with baseline TTS; however, multi-model memory footprint is unaddressed. - Effective: UNISCALE consistently Pareto-dominates all baselines across all three scenarios (TTS-only, Routing-only, UIS) on math and coding benchmarks with verifier-measured correctness, and remains below the Oracle by 10–15pp in Quality-Priority mode. - Scalable: Tested only at 210 instances with models up to 32B; performance under thousands of queries with distributional shift, latency under concurrent serving load, and behavior with 70B+ models are not evaluated — scalability is claimed but not measured. The framework exists as an integrated system on OpenR + vLLM, but no publicly released code or model checkpoint is mentioned. Strengths - The UIS Pareto frontier analysis (Figure 1, Oracle comparisons) is a clean, convincing empirical argument that the joint optimization space has strictly higher ceiling quality-efficiency than either routing or TTS alone; the Oracle gap tables quantify this precisely. - Path-aware early exiting achieves a 79% cost reduction with sub-1pp accuracy loss — a practical result with a tight, well-motivated pruning criterion and dedicated ablation. - Semantic action embeddings over structured textual descriptions is a genuinely practical solution to cold-start exploration in a high-dimensional heterogeneous configuration space, enabling cross-action knowledge transfer validated by regret curve comparisons. - The robustness experiments under four non-stationary drift conditions (model addition, model removal, bidirectional reward shifts) are a concrete engineering contribution absent from prior routing work, and the results are convincing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that performance is "currently modulated by the precision of Process Reward Models (PRMs)" and commit to exploring "more generalizable verification mechanisms" for broader task coverage. They also acknowledge that extensive evaluation on "broader open-ended task environments" is left to future work. These are confronted directly rather than minimized — a credibility positive — though neither limitation receives a quantitative bound. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 210-instance evaluation is very small: 50 warm-up steps consume 24% of all instances for random exploration, leaving only 160 steps to measure policy performance. Whether observed regret advantages persist over thousands of queries is unknown. - The linear reward assumption r̂ = θ^T x, while computationally attractive, may fail at extreme model scale contrasts (0.6B vs 32B have qualitatively different reasoning failure modes); no analysis shows the linear model fits well across the full action space, and NeuralUCB (which uses non-linear approximation) does not outperform LinUCB, which may reflect NeuralUCB's poor sample efficiency rather than linear sufficiency. - The reward signal used for bandit learning incorporates the PRM verifier score (Score(a_t)), the same PRM used to select TTS paths — creating a feedback loop where the policy may overfit to configurations that exploit PRM biases rather than improve true task accuracy. This is not acknowledged. - All primary benchmarks are mathematical reasoning tasks (AIME, MATH-500); the single coding experiment uses only 2 of 6 candidate models and is limited to 210 instances. Tasks without natural process reward models (summarization, dialogue, instruction following) are entirely absent despite the paper's broad claims. - Total GPU memory requirements for simultaneously hosting 6 Qwen3 models (0.6B–32B parameters, up to ~65GB for 32B in BF16) plus PRM verifiers are not reported; the claimed edge-cloud and democratization applications are speculative given these infrastructure demands. Bottom Line UNISCALE is a well-executed, peer-reviewed system paper with a clean central insight and strong ablations; practitioners building multi-model inference serving pipelines for reasoning tasks will find the UIS framework, semantic bandit approach, and early-exiting mechanism directly applicable, but should note that the evaluation rests on a small math benchmark corpus and that deployment requires hosting the full model pool simultaneously.
Search Results 2605.30898 PDF
#49 — Innovative (68)

Found in Conversation: LLMs Teach Themselves to Close the Multi-Turn Gap

T Chen, S Wu, J Leskovec · arxiv.org · Jure Leskovec - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FOUND IN CONVERSATION (FIC), a two-stage post-training framework that combines supervised fine-tuning and View-Asymmetric Self-Distillation (VASD) to recover an LLM's single-turn task performance when the same information is delivered across underspecified multi-turn conversations. - Why it's important: Deployed LLMs lose an average 39% task accuracy when users provide information gradually across turns rather than all at once—a failure mode affecting the dominant real-world usage pattern—and even frontier models exhibit this gap, making stronger-teacher distillation impossible. - How it does it: VASD runs the same frozen backbone simultaneously as a teacher (conditioned on all information in single-turn form) and a student (conditioned on the multi-turn sharded conversation with on-policy intermediate responses), then aligns the student's next-token distributions to the teacher's via Jensen-Shannon divergence on the final answer span only. - The good: Across five models from four families (Llama, Qwen, Phi, OLMo; 3B–14B), FIC recovers ≥92% of single-turn performance and achieves 100% recovery on two Llama backbones, while also reducing per-conversation token usage by up to 33%. - The missing: The evaluation rests on ~103 test instances per task with no confidence intervals or significance tests; the claim that the gap is fully closed is limited to this small benchmark, and no experiments exceed 14B parameters. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on LLM post-training, multi-turn dialogue, or knowledge distillation—the VASD framing is conceptually clean, the ablations are honest, and the result that prompting-based fixes uniformly fail is practically important.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (76) carry the score: VASD's input-view (presentation) asymmetry is a clean, genuinely novel departure from prior self-distillation that relies on information asymmetry, and the behavioral-not-capability framing of the multi-turn gap is a non-obvious insight that directly enables a no-stronger-teacher solution. Replication across five backbones from four families substitutes partially for missing error bars. Rigor (65) and Resilience (65) are limited by the ~103-instance test set, absent confidence intervals, no released code/checkpoints, a GPT-4o/4o-mini corpus dependency, and a cooperative non-pivoting simulator — making the '100% recovery' headline fragile. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*76 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*68 = 68.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 76 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real users interact with LLMs by starting with an underspecified request and supplying details incrementally across turns—a pattern rooted in Zipfian communication economy and confirmed as the dominant mode of real-world LLM use. Prior work by Laban et al. (2025) documented that across 15 open- and closed-weight models this "underspecified multi-turn" delivery incurs an average 39% drop in task accuracy compared to presenting the same information in a single prompt. Critically, even frontier models (GPT-4o, Llama-3 large) exhibit the gap, ruling out stronger-teacher distillation as a remedy. Inference-time fixes (prompting, self-check, judge gating) are prompt-brittle and impose latency. Standard SFT overfits to surface patterns. Multi-turn RL suffers from sparse credit assignment over long trajectories and heavy rollout costs. The problem is therefore how to close this gap parametrically, without any external stronger model. Key Idea The multi-turn performance gap is behavioral, not capability-bound: the same model that fails in the sharded multi-turn setting succeeds when given the identical information concatenated into a single prompt. VASD exploits this observation by treating the model's own strong single-turn behavior as its teacher. Teacher and student share the same backbone and the same task information; they differ only in how that information is presented (single-turn CONCAT vs. multi-turn SHARDED). This "input-view asymmetry" provides a clean, capability-invariant learning signal: the entire teacher-student gap is exactly the multi-turn-vs-single-turn gap being targeted, with no other distributional difference to confound the loss. Methodology FIC proceeds in two stages. Stage 1 (SFT warm-start): A multi-turn training corpus is constructed from GSM8K by applying the "lazification" sharding pipeline of Laban et al. to a pool disjoint from the test set. Each training trace is formatted as (system, u1, a1, ..., uN, aN). Intermediate-turn gold responses at (t < N) are synthesized by GPT-4o-mini with two design choices: (i) stylistic diversity across format, phrasing, and length to prevent surface-pattern memorization, and (ii) position-aware specificity—generic deferrals early, specific clarification requests later—admitted only when a judge verifies the requested information is missing and necessary. Final-turn gold responses aN are drawn from the model's own correct CONCAT rollouts, keeping the SFT signal close to the model's prior. The SFT loss is standard causal-LM; a LoRA adapter is trained on a frozen backbone. Stage 2 (VASD): The LoRA adapter is further trained using Algorithm 1. At each step, intermediate responses m1...mN-1 are sampled on-policy from the current student (gradients off), then the student is run on the full SHARDED context up to the final turn (gradients on) and the teacher is run on the CONCAT context (gradients off). The VASD loss is the mean Jensen-Shannon divergence between student and teacher next-token distributions over the final answer span Y only. Intermediate turns receive no gradient signal; their presence in the student context enables the student to condition its final answer on its own training-time trajectory. Three design decisions are well-justified via ablation: (1) SFT before VASD is necessary for grounded deferral behavior; (2) on-policy intermediate sampling outperforms off-policy fixed traces; (3) VASD outperforms vanilla stronger-to-weaker distillation (which actually degrades performance). Key Results On the multi-turn Math task (103-instance test set, 10 simulations per instance, GPT-4o-mini user simulator): baselines drop 31–39% from CONCAT to SHARDED. FIC achieves recovery rates of 100% (Llama-3.2-3B: 60.4% vs. 60.2% CONCAT ceiling), 100% (Llama-3.1-8B: 71.7% vs. 71.5%), 93% (Qwen2.5-7B: 78.7% vs. 85.0%), 94% (OLMo-2-13B: 77.1% vs. 82.2%), 92% (Phi-4-14B: 82.4% vs. 89.4%). Single-turn FULL accuracy is preserved within 5.4% for four of five models. Token usage per conversation is reduced 0–33% (LLAMA-3.1-8B: 1175→979; QWEN2.5-7B: 1693→1143; OLMO-2-13B: 1905→1292; PHI-4-14B: 1683→1247). Ablation on Llama-3.1-8B: baseline 44.7%, SFT-only 55.6%, +off-policy VASD 60.0%, +on-policy VASD (FIC) 71.7%. Vanilla distillation (Llama-8B→3B) degrades accuracy from 39.2 to 30.7 vs. FIC's 60.4. Four inference-time interventions (PROMPT-RULE, PROMPT-SELFCHECK, GATED-EXTERNAL/4o-mini, GATED-SELF) uniformly fail, with several reducing accuracy below the no-intervention baseline.
