The matched set replaces top-k retrieval. Stage 3: the executor receives only the actions of matched rules; conditions and source spans are withheld to enforce matcher-executor role separation. DeepSeek-V4 serves as both matcher and executor in main experiments; Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 is a secondary executor. Key justified design choices: pairwise (not batched) judgment addresses attention dilution in long contexts; action withholding prevents a known contamination bias; domain-specific matcher schemas for the NBA domain handle exception-type boundary conditions that a generic prompt misses. Key Results: On Wikipedia NPOV rewriting (n=107, high semantic-applicability mismatch): TAG reaches 68.2% Violation Fix Rate with DeepSeek-V4, vs. 60.8% for the best Standard RAG baseline (top-20, BGE) and 63.5% for the All-Rules baseline (full rule set in context); using 21.0 average rules vs. 131.0 for All-Rules (6.2× context reduction). A relevance-reranker baseline — identical setup but asking generic relevance rather than applicability — yields 63.6% VFR, essentially matching All-Rules and falling 4.6 points below TAG, isolating the framing contribution from LLM-judge use. Human evaluation on 40 cases (two annotators, κ=0.68) ranks TAG (92.4% VFR) > All Rules (85.0%) > Std RAG (65.0%); TAG vs. Std RAG McNemar p=0.003. On HumanEval × PEP 8 (n=164, low mismatch): TAG achieves Pylint 9.36 vs. 9.21 best Standard RAG (+0.15 points), a marginal gain consistent with the claim that similarity already works when code structure and style rules share vocabulary. Pass@1 functional correctness is 98.1% (TAG) vs. 98.8% (no retrieval), essentially unchanged. On RuleArena-NBA (n=156, high conditionality, multi-step): TAG reaches 44.2% strict accuracy (DeepSeek-V4), vs. 43.6% best Standard RAG overall — but 65.6% vs. 55.7% on L0 single-transaction cases (+9.9 points). L1 and L2 multi-step cases show no consistent improvement over baselines (36.2% and 15.4% for TAG respectively, with L2 matching No Retrieval at 15.4%). The 2×2 factorial ablation on NPOV (Qwen3-30B) shows super-additivity: the linear prediction from single-axis improvements (53.3 + 6.5 + 0.9 = 60.7%) underestimates the observed 64.5% by 3.8 points. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The reformulation of retrieval as condition entailment over structured rules rather than embedding similarity is the primary contribution; prior rule-guided RAG systems (RuleRAG, RuAG) either provide rules externally or mine them from training data, and neither changes the retrieval predicate from similarity to applicability. The formalization of retrieval objective mismatch and the empirical characterization of when the divergence between similarity and applicability is large (NPOV) vs. small (PEP 8) is a genuinely useful empirical finding. The five-phase extraction pipeline combines known techniques (LLM-based annotation, deduplication, programmatic verification) but the specific pipeline design targeting judgeable conditions and executable actions is not previously published in this form. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and informative: leave-one-out phase ablation across two domains reveals operationalization (Phase 3) as the dominant contributor by a clear margin, with a mechanistic explanation (vague conditions inflate #picks while reducing action quality). The 2×2 factorial ablation is the clearest piece of experimental design in the paper. Significant concerns: (1) no confidence intervals or error bars on any metric in the main tables; with n=107–164, differences of 1–3 points are within plausible noise; (2) NPOV VFR and auxiliary scores are produced by an LLM judge using the same model family (DeepSeek) as the executor, creating potential circularity not acknowledged; (3) the 40-case human evaluation is sequential (Q001–Q040), not randomly sampled, limiting its validity as a representativeness check; (4) no code, dataset, or model weights are released. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.27951v1, 27 May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Stage 1 extraction runs offline once per source document and is feasible on any LLM API; Stage 2 requires up to M sequential LLM calls per input (M=119–195 in the tested domains), which is deployable but substantially more expensive than a single dense retrieval pass. - Efficient: Context passed to the executor is reduced by up to 93% compared to the All-Rules baseline, but the pairwise matching cost — O(M) LLM calls per input — is not quantified in wall-clock time or tokens, making the net cost comparison with Standard RAG impossible to evaluate from the paper. - Effective: Evidence is strong for high-mismatch, rule-dense tasks (NPOV: +7.4 points over best RAG, human-validated); marginal for low-mismatch tasks (PEP 8: +0.15 Pylint); and limited for composition-bottlenecked tasks (NBA L1/L2 provide no benefit over baselines). - Scalable: The largest tested source document is the NBA CBA (~60K words, 195 rules); the matching phase scales linearly in M, but performance at 10× the rule count or document length has not been tested. No software artifact is released. Strengths: - The controlled relevance-reranker baseline (identical setup, relevance predicate instead of applicability, action exposed) directly isolates the applicability framing as the driver of gain, not just LLM-based reranking — this is methodologically unusually careful. - The operationalization phase ablation reveals that vague conditions simultaneously inflate #picks and reduce downstream quality, providing a concrete design principle beyond this paper: rule quality depends on condition specificity, not just rule count. - Cross-domain analysis honestly characterizes the conditions under which TAG helps (high abstraction gap, strong conditionality, high distractor density) and where it does not, rather than treating all domains as equally favorable. - Exposing the action field to the matcher is shown to bias selection toward desirable actions regardless of condition satisfaction; withholding it is a principled design choice with theoretical motivation and empirical support. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge four limitations: TAG requires source documents that can be faithfully converted to explicit condition-action rules, excluding highly implicit or purely factual knowledge bases; pairwise matching adds inference cost not fully offset by context reduction; multi-step rule composition (NBA L1/L2) is a downstream reasoning bottleneck TAG does not address; and three domains may not generalize to all instruction-governed tasks. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The inference cost of O(M) LLM calls per input is never quantified in time or tokens — a critical omission for any deployability claim. The assertion that cost is "partly offset" by shorter executor contexts is unverified. - All NPOV evaluation scores, including the primary VFR metric, are produced by an LLM judge from the same model family as the executor. If DeepSeek-V4 has systematic biases toward outputs of a particular style, both generation and evaluation are affected in correlated ways. - The 40-case human validation is sequential from Q001–Q040, not randomly sampled; the authors report this is not a random sample but do not analyze whether violation types are uniformly distributed in that prefix. - NBA L2 results show TAG at 15.4% strict accuracy, identical to No Retrieval — the method provides zero benefit at the highest complexity level. The paper notes this but does not analyze whether L2 failures are due to retrieval errors (missed rules) or composition errors (correct rules, wrong reasoning). - Rule extraction quality is self-validated by faithfulness, coverage, and independence metrics all exceeding 0.94, but there is no independent human assessment of whether extracted rules are semantically accurate representations of the source document's intent. Bottom Line: The applicability-vs-similarity reframing is a real and well-evidenced contribution for rule-governed RAG tasks, with the NPOV results and factorial ablation providing genuine mechanistic support — but unquantified inference cost, marginal gains on two of three domains, and the absence of code or data release limit immediate practical uptake; compliance and regulatory RAG practitioners have a concrete reason to read this, while general RAG researchers should treat the gains as promising but bounded.
Recommended 2605.27951 PDF
#61 — Innovative (66)

Rethinking Post-Training Recipes for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting

H Liu, Y Zhou, R Sen, BA Prakash, A Das · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces POSTTIME, a two-stage post-training recipe (CoT-SFT followed by RLVR with a revision-relative reward) that teaches a 4B-parameter LLM to act as a context-guided revisor over Time-Series Foundation Model (TSFM) numerical priors for multimodal forecasting. - Why it's important: Real-world forecasting trajectories are shaped by non-numerical exogenous factors (news events, policy changes, metadata) that TSFMs cannot ingest, yet LLMs alone are demonstrably poor numerical extrapolators — closing this gap has direct practical value in finance, supply chain, and other event-driven domains. - How it does it: Rather than training the LLM to forecast from scratch, POSTTIME trains it exclusively as a conditional editor that decides whether and how to revise a TSFM prior based on textual context, using diverse frontier-LLM-generated reasoning traces for SFT and an improvement-ratio reward (ImpRatio) instead of absolute accuracy for RL. - The good: A 4B-parameter post-trained Gemma-3-4B outperforms all 40+ baselines on the TimesX benchmark — including GPT-5, GPT-OSS-120B, and specialized TSLMs — improving TimesFM-2.5 by 6.38% nMAE and 12.43% nMSE on in-domain data, with the same progression pattern replicated on Qwen3-4B. - The missing: All evaluation is conducted on a single benchmark (TimesX) built by the same lead author group, with an OOD set of only 11 variables — insufficient to credibly assess generalization beyond the authors' own data curation choices. - Read it or not: Practitioners building systems that fuse numerical time-series forecasts with text events or metadata should read this; those seeking pre-training or architecture-level multimodal solutions can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Composite is pulled to the upper-Innovative band rather than Highly Innovative by Rigor (60) and Resilience (60): all evaluation is on a single benchmark (TimesX) co-developed by the lead author with only 11 OOD variables, no standard deviations are reported, and code/weights are unreleased — a real Goodhart's-Law risk. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are genuine: the reviser-not-forecaster role decomposition, fallback supervision for hard cases, and the ImpRatio revision-relative reward (a transferable fix for GRPO reward collapse) are a novel composition. Completeness (68) is good but omits acknowledgment of the benchmark concern. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*60+0.15*72+0.15*68+0.15*60+0.10*65 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Time-series forecasting in finance, supply chain, healthcare, and climate is rarely purely numerical — future trajectories are shaped by breaking news, policy shifts, and domain-specific event context. TSFMs (TimesFM, Moirai, Chronos) achieve strong zero-shot accuracy from numerical histories but are structurally unable to consume text. Conversely, LLMs understand rich multimodal context but substantially underperform TSFMs as standalone numerical forecasters — a finding established by prior benchmark work that the paper cites. Prior multimodal approaches either require expensive human-annotated traces (Time-Omni), use hindsight-biased training that leaks the observed horizon into SFT supervision (Time-R1), or discard the TSFM numerical prior by treating the LLM as a standalone forecaster. The gap is: how to teach an LLM to make calibrated, context-conditioned adjustments over an already-strong numerical prior without corrupting the prior's numerical reliability. Key Idea The single core insight is role decomposition: the TSFM handles numerical extrapolation (where it excels) and the LLM handles context-conditioned revision (where it can add value). Crucially, the LLM's task is framed as a three-way decision policy — revise, preserve, or pass — rather than end-to-end forecasting. This simplification makes both the SFT target and the RL reward well-defined: validity of a revision is measured relative to the TSFM prior, not relative to the unknowable ground-truth trajectory. Methodology POSTTIME is a two-stage pipeline applied to Gemma-3-4B-PT with LoRA adapters throughout. Stage 1 (CoT-SFT): Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite generates 5 independent reasoning traces per training example at forecast time (without seeing the realized future). A trace is deemed effective if its revised forecast beats the TSFM prior by MAE (Eq. 1). Up to 3 effective traces ranked by MAE are retained per example (Top-3 selection). For examples where no trace improves the prior (29.2% of data), a single fallback trace is inserted instructing the LLM to preserve the numerical prior unchanged — explicitly teaching "when not to revise." The resulting SFT corpus has 10,073 rows from 4,587 source samples (8,798 verified interventions + 1,275 fallbacks, ~62.5M total tokens). Training uses LoRA rank 8/alpha 16, lr=1.3×10⁻⁴, 3 epochs on a dual A100 server. Three critical design decisions: (1) hiding the realized horizon from the trace generator (using it only as a verifier, not an imitation target); (2) preserving hard/ambiguous cases with fallback supervision rather than filtering to easy examples; (3) retaining up to 3 diverse effective traces rather than a single best trace. Stage 2 (RLVR with GRPO): The improvement ratio ImpRatio = 1 - MAE(ŷ,y) / (MAE(ỹ,y) + ε) measures how much a revision improves over the TSFM prior, mapped to a clipped reward centered at 0.5 when the revision ties the prior (Eq. 6-7). This is contrasted with the standard ExpMAE reward (Eq. 4), which collapses numerically under GRPO's group-normalized advantage when all completions in a group share similarly high MAE. GRPO uses LoRA rank 8/alpha 32, lr=1.0×10⁻⁵, 8 epochs, 4 generations per prompt, KL beta=0.04. Key Results On the TimesX benchmark (99 variables, 2022-2025, 88 ID + 11 OOD variables at forecast horizons post-Jan 2025): - POSTTIME SFT+RL achieves ID nMAE 0.738 / nMSE 0.638, OOD nMAE 0.746 / nMSE 0.597 — best average rank (1.0 ID, 1.5 OOD) across 46 evaluated methods. - Improves over the TimesFM-2.5 prior (ID nMAE 0.788 / nMSE 0.729) by 6.38% nMAE and 12.43% nMSE on ID; 3.93% nMAE and 11.24% nMSE on OOD. - Beats the strongest training-free baselines: ENS(GPT-5+TimesFM) at ID nMAE 0.760 / nMSE 0.791, and REV(GPT-5+TimesFM) at ID nMAE 0.783 / nMSE 0.725. - All supervised TTS baselines (LM-augmented TSF models) are substantially worse (best: 1.289 nMAE), and all TSLMs (TimeOmni-1-7B: 1.088, Aurora: 1.084) trail badly. - Generalizes to Qwen3-4B with the same monotonic progression (initial 1.054 → SFT 0.760 → SFT+RL 0.747 nMAE on ID), confirming recipe-level rather than model-specific results. - ImpRatio reward raises mean within-group reward STD from 0.0171 to 0.0719 and eliminates all 16 reward-collapse steps seen with ExpMAE. - Fallback supervision reduces p99 nMSE from 3.704 (high-validity-only filtering) to 1.742 (all buckets with fallback), outperforming the prior even in the tail. - Experiments run 3 times and averaged on dual A100 (80GB). This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.29401v1, May 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The complete POSTTIME recipe — role decomposition (reviser not forecaster), diverse fallback-aware SFT data construction, and the ImpRatio revision-relative RL reward — is a novel combination with genuine engineering contributions. The individual components (CoT-SFT, GRPO, LoRA) are standard; novelty lies in the composition and the ImpRatio formulation. The empirical finding that TSFM-prior revision is categorically better than standalone LLM forecasting across 8 diverse LLMs, and that hard-case fallback supervision is essential for tail robustness, is a concrete contribution to the emerging multimodal forecasting literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are well-structured and cover the three main design dimensions: reviser vs. direct forecaster role (Table 1, 8 LLMs), SFT data selection strategy (Tables 4-6, validity buckets × trace selection), and RL reward formulation (Table 7, statistical collapse analysis). Per-quantile reporting (p50/p75/p90/p95/p99 nMSE) is a strength for tail behavior. The paper reports averages over 3 runs but provides no standard deviations, making statistical significance unverifiable. Code, data, and model weights are promised upon acceptance but not yet released. The primary concern is benchmark co-development: TimesX is built by the same lead author (H. Liu, co-author on arXiv:2605.29401 and the TimesX dataset agent paper Liu et al. 2026a); the variable selection, context alignment, and evaluation protocol all emerge from the same research group, creating a potential Goodhart's Law risk. The SFT trace generation depends on Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite API behavior, which may change over time and limits reproducibility. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods, experiments, and model training are actually run. - Practical: Training requires a dual A100 (80GB) server; inference requires running both TimesFM-2.5 and the 4B revisor in sequence, which fits on a single A100 — accessible but not commodity-CPU deployable. - Efficient: SFT over ~10K rows with LoRA is lightweight; RL adds 8 epochs on the same data. The two-model inference pipeline (TSFM + LLM revisor) adds latency relative to TSFM-only, and the SFT trace generation required 38,160 Gemini API calls (cost not reported). - Effective: The evidence directly demonstrates improvement over the TSFM prior on both ID and OOD splits, and the fallback analysis (Table 12) confirms the model learned to distinguish informative from non-informative context — not just a proxy measurement. - Scalable: Tested only on univariate series at H=12 steps across 99 variables; behavior on longer horizons, multivariate settings, or higher-frequency data is not evaluated and cannot be inferred from the experiments. The paper stops at paper form with promised artifact release; no software library, trained model weights, or dataset is currently available. Strengths - The role decomposition principle (TSFM for numerics, LLM for context revision) is empirically validated across 8 LLMs with a clean zero-shot ablation — not just asserted. - ImpRatio is a concrete, reusable algorithmic fix for the reward-collapse problem in GRPO applied to regression tasks; the statistical evidence (Table 7) is specific and quantified. - Preserving hard cases with fallback supervision is a counter-intuitive but well-supported finding — dropping them worsens p99 nMSE from 1.742 to 3.704, an effect that mean-only reporting would mask. - The two-LLM and two-trace-generator generalization experiments (Tables 9-10) provide meaningful evidence that POSTTIME is a recipe-level contribution rather than a Gemma-3-4B-specific artifact. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify four limitations in Appendix A: no image modality support (e.g., financial report charts); revision only via text output, not tool use or code execution; no web or knowledge-graph querying when context is weak; limited to univariate time-series forecasting. These are presented directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. The authors do not acknowledge the benchmark co-development concern or the thin OOD evaluation set. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single benchmark, author-affiliated: TimesX is co-developed by the same lead author (H. Liu); an independent benchmark evaluation (e.g., CiK, Time-MMD) is absent, leaving open whether gains are specific to TimesX's construction choices. - OOD set of 11 variables is too small for robust out-of-distribution conclusions — error variance across 11 series could easily flip rankings. - Task-specific supervised baselines are weak: TTS uses GPT2-based LM components from 2019; stronger text-conditioned supervised models (e.g., fine-tuned TimesFM with event features) are not included. - Single horizon (H=12) throughout: real applications frequently require H=48 or H=96 steps, and the revision policy's informativeness may degrade at longer horizons where event context is more temporally distant. - RL marginal gain is modest: SFT achieves nMSE 0.650 on ID; SFT+RL reaches 0.638 — a 1.8% improvement that may not justify the GRPO training complexity for practitioners. - Trace generation API cost is unreported, which matters for practitioners attempting to reproduce or extend POSTTIME to new domains. Bottom Line Practitioners who forecast event-driven time series (commodity prices, retail demand, financial indicators) with available textual context should read this — POSTTIME is the strongest demonstrated recipe for that setting, the ImpRatio reward is a transferable design principle, and the 4B model size makes deployment plausible; skeptics should verify on an independent benchmark before production use.
Citations 2605.29401 PDF
#62 — Innovative (66)

Falcon-X: A Time Series Foundation Model for Heterogeneous Multivariate Modeling

Y Liu, Y Hu, H Xia, P Liu, H Chen, X Dai, Z Dong… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Falcon-X, a 591M-parameter encoder-only time series foundation model that projects heterogeneous physical variates into a shared fixed-dimensional latent prototype space to enable principled cross-variate modeling across datasets and domains. - Why it's important: Existing multivariate time series foundation models either operate in the raw variate space — causing semantic collapse when mixing physically heterogeneous signals — or restrict attention to within-dataset groups, preventing transferable cross-domain relational learning. - How it does it: Three stacked modules — Unified Prototype Diff-Attention (cross-attention from variates to learnable positive/negative prototype keys), Latent Entity Attention (self-attention entirely within the fixed-C latent space), and a Variate Reassembly Router (soft-routing back to physical dimensions) — decouple temporal from cross-variate modeling and replace quadratic variate-level attention with linear-complexity prototype interaction. - The good: On GIFT-Eval, Falcon-X achieves 0.666 MASE (best on the leaderboard), improving 3.9% over Timer-S1 and 1.2% over STRIDE+Chronos-2, with ablations confirming that removing the negative prototype key causes the largest single performance drop. - The missing: All evaluations are zero-shot on the same benchmark suites used to select hyperparameters (prototype dimension C, depth ratio n/l), introducing potential optimistic bias; no held-out benchmark or truly unseen domain appears. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or benchmarking multivariate TSFMs — the latent prototype paradigm is a clean, implementable design alternative to raw-space group mixing, backed by scaling experiments from 59M to 591M parameters.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (per the paper's stated Deployability profile). Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) are real: the latent prototype paradigm for heterogeneous multivariate modeling and the repurposing of differential attention for signed (antagonistic) dependencies is a non-obvious structural design, and the inference-mode ablation is unusually direct evidence that the cross-variate mechanism actually transfers. Rigor (62) and Resilience (62) are the limiters: hyperparameters (C, n/l) are tuned against the same GIFT-Eval/fev-bench used for final evaluation (optimistic-bias risk), no error bars, leaderboard comparisons are not compute-controlled, and Falcon-X trails Chronos-2 on fev-bench without a covariate-augmented ablation to test the offered explanation. Impact (70) — public code, weights pending. Weighted: 0.20*72+0.25*62+0.15*72+0.15*62+0.15*62+0.10*70 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern time series foundation models (TSFMs) are largely univariate: they forecast each channel independently, ignoring co-evolving dependencies that dominate real systems (energy grids, financial markets, climate sensors). The emerging class of multivariate TSFMs that attempt to capture cross-variate interactions faces two structural problems. First, group attention (Toto, Chronos-2) confines interaction to within-dataset groups but operates directly in the raw variate space, where physically heterogeneous signals (temperature, price, load) carry incompatible semantics — the model must absorb cross-domain heterogeneity implicitly, making transfer a side-effect of parameter sharing rather than a principled process. Second, standard softmax attention cannot represent antagonistic (negative-correlation) relationships because its scores are non-negative, systematically underweighting opposing dynamics ubiquitous in real systems. Key Idea: Instead of mixing variates in their native physical space, map every variate — regardless of dataset or dimensionality — into a fixed small-dimensional latent prototype space spanned by C globally learnable prototype vectors, perform all cross-variate interaction there, then route each variate back to its own physical dimension. Because all variates share the same C-dimensional coordinate system, cross-dataset transfer becomes structural rather than incidental. The additional trick is replacing standard (non-negative) prototype attention with a differential mechanism — one positive and one negative learnable key matrix — so the model can explicitly represent both synergistic and antagonistic variate relationships. Methodology: The pipeline is: (1) instance-wise arcsine normalization using observed-value statistics only; (2) tokenization into non-overlapping patches of length Lp=16, augmented with normalized relative timestamps and a binary observation mask; (3) n=16 Time Attention layers (standard multi-head self-attention along the time axis, per variate independently) producing HT ∈ R^{M×P×D}; (4) Unified Prototype Diff-Attention (UPDA): query Q and value V projections of HT cross-attend to two globally-shared learnable key matrices Kpos, Kneg ∈ R^{C×D}, yielding differential attention scores A_pos − λ·A_neg projected onto V, mapping all N entities into HC ∈ R^{N×C×P×D}; an orthogonality loss L_orth = cos_sim(Kpos, Kneg) is added to keep the two prototype sets semantically distinct; (5) Latent Entity Attention (LEA): l=16 standard MHA layers over the combined (N×C) spatial sequence in the latent space; (6) Variate Reassembly Router (VRR): each variate's temporal embedding acts as a routing request, matched via scaled dot-product softmax against a prototype index projected from the refined latent context H'C, retrieving source context back to physical dimensionality; a gated residual connection (sigmoid-gated pointwise product) fuses HT with HV, allowing the model to attenuate cross-variate context when variates are weakly correlated. Final objective is quantile loss plus α·L_orth. Model is 591M parameters (D=1024), trained on NVIDIA B200 GPUs for 1M iterations with AdamW, bf16, global batch 384. Key Results: On GIFT-Eval (using MASE and CRPS): Falcon-X achieves 0.666 MASE / 0.453 CRPS, best on the leaderboard — improving 1.2% MASE over STRIDE+Chronos-2, 1.9% MASE / 2.2% CRPS over Toto-2.0-FT, and 3.9% MASE / 6.6% CRPS over Timer-S1. Chronos-2's MASE degrades from 0.67 (short) to 0.76 (long-term) while Falcon-X degrades from 0.65 to 0.70, demonstrating greater horizon robustness. On fev-bench, Falcon-X achieves 0.652 MASE / 0.490 CRPS, narrowly behind Chronos-2 (0.645 / 0.485) but ahead of TiRex, Toto-1.0, and Moirai 2.0. Ablation identifies removing Kneg (only Kpos) as the single most damaging architectural change; removing the gated residual and removing timestamp/mask tokens are secondary. Scaling from 59M → 253M → 591M yields strictly monotone improvements in both MASE and CRPS with no apparent saturation. Inference comparison shows Chronos-2's multivariate mode produces nearly identical results to univariate mode (semantic collapse), while Falcon-X's multivariate mode consistently outperforms its own univariate mode. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The latent prototype paradigm for heterogeneous multivariate TSFM is a genuinely new architectural design: the combination of (a) cross-attention to globally shared positive/negative prototype keys, (b) latent-space-only entity interaction, and (c) soft-routing reassembly does not appear as a unified system in prior work. The differential attention mechanism is borrowed from Ye et al. (2025) but repurposed from noise suppression to signed-dependency modeling — a non-obvious reuse. The orthogonality regularizer on prototype keys is a straightforward addition. The overall contribution goes beyond applying known techniques to a new setting: the prototype space alignment is a structural design choice that changes how multivariate interaction scales with dimensionality. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and cover both architectural components and training strategies (sampling shuffle, flexible horizons, joint vs. two-stage training), with results presented as bar charts on GIFT-Eval. Error bars and significance tests are not reported. The prototype dimension and depth-ratio sensitivity sweeps are shown (Figures 8a–b), providing useful hyperparameter guidance. Scaling experiments span 59M to 591M with three data points. The code repository is public (https://github.com/ant-intl/Falcon-TST), but model weights are listed as "coming soon" as of the preprint date. A potential concern: hyperparameter choices (C=6–8, n/l=16/16) appear tuned against GIFT-Eval and fev-bench, the same benchmarks used for final evaluation, introducing possible optimistic bias. No independent holdout benchmark is used to confirm generalization of the architectural choices. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: Inference runs on standard GPU hardware; training required a cluster of NVIDIA B200-180GB GPUs for 1M iterations, placing pretraining out of reach for most practitioners, though inference from released weights is practical. - Efficient: The UPDA module reduces cross-variate attention from O(M²) (dense) to O(M·C) where C≪M (e.g., C=6–8), a substantial efficiency gain for high-dimensional datasets; overall 591M parameters is on par with competing TSFMs in the class. - Effective: GIFT-Eval results show consistent improvement over strong baselines; the inference mode ablation (Figure 7b) directly demonstrates that multivariate modeling adds value beyond univariate inference on the same backbone — a direct test of the core claim. - Scalable: Scaling from 59M to 591M is demonstrated with monotone improvement; behavior beyond 591M or on datasets with thousands of variates (e.g., large sensor arrays) is not tested and only claimed. The work delivers a code repository; trained model weights are pending release. Strengths: - The prototype-space design cleanly solves the dimensionality mismatch problem (varying M across datasets) without padding or truncation, enabling genuine cross-dataset transfer in a principled way. - The inference mode comparison (Figure 7b) provides direct, compelling evidence that Falcon-X's cross-variate modeling actually transfers information — unlike Chronos-2's group attention which degenerates to univariate-equivalent behavior — making this one of the few papers to empirically prove the value of its multivariate mechanism rather than just claiming it. - The ablation that removing Kneg causes the largest single performance drop is non-obvious and gives concrete empirical support to the signed-dependency hypothesis. - Smooth scaling behavior from 59M to 591M with no observed saturation is a positive signal for future model size increases. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that Falcon-X relies strictly on endogenous target series (no past-only or future-known covariates), which they cite as a constraint when comparing against Chronos-2 on fev-bench. They do not explicitly discuss the compute cost of pretraining (B200 cluster for 1M iterations) as a reproducibility barrier, nor the pending model weight release. Limitations section is not a dedicated subsection — constraints are mentioned inline in the results discussion. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) All hyperparameters (C, n/l ratio) are tuned and reported against the two evaluation benchmarks; there is no independent validation set, so reported numbers may be optimistic. (2) The GIFT-Eval leaderboard includes the authors' own system, and comparisons rely on publicly available numbers from other teams' submissions — no controlled side-by-side training on the same data/compute budget. (3) fev-bench results show Falcon-X trailing Chronos-2; the explanation offered (covariate absence) is plausible but not tested by a covariate-augmented ablation. (4) The orthogonality loss L_orth weight α is mentioned but not ablated — it is unclear how sensitive performance is to this regularizer. (5) Zero-shot transfer quality to truly out-of-distribution domains (e.g., genomics, network traffic) not in the pretraining corpus is not evaluated. Bottom Line: Falcon-X is a technically credible and cleanly motivated step forward for multivariate TSFMs — the latent prototype paradigm directly addresses the semantic alignment problem that cripples raw-space group attention, and the inference-mode ablation provides unusually direct evidence that the cross-variate mechanism works; practitioners building or evaluating multivariate forecasting foundations should read it.
Citations 2605.27286 PDF
#63 — Innovative (66)

Block Redundancy in Tabular Foundation Models for Clinical Applications

I Cannistraci, JE Vogt · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that 12–16 block tabular in-context-learning foundation models (TabPFNv2, TabICL, TabDPT, Mitra) can have up to ~94% of their transformer blocks replaced at inference time by a closed-form affine translator fitted on 500 calibration samples, while preserving downstream AUROC across eight clinical benchmarks and three general tabular controls. - Why it's important: On-premise hospital deployment of tabular foundation models is blocked by GPU requirements; compression to a single active block could enable CPU-only inference without sacrificing the zero-shot, tuning-free convenience that makes these models clinically attractive over GBDTs. - How it does it: A least-squares affine map H_e ≈ H_s W + b is fitted between block representations on 500 calibration samples using forward hooks, replacing the intervening blocks entirely at inference time with no gradients or parameter updates. - The good: TabICL compressed to a single retained block (11/12 blocks replaced) stays within ±2.3% AUROC on every evaluated dataset, including MIMIC-III (.785 vs. .800 full) and multi-class benchmarks — versus naive layer dropping, which collapses every model to near-random AUROC. - The missing: The paper never measures actual CPU inference latency or memory footprint on clinical hardware, leaving the central practical claim — CPU-deployable tabular foundation models — empirically undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on efficient deployment of tabular transformers in resource-constrained environments; clinical practitioners should not treat this as deployment-ready until wall-clock CPU latency is measured.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the high point — the flat depth-wise attention-entropy insight gives a principled, structurally grounded explanation for ~94% linear block redundancy, and the per-feature translator for dual-axis models is a real adaptation. Rigor (70) is good for a workshop paper (multi-seed, an unusually thorough ablation suite covering fairness, missingness, and a 416-config early-exit sweep), but Impact (50) and Completeness (65) are dragged down because the central CPU-deployability claim is never measured, GBDT baselines are untuned by design, and no code is released. Weighted composite ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Hospitals and clinical environments typically operate on CPU-only hardware without GPUs. Tabular foundation models (TFMs) such as TabPFNv2, TabICL, TabDPT, and Mitra require GPU inference due to their 12–16 transformer block architectures. Simpler alternatives — LightGBM and XGBoost — run on CPU and match TFM performance on many benchmarks, but demand per-dataset feature engineering, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning. The research question is whether TFMs are vastly overparametrized and can be compressed to the point of CPU-only inference while retaining the zero-shot, tuning-free property that differentiates them from GBDTs. Prior redundancy work in NLP and vision transformers does not transfer directly because tabular transformers process short sequences (8–74 tokens), heterogeneous feature types, and lack sequential grammar — making it non-obvious whether the same block redundancy exists. Key Idea The core insight is that contiguous stacks of transformer blocks in tabular foundation models compute transformations that are well-approximated by a single affine map — the blocks are linearly redundant. Unlike NLP and vision transformers, tabular transformer attention entropy is essentially flat across depth (TabICL range ≈ 1.23–1.38 nats, std ≤ 0.06 vs. Qwen3-4B std ≈ 0.4–0.5 nats across 36 layers), indicating blocks do not specialize progressively. Because later blocks produce predominantly low-rank residual corrections rather than novel nonlinear computation, a single affine map fitted on 500 calibration samples can bridge any contiguous sub-stack while leaving the pre-trained classification head intact. Methodology The method fits an affine translator (W, b) by closed-form least squares on calibration representations extracted via forward hooks, minimizing the Frobenius residual ε(s,e) = ‖H_e − (H_s W + b)‖_F / ‖H_e‖_F. For single-axis self-attention models (TabICL, TabDPT), a single shared (W ∈ ℝ^{d×d}, b ∈ ℝ^d) is fit on flattened (seq·N, d) data — well over-determined. For dual-axis attention models (TabPFNv2, Mitra), a separate (W_f, b_f) is fit per feature index f because the shared map would mix axes and destroy feature correspondence; Mitra's two-stream architecture requires independent per-feature fits per stream, giving 2·n_features·(d²+d) parameters per skip. The skip window (s, e) is selected by calibration-set AUROC — the same 500-sample split already used for hyperparameter and model selection — never touching test labels. Three compression regimes are evaluated: mild (width 3–4 blocks), intermediate (8/12 or 11/16), and maximum (all but 1 block). Key design decisions: (1) the per-feature parameterization for dual-axis models is well-justified and reduces to the single-(W,b) case when n_features=1; (2) skip-window selection by calibration AUROC rather than representational ε is well-motivated since small ε does not guarantee AUROC preservation; (3) the retained block is selected by calibration AUROC, not fixed a priori. Ablations cover per-patient fairness (ε stratified by clinical outcome and feature completeness), robustness under 10–50% random feature corruption, structured clinical missingness (whole-group feature dropout), and a head-to-head early-exit comparison across 416 (model, dataset, depth) configurations. Key Results Mild compression (width 3–4): |ΔAUROC| ≤ 2.0% across all four models and 11 datasets (worst: Mitra on Diabetes, −2.0%). Maximum compression (1 retained block): TabICL stays within ±2.3% AUROC on every dataset including 4-class Jannis (.843 vs. .866) and 7-class Covertype (.943 vs. .962); TabDPT stays within −1% on all clinical datasets (worst −4.3% on Higgs .692 vs. .734); TabPFNv2 and Mitra degrade −3 to −10% on larger datasets at maximum compression. Naive identity dropping at the same compression collapses every model: e.g., TabPFN on Heart drops from .897 to .428; Mitra on Breast Cancer to .157. Regression (MIMIC-III ICU length-of-stay): TabICL near-lossless at widths 3–4 (ΔR² = −0.004), confirming redundancy is not an artefact of binary classification. Early-exit: block approximation matches or exceeds early-exit logistic regression in 83% of 416 (model, dataset, depth) configurations (TabICL 94%, TabDPT 91%). Fairness: per-patient ε(0,1) differs by |Δ| ≤ 0.041 between survived/died subgroups; no quartile gradient by feature completeness. GBDT baselines (XGBoost, LightGBM) use intentionally untuned defaults, which limits their strength as comparators — the paper acknowledges this. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD (applied to a new domain). The finding that tabular foundation models exhibit ~94% linear block redundancy — with flat depth-wise attention entropy fundamentally unlike NLP/vision transformers — is a genuine new empirical result. The linear translator technique is adapted from Cannistraci et al. (2024) (the same first author's prior NLP/vision work); the per-feature parameterization for dual-axis attention is a meaningful architectural adaptation, not a trivial extension. The clinical application and fairness analysis are new contributions of scope. Rigor & Reproducibility Error bars are reported as mean ± std over 3 seeds (clinical) and 5 seeds (TabZilla). The ablation suite is unusually thorough for a workshop paper: per-patient fairness by outcome and feature completeness, random and structured missingness, and a comprehensive early-exit sweep. The appendix explicitly notes that ε is a calibration training residual, not a generalization metric. No code or model artifacts are stated as publicly released. The calibration split doubles as the validation set — while acknowledged and plausibly standard, this means skip-window selection uses the same 500 samples as hyperparameter tuning, which could introduce optimistic bias particularly on small datasets (Diabetes: 768 samples total with 68/12/20% splits gives ~61 calibration samples before subsampling to 500 — actually the 500 is a cap, so small datasets may be near-fully exploited). The GBDT baselines are intentionally untuned (standard defaults), which artificially constrains the GBDT upper bound. This is a workshop paper (ICML 2026 SD4H), not a main-track submission; peer review was correspondingly lighter. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The translator fitting step (33–109ms, 500 calibration samples) requires GPU; the compressed model at inference uses only 1–2 transformer blocks, which plausibly runs on CPU, but no CPU inference benchmark is reported — the central practical claim is stated but not measured. - Efficient: Parameter count for the translator ranges from d²+d (shared, ~37K for d=192) to n_features×(d²+d) (per-feature, up to ~4.6M for Mitra with n_features=31 and d=512); all solves complete in <50ms per skip on a single GPU; GPU memory and CPU runtime are not measured. - Effective: For TabICL and TabDPT at mild compression (3–4 blocks), AUROC is preserved within 1–2% on all 11 datasets; at maximum compression, TabICL remains within 2.3%. TabPFNv2 and Mitra degrade more substantially at extreme compression, limiting their practical compressibility. - Scalable: Largest dataset tested is eICU-CRD with 30K samples (subsampled); MIMIC-III uses 26,538 samples. No evaluation at larger scales, and the in-context-learning paradigm faces fundamental context-length limits regardless of block count. The work stops at paper form — no software artifact is released. Strengths - The mechanistic insight that tabular transformer attention entropy is flat across depth (std ≤ 0.06 nats) versus highly structured in NLP (std ≈ 0.4–0.5 nats), demonstrated via a 2-vs-2 cross-domain comparison, provides a principled explanation for why the linear approximation succeeds — moving the result from empirical curiosity toward structural understanding. - The per-feature translator variant for dual-axis attention models is correctly designed and well-justified; using a shared translator on these models would conflate feature axes and produce meaningless results, and the paper demonstrates this distinction matters in practice. - The fairness ablation — per-patient ε stratified by clinical outcome (survived/died) and feature completeness quartile — directly addresses the most clinically relevant concern about compression and finds negligible subpopulation-specific degradation (|Δ| ≤ 0.041 across all models and datasets). - Evaluating across four architecturally diverse TFMs with different pre-training regimes (synthetic-only, real-data, mixed) and finding redundancy in all of them — albeit at different rates — substantially strengthens the claim that this is a structural property of the tabular transformer paradigm rather than an artifact of one model's training. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that TabPFNv2 and Mitra degrade more at maximum compression than TabICL/TabDPT. Future work is flagged for real-time CPU latency measurement and dynamic routing (per-patient adaptive depth). The use of calibration residual ε as a proxy (not generalization metric) is called out in the appendix. The paper directly acknowledges that untuned GBDT defaults are used for context, not as optimized comparators. These are candid disclosures that credit the authors. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No CPU inference latency is measured anywhere in the paper. The entire practical motivation is CPU-deployability in hospitals without GPUs, but the paper reports only GPU fitting time (33–109ms). A compressed 1-block tabular transformer inference time on commodity CPU hardware versus a full 12–16 block model versus LightGBM is the key number the paper never provides. - GBDT baselines are untuned by design, but untuned GBDTs on datasets like Covertype (7-class, 30K samples) substantially underperform their tuned versions — the resulting comparison overstates TFM advantages on large tabular datasets. - The calibration/validation split identity on small datasets (e.g., Diabetes with ~92 calibration samples after 12% of 768) means skip-window selection and hyperparameter selection share exactly the same data; on small datasets this could inflate the reported benefit of calibration-based window selection. - The regression evaluation uses only MIMIC-III LOS and only width 3–4 (not maximum compression or identity baseline), leaving the robustness of the regression result understated compared to the classification results. - No released code makes independent reproduction non-trivial, as the per-feature hook extraction and dual-stream Mitra handling involve architecture-specific engineering not fully specified in the main text. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying tabular foundation models in clinical environments will find the compression result compelling and the ablation suite unusually thorough for a workshop paper, but cannot currently act on the CPU-deployability claim without measuring actual inference latency on the target hardware — a gap the authors should close before positioning this as a deployment solution.
Search Results
#64 — Innovative (66)

DeltaQuant: 4-bit Video Diffusion Models with Spatiotemporal Delta Smoothing

X Li, S Tesfai, Z Zhang, H Xi, S Yang, L Zhang, Y Sun… · openaccess.thecvf.com · Ion Stoica - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DeltaQuant, a W4A4 post-training quantization method for video diffusion transformers that partitions activation tensors into local 3D spatiotemporal cubes and quantizes only the per-cube delta tokens to 4 bits while retaining the mean core token at FP8 precision. - Why it's important: State-of-the-art open-source video diffusion models (e.g., Wan2.2 at 27B parameters) cannot run on consumer GPUs without CPU offloading, and existing attention optimizations shift the compute bottleneck to linear layers while doing nothing for memory — 4-bit weight-activation quantization addresses both simultaneously. - How it does it: It exploits the same spatiotemporal redundancy video codecs use: adjacent tokens in a local t×h×w cube are highly similar, so subtracting the cube mean before quantization dramatically reduces outlier magnitude, making 4-bit quantization tractable where static smoothing methods fail. - The good: On Wan2.2-I2V-A14B, DeltaQuant achieves LPIPS 0.128 and PSNR 23.2 at W4A4, matching the W4A16 GGUF baseline (LPIPS 0.137, PSNR 23.2) while delivering 1.9× lower latency on an RTX 5090 — closing the quality gap between weight-only and weight-activation quantization at lower latency. - The missing: All hardware experiments run on the brand-new RTX 5090 with NVFP4 tensorcores; whether the approach and speedup numbers translate to more widely deployed GPUs (A100, H100, RTX 4090) is not evaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on video generation deployment or researchers in diffusion model quantization — the spatiotemporal delta insight is clean and the kernel engineering is production-grade; skip if your hardware predates NVFP4 tensorcore support.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (per the Deployability tag), though the contribution is strongly systems-flavored. Rigor (70) anchors the score: peer-reviewed CVPR 2026, informative ablations (cube size, core-token precision sweep, adaptive vs. uniform allocation), and a clean Pareto-frontier comparison rather than cherry-picked points. Non-Obviousness (72) and Novelty (68) credit the spatiotemporal delta-coding insight (FP8 core / FP4 delta), an elegant, well-motivated H.264 analogy that fills a real gap left by static-smoothing methods, tempered because it is an additive extension of SVDQuant. Impact (75) reflects production-grade fused CUDA kernels enabling 27B video models on consumer GPUs, discounted by unconfirmed code release and RTX-5090/NVFP4-only validation. Completeness (55) and Resilience (58) are the limiters: no technical limitations are acknowledged, the hardware dependency is unaddressed, the headline 111.8x conflates four independent techniques (DeltaQuant alone is ~3x), and the implausibly weak S²Q-VDiT/Q-VDiT baselines (negative Vision Reward) are unexplained. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*75 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 55 Resilience 58 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large video diffusion transformers (DiT-based, e.g., Wan2.2 at 27B parameters) are prohibitively slow and memory-intensive for consumer GPU deployment — generating a 720p, 5-second video takes over 75 minutes on an RTX 5090 in BF16. Recent sparse and quantized attention techniques reduce attention cost by up to 8.6× but shift the dominant bottleneck to linear layers, which then account for 65% of remaining compute and do nothing to reduce the model's memory footprint. Prior 4-bit weight-activation quantization methods designed for image diffusion models (SVDQuant) rely on static, offline-calibrated per-channel smoothing for activations. This assumption breaks for video models because activation outlier channels and their magnitudes vary dramatically across denoising timesteps: a smoothing factor calibrated for timestep 0 actively worsens quantization difficulty at timestep 20, causing severe quality degradation. On-the-fly SVD for dynamic activation decomposition is prohibitively expensive during inference. Key Idea: Video activations exhibit strong spatiotemporal locality — tokens at the same spatial location across adjacent frames and tokens within a spatial neighborhood within a frame are similar in distribution. This mirrors H.264's exploitation of inter- and intra-frame redundancy. By computing the mean token of each small local 3D spatiotemporal cube on the fly and treating it as a high-precision core, the residual delta tokens have dramatically smaller magnitude and fewer outliers than the original activations, making them amenable to 4-bit quantization without offline calibration. This dynamic, data-driven decomposition sidesteps the static smoothing failure mode of prior work at negligible compute cost. Methodology: DeltaQuant operates as a post-training quantization scheme applied to all linear layers in video DiT transformer blocks. The pipeline for activation quantization proceeds as follows: (1) the flattened N-token sequence is reshaped back to its spatiotemporal structure T×H×W×Ci, then partitioned into K non-overlapping cubes of size t×h×w×Ci; (2) for each cube, the token-wise mean is computed as a core token (1×Ci), and the delta is X̃ = X - X̄ (broadcast subtraction); (3) the core token is quantized to FP8 (per-group, group size 64) and the delta tokens to NVFP4; (4) the linear layer output for cube k is computed as Q8(X̄)Q4(W) on FP8 tensorcores (a dynamic token bias broadcast to all thw tokens) plus Q4(X̃)Q4(W) on FP4 tensorcores. For weights, DeltaQuant inherits SVDQuant's offline SVD low-rank decomposition: W = L1L2 + R, where the low-rank branch L1L2 handles weight outliers and the residual R is quantized to NVFP4. Three critical design decisions: (a) adaptive cube sizing — smaller cubes (t=4,h=1,w=4, 16 tokens) for the first 25–30% of noisy denoising steps where activations vary most, larger cubes (t=4,h=2,w=8, 64 tokens) for the remaining steps, yielding 0.5 dB PSNR improvement over uniform sizing; (b) FP8 (not BF16) core tokens, which actually achieves higher PSNR (23.2 vs 22.8) than BF16 while enabling 4× throughput via FP8 tensorcores; (c) cross-attention key/value projections (operating solely on text tokens) are quantized to 6-bit instead of 4-bit due to negligible token count. Custom CUDA kernels fuse core token generation into activation quantization, implement a W4A8 GEMM for core tokens, and integrate partial sum accumulation into the W4A4 GEMM for delta tokens, all built atop the SVDQuant Nunchaku engine. Key Results: On Wan2.2-I2V-A14B (27B params), DeltaQuant (W4A4) achieves LPIPS 0.128, PSNR 23.2, SSIM 0.742, Vision Reward 0.143 versus BF16 baseline 0.145 Vision Reward, outperforming SVDQuant (LPIPS 0.165, PSNR 21.9, Vision Reward 0.146) and matching W4A16 GGUF 4 (LPIPS 0.137, PSNR 23.2) with significantly lower latency. On Wan2.2-T2V-A14B, DeltaQuant (LPIPS 0.338, PSNR 17.7) surpasses SVDQuant (LPIPS 0.354, PSNR 17.5) and ties GGUF 4. On LTX-Video T2V-13B, DeltaQuant (LPIPS 0.159, PSNR 21.6) exceeds SVDQuant (LPIPS 0.193, PSNR 20.1). Memory: 2.9× model size reduction and 2.3× peak inference memory reduction for Wan2.2 on RTX 5090 (26.8 GB vs 61.3 GB), making single-GPU deployment feasible on consumer hardware. Latency on RTX 5090: 4.6× single-step speedup at 720p and 7.2× at 480p over BF16 (with Radial+Sage Attention enabled), versus GGUF 4 at 2.7× and 3.7×. When stacked with LightX2V (4-step LoRA), Radial Attention, and Sage Attention, total end-to-end speedup reaches 111.8× (43.1s vs 4818s), with Vision Reward 0.148 vs BF16's 0.145. Baselines include SmoothQuant, SVDQuant, S²Q-VDiT, Q-VDiT, and GGUF 4 — though S²Q-VDiT (LPIPS 0.366) and Q-VDiT (Vision Reward –0.085) perform surprisingly poorly, suggesting possible implementation or hyperparameter issues with those baselines. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The application of video-codec-style delta coding (inter-frame residual quantization) to transformer activation tensors in a mixed-precision (FP8 core / FP4 delta) scheme, executed with custom fused CUDA kernels, is genuinely novel. The combination of SVDQuant weight decomposition with spatiotemporal activation decomposition is an additive extension rather than a conceptual leap, but the activation-side insight is non-obvious and fills a real gap left by prior static-smoothing approaches. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: cube size sensitivity, rank–latency trade-off versus SVDQuant, core token precision sweep (FP4/FP8/BF16), and adaptive vs. uniform cube allocation. Error bars and multiple-seed results are not reported — all numbers are single-run point estimates, which is standard in this subfield but limits statistical confidence. Code is not explicitly released and no repository URL is mentioned. The paper is peer-reviewed (CVPR 2026 Open Access). One methodological concern: all hardware benchmarks use the RTX 5090 with NVFP4 tensorcores — a hardware feature not yet widely available at the time of publication — making the speedup numbers non-reproducible on any other GPU. The weak performance of S²Q-VDiT and Q-VDiT in Table 1 (the former with LPIPS 0.366 vs. the proposed method's 0.128 on the same model) is unexplained and warrants scrutiny; these may be misconfigured baselines. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and systems with hardware experiments). - Practical: Requires NVIDIA GPU hardware supporting NVFP4 tensorcores (currently RTX 5090 generation); the method is not demonstrated to accelerate computation on any other GPU, limiting current practical scope. - Efficient: Core token computation adds only 3.2% overhead for cube size 64 over naive 4-bit quantization; model size reduces from 26.6 GB (BF16) to 9.2 GB, and inference memory from 61.3 GB to 26.8 GB on Wan2.2. - Effective: Evidence strongly supports the claim for Wan2.2-I2V and LTX-Video; Wan2.2-T2V shows marginal quality (Vision Reward 0.083 matching SVDQuant's 0.083 at BF16's 0.091), indicating the method does not universally preserve quality across all tasks at equal strength. - Scalable: Tested on 27B-parameter models generating 77–81 frame 720p video — a large but not unprecedented scale; the approach has not been tested on models beyond 27B parameters or at significantly longer video durations. The work delivers a systems artifact (custom CUDA kernels integrated into the Nunchaku engine), but code release is not confirmed. Strengths: - The spatiotemporal delta decomposition insight is elegant and well-motivated: the analogy to H.264 inter-frame coding is precise, and Figure 5 provides clear empirical evidence that delta tokens have dramatically reduced outliers, making the motivation for the design quantitative rather than hand-wavy. - The adaptive cube size strategy (smaller cubes for early high-noise timesteps, larger for later) is a thoughtful engineering decision grounded in the observation that activation distributions are more variable during early denoising, and the ablation confirms a meaningful 0.5 dB PSNR gain. - DeltaQuant strictly dominates SVDQuant on the LPIPS–latency Pareto frontier across all tested rank configurations (Figure 9b), providing a rigorous efficiency–accuracy comparison rather than cherry-picked operating points. - Full systems integration (custom CUDA kernels, compatibility with Radial Attention, Sage Attention, and few-step LoRAs) demonstrates the work goes beyond algorithmic contribution to a deployable solution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge only in the Broader Impact section that democratized access to high-quality video generation may facilitate creation of misleading content. No technical limitations are explicitly stated — the paper does not acknowledge the RTX 5090 hardware dependency, the lack of multi-seed evaluation, the absence of code release, or the cases where T2V quality preservation is weaker. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Hardware scope: all speedup results require NVFP4 tensorcore support (RTX 5090). Whether the method degrades gracefully to INT4 or FP8 on more accessible hardware (RTX 4090, A100, H100) is not addressed. The practical impact claim is undermined by this constraint. - Baseline quality: S²Q-VDiT and Q-VDiT perform far below expectations in Table 1. Q-VDiT achieves a negative Vision Reward (–0.085) on Wan2.2-I2V, which is physically implausible for a calibrated method. These results should be explained or the baselines flagged as possibly misconfigured — leaving them creates a misleadingly favorable comparison. - The 111.8× speedup is a product of four independently developed techniques (DeltaQuant × LightX2V × Radial Attention × Sage Attention). The marginal contribution of DeltaQuant alone is 3× (131s → 43s). The headline figure conflates system integration with algorithmic contribution. - No evaluation on image diffusion models to compare against SVDQuant on its home turf, which would isolate the activation-quantization improvement. - No ablation on the choice of mean as the core token versus other aggregation functions (median, max-pooling), or on the sensitivity to the 25–30% timestep boundary for adaptive cube sizing. Bottom Line: Practitioners building video generation inference pipelines on RTX 5090-class hardware will find DeltaQuant a concrete, well-engineered step that closes the quality gap between weight-only and weight-activation quantization; researchers working on quantization for dynamic activation distributions will find the spatiotemporal delta decomposition idea directly usable, though the hardware dependency and unexplained baseline anomalies warrant caution before treating the numbers as definitive.
Recommended
#65 — Innovative (66)

BuddyBench: A Privacy-Constrained Multi-Task Benchmark for Pediatric Social-Communication Personalization

J Eo, JY Kim, R Ju, M Jung, U Lee · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BuddyBench, a multi-task benchmark built from two real pediatric cohorts (269 participants total) that links drill-level interaction logs, standardized clinical assessments, self-report instruments, and randomized-treatment endpoints to support knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference for ASD/SCD social-communication digital therapeutics. - Why it's important: No prior public benchmark connects structured therapeutic interaction logs to validated pediatric clinical outcomes within the same cohort, leaving personalization and treatment-response methods without a credible evaluation substrate specific to neurodevelopmental intervention. - How it does it: Two complementary cohorts — ND-03 (observational, n=183, 88.1% drill-response fill rate) for behavioral tasks and ND-02 (RCT, n=86, pre/post clinical endpoints) for clinical tasks — share a 153-item drill bank and BuddyPlan self-report instrument, unified into a four-task benchmark interface with leakage-controlled feature tiers and a physics-informed VAE synthetic companion (BuddyBench-Sim, n=1,000) for open-access development. - The good: The benchmark spans an unusually broad task spectrum — from sequence modeling to causal inference — within a single clinical context, and it explicitly quantifies both the signal (T1 AUC 0.54–0.72, T3 AUPRC best 0.860 vs. 0.747 baseline) and the hard floor imposed by small-RCT underpowering (T4 Δ=0.009 between CausalForest and constant-effect baseline), which is intellectually honest self-characterization. - The missing: The real cohorts are from a single institution, both are too small for subgroup analysis (ND-02 has n=83 FAS with 13:1 M:F ratio), and Task 2 uses inferred drill order from column position rather than actual timestamps, meaning the recommendation task evaluates curriculum-consistency offline rather than adaptive policy quality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building personalization or causal-inference methods for small-n digital therapeutics or pediatric AI, where the benchmark's honest difficulty characterization and hybrid access model offer a useful template; less valuable for those seeking benchmark scale, cross-institution generalizability, or a direct clinical deployment path.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (80) is the dominant driver — this is an unusually self-aware benchmark paper that confronts its limitations head-on: it quantifies hard floors (T4 Δ=0.009 CausalForest vs. ATEOnly), documents majority-class threshold collapse in T1, and supplies subsampling curves proving data-quantity rather than model-capacity limits. Rigor (70) is strong: BH-FDR correction, BCa bootstrap CIs, five seeds, a rigorous privacy audit (membership-inference ≤0.52, min-k ≥5), and public release of code plus the BuddyBench-Sim synthetic companion — though preprint status and single-site sourcing hold it back. Novelty (65) reflects a genuine data gap filled (first to link drill-level therapeutic logs to randomized pediatric clinical endpoints in one cohort), but the modeling components and hybrid-access pattern are all borrowed. Resilience (70) is high because claims are well-bounded and the authors pre-empt the obvious objections. Impact (55) is moderate — a usable method-screening artifact, but real cohorts are IRB-gated and no deployment path is claimed. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 80 Resilience 70 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Digital therapeutics for autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and social communication disorder (SCD) generate dense app-based interaction logs, yet no public resource links those logs to validated pediatric clinical outcomes within the same cohort. Educational AI benchmarks (e.g., XES3G5M, EdNet) supply rich interaction histories but target math and science with no clinical endpoints. Clinical prediction benchmarks (e.g., CliniBench on MIMIC-IV) carry validated outcomes but lack structured therapeutic logs. Existing neurodevelopmental datasets (ABIDE I/II, SPARK, HBN) emphasize neuroimaging or cross-sectional phenotyping, and small behavioral datasets (MMASD n=32, Engagnition n=57) lack benchmark-grade splits, metrics, and baselines. The gap is the absence of a benchmark that enables simultaneously benchmarking personalization algorithms and clinical outcome prediction on the same population, while respecting IRB constraints that prevent open redistribution of pediatric clinical records. Key Idea The core design insight is the hybrid access architecture: real IRB-gated cohorts provide genuine clinical signal, while a schema-aligned synthetic counterpart (BuddyBench-Sim, generated via a physics-informed VAE) enables open development and method screening without exposing protected data. This is paired with a leakage-controlled feature-tier scheme (F0–F3) that enforces strict temporal separation between pre-treatment covariates and clinical outcomes, and with per-run opaque participant labels to prevent inadvertent identity leakage into model prompts. The benchmarking novelty is the deliberate structural split between tasks: ND-03's dense drill-response matrix (88.1% fill, 137.4 mean attempts) serves the behavioral tasks, while ND-02's randomized design and pre/post clinical endpoints serve the clinical and causal tasks, with the two cohorts sharing a drill bank and skill taxonomy. Methodology BuddyBench integrates three data modalities — a 153-item binary drill-response matrix (pseudonymized, timestamps withheld), standardized clinical assessments (SRS-2, VABS-II, CGI-S/I), and the BuddyPlan self-report instrument (52 items in ND-03, 18-item subset in ND-02) — around a pseudonymous participant ID. Task 1 (knowledge tracing) encodes each participant's drill history as a masked token sequence; the model predicts held-out correctness for a target drill. Evaluation uses a participant-disjoint 5-fold subject split (AUC primary metric) across 36 models from the pyKT framework plus five LLM-based zero-shot models (Qwen3-1.7B backbone with a five-component Bayesian Wilson lower-bound history prior and temperature-scaled blending: p̂ = (1−α)π + α·σ(logit(s_LLM)/T), α=0.45, T=4.0). Task 2 (next-drill recommendation) reconstructs within-participant drill order from column position in the wide data matrix (timestamps withheld for re-identification risk) and ranks candidate drills using 25 recsys baselines. Primary metrics are R@5, R@10, NDCG@10. Task 3 (clinical prediction) uses leakage-safe F2 pre-treatment covariates (demographics + baseline SRS-2/VABS-II + BuddyPlan; post-treatment outcomes excluded) to predict non-response risk. Non-response is defined as no improvement or any deterioration across all three endpoints (CGI-S/I, SRS-2, VABS), yielding a 74.7% positive rate. Nested 5-fold/3-fold CV tunes 20 tabular models on AUPRC. Task 4 (causal inference) applies residualized R-loss over a 5-fold outer CV to evaluate 17 causal estimators (meta-learners, forest-based, Bayesian, representation-learning) on endpoint-level individual treatment effect estimation from the ND-02 RCT. BuddyBench-Sim uses a PI-VAE generating 1,000 synthetic records, audited for utility (pairwise Spearman feature-correlation alignment ≥ 0.968, record-level clinical validity = 0.990) and privacy (membership-inference accuracy ≤ 0.52, min-k ≥ 5 across demographic-diagnostic-severity groupings). Critical design decisions: (1) Timestamps withheld in T2 to reduce re-identification risk — well-justified from a privacy standpoint but limits the recommendation task to an offline curriculum-consistency proxy, a real trade-off the authors acknowledge. (2) Participant-level splits across all tasks — correct and necessary; prevents student-leakage and clinical-correlation leakage. (3) F2 (not F3) as the primary T3 reporting point — AUPRC is flat or slightly lower at F3 (0.853 vs. 0.860 at F2), justified by ablation evidence that additional drill-summary features add noise at n=83. Key Results Task 1: PEBG leads at AUC 0.72 ± 0.03 (5-fold, n=183); HISE-KT, IEKT, CIKT follow at 0.71. The 36-model leaderboard spans AUC 0.54–0.72. ACC converges near 0.79 and F1 near 0.88 for most neural models due to majority-class threshold collapse, making AUC the only interpretable discriminative metric. Five LLM-based zero-shot models (Qwen3-1.7B) achieve AUC 0.700–0.712 without any gradient adaptation, competitive with trained neural baselines; however, all five share a synchronized AUC drop of ≈0.10 on fold 1 (cross-model Pearson r ≥ 0.944, p < 0.02), showing they are not informationally independent — a shared Wilson prior and backbone failure mode, not architecture diversity. BH-FDR significance testing distinguishes only a two-model competitive cluster (PEBG + IEKT). Task 2: Best real-cohort R@10 is 0.322 (DiffDiv), NDCG@10 is 0.431 (DiffDiv). Heuristic difficulty-aware methods remain competitive with learned recommenders across the warm-start F3 protocol, indicating the task is informative but unsaturated. Task 3: tabm achieves AUPRC 0.860 ± 0.05 (F2, n=83), a +0.113 lift over the 0.747 prevalence baseline. Gradient-boosted trees (tabboost 0.843, lightgbm 0.842) are well-calibrated (ECE ≤ 0.082) while transformer-based tabular architectures show large miscalibration (saint ECE 0.310). Subsampling to n=45 reduces AUPRC by 15 points, confirming data quantity rather than model capacity as the bottleneck. Task 4: Best CGI-S/I residualized R-loss is 0.240 (CausalForest) vs. ATEOnly 0.249 — a Δ=0.009 that is not practically significant. No causal estimator reliably outperforms constant-effect baselines at n=86, consistent with the RCT being severely underpowered for heterogeneous treatment-effect detection. SRS-2 R-loss decreases 29% from n=30 to n=75, confirming the estimators are data-starved, not misspecified. BuddyBench-Sim rank preservation: Spearman ρ = 0.746 (T1 AUC), ρ = 0.675–0.752 (T2 R@10/NDCG@10), supporting method screening; T3 synthetic AUPRC is inflated to 0.979–0.981 at n=1,000 (vs. 0.860 real), confirming data-quantity rather than model ceiling limits real T3 performance. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The benchmark itself is the contribution — linking drill-level learning trajectories to randomized clinical endpoints in pediatric neurodevelopmental intervention is new. The individual modeling components (knowledge tracing, recommendation, clinical prediction, causal inference with R-loss, PI-VAE synthesis, membership-inference audit) are existing techniques applied to a novel cohort configuration. The hybrid access model (IRB-gated real data + open synthetic companion) is a replication of a known design pattern (Stadler et al. 2022) applied to this specific domain. Nothing in the methodology is algorithmically novel; the contribution is the integrated benchmark design and the dual-IRB/BuddyBench-Sim access architecture. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: F0–F3 feature-tier ablations for T2–T4 are reported, with T3 showing AUPRC peaks at F2 not F3. Fold-wise standard deviations are reported for all tasks. BH-FDR correction is applied for T1 and T3 significance claims. BCa bootstrap is used for confidence intervals where reported. Five random seeds are used for subsampling analyses. Code and BuddyBench-Sim are publicly released (CC BY-NC 4.0) at the anonymous review repository. Real cohorts are not public — dual-IRB approval required. Hyperparameter search spaces and tuning protocol (nested CV) are documented. Methodological concerns: (1) Task 2's offline evaluation using column-order-inferred drill sequences rather than timestamps is a significant proxy; it measures rank consistency with historical curriculum choices, not adaptive therapeutic benefit. (2) T4's n=86 ITT is structurally too small for heterogeneous treatment-effect estimation; the paper is transparent about this but includes the task anyway as a methodology stress test. (3) Task 3 has a 74.7% positive rate making AUROC (best 0.614) nearly uninformative; the paper correctly uses AUPRC but the practical clinical screening utility is minimal. (4) The paper self-reports arXiv preprint status; results are not peer-reviewed. (5) One reference note in the paper itself flags an unverified arXiv ID (LOKT, claimed 2410.12872 resolves to a different paper), raising minor bibliography integrity concerns. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (the benchmark evaluates existing methods experimentally; no primary theoretical claim). - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU; tabular T3/T4 methods run in under 6 hours per tier, making them accessible on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Task 1 KT models require 2–8 hours of GPU training per fold per model; LLM-based KT zero-shot inference runs a full fold in 45–50 seconds on the same hardware, offering a practical efficiency advantage for privacy-constrained settings where retraining on sensitive data is restricted. - Effective: The benchmark confirms learnable pre-treatment risk-stratification signal (T3 +0.113 AUPRC lift) and KT signal (T1 AUC 0.54–0.72), but no model achieves deployment-ready discrimination on any task; authors explicitly disclaim deployment readiness and state that prospective validation, safety review, and regulatory assessment are required before clinical use. - Scalable: Not tested at scale — cohorts are inherently constrained (n=83–183) and the synthetic scale-up to n=1,000 confirms T3 AUC saturation is achievable with more data, but neither real-cohort expansion nor cross-site replication is demonstrated. The work delivers a software benchmark artifact (BuddyBench-Sim dataset + evaluation code + leaderboard) and stops at paper form for the real cohorts (access gated). Strengths - The integrated benchmark design linking behavioral personalization tasks (KT, recommendation) to clinical outcome tasks (prediction, causal inference) within a single cohort pair, using a shared drill bank and clinical assessment battery, is genuinely new for the ASD/SCD digital-therapeutics domain. - The honest self-characterization of task difficulty — quantifying the hard floor in T4 (Δ=0.009 CausalForest vs. ATEOnly), documenting majority-class threshold collapse in T1, and providing subsampling curves that demonstrate data-quantity rather than model-capacity limits — is a credibility positive and unusual in benchmark papers. - The LLM-KT zero-shot analysis (Appendix B) is a substantive secondary contribution: the synchronized fold-1 AUC drop across all five architectures (cross-model r ≥ 0.944), explained through the shared Wilson prior and backbone, is a concrete diagnostic that reveals informational non-independence among architecturally diverse LLM-KT models — this finding is applicable beyond BuddyBench. - The privacy-audit pairing (membership-inference accuracy ≤ 0.52, k-anonymity min-k ≥ 5, utility/privacy jointly reported) is rigorous relative to common synthetic data releases, which often report utility alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) single-site cohorts with imbalanced sex ratios (2.4:1 ND-03, 13:1 ND-02) that limit generalizability; (2) ND-02 is underpowered for heterogeneous treatment-effect estimation (n=86), making T4 a methodology stress test rather than clinical-effect discovery; (3) Task 2's offline ranking proxy does not estimate therapeutic efficacy; (4) BuddyBench-Sim alters task difficulty rather than reproducing real-cohort behavior (documented task-by-task in Appendix E); (5) BuddyBench is positioned upstream of deployment — no clinical screening or treatment recommendation claim is made. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Task 2's timestamp withholding forces drill-order reconstruction from column position, which assumes that the column order of the wide data matrix faithfully represents within-participant practice sequence. This assumption is not validated and may introduce systematic ordering artifacts. - The five LLM-KT models all use the same custom Bayesian Wilson lower-bound history prior (not the published methods' default priors), making the leaderboard a prior-strategy comparison under controlled backbone rather than a faithful reproduction of published LLM-KT methods. This is disclosed but may mislead readers comparing against published KT benchmarks. - Task 3's non-response label (no improvement on all three endpoints simultaneously) is a conservative, conjunctive definition yielding a 74.7% positive rate that may not align with any single clinically actionable endpoint; no sensitivity analysis on alternative label definitions is provided. - BuddyBench-Sim's aggregate ML utility score (0.780) is computed as an unweighted mean of Spearman rank correlations and a normalized retention ratio across T1–T3, excluding T4 — the composite is not independently validated and the weighting choice is unexplained. - The benchmark is derived from a single app and single institution (Neudive Inc.); no analysis of selection effects from app-based deployment (e.g., whether families who adopt digital therapeutics differ systematically from the ASD/SCD population) is provided.
