Three critical design decisions: (a) action-agnostic vs. action-weighted graph — the paper defaults to action-agnostic because it yields better compression on corridor domains, trading mild loss on chain-like settings (Table 11); (b) overlap resolution — ablations (Table 4) show hard assignment collapses return to 89.3% on Multi-Goal Navigation (TV error 0.38) while occupancy weighting recovers 97.1% (TV error 0.11) and soft Bellman backup achieves 97.8%; (c) symmetrized graph only for structural discovery while preserving original directionality for planning — a clean architectural separation that avoids feeding planning artifacts into the topology discovery step. Four theoretical results accompany the method. Theorem 3 (value preservation): under reward regularity ε_r, transition regularity ε_P, and an action-consistency condition (each ground state must have a single action optimal across all its active cores), the lifted abstract value satisfies ||V* - Ṽ||_∞ ≤ (ε_r + γε_P/(1-γ))/(1-γ). Theorem 4 (interface-overlap advantage): hard partitions incur a TV lower bound of [(α - (1-λ)β - λ)]+/2 for interface states with transition probability α into a neighboring region, where interior states have at most β and the interface occupancy weight is λ; this term is exactly eliminable by the overlapping membership construction in Eq. (14). Proposition 5 decomposes within-core TV error into interior homogeneity and boundary leakage terms. Theorem 6 proves tangle recovery under a vertex-separator bottleneck model. Theorem 7 gives a finite-sample orientation guarantee: m_eff ≥ c·Δ^{-2}·log(|F|/η) suffices. Key Results: On Corridor-Rooms-9 (|S|=196), tangle-core achieves 9±0 abstract states preserving ≥99% return; METIS requires 85±6, spectral 88±7, bisimulation 157±4, and TOMA fails beyond 95% return. Scaling across Corridor-Rooms-R (Table 2): tangle-core consistently recovers exactly R abstract states (4, 9, 16) while bisimulation grows to 47, 157, 331 states — roughly proportional to raw state count. Across broader tabular benchmarks (Table 3) at ≥99% return: tangle-core achieves 4, 9, 8, 10, 11 abstract states for FourRooms (|S|=104), Corridor-9 (|S|=196), Multi-Goal (|S|=1600), Synth-Fold (|S|=5000), and Taxi (|S|=500) respectively. On 100 procedural random mazes (Table 5): 12.4±3.6 mean abstract states at 98.6±0.4% return vs. 38.6±7.4 (METIS, 98.2%). On MiniGrid FourRooms-16×16 with 64-dim VAE latent space (Table 9): 4 abstract states at 96.8% return, recovering 4/4 rooms and identifying 3/4 doorways as overlaps, vs. spectral clustering's 4 states but only 91.4% return and 1/4 doorways identified. The predicted failure on Chain-100 (no bottlenecks) is confirmed: tangle-core finds 94±3 abstract states vs. bisimulation's 8±1. The ϕ_min/β diagnostic reliably discriminates success (>4.0) from failure (≈1). Baselines include bisimulation (Ferns et al. 2004), DeepMDP (Gelada et al. ICML 2019), TOMA (Savinov et al. 2018), spectral clustering, METIS, and Louvain. The deep RL baselines are circa 2019; more recent representation learning methods (DreamerV3, TD-MPC2, SPR) are not compared. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. Translating graph tangles from structural graph theory into a sequential decision-making framework with overlapping abstract MDPs is genuinely novel — to the authors' knowledge and the reviewer's assessment, this is the first application of tangles to MDPs. The soft membership kernel formalization, the value-preservation analysis for overlapping (non-partition) abstractions, and the quantitative interface-overlap advantage theorem (Theorem 4) are new theoretical contributions. The use of the Klepper et al. tree-of-tangles algorithm is applied rather than novel; the Bellman-contraction proof technique is standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are targeted and informative: Table 4 isolates the overlap mechanism; Tables 10–11 examine runtime and graph construction. Error bars (95% bootstrap CI) appear on all results, over 10 seeds per fixed environment and 100 layouts for procedural mazes. A reproducibility table (Table 7) lists seeds, k-ranges, core counts, overlap state counts, and runtimes per environment. No public code repository exists (NeurIPS checklist item 5 is [No], citing anonymity); hardware specifications for timing comparisons are absent (checklist item 8 is [No]), which prevents verifying the 3.4s runtime claim. The action-consistency assumption in Theorem 3 — load-bearing for the optimal-control guarantee — is argued to hold informally in bottleneck domains but is not empirically verified, and no experiment characterizes degradation when it fails. All test environments are by-construction favorable to tangle topology (rooms separated by corridors); the chain MDP is the only adversarial case tested. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods-with-experiments paper; theoretical results serve to support and scope the empirical claims). - Practical: All experiments run on tabular MDPs and small procedural mazes; runtimes of 0.4–18.5s suggest a standard CPU workstation, though hardware is not specified. - Efficient: Tangle-core construction costs 3.4s on Corridor-Rooms-9 vs. 0.6s for bisimulation, 48.3s for DeepMDP, and 2.1s for TOMA; the authors acknowledge the cost is not amortized for single-query planning and depends on min-cut solver performance. - Effective: In bottlenecked tabular settings and small latent-space navigation, the method delivers substantially better compression-return tradeoffs than all six baselines, with the structural diagnostic (ϕ_min/β) providing a checkable pre-condition for applicability. - Scalable: The largest tabular evaluation is 5,000 states; the VAE experiment runs on a 16×16 MiniGrid grid; behavior at 10×–100× scale or in continuous-control environments with dense, non-bottlenecked transition graphs is untested and cannot be extrapolated from present results. The paper delivers Algorithm 1 and mathematical guarantees; no software library, trained model, or dataset is released. Strengths: - Theorem 4 precisely characterizes when overlapping membership avoids a boundary error that hard partitions cannot escape, providing a principled rather than heuristic motivation for the overlap mechanism — the ablation in Table 4 directly confirms this theoretical prediction empirically. - The paper is deliberately scoped: it explicitly predicts and empirically confirms the failure regime (chain MDPs, ϕ_min/β ≈ 1), which makes the scope boundaries trustworthy rather than ad hoc. - The compression results in bottlenecked domains are qualitatively distinct from all baselines — achieving exactly the topologically correct number of abstract states (one per room-like region) rather than approximating it — and this holds across procedural layout distributions, reducing the risk of handcrafted-grid overfitting. - Extending to learned latent representations (MiniGrid VAE, Table 9) is an important validation step that bridges the tabular theory to representation learning settings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify: (1) failure when ϕ_min/β ≈ 1 (uniform topology, no bottleneck structure), empirically confirmed on Chain-100; (2) the action-consistency assumption required for Theorem 3's optimal-control bound; (3) abstraction discovery is not amortized for single-query planning; (4) dependence on the underlying min-cut solver. These are confronted directly in Section 6 and the NeurIPS checklist, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All evaluation environments are tabular or small discrete (max 5,000 states); no continuous-control benchmark (MuJoCo locomotion, DMControl) is tested where state-abstraction methods face their hardest practical challenge — the gap between tabular and continuous-space performance is not addressable by the current experiments. - The action-consistency assumption in Theorem 3 is load-bearing for the optimal-control guarantee but verified only by informal argument; no experiment measures how often it holds, under what conditions it breaks, or what the resulting suboptimality gap is when it fails. - All test domains except Chain-100 are by construction favorable to tangle topology; intermediate-regime environments with partial bottleneck structure (some rooms separated by narrow corridors, others by open spaces) are absent, leaving the transition boundary between the success and failure regimes empirically uncharacterized. - Deep baseline comparisons are limited to DeepMDP (2019) and TOMA (2018); representation learning methods from 2022–2025 that also exploit structural or latent-space topology (e.g., successor representation methods, spectral representation learning) are not compared. - No measurement of downstream sample efficiency — the paper reports return of a policy derived from the abstract MDP but not how many environment interaction samples are required to learn the abstraction, which is the quantity practitioners care most about. - Code is not released and hardware is unspecified, making the runtime comparisons in Table 10 non-reproducible. Bottom Line: Researchers working on hierarchical RL, graph-structured MDPs, or the theory of state abstraction gain a well-characterized, theoretically grounded tool with striking compression results in bottlenecked domains; the paper should not be read as evidence of broad practical utility until it is validated on continuous-state or large-scale environments.
Search Results 2606.00427 PDF
#78 — Innovative (64)

PhAME: Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing via Latent Diffusion

Ł Janisiów, S Musiał, B Zieliński, D Rymarczyk, T Danel · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PhAME, a latent diffusion framework for molecular editing that simultaneously steers a seed compound toward a target phenotypic signature (cell morphology or transcriptomics) while preserving structural proximity to the seed, using a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme with two independent control scales. - Why it's important: Hit-to-Lead and Lead Optimization — the dominant bottleneck in drug discovery — require compounds that match a desired biological effect AND remain close to a validated scaffold; no prior method satisfies both constraints simultaneously, forcing practitioners to choose between phenotypic fidelity and structural conservation. - How it does it: A denoising network in the frozen DGVAE latent space is trained with two additive guidance terms (wc for phenotype, wa for structural proximity measured in InfoAlign embedding space), replacing the single-scale CFG interpolation with an independent-scale decomposition that eliminates the forced tradeoff. - The good: On the MOOD docking benchmark, PhAME achieves the best novel top-5% docking scores across all five protein targets and a +332% improvement in novel hit ratio over the second-best method on FA7; on Cell Painting MoA generation, it achieves 27.2% Top-1 Cluster Accuracy vs. GFlowNet's 17.0% (chance = 14.3%), with the gap holding across three structurally unrelated embedding spaces. - The missing: All phenotypic conditioning is evaluated via computational proxies (docking scores, Tanimoto/CLOOME/InfoAlign similarity) with no wet-lab validation that generated molecules actually induce the target cellular phenotype — the central biological claim is demonstrated by proxy only. - Read it or not: Researchers working on ML-guided phenotypic drug discovery should read this; the compositional guidance mechanism is clean, reusable, the code is public, and the benchmark coverage is unusually broad — though wet-lab scientists should treat the phenotypic claims as computationally validated hypotheses.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (68) is the strongest dimension and the best of the molecular papers: results reported as mean±std over 5 seeds, an unusually thorough ablation suite (compositional vs. single-scale CFG, alignment loss, curriculum, three morphology and three alignment-encoder choices), public code, and a proactively identified-and-fixed data-leakage bug in a prior benchmark — a concrete methodological positive. Completeness (65) and Novelty (62) credit broad coverage across four mechanistically distinct tasks and the clean compositional-CFG decomposition (two independent guidance scales), which is mathematically straightforward but non-obvious in application (Non-Obviousness 65). Resilience (58) and Impact (58) cap the composite: all phenotypic claims rest on computational proxies with no wet-lab validation, the docking oracle is fast/approximate QuickVina2, the MoA benchmark is thin (56 reference molecules), and it is a preprint on non-standard GH200 hardware with no released weights. Weighted: 0.20*62 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*58 ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Hit-to-Lead (H2L) and Lead Optimization (LO) in small-molecule drug discovery require iteratively reshaping a screening hit into a potent, drug-like candidate while maintaining structural similarity to the validated scaffold. The field is shifting from single-target binding assays toward phenotypic readouts — cell morphology (Cell Painting) and gene expression perturbations (L1000) — that capture whole-system biological response. Existing generative methods fail one or both of two requirements: ligand-centric generators (JTVAE, EDM) produce valid molecules without any phenotypic conditioning; phenotype-conditioned generators (GxVAE, SmilesGEN, GLDM) generate de novo from a phenotypic target but cannot anchor generation to a known hit. Standard classifier-free guidance (CFG) does not resolve this because its single guidance scale forces a tradeoff between phenotype-matching and structural conservation — increasing w to push toward the target simultaneously pulls the molecule away from the seed. Key Idea The core technical insight is to decompose the standard CFG interpolation formula into two additive guidance directions with independent scales: ε̃θ = ε_uncond + wc·Δc + wa·Δa, where Δc is the phenotypic guidance direction and Δa is the structural alignment direction. This replaces a single knob that conflates two objectives with two orthogonal controls, allowing phenotype fidelity and structural proximity to be set independently. Structural proximity is measured not in raw fingerprint space but in InfoAlign embedding space, which encodes phenotypically relevant molecular similarity via an information bottleneck over Cell Painting and L1000 — making the structural anchor phenotypically informed rather than purely topological. Partial noising of the seed molecule (at timestep t* < T) provides a third dial controlling the structural exploration radius. Methodology PhAME operates in the latent space of a frozen pretrained DGVAE (a graph-VAE that encodes molecular graphs and decodes SMILES). The pipeline has four stages: (1) Encoding — the seed molecule is encoded to z0 = E(m); a structural anchor embedding zalign is computed once per seed via InfoAlign; (2) Training data construction — molecules are partitioned by a property threshold (logP median, docking percentile, or phenotypic cosine dissimilarity), then paired by selecting the nearest Tanimoto neighbor in the opposite partition, providing supervision for threshold-crossing transformations while preserving structural similarity; (3) Denoising network training — ε_θ(zt, t, c, zalign) is trained on the standard noise-prediction objective (L_diff), augmented by an alignment loss L_align = max(0, τ − cos(ẑ0, ψ(zalign))) with τ=0.8 that penalizes denoised predictions that drift too far from the seed; both c and zalign are independently dropped with p=0.1 to enable all four conditioning combinations; total loss L = L_diff + γ·L_align; (4) Compositional guidance at inference — guided prediction uses Eq. 6 with independent wc and wa. The docking task uses a three-stage curriculum: thresholds tighten from the 50th to the 90th to the 99.8th percentile of ZINC250k docking scores, progressively sharpening the binder/non-binder gradient the model must learn. Critical design decisions: using InfoAlign (not ECFP or DGVAE latents) for zalign is justified by ablation (Figure 11); the alignment loss L_align is justified by Table 7, where its removal reduces Sim from 0.39 to 0.35 and NTS from 0.30 to 0.28; the three-stage curriculum is justified by Figure 9, where single-stage drops FA7 novel hit ratio from 5.75% to 0.05%. Key Results logP editing (MOSES benchmark): PhAME NTS = 0.30±0.01 (novelty × target-success × similarity) vs. PURE = 0.26±0.00 (next best). PhAME achieves Tgt=0.78, Sim=0.39 simultaneously, whereas DiGress achieves Tgt=1.00/Sim=0.13 and PURE achieves Tgt=0.45/Sim=0.58, confirming superior balance. Docking optimization (MOOD benchmark, 5 targets, 3,000 candidates each): PhAME best Dock score on all 5 targets (PARP1: -11.17±0.04 vs. MOOD -10.87±0.11; FA7: -9.09±0.10 vs. MOOD -8.16±0.07; 5HT1B: -11.29±0.12 vs. MOOD -11.15±0.04). Novel hit ratio: best on 4/5 (PARP1 11.04% vs. MOOD 7.02%; FA7 5.75% vs. MOOD 0.73%; JAK2 14.84% vs. MOOD 9.20%). Trails MOOD on 5HT1B (14.62% vs. 18.67%), which the paper credibly attributes to ZINC250k itself having a 27.4% hit rate on that target, making the novelty filter (Tanimoto < 0.4 to training) the binding constraint rather than generative capability. Baselines include REINVENT, FREED, FREED-QS, GDSS, MOOD — all current and strong. Transcriptomic generation (10 targets, LINCS MCF7): PhAME QED=0.71±0.03, SA=2.94±0.17, Uniqueness=1.00, Novelty=1.00 — highest drug-likeness and lowest synthetic accessibility among all baselines. Highest Tanimoto to known binders on 8 of 10 targets. Cell Painting MoA generation (7 MoA classes, 8 reference compounds each): PhAME Top-1 Cluster Accuracy 27.2%±1.8 vs. GFlowNet 17.0%±1.8 and chance 14.3%; gains hold across ECFP, CLOOME, and InfoAlign spaces (Table 11). PhAME is the only method clearing the chance baseline in all three spaces simultaneously. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The compositional CFG formulation (Eq. 6) is the primary novel contribution — extending single-scale CFG to two additive, independent guidance terms is mathematically straightforward but non-obvious in application and directly addresses a real limitation of prior phenotypic generation methods. The combination with partial noising in a graph-VAE latent and InfoAlign-based structural anchoring is novel in the molecular design context, though each constituent (DDPM/DDIM, CFG, InfoAlign, DGVAE) is established individually. The factorized guidance idea has appeared in multi-attribute single-cell settings (scPPDM, CFGen) but was not applied to paired molecular editing or phenotypic guidance. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantive and cover the key components: single-scale vs. compositional CFG, presence/absence of L_align, individual vs. joint guidance scales (Table 7), three-stage vs. single-stage curriculum (Figure 9), three morphology representation choices (Figure 10), and three structural alignment encoders (Figure 11). All results reported as mean±std over 5 seeds. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/gmum/PhAME. The authors identify and fix a data leakage issue in the transcriptomic benchmark (3% overlap between training and evaluation molecules, removed from training) that prior work overlooked — a credibility positive. Methodological concerns: (a) docking evaluation uses QuickVina2 (fast approximate docking), not higher-accuracy tools, so score magnitudes and rankings may not transfer to experimental binding affinity; (b) the MoA evaluation uses only 7 classes × 8 reference compounds = 56 total reference molecules, a thin benchmark with limited statistical power; (c) experiments were conducted on a single NVIDIA GH200 (96 GB), which is specialized hardware not available in most academic labs; (d) training pair construction via nearest Tanimoto neighbor is not ablated for sensitivity to pairing strategy. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.28226v1, May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: The model architecture (denoising network in DGVAE latent space) is compact, but experiments ran on a single NVIDIA GH200 GPU (96 GB); the paper does not report parameter count or minimum hardware requirements, so accessibility on standard academic GPUs (A100 40 GB or smaller) is unconfirmed. - Efficient: Not stated — no inference latency, throughput, or model parameter count is reported; training uses up to 4,000 epochs with 1,000 diffusion steps and batch size 512, and the three-stage docking curriculum involves sequential fine-tuning, suggesting non-trivial training cost. - Effective: Strong evidence on four benchmarks using computational proxies (docking, transcriptomic similarity, structural similarity to known binders, MoA clustering), but no wet-lab experimental validation of phenotypic activity in any assay; the core claim that generated molecules actually induce the target cellular phenotype is unverified. - Scalable: Tested only on ZINC250k-scale training (~250K molecules) and narrow benchmark tasks; no analysis of how performance degrades on novel phenotypic targets outside training distributions or at larger chemical spaces. The work delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) but not trained model weights or ready-to-use inference containers. Strengths - The compositional guidance mechanism (Eq. 6) is a clean, principled extension of CFG that directly solves the stated problem of conflicting objectives. The ablation (Table 7) provides a direct, quantitative demonstration that neither guidance alone achieves the best NTS, and that the gains from each scale are largely orthogonal — this is the strongest piece of evidence in the paper. - Empirical breadth is unusual: the same architectural framework is evaluated on logP editing, docking optimization, transcriptomic generation, and Cell Painting MoA generation — four mechanistically distinct tasks — with competitive or leading results on all four, strengthening the generality claim. - The three-stage curriculum ablation (Figure 9, FA7: 5.75% vs. 0.05%) cleanly isolates the curriculum's contribution, distinguishing it from the backbone or guidance mechanism. - The data leakage identification and fix in the transcriptomic benchmark is a concrete contribution to evaluation methodology in the field that corrects an error in prior work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state that (1) the framework is sensitive to extreme guidance scales, where overly strong structural guidance degrades chemical validity; and (2) performance depends on the availability and quality of high-dimensional paired data (Cell Painting morphologies, L1000 profiles). These are noted briefly in Appendix H and are accurately identified, though they are not deeply analyzed. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No wet-lab validation: Every phenotypic claim is evaluated by structural or computational proxies. Whether generated molecules actually perturb cells in the target direction is the central unverified hypothesis. Generating molecules structurally similar to known binders under transcriptomic guidance (Section 4.3) suggests the approach has biological plausibility, but is not evidence of phenotypic activity. - MoA benchmark is minimal: 7 MoA classes × 8 reference compounds = 56 total reference molecules drawn from a heavily curated intersection (JUMP × ChEMBL2K × Broad Repurposing Hub). This is too small to draw confident conclusions about generalization to novel MoA classes or rare targets. - QuickVina2 as docking oracle: The docking results use fast approximate docking; top-scoring molecules by QuickVina2 are known to frequently fail more rigorous docking or experimental binding assays, and the paper does not discuss this limitation. - No model size reporting: Parameter count, FLOPs per inference step, and memory at inference are not reported, making hardware planning impossible for practitioners. - Training pair construction not ablated: The nearest-Tanimoto-neighbor pairing strategy is a design choice that could favor conservative edits; its sensitivity relative to random pairing or other strategies is not studied. - GH200 hardware: The NVIDIA GH200 (Grace-Hopper superchip, 96 GB HBM3) is not standard academic hardware; reproducibility and timing for labs with A100 or H100 cards is unaddressed. Bottom Line A drug discovery ML practitioner will find in PhAME a well-ablated, open-source method with broad benchmark coverage and a reusable compositional guidance idea — the primary limitation is that the core phenotypic claim has not been tested outside computational proxies, making this a strong computational result awaiting experimental validation.
Citations 2605.28226 PDF
#79 — Innovative (64)

FERMI: Feature-Mapping for Relational Membership Inference on Tabular Diffusion Models

A Mahyar, M Shafieinejad, Y Liu, X He · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FERMI, a feature-mapping framework that distills relational membership signals from auxiliary multi-table data into the single-table denoising-loss feature space, enabling stronger membership inference attacks on tabular diffusion models at inference time using only a single candidate row. - Why it's important: Organizations increasingly release synthetic tabular data from diffusion models as a privacy substitute for real relational records, but existing MIA evaluations assume a single-table setting, systematically underestimating privacy risk for data that actually lives across dozens of linked tables. - How it does it: A neural mapper trained with MSE plus CORAL covariance-alignment loss on shadow-model data translates single-table denoising-loss fingerprints toward their merged-table counterparts; at inference the mapper recovers the relational signal without requiring the related tables. - The good: Under full-relational auxiliary data, the merged-table upper-bound attack on TabDDPM (Instacart) achieves TPR@0.01 = 0.994 — virtually every training member is identifiable at a 1% false positive rate — concretely demonstrating the severity of relational privacy leakage. - The missing: Challenge sets use an artificial 50/50 member/non-member balance, inflating TPR estimates relative to realistic deployment priors; no analysis at realistic membership rates is presented. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers auditing privacy in tabular synthetic-data pipelines or studying membership inference under adversarial auxiliary knowledge; can be skipped by those focused on differential privacy mechanisms or image diffusion.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (62) and Resilience (58) pull the composite down: no code release, no significance tests, an adversarially optimistic 50/50 balanced challenge set with no realistic-prior analysis, and — most damaging — no direct comparison to the closest prior SOTA (Ward et al. 2026), so gains are quantified only against single-table baselines. Novelty (75) is solid (NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION of the training-inference asymmetry plus a CORAL-regularized mapper), and the identity-mapper ablation cleanly rules out the capacity confound. But the unverified standing relative to SOTA and workshop-preprint status mean the quantitative claims read as lower bounds. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*45 = 64.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sensitive tabular records in healthcare, finance, and social science reside in relational databases where a single individual's data spans tens or hundreds of linked tables. Tabular diffusion models are trained on this data and their synthetic outputs are shared as a privacy-preserving release, but the credibility of this practice depends on whether the models actually protect membership. Existing MIA tools (shadow-model attacks, likelihood-ratio attacks) assume a single-table victim model and a single candidate row at inference time, ignoring that an adversary may have accumulated rich relational auxiliary data through prior breaches or institutional access. Evaluations that ignore relational structure underestimate privacy exposure. The specific gap addressed is a training-inference asymmetry: the adversary has full relational context during attack training but only a single target-table row at query time. Key Idea: The core insight is that denoising-loss fingerprints extracted from a diffusion model trained on a merged (denormalized) table carry substantially stronger membership signals than those from the single-table model, and a neural mapper trained on auxiliary paired (single-table, merged-table) feature vectors can transfer this stronger relational signal into the single-table feature space — making the richer signal available at inference even when the related tables are absent. Methodology: FERMI follows the shadow-model paradigm across three stages. (1) Paired Feature Extraction: For each shadow-model record, two loss-fingerprint vectors are extracted — Φ_single from a diffusion model trained on the target table alone, and Φ_multi from one trained on the denormalized (joined) table. Features are 500 independent noise samples × 7 probed timesteps/noise scales, yielding d = 3,500–7,000 dimensional vectors. (2) Mapping: A 3-residual-block MLP with layer normalization is trained on (Φ_single, Φ_multi) pairs using L_map = MSE + λ_coral × CORAL covariance-alignment loss (λ_coral = 0.01). CORAL is a domain-adaptation technique that matches second-order statistics between the mapped and target distributions, preventing the mapper from simply memorizing point-wise correspondences while ignoring relational geometry. (3) Membership Prediction: A 4-hidden-layer MLP classifier is trained on mapped features with binary cross-entropy. After independent pretraining of mapper and classifier, both are jointly fine-tuned with L_total = λ_map × L_map + λ_cls × L_cls (λ_map = 0.05), allowing classification gradients to refine the mapper toward membership-discriminative directions. Two adversarial knowledge settings are evaluated: PCSI (auxiliary includes target table and one parent) and FRSI (full relational neighborhood). Three diffusion architectures are attacked: TabDDPM (data-space Gaussian), TabDiff (continuous-time mixed-type), and TabSyn (VAE latent-space diffusion). The critical design decision of using CORAL alignment is motivated but not ablated independently of the MSE term. Key Results: Single-table baseline: TabDDPM is most vulnerable (AUC 0.753–0.949 across three datasets), TabDiff is intermediate and dataset-dependent (AUC 0.619–0.806), and TabSyn is near-invulnerable (AUC 0.494–0.518). Under PCSI, FERMI improves TabDDPM TPR@0.1 from 0.640 to 0.722 on California and from 0.806 to 0.892 on Instacart; TabDiff California improves from 0.512 to 0.752 (+46.9% relative). Under FRSI, the largest gain is TabDiff Berka: 0.258 → 0.394 TPR@0.1 (53% relative, as claimed in the abstract). The merged-table upper bound under FRSI reaches TabDDPM Instacart TPR@0.01 = 0.994. Black-box extension on TabDDPM/California: FERMI achieves AUC 0.725 vs. single-table 0.687, recovering ~55% of the AUC gap and ~51% of the TPR@0.1 gap to the merged-table ceiling. TabSyn resists all conditions, improving by at most AUC +0.036 over single-table baseline. Baselines are current (TF attack paradigm, shadow-model standard); evaluation sets use 100 members and 100 non-members per model across 5 independent target models, with mean ± std reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION. The training-inference asymmetry formalization — adversary has relational context at training time but not at inference — is a genuinely new problem setting in tabular MIA. The technical contribution (CORAL-regularized neural mapper for cross-table feature distillation) is novel as applied to this problem, though it assembles established components: shadow-model attack, denoising-loss fingerprinting (from TF/Wu et al. 2025), and CORAL domain adaptation. The empirical finding that latent-space diffusion (TabSyn) is structurally resistant to relational augmentation while data-space models are not is a new finding with architectural design implications. Rigor & Reproducibility: Two ablations are present. Figure 2 visualizes per-timestep loss separation across feature regimes, demonstrating the mapper's intermediate positioning. The identity-mapper ablation is methodologically clean: replacing relational supervision with self-reconstruction targets yields AUC 0.893 ± 0.021 on California/TabDDPM, statistically indistinguishable from the single-table baseline (0.896 ± 0.022), ruling out capacity as the explanation for gains. Standard deviations are reported across 5 independent target models. No significance tests are run. Code is not released (anonymous workshop submission). The balanced 50/50 challenge set is standard in this literature but represents an adversarially optimistic prior; no analysis at realistic membership rates (where members are a tiny fraction of queries) is provided. This is a preliminary workshop paper under review for FoGen @ ICML 2026 — results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL paper. - Practical: Feature extraction requires 3,500–7,000 diffusion forward passes per candidate record (500 noise samples × 7 timesteps); this runs on commodity GPU hardware but adds non-trivial overhead for large audit batches. - Efficient: Training overhead scales with the number of shadow models (10 used here) and the join cost on auxiliary data; at inference the marginal cost is a single mapper forward pass with no merged-table model or related tables needed, which is the framework's principal practical advantage. - Effective: FERMI demonstrably improves attack performance for data-space models (TabDDPM, TabDiff) but provides no meaningful gain for TabSyn (AUC improvement ≤ +0.036), limiting effectiveness to two of three tested architectures. - Scalable: Datasets reach 1.69M rows (California); shadow-model training cost on large relational schemas is not reported, and no analysis of how attack quality degrades with fewer shadow models or smaller auxiliary datasets is provided. The work delivers a methodology and experimental evaluation at paper form; no software artifact or released model is mentioned. Strengths: - The training-inference asymmetry formalization is a practically motivated and cleanly stated contribution that distinguishes this work from prior multi-table MIA efforts. - The identity-mapper ablation directly addresses the most likely confound (added capacity rather than relational signal) and is a credibly designed check. - The near-ceiling merged-table result (TPR@0.01 = 0.994 for TabDDPM/Instacart under FRSI) is a striking and concrete finding that motivates the entire framework beyond incremental improvement claims. - Coverage across 3 architectures × 3 datasets × 2 side-information levels × white-box and black-box settings is unusually broad for a workshop-length paper. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that FERMI fails for TabSyn because the mapper cannot distill signal that is absent in the relational upper bound, and they leave latent-space-specific attacks for future work. They note that join operations on large relational databases are expensive but argue auxiliary partitions remain tractable at shadow-training scale. These limitations are addressed directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 50/50 balanced challenge set is the single largest methodological concern: real deployments involve far lower membership rates, and TPR at very low FPR degrades sharply under realistic priors. The paper makes no claim about this, but practitioners may misread TPR@0.1 numbers as deployment-relevant. - The black-box extension is evaluated only on TabDDPM/California; whether FERMI's black-box gains generalize to TabDiff or to Berka/Instacart is assumed but not shown. - There is no direct experimental comparison against Ward et al. 2026 (the closest prior work using heterogeneous GNNs over full relational subgraphs) on the same datasets and metrics, making it impossible to quantify FERMI's gain over the existing state of the art rather than over single-table baselines. - The CORAL weight (λ_coral = 0.01) and other hyperparameters are fixed across all experiments; sensitivity analysis is absent, leaving it unclear how much tuning was implicitly done. - How the mapper generalizes to novel relational schemas not seen during auxiliary training is unaddressed, which matters for practical deployment across different institutions or domains. Bottom Line: This is a well-motivated and cleanly executed preliminary study that identifies a real and underaddressed privacy risk in relational tabular synthesis; privacy auditors working on such pipelines should read it, but the absence of a direct comparison to Ward et al. 2026 and the lack of realistic-prior analysis mean its quantitative claims should be treated as lower-bound estimates pending a full venue submission.
Search Results
#80 — Innovative (64)

PR2: Predictive Routing Replay for MoE-Based LLM Reinforcement Learning

D Dong, J Chen, H Jia, J Wu, H Di, J Liu, J Wu, Z Liu… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Predictive Routing Replay (PR²), a method that augments each MoE router with a lightweight per-layer linear evolution predictor to anticipate short-horizon router drift during PPO-style RL training of MoE-based LLMs, enabling cached replay indices to track the current router's evolving preferences. - Why it's important: Router drift in disaggregated MoE RL pipelines causes the same token to be assigned to different experts at rollout vs. training time, amplifying importance-ratio variance and destabilizing PPO-style optimization — a problem that worsens as rollout reuse (off-policy strength) increases. - How it does it: PR² adds a zero-initialized linear predictor W_p^(l) per MoE layer that outputs an additive logit bias at rollout time; top-k selection is performed on the biased logits, the resulting predicted indices are cached, and during training those indices are replayed deterministically while the predictor is trained by KL divergence against the current router's distribution on the same batch. - The good: On AIME24 with Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base, GRPO+PR² achieves 47.71% (off-2), 47.40% (off-4), and 40.31% (off-8) average accuracy, versus 35.73%, 28.13%, and 28.02% for routing replay — gains of 12, 19, and 12 percentage points. - The missing: Main results (Tables 1–2) report no error bars or multiple seeds, making the small gains on Moonlight-16B (1.19%) and OLMoE-1B-7B (<1%) statistically unverifiable, and the method is evaluated exclusively on math reasoning tasks, leaving generalization to other RL domains untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers working on RL post-training of MoE LLMs (Qwen3, DeepSeek, Mixtral architectures) — the method is principled, adds <1% compute overhead, integrates into VeRL/TRL/Slime without changing the PPO objective, and shows the largest gains reported to date over routing replay on competition math.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (58) is the primary drag: this is a preprint with no released code, and the headline Tables 1–2 report no error bars or seed counts, leaving the small Moonlight-16B (1.19%) and OLMoE-1B-7B (<1%) gains below the noise floor and statistically unverifiable, while the un-ablated feature sub-sampling (T_c=2K/16K) and questioned GSPO baseline configuration add further uncertainty. Completeness (60) also pulls the composite down — there is no formal limitations section, and the missing error bars, math-only scope, and ~29 GB batch-level memory cost go unacknowledged. Offsetting these, Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) are real — predicting router drift and replaying predicted top-k indices is a genuinely new MoE-RL mechanism with a rigorous TV-staleness → Pinsker → KL-objective derivation, and the large, consistent Qwen3 AIME gains are credible. Impact (55) reflects framework integration (VeRL/TRL/Slime) but no released artifact: 0.20*72 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Training MoE-based LLMs with PPO-style RL in disaggregated pipelines (separate rollout and training engines) creates a router drift problem: the same token may be routed to different experts by the old policy snapshot used for rollout and by the current training policy. Because token-level importance ratios are computed under both policies' computation graphs, router drift alters the expert activation sets underlying those ratios, amplifying their variance and destabilizing optimization. Existing routing replay fixes cached expert indices during training to enforce routing consistency, but this causes router staleness: as the training router evolves under repeated off-policy updates, the frozen cached routes drift away from the routes the current policy prefers, preventing gradients from reaching experts that would be active under the updated router. The problem worsens monotonically with off-policy reuse strength (κ = rollout batch / update batch), making it especially acute in efficient disaggregated training pipelines. Key Idea The central insight is that the tension between stable importance estimation (which needs deterministically frozen routes) and effective learning (which needs routes that track router evolution) can be resolved by predicting where the router will drift before committing to a replay route. Rather than caching the old policy's current routes (which become stale) or abandoning route freezing altogether (which destabilizes importance ratios), PR² trains a lightweight per-layer linear predictor to output an additive logit bias that shifts the top-k selection at rollout time toward experts likely to be active after the next update, then replays those predicted indices deterministically during training — preserving importance-ratio stability while reducing staleness. Methodology For each MoE layer l, PR² adds a linear predictor W_p^(l) ∈ R^(d×N) (d = hidden dimension, N = number of experts), zero-initialized so PR² starts as plain routing replay. During rollout under old policy π_θ_old, for each token t and layer l: (1) compute old router logits p_old = h_old W_old; (2) compute predictor bias b = h_old W_p; (3) form predicted distribution ρ̂ = Softmax(p_old + b); (4) select top-k indices from ρ̂ and cache them alongside route-recording features (h_old, p_old). During training, cached predicted indices are replayed without running fresh top-k selection; expert outputs and policy gradients are computed under current parameters θ, but only for the cached expert set. The predictor is updated by the predictive KL loss L_PR² = Σ_l E_t[D_KL(⟨ρ^(l)_t⟩ ‖ ρ̂^(l)_t)], where the current router's distribution ρ^(l)_t is the stop-gradient teacher and ρ̂^(l)_t is the predictor's output given cached features; the loss is skipped on the first inner mini-step (when the current router has not yet diverged from the snapshot). A dedicated learning-rate multiplier α (10^2–10^4 across models) allows fast predictor adaptation without disturbing the base RL objective. The theoretical chain is: staleness bound (TV distance controls gradient deviation) → Pinsker's inequality → KL loss controls the TV distance → minimizing L_PR² directly bounds route-induced gradient deviation. Lemma B.2 additionally shows that if KL(p ‖ p̂) < Δ_k(p)²/2 (where Δ_k is the top-k margin), the predicted top-k set exactly matches the current router's top-k. Three critical design decisions: zero-initialization (PR² degrades gracefully to routing replay at the start); feature sub-sampling (only T_c tokens cached per response to bound memory under long contexts — 2K of 16K for Qwen3, uniform sub-sampling, not ablated); loss-skip on mini-step 1 (ensures nontrivial target before predictor training begins, well-justified). Key Results Primary model: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base trained on DAPO-17K, evaluated on AIME24, AIME25, AMC23, HMMT25 via GRPO+PR² vs. GRPO, GSPO, and routing replay. Average accuracy: PR² 46.80% / 46.24% / 41.90% at off-2/4/8, vs. routing replay 37.55% / 33.24% / 33.74% and GSPO 35.76% / 36.51% / 33.16% — PR² gains of 9.25%, 13.00%, 8.16% over routing replay and 11.04%, 9.73%, 8.74% over GSPO. AIME24 specifically: PR² 47.71% (off-2) vs. routing replay 35.73% and GSPO 30.42%. Cross-model on Moonlight-16B-A3B: PR² best in all three off-policy settings, gains of 1.19%, 1.19%, 3.44% over routing replay on off-2/4/8. On OLMoE-1B-7B: PR² best in all settings with gains under 1 percentage point. Routing analysis: PR² increases zero-deviation rate from 76.9% (GRPO) and 88.8% (routing replay) to 91.5–92.2%, reduces ≥2-slot expert mismatch from 3.4% to 0.1%. Compute overhead: 0.69% of FFN FLOPs per token for Qwen3 (25.2M FLOPs/token from predictor projection), with a 453 MB feature cache and 25.2 MB index cache per response. Baselines are current (2024–2025 vintage: DAPO/GRPO, routing replay, GSPO). Scale tested: up to 30B total / 3B active parameters, 32 H200 GPUs, 72 hours per run. Counter-intuitive finding: vanilla GRPO (30.60%) outperforms routing replay (28.13%) at off-4, suggesting frozen routes can actively harm optimization at higher off-policy strength — the regime where PR² provides its largest gain. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The predictive routing mechanism for MoE RL training is genuinely novel: prior work either froze routes (routing replay), redefined importance ratios at the sequence level (GSPO), or modified clipping behavior — none attempted to predict where the router would drift and cache those predicted indices. The theoretical connection from staleness-controlled gradient deviation to a KL training objective is non-trivial. Individual components (KL distillation, top-k routing, off-policy PPO) are standard; the novelty lies in using rollout-time features of the old snapshot to predict training-time routing preferences and feeding that prediction back into the replay cache. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover predictive loss form (standard KL vs. delta-matching — both comparable, KL slightly better), predictor learning-rate multiplier (10^4 vs. 10^6 — both stable), and layer-wise top-k accuracy across mini-steps. PR³ results (Table 4) are averaged over 3 seeds, but the main PR² results in Tables 1 and 2 report no error bars and no seed count — a significant gap for competition-math accuracy where high variance is expected (Avg@32 and Avg@16 help but do not characterize run-to-run variability). No code or model weights are released (preprint, no GitHub mentioned). The feature sub-sampling ratio (T_c = 2K / 16K = 12.5% for Qwen3) is not ablated. Hyperparameter sensitivity of the predictor learning-rate multiplier is partially addressed but not swept broadly. The paper is a preprint; results carry commensurate uncertainty. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs for the 30B model and 8 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs for the 1B model; hardware is commercially available, and the method integrates into VeRL, Slime, and TRL without modifying the PPO objective. - Efficient: Adds 0.19–0.69% of FFN FLOPs per token during route recording; feature cache contributes up to 453 MB per response for Qwen3 at T_c = 2K, which at batch size 64 implies ~29 GB of additional memory per rollout — a non-trivial overhead under memory-constrained setups, though the index cache is small (25.2 MB per response). - Effective: Strong evidence that PR² reduces routing mismatch (zero-deviation rate raised to 92.2%), stabilizes training dynamics (lower and smoother clip rates, no entropy collapse), and improves downstream accuracy on hard math benchmarks consistently across three MoE architectures and three off-policy strengths. - Scalable: Tested only up to Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base (30B parameters, 3B active); behavior at larger MoE models (e.g., DeepSeek-V3 scale) or off-policy strengths beyond off-8 is not evaluated and not claimed. The paper delivers an implementation integrated into existing frameworks but no released software artifact. Strengths - The theoretical chain from TV-staleness bound → Pinsker inequality → KL training objective is rigorous and provides a principled motivation for exactly the proposed loss function, rather than a post-hoc justification. - Consistent gains across three architecturally distinct MoE backbones (Qwen3-30B-A3B, Moonlight-16B-A3B, OLMoE-1B-7B) and three off-policy strengths suggest the method is not overfit to one setting. - The predictor adds only a single linear layer per MoE router (d×N parameters, <1% FFN FLOPs), making the cost/benefit ratio highly favorable — 9–13 point gains on Qwen3 AIME24 from a module smaller than a single attention head. - The routing deviation distribution analysis (Figure 4b) and layer-wise top-k accuracy plots (Figure 6) characterize the mechanism at the level of individual expert slots and mini-steps, providing mechanistic evidence beyond aggregate accuracy. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a formal limitations section. They note in passing that feature sub-sampling (T_c = 2K out of 16K) is used for memory efficiency, that the predictor targets short-horizon evolution rather than long-range forecasting, and that OLMoE-1B-7B gains are smaller without explanation. No acknowledgment of the missing error bars on main results, the restriction to math tasks, or the potential memory cost at large batch sizes. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Tables 1 and 2 report no error bars and no seed count for PR²'s main results; gains on Moonlight (1.19%) and OLMoE (<1%) are below the noise floor typical of these evaluations without statistical characterization. - Feature sub-sampling (T_c = 2K / 16K for Qwen3) is not ablated: the predictor trained on only 12.5% of positions could miss routing behaviors in the later context of long reasoning chains, and it is unknown whether T_c = 16K would meaningfully improve results or be prohibitively expensive. - All evaluations are on mathematical competition benchmarks; whether router drift is equally problematic for code generation, instruction following, or agentic RL — and whether PR² helps there — is unknown. - The comparison with GSPO uses GSPO's very tight clipping (ε_low = 3×10^-4), which is known to be sensitive to implementation details; it is not verified that GSPO is at its optimal configuration. - No wall-clock or throughput comparison is reported: at batch size 64 and 453 MB feature cache per response, the Qwen3 setting requires ~29 GB of additional GPU memory per step, which could be a practical blocker that the FLOPs ratio alone does not capture. Bottom Line PR² is a practically valuable, principled contribution to MoE RL training: the method is lightweight, integrates into existing frameworks, and delivers the largest reported accuracy gains over routing replay on hard math benchmarks — practitioners training MoE LLMs with PPO-style RL should read this, with the caveat that the main results lack error bars and the scope is currently limited to math reasoning.
Recommended 2606.00395 PDF
#81 — Innovative (64)

SAGE: A Dataflow-Native Framework for Modular, Controllable, and Transparent LLM -Augmented Reasoning

J Liu, P Liu, R Zhang, S Zhang, Y Chen, Z Wang… · home.shuhao.sage.org.ai · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SAGE (Streaming-Augmented Generative Execution), a full-stack system that compiles LLM inference pipelines — coupling retrieval, memory, refinement, and generation — into distributed physical execution graphs with bounded-queue backpressure and operator-level observability. - Why it's important: Production LLM pipelines built by stitching services over RPC boundaries make cross-stage queueing, interference attribution, and tail-latency control intractable; this prevents systematic SLO enforcement and automated diagnosis in multi-stage deployments. - How it does it: Each pipeline stage declares an explicit operator contract (resource requirements, state semantics, I/O behavior) that the compiler uses to lower a logical DAG into a physical ExecutionGraph with materialized bounded queues, replica placements, and routing policies, enabling pipeline-level scheduling and observability. - The good: On a 16-node cluster with a real RAG workload, SAGE achieves 11.4× throughput scaling at near-linear efficiency and reduces multi-tenant P99 latency by 57% via simple admission control, with what-if predictions that match measured outcomes within 4.6% MAE. - The missing: The evaluation uses a small model (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, 3B parameters) on a modest 16-node commodity CPU cluster with 1 GbE networking; no direct head-to-head comparison is made against existing orchestration frameworks such as Ray Serve, LangGraph, or Haystack under equivalent workloads. - Read it or not: Worth reading for MLSystems researchers and engineers building production RAG or agentic pipelines who want an existence proof that dataflow compilation applied to LLM pipelines yields measurable, reproducible SLO control; practitioners using frameworks like LangChain at scale will find the architecture and observability design directly actionable.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) carries the most weight (0.30) and is the strongest dimension — an open-source dataflow framework with genuinely useful operational capabilities (what-if prediction within 4.6% MAE, automated attribution, explainable scheduling). Rigor (62) is the main drag: no quantitative comparison against any peer system (Ray Serve, LangGraph, Haystack), all single-run point estimates with no seeds, an 8-participant user study, and synthetic bottleneck injection rather than organic failures. Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (55) are modest — pipeline-as-compilation-target and bounded-queue backpressure are well-established streaming-systems ideas specialized to LLM serving; the theorems are construction arguments and standard M/M/1 results. Completeness (65) and Resilience (58) are limited by the 3B-model / 16-node / no-peer-baseline evaluation, leaving the central scalability and competitiveness claims unanswered. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*72 ≈ 64.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM applications increasingly run as multi-stage inference pipelines where a single request threads through embedding, vector retrieval, context refinement under token budgets, stateful session memory, and autoregressive generation. These stages span heterogeneous CPU and GPU resources and must jointly satisfy strict tail-latency SLOs. The dominant construction method — wiring stages as independent RPC-connected services — leaves the pipeline implicit: no single component can observe cross-stage queueing or contention, tail-latency failures require manual correlation of distributed traces, and policy changes (parallelism, admission, scheduling) require redeployment. LLM serving engines (vLLM, Orca, Sarathi-Serve) optimize a single generation backend. Orchestration frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack) ease construction but delegate all performance-critical concerns to external services. The gap is an end-to-end execution substrate that treats the multi-stage pipeline as the optimization and scheduling unit. Key Idea The central insight is to treat the inference pipeline as a first-class compilation target: each stage declares an operator contract capturing resource requirements, state semantics, and I/O behavior, and a compiler transforms the logical DAG into a concrete physical ExecutionGraph with materialized replica-to-replica edges, per-replica bounded input queues, and explicit routing metadata. Because the execution plan is a concrete, inspectable artifact, scheduling policies can act on replicas rather than opaque services, enabling what-if analysis, automated bottleneck attribution, and explainable scheduling decisions — capabilities that are architecturally impossible when the pipeline remains implicit across RPC boundaries. Methodology The system follows a five-layer architecture (L1 foundation, L2 platform services, L3 core dataflow runtime, L4 performance-critical middleware operators, L5 developer-facing interfaces) with strict downward-only dependencies. The logical pipeline is modeled as a DAG G_L = (V_L, E_L), where each operator v declares a contract C(v) = (R(v), S(v), I(v)) covering CPU/GPU affinity and memory budget, stateless vs. stateful semantics (with checkpointable vs. restartable modes), and admission/batching/streaming I/O behavior. The compiler performs ExecutionGraph lowering by replicating operators at declared parallelism, materializing logical edges as per-replica bounded input channels (QueueDescriptor), and assigning routing policies (round-robin, broadcast, KeyHash for stateful operators). Theorem 3.1 (Semantic Preservation) asserts that the lowering preserves item order for deterministic operators and state consistency for stateful operators regardless of parallelism or placement — this follows directly from the KeyHash routing invariant and blocking backpressure semantics, and the proof is essentially a construction argument rather than a non-trivial formal result. At runtime, each replica runs a blocking worker loop (Algorithm 1); when a downstream queue saturates, upstream writes block, creating explicit backpressure. Three pluggable schedulers are evaluated: FIFO (throughput-oriented), LoadAware-Spread (queue-depth-balancing), and Priority (preferential service). Theorem H.1 provides M/M/1-based tail-latency bounds under Poisson arrivals and exponential service times — standard queueing results applied to the SAGE model, not new mathematics. The what-if analysis engine replays execution traces under counterfactual policy modifications (parallelism scaling, policy swap, routing change) to predict throughput and P99 latency before deployment. A performance attribution pipeline decomposes end-to-end latency into per-operator queue-wait, service, and blocking components via critical-path traversal. Critical design decisions: (1) operator contracts as compiler-visible metadata rather than runtime hints — well-justified as it enables static reasoning about placement; (2) bounded blocking queues rather than unbounded buffers or admission-drop — justified by the queueing stability analysis and experimentally validated; (3) model inference integrated as a configurable OpenAI-compatible endpoint stage rather than embedded in the runtime — justified to maintain independence between orchestration and model-serving scaling. Key Results Experiments run on 16 CPU nodes (8 cores, 32 GB RAM, 1 GbE) plus one NVIDIA A6000 48 GB GPU server running vLLM with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and CPU embedding (BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5). Three workloads: COMPUTE (synthetic CPU-busy loop), RAG (embedding → FAISS IVF-Flat top-5 retrieval over 1M vectors → LLM generation), and MIXED (50/50 interleave). RAG achieves 11.4× throughput at 16 nodes (71% efficiency), approaching linear scaling. COMPUTE saturates at 3.8× because scheduling overhead dominates task duration for sub-second tasks. Scheduling policy comparison on MIXED: FIFO achieves 18.5 tasks/s at 52% load balance; LoadAware-Spread achieves 16.4 tasks/s at 98% balance and the lowest P99 (6.5 s vs. 7.0 s for FIFO); Priority achieves 19.2 tasks/s but P99 degrades to 12.0 s due to starvation — a known priority-inversion effect. Admission control: staggered pipeline starts reduce P99 by 57% (77 s → 33 s) at the cost of 30% throughput reduction. Per-job efficiency degrades from 100% to 71% at 8 concurrent pipelines. What-if prediction accuracy: 1.8–7.9% error on throughput and P99, 4.6% MAE. Automated attribution: 87.5% precision on 12 production RAG pipelines (1 false positive in 8 flagged operators); controlled bottleneck injection shows +73% throughput and −54% P99 improvements following recommendations; recommendation-vs-actual improvement R² = 0.84. Explainable scheduling: 6 min vs. 47 min diagnosis time vs. manual logs; 100% root cause accuracy vs. 62.5% for manual; replay error < 2%. No direct quantitative comparison against Ray Serve, LangGraph, DSPy, or other orchestration systems is presented. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The pipeline-as-compilation-target abstraction applied specifically to LLM inference pipelines is genuinely novel in this domain; the core ideas of declarative DAG compilation, bounded-queue backpressure, and operator contracts are well-established in streaming systems (Flink, Naiad, Differential Dataflow). The novelty lies in specializing this paradigm to the LLM serving context — heterogeneous CPU/GPU stages, token-streaming I/O semantics, SLO-aware scheduling, and LLM-specific operator contracts — and demonstrating measurable systems benefits in that specific setting. The queueing-theoretic analysis and what-if machinery are applications of known techniques, not new theory. Rigor & Reproducibility Abstions are present: scheduling policy comparisons fix the ExecutionGraph and vary only the scheduling logic, correctly isolating the variable of interest. Concurrency scaling is swept from 1 to 32. The what-if and attribution evaluations use controlled bottleneck injection and production trace replay. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported — all numbers appear to be point estimates from single runs. The user study for explainability has only 8 participants, which is insufficient to draw statistically reliable conclusions. The bottleneck injection experiments (Table 5) use synthetic, deliberately constructed failures; performance on organically occurring failures in real production pipelines is not established. The 87.5% attribution precision comes from 12 pipelines with 8 flagged operators — a very small sample. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/CGCL-codes/SAGE. Experiment configurations are stored as versioned YAML files. The paper is peer-reviewed at ICML 2026. One methodological concern: the what-if simulation assumes service time stability across policy changes and Poisson arrivals, which the paper itself acknowledges underestimates LLM tail behavior by 6–8%; this is disclosed but not fully addressed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built framework evaluated by deployment. - Practical: SAGE runs on commodity CPU nodes plus one consumer/prosumer GPU server (A6000), and integrates with vLLM via OpenAI-compatible endpoints — no specialized hardware is required beyond what production LLM serving already demands. - Efficient: Scheduling overhead is 1 ms median, representing <0.5% of typical task latency for RAG workloads (>100 ms tasks); the Ray-actor-based dispatcher degrades superlinearly above 64 concurrent tasks, which the paper acknowledges as a known limitation requiring future work. - Effective: The system demonstrably controls P99 latency (57% reduction) and achieves measurable throughput scaling on a real end-to-end RAG pipeline; however, all tests use a 3B-parameter model on a 16-node cluster, and whether the orchestration overhead remains negligible relative to inference cost for 70B+ models on GPU-heavy clusters is not tested. - Scalable: Near-linear scaling to 16 nodes is shown for RAG; the paper acknowledges centralized service endpoint bottleneck (shared LLM and vector DB) as the dominant performance limiter under high concurrency, and does not test beyond 16 nodes or the stated 1–8 concurrent pipeline regime. The system delivers a software artifact (open-source Python framework at github.com/CGCL-codes/SAGE) with C++ backends for performance-critical operators. Strengths - The pipeline-as-compilation-target abstraction is a clean and well-motivated architectural principle: making operator contracts compiler-visible genuinely enables whole-pipeline reasoning that is architecturally impossible with ad-hoc RPC composition, and the paper articulates this distinction precisely. - The what-if analysis capability — predicting performance of policy changes within 4.6% MAE from execution traces, validated against live deployments — is a practical and under-explored capability for LLM pipeline operators. - The explainable scheduling comparison against manual log analysis and commercial APM tools (Table 8: 6 min vs. 47 min, 100% vs. 62.5% root cause accuracy) provides concrete evidence that structured decision traces have practical debugging value. - The policy-swap experiment (Table 1) correctly isolates scheduling policy as the independent variable by holding the compiled ExecutionGraph constant, enabling clean attribution of performance differences to the scheduling logic. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations: (1) coordination-bound performance for very fine-grained operators (<10 ms tasks), where dispatch overhead exceeds task duration — they recommend >100 ms task granularity; (2) reliance on user-specified operator contracts, where inaccurate resource hints degrade placement quality; (3) centralized service endpoint bottleneck (shared LLM server, shared vector DB) as the dominant limiter under high concurrent pipeline count. These are stated directly in §5.9 and Appendix F.3, which is credible. The authors do not acknowledge the absence of comparison against peer systems, the small model size in evaluation, or the limited generalizability of results from synthetic bottleneck injection experiments. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No direct quantitative comparison against peer orchestration or serving systems (Ray Serve, LangGraph, DSPy compiled pipelines, Haystack) under identical workloads. The paper's claim to uniqueness is architectural, not benchmarked against alternatives, so the performance advantage over existing stacks is unquantified. - All generation experiments use Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, which fits on a single A6000 without tensor parallelism. For larger models requiring multi-GPU serving (70B+), the relationship between SAGE's orchestration overhead and inference cost may differ substantially, and pipeline bottlenecks would shift. - The 1 GbE network is below what production GPU clusters typically deploy (InfiniBand or 100 GbE). The near-linear scaling claim may not hold under high-bandwidth embedding or KV-cache transfer scenarios at larger scale. - The what-if simulation assumes service time independence from scheduling policy, which is violated for I/O-bound operators with cache effects — acknowledged in a footnote but the boundary conditions are not quantified. - Attribution precision (87.5%) is measured on only 12 pipelines with 8 flagged operators — insufficient to characterize false-positive rate reliably under diverse real-world workloads. - The user study (8 participants) supporting explainability claims is too small to generalize; the comparison condition (raw log analysis) is not a strong baseline — a structured tracing tool like Jaeger or OpenTelemetry with appropriate dashboards is the missing comparison. Bottom Line SAGE makes a credible and useful systems contribution — applying dataflow compilation to LLM inference pipelines is the right architectural direction, the implementation is open-sourced, and the observability features (what-if analysis, automated attribution, explainable scheduling) address real operational pain points — but the evaluation scale (3B-parameter model, 16-node commodity cluster, no peer-system comparison) leaves the most important scalability and competitiveness questions unanswered.
Search Results
#82 — Innovative (64)

Frontier LLM-based agents can overcome the ontology curation bottleneck for natural phenotypes

JP Balhoff, H Lapp · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that five frontier LLM agents (Claude Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7, Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-mini), operating in a structured agentic workspace, perform EQ (Entity–Quality) phenotype ontology annotation within the range of trained human inter-curator variability on the Dahdul et al. 2018 Gold Standard benchmark. - Why it's important: Ontology-based phenotype annotation of comparative morphology literature is a recognized bottleneck gating large-scale evolutionary and genomic data integration, and the prior automated baseline (Semantic CharaParser) was far below human quality — leaving the task dependent on scarce expert biocurators. - How it does it: Each LLM is placed in a self-contained workspace containing the source publication PDF, the same human annotation guide, four OBO ontology files, and a Scala validator; a skill definition encodes the six-step EQ procedure, and the agent iterates until the validator reports a clean output. - The good: All five agents fell within the human inter-curator variability band on all four semantic similarity metrics (SimJ, NIC, Partial Precision, Partial Recall), with SimJ roughly 2× Semantic CharaParser's best variant. - The missing: The 2018 Gold Standard and the ontologies have been publicly available since publication and may appear in LLM training data, making benchmark leakage impossible to rule out as a confound. - Read it or not: Worth reading for biocuration researchers and anyone designing agentic workflows for knowledge engineering tasks; less relevant for ML researchers seeking algorithmic contributions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The headline is a strong NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (Novelty 64): frontier LLM agents, with no task-specific training, reach trained-human inter-curator variability on the formally-established 2018 Dahdul Gold Standard — a qualitative jump over the prior Semantic CharaParser baseline (~2× SimJ). Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) are buttressed by unusual honesty: the authors restrict comparison to characters 51–203 to avoid refinement-set overfitting, do NOT claim to beat the best human curator, and flag contamination risk. Rigor (52) is the limiting dimension — each [model,paper] pair was run exactly once (agent variance uncharacterized), only three human curators set a coarse band, and benchmark-leakage is acknowledged but not investigated. Non-Obviousness (60); Impact (58, reusable GitHub scaffold but no cost-per-annotation). Composite: 0.20*64 + 0.25*52 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*58 = 59.9, lifted toward 64 for resembling the user's positively-rated rigorous-arXiv calibration set in scope and honesty.
empirical Novelty 64 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Comparative morphology research — including morphological phylogenetics — depends on linking natural-language phenotype descriptions to formal ontology terms in the Entity–Quality (EQ) framework, enabling semantic integration across studies and with model organism databases. Producing these annotations requires biocurators with deep expertise in both comparative anatomy and the relevant ontologies (UBERON, PATO, BSPO, GO). The 2018 Dahdul et al. benchmark showed that the best available NLP tool (Semantic CharaParser) scored significantly below trained human curators on four semantic similarity metrics, establishing a clear automation gap. This gap has left structured re-annotation of the existing comparative morphology literature — thousands of phylogenetic matrices — impractical at scale. Key Idea: The core insight is that a frontier LLM, given the same informational affordances as a human curator (source paper, annotation guide, ontology files, a validation script), can autonomously compose those resources through multi-step agentic behavior — reading, searching, constructing OWL Manchester syntax expressions, and self-correcting via validator feedback — to produce annotations competitive with trained human experts, without any task-specific fine-tuning or specialized NLP pipeline. Methodology: The workspace (Fig. 1) contains: source PDFs, per-character TSV inputs, four OBO ontology files, a human annotation guide translated to OWL Manchester syntax, and a Scala validator script. A skill definition (SKILL.md in Agent Skills format) encodes six procedural steps: (1) read the relevant publication section, (2) decompose character/state combinations into EQ rows, (3) search ontologies for Entity, Quality, and Related-Entity terms, (4) construct post-composed OWL expressions when no atomic term suffices, (5) write 10-column TSV rows pairing opaque identifiers with labels, and (6) run the validator and iterate until clean. The validator catches identifier hallucinations, label mismatches, obsolete classes, unbalanced parentheses, and structural errors. Five models were tested: Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7, Sonnet 4.6 (via Claude Code CLI) and GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-mini (via Codex CLI). Two annotation rounds were run: Round 1 (R1) was exploratory using Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 to identify workspace weaknesses; Round 2 (R2) refined the skill by mandating source-paper reading, expanding term-search guidance, integrating the validator as a mandatory final step, and prohibiting sub-agent spawning. Because the first 50 characters of the 203-character Gold Standard were used during workspace refinement, all cross-model comparisons are restricted to characters 51–203 (344 states). Metrics — SimJ, NIC, Partial Precision, and Partial Recall — are computed by the ELK OWL reasoner over a merged ontology, following the exact protocol of Dahdul et al. 2018. Critical design decisions: (a) mandating source-paper reading before annotation (well-justified, improved R1→R2 quality); (b) a validation loop as a hallucination guard (well-justified, directly reduces identifier fabrication); (c) restricting evaluation to characters 51–203 to avoid benchmark overfitting on the refinement set (methodologically sound). Key Results: All five R2 agents fell within the 95% CI ± 1 SD human inter-curator band on nearly every metric. The three strongest agents (Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7, Sonnet 4.6) exceeded two of three human curators (AD, NI) on SimJ, PP, and PR, and matched or exceeded them on NIC. The best human curator (WD — also the most deeply trained) remained the top performer overall by a clear margin on SimJ and PP; agents were closer behind on NIC and PR. All five agents outperformed Semantic CharaParser on all four metrics; SimJ was approximately 2× higher. Token output ranged from ~444K tokens total (GPT-5.4) to ~1.95M tokens (Opus 4.7) summed across 7 publications (7 × 29 = 203 characters total). Per-paper effort was strongly correlated across models, suggesting some publications are intrinsically harder. Locally-hosted open-weight models (qwen3-coder:30b, qwen3-coder-next on MacBook Pro M2 Max / 64 GB) failed to complete annotation runs — hallucinating ontology identifiers, abandoning the validator loop, or terminating early — and were excluded from quantitative comparison. No specific numerical scores for individual agents are reported in the text; results are presented graphically in Figure 2. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The agentic workspace design is a straightforward assembly of existing components (LLM CLI, OBO files, annotation guide, Scala validator); what is genuinely novel is the empirical demonstration that frontier general-purpose LLM agents, with no task-specific training, can match human expert biocurators on a formally defined, independently established benchmark — a qualitative shift from the prior NLP baseline. Rigor & Reproducibility: Each [model, paper] pair was run exactly once — no repeated runs, so inter-run variance for agents is unknown and results could reflect a lucky or unlucky sample. The paper correctly restricts cross-model comparison to characters 51–203 to account for workspace refinement, but even R2-only models benefited from that refinement, introducing mild benchmark overfitting. The comparison baseline is thin: only three human curators, making the inter-curator variability band a relatively coarse criterion. Training data contamination is explicitly acknowledged but not investigated; a held-out annotation set never published online would have been more probative. No ablation studies are reported to show which workspace components (validator, source-paper mandate, post-composition guidance) individually contribute to quality. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/phenoscape/goldstandard; the original Gold Standard is archived at https://zenodo.org/records/1345307. This is a preprint-style venue paper (CEUR proceedings, CC BY 4.0); not peer-reviewed at the level of a journal submission. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs via commercial API (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) on standard hardware; no specialized compute required. - Efficient: Token costs are substantial — up to ~1.95M tokens for Opus 4.7 across 7 publications (29 chars each), implying significant API cost per paper at production scale; cost-per-annotation is not calculated in the paper. - Effective: Agents match human inter-curator variability on semantic similarity metrics, but whether agent annotations produce correct downstream scientific inferences (e.g., semantic integration with model organism phenotypes) is not tested. - Scalable: Tested only on 203 characters from 7 publications; authors claim the results make large-scale re-annotation feasible but provide no evidence at 10× or 100× corpus size, and token costs at scale are a real constraint. The work delivers a reusable workspace scaffold (code on GitHub) rather than a trained model or packaged tool. Strengths: - Direct comparability: reuses the identical Gold Standard, metrics, annotation guide, and ontologies from Dahdul et al. 2018, enabling a clean 8-year longitudinal comparison with no methodological ambiguity. - Hallucination mitigation via validator: the Scala validation loop that catches identifier fabrication and label mismatches is a practical and transferable design pattern for agentic knowledge engineering tasks. - Multi-vendor, multi-tier model comparison provides meaningful evidence that the result is not specific to one model or provider. - Honest framing: the authors do not claim agents surpass the best human curator, correctly note contamination risk, and describe specific workspace limitations for production deployment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The Gold Standard and ontologies have been publicly available since 2018 and may appear in LLM training data. The best human curator (WD) still outperforms all agents. Locally-hosted open-weight models failed the task. Production deployment would require a database-backed ontology API, named post-composition skill templates, and a logical-reasoning validation pass using an OWL reasoner. A fair comparison of hosted vs. local open-weight LLMs was out of scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Single-run-per-session design means agent score variance is uncharacterized — results could be atypically good or bad for any given model.
