TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DRL-ITS, an LLM-augmented Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm that jointly selects serial/parallel inference modes and analytically allocates CPU/GPU compute and bandwidth for collaborative visual generative AI inference across edge-cloud networks. - Why it's important: Serving visual GenAI (e.g., diffusion model image generation) from resource-constrained edge servers requires coordinating specialized small models at the edge with large generic models in the cloud under stochastic task arrivals—static and pure-DRL approaches fail to maintain queue stability and achieve low latency simultaneously. - How it does it: Lyapunov optimization converts the long-term stochastic MINLP into per-slot sub-problems; continuous resource variables (transmission rates, CPU/GPU frequencies) are solved via closed-form KKT conditions proportional to the square root of weighted task workloads, leaving only binary inference-mode and aggregation-location decisions for the LLM-augmented TD3 agent. - The good: The [O(1/V), O(V)] latency-queue stability tradeoff is formally proven with full appendix proofs and empirically confirmed; DRL-ITS converges within ~150 episodes vs. 300+ for DDPG/DDQN, reducing steady-state inference delay to approximately 140s and queue backlog to approximately 600 Mbit. - The missing: No ablation compares LLM-augmented TD3 against plain TD3, so the claimed benefit of the LLM module specifically cannot be attributed—gains may come entirely from the KKT action-space reduction rather than LLM semantic guidance. - Read it or not: Worth reading for edge-cloud scheduling researchers who want a clean Lyapunov + KKT decomposition for a serial/parallel GenAI inference problem; researchers expecting empirical insights about real diffusion model inference will find the workload abstraction too removed from actual systems.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (the summary's tag; evaluation is simulation-based despite heavy theory). Rigor (43) and Resilience (40) pull the composite down: there is no plain-TD3 ablation to isolate the LLM contribution — the paper's central framing — plus no error bars or seeds, no released code, weak baselines (DDPG/DDQN rather than SAC/PPO), and a workload model (EfficientNet-B7 FLOPs) that does not match diffusion inference, making the 'visual GenAI' claim largely cosmetic. Novelty (57) is a new problem instantiation built from well-known machinery (Lyapunov + KKT + TD3); Non-Obviousness (55) credits the action-space-reduction trick but the components are standard. Impact (30) reflects no released artifact and simulation-only, toy-scale (N=4, ≤60 tasks) evaluation. Weighted composite: 0.20*57 + 0.25*43 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*30 = 46.
empirical Novelty 57 Rigor 43 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Operators of mobile edge-cloud networks face the challenge of serving user requests for visual generative AI inference under stochastic arrival patterns and heterogeneous resources. Large generic models (e.g., Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3) hosted on resource-rich cloud servers generalize broadly but suffer high round-trip latency. Specialized small models at the edge are faster and domain-adapted but lack generalization. Prior collaborative inference work treats edge-cloud handoff as a static offloading decision and ignores the compound decision space of: (1) whether to run edge and cloud models in serial (sequential refinement) or parallel (independent generation then aggregation), (2) where to aggregate results (edge or cloud), and (3) how to allocate CPU, GPU, and bandwidth per task. Without a framework that jointly addresses these decisions while keeping task queues bounded over time, the system either congests or leaves resources underutilized. Key Idea: The core trick is to decouple the intractable joint MINLP into two independent layers by exploiting the separable structure of the Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty objective: for any fixed binary inference mode, the continuous resource allocation sub-problems (transmission rates, CPU/GPU frequencies) become convex and admit closed-form KKT solutions where capacity is allocated proportional to the square root of each task's weighted workload. This collapses the action space that the DRL agent must explore to only the binary variables {I_{n,k}, O_{n,k}}, which is then tractable for TD3. The LLM contributes a semantic embedding of task prompts concatenated to the raw state vector, intended to provide prior knowledge that guides exploration. Methodology: The system model considers N edge servers (N=4 in experiments) and one central cloud operating in discrete time slots. Image data size is modeled as ς_{n,k}(t) = β·g²_{n,k}(t) bits (bit-depth times pixel count squared). Computation requirement is C_{n,k}(t) = U₀·ς_{n,k}(t) FLOPs where U₀ = 6.4×10³ FLOPs/bit (calibrated from EfficientNet-B7). Edge and cloud servers each split workload between CPU and GPU with a load-balance constraint, yielding computation time D_{n,k} = C_{n,k}/(f_cpu + f_gpu). Parallel inference delay is max(D_edge, D_cloud) plus transmission and lightweight aggregation (cost ratio χ_agg ∈ [0.01, 0.05]); serial delay is D_edge + T_upload + D_cloud + T_download. The long-term problem P1 minimizes time-average expected latency subject to strong queue stability (lim sup (1/T)·E[ΣQ_n + Q_c] < ∞). The Lyapunov function L = ½ΣQ²_n + ½Q²_c and drift-plus-penalty GK(t) = ΔL + V·E[ΣM_{n,k}] yield Lemma 1 (upper bound B) and Theorems 1–2 ([O(1/V), O(V)] bounds). Per-slot problem P2 decomposes into four KKT sub-problems whose solutions are: r*_{n,k,1}(t) = R_{n,1}·√(x_{n,k}·ς_{n,k}) / Σ√(x_{n,j}·ς_{n,j}) (tight capacity allocation), and analogously for cloud-to-edge rates and CPU/GPU frequencies. The residual binary problem P2.5 is solved by LLM-enhanced TD3 with Gumbel-Softmax output for discrete actions, twin critics, delayed actor update (every ξ=3 steps), and orthogonal initialization. Training runs 500 episodes × 300 time slots; replay buffer capacity 10,000; batch size 256; discount γ=0.99; soft update ω=0.995. Critical design decisions: (1) KKT decomposition—well-justified mathematically and reduces DRL action space substantially; (2) Gumbel-Softmax for discrete TD3 actions—standard technique, not novel; (3) Lightweight LLM (<7B parameters) for semantic embedding—justified by overhead argument but contribution is never isolated experimentally. Key Results: Baselines: Stochastic Inference Algorithm (SIA, random selection), DDPG, and DDQN. Evaluation metrics: cumulative reward, total GenAI inference delay, and task queue length per episode. All results are from simulation on synthetic parametric workloads. Convergence (Fig. 3): DRL-ITS reaches stable reward at ~150 episodes; steady-state total delay ≈140s; queue backlog ≈600 Mbit. DDPG and DDQN stabilize at higher delay and queue lengths. Task load sensitivity (20–60 tasks, Fig. 5): At 60 tasks, DRL-ITS reduces delay 9.43% vs. DDPG, 17.35% vs. DDQN, 31.45% vs. SIA; queue length reductions 10.42%, 24.51%, 38.21%. Resolution sensitivity (400–800 pixels, Fig. 6): At 800px, delay reductions 8.24%, 9.43%, 25.17%; queue reductions 9.53%, 17.61%, 42.16%. Edge compute scaling (20%–100%, Fig. 7): At 20% capacity, delay reductions 8.84%, 17.97%, 50.12%. Lyapunov V sweep (Fig. 4): Confirms delay decreases ~inversely with V, queue increases linearly with V, matching the [O(1/V), O(V)] theoretical result. The margins over DDPG are modest (8–10%), which is the strongest reasonable baseline. The scale tested—4 edge servers, 60 tasks maximum—is small relative to real deployments. No significance tests or error bars are reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (engineering integration) + NEW PROBLEM INSTANTIATION. The joint formulation of serial/parallel inference mode selection with aggregation location and CPU/GPU/bandwidth allocation in an edge-cloud visual GenAI context is a new problem statement. The algorithmic machinery—Lyapunov optimization, KKT-based resource allocation, and TD3—are individually well-known; the novelty lies in their particular combination and the binary-action reduction trick that makes the DRL component tractable. The LLM integration as a semantic state augmentation follows recent LLM-for-DRL patterns and is not itself novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation isolates the LLM's contribution from plain TD3—this is the paper's most serious methodological gap. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported for any simulation result. Code and data are not released; no repository is mentioned. The aggregation cost ratio χ_agg is assumed (no prior measurement exists per the authors), and the specific value used in convergence experiments is not clearly stated. The theoretical bounds (Theorems 1–2) apply to an ideal opportunistic controller, not the learned DRL policy; the authors acknowledge this honestly, calling the bounds "theoretical benchmarks/design guidance rather than strict guarantees." Proofs in Appendices A–E are complete and mechanically correct. The workload model (U₀ from EfficientNet-B7) does not correspond to diffusion model compute profiles, which involve iterative denoising steps with fundamentally different FLOP-per-output-bit ratios. Evaluation is entirely on the authors' own simulation environment. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The KKT solver and TD3 inference are lightweight and runnable on standard hardware; however, the cognition module requires a lightweight LLM (≤7B parameters) co-located with the scheduler, which itself needs GPU resources at an entity already described as resource-constrained. - Efficient: Per-slot complexity is O(NK·(L²_seq·d_model + H + 1) + ΛH²); KKT resource allocation is O(NK); the LLM term is claimed negligible relative to visual inference payloads, but this claim is unvalidated by measurement. - Effective: Effective against a parametric workload simulation, not real diffusion inference; the 140s steady-state delay for 40 tasks reflects model parameters chosen by the authors, not measured system behavior. - Scalable: Only tested at N=4, up to 60 tasks; linear scaling is argued from complexity analysis but not empirically verified. The work delivers an algorithm and theoretical analysis in paper form; no software artifact, trained model, or dataset is released. Strengths: - The Lyapunov + KKT decomposition that reduces the TD3 action space to binary variables is the paper's most substantive contribution: it is mathematically clean, the closed-form solutions (proportional to √(weighted workload)) are interpretable, and the technique is directly applicable to analogous scheduling problems. - Theorems 1–2 with complete proofs in five appendices provide practitioners with a principled method to tune V for the desired latency-stability operating point, which is absent from most empirical edge-computing papers. - The serial/parallel/aggregation-location tri-modal formulation is more complete than prior work that treats collaboration as static offloading, and the explicit handling of CPU-GPU heterogeneity at both edge and cloud is practically relevant. - Fig. 4's empirical confirmation that delay and queue length track the [O(1/V), O(V)] prediction is a credibility-positive consistency check between theory and simulation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that Theorems 1–2 bound an ideal opportunistic controller, not the DRL policy, and label them design guidance rather than strict guarantees. They mention extending to multi-agent 6G scenarios as future work, implicitly acknowledging the current framework is centralized. They do not acknowledge the absence of real-system evaluation, the missing LLM ablation, weak baselines, or the mismatch between the EfficientNet workload model and diffusion model inference characteristics. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No LLM ablation: The central architectural claim—that LLM semantic embeddings accelerate convergence and improve rewards—is never tested against a TD3 baseline without the LLM. All observed gains could come entirely from the KKT action-space reduction. - Simulation-only evaluation with a mismatched workload model: Real diffusion model inference involves iterative denoising (e.g., 20–50 DDPM steps per image), producing FLOP-per-output-bit requirements orders of magnitude different from the EfficientNet-B7 calibration used here. Latency figures therefore cannot be extrapolated to actual systems. - Weak baselines: DDPG is not the current state-of-the-art in continuous control; SAC or PPO would be more appropriate competitors. No comparison to model-based RL or to a greedy/rule-based scheduler that always picks the lower-latency mode given current queue states. - Untested scalability: 4 servers and 60 tasks is a toy scale; the linear complexity argument does not substitute for experiments at 20–100 servers. - Unvalidated aggregation cost: χ_agg is freely chosen in [0.01, 0.05]; this ratio is the key parameter determining whether parallel-with-aggregation beats serial, yet no measurement of actual feature fusion latency for diffusion models is provided. - LLM inference overhead not measured: A 7B parameter LLM invoked per scheduling slot adds non-trivial latency; the claim of negligibility is asserted, not demonstrated. Bottom Line: The KKT decomposition and Lyapunov stability analysis form a reusable and correct framework for edge-cloud inference scheduling, but the absence of a plain-TD3 ablation means the paper's LLM-centric framing is unsubstantiated, and the simulation-only evaluation against a diffusion-model-incompatible workload model makes the "visual GenAI" application claim largely cosmetic—systems researchers should treat this as a scheduling theory paper with interesting mathematics rather than an operational blueprint for diffusion model deployment.
Search Results
#166 — Moderately Innovative (46)

MAECO-Lite: Modular Ontology for Dynamic Malware Analysis

Z Adams, P Švec, J Kľuka, R Ploszek, M Onoja… · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MAECO-Lite, a lightweight modular OWL ontology for representing dynamic malware execution behavior, derived from a UFO-grounded ontological analysis of the MAEC 5.0 and STIX 2.1 standards. - Why it's important: Widely adopted malware intelligence standards (MAEC, STIX) conflate enduring artifacts, dispositions, and runtime events in ways that cause description logic concept-learning algorithms to produce trivial or degenerate classifiers, blocking automated reasoning over execution traces. - How it does it: The authors apply the Unified Foundational Ontology (UFO) as a formal lens to identify specific category mismatches in MAEC/STIX, then redesign a five-module ontology (Core, Actions, Files, Registry, Techniques) that cleanly separates endurants (files, processes) from perdurants (API call events, behaviors) and dispositions (capabilities). - The good: On a 1,000-sample balanced dataset (500 malware / 500 benign), DL concept learning under MAECO-Lite achieves F1 scores of 0.62–0.84 across four algorithms, compared to near-trivial results (F1 0.00–0.66) on the naive MAEC flat-JSON baseline — a concrete demonstration that ontological correctness has measurable computational consequences. - The missing: The evaluation uses only 1,000 samples from an undisclosed dataset with no comparison to non-ontological baselines (e.g., LightGBM on API call bags), making it impossible to judge whether the absolute performance is competitive with the state of practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers combining knowledge representation with malware analysis or building explainable AI pipelines for cybersecurity; practitioners seeking a production-ready detection system can skip it pending larger-scale evaluation.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (designed OWL ontology evaluated by DL reasoning). Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (62) are real — the UFO-grounded analysis names concrete category mismatches in MAEC/STIX and the finding that ontological correctness shifts DL-Learner from degenerate predictions to F1 0.84 is non-obvious. But the weighted-dominant Practical Impact (35) is low: no public artifact (no repository, undisclosed dataset), and the preprocessing pipeline mapping sandbox JSON to OWL instances is entirely undescribed. Rigor (40) and Resilience (38) are undermined because the only baseline is a deliberately weak flat-JSON mirror, with no comparison to LightGBM/RF on API-call bags (which routinely hit 0.97+), leaving the absolute F1 uninterpretable. Composite: 0.15*60 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*52 + 0.15*38 + 0.30*35 = 44.3.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 52 Resilience 38 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Security analysts and cyber threat intelligence systems need to reason about dynamic malware behavior observed during sandbox execution. MAEC 5.0 and STIX 2.1 are the dominant schemas, but their flat, data-driven structures conflate ontologically distinct categories: enduring artifacts (files, processes), their latent capabilities (dispositions), and runtime events (API calls, file writes, registry accesses). This conflation makes it difficult to apply formal description-logic reasoning or machine learning methods that depend on relational structure. Existing malware ontologies either focus on static PE analysis (susceptible to obfuscation) or incorporate dynamic features as flat attribute lists without principled semantic grounding — specifically, they treat actions and behaviors as labels of artifacts rather than as temporally bounded events involving participants. The gap is the absence of a computationally tractable, foundationally correct ontology that supports automated reasoning over dynamic execution traces. Key Idea The core insight is that MAEC's conflation of artifacts (endurants), capabilities (dispositions), and behaviors/actions (events/perdurants) is not merely a theoretical imprecision but a practical computational obstacle: when these categories collapse, DL concept-learning algorithms either hallucinate trivial signatures or fail to find any discriminative concept. Reformulating the representation under UFO's endurant/perdurant distinction — treating processes as kinds, API calls and actions as events (perdurants), capabilities as dispositions inhering in file objects, and STIX observables as role mixins — simultaneously reduces representational complexity (14 MB vs. 90 MB RDF) and restores the relational structure that makes concept learning tractable. Methodology The work has two stages. First, a formal ontological analysis: the authors map every major MAEC 5.0 top-level object and STIX 2.1 Cyber Observable Object to UFO categories (kinds, roles, subkinds, dispositions, events, relators, role mixins, qualities), documenting specific mismatches — e.g., MAEC's Behavior is simultaneously used as a disposition ("purpose associated with code") and a runtime event occurrence; ProcessTreeNode conflates three incompatible UFO roles; Dynamic Features acts as a relator that inappropriately bundles entities, events, and their descriptions. Second, MAECO-Lite is designed as five OWL modules: Core (6 classes, 5 object properties: Sample, Process, Action, File, WindowsRegistryKey, Technique, linked by executedIn, accessedFile, accessedRegistry, performedAction, performedTechnique); Actions (146 leaf classes mapping MAEC API call vocabulary, grouped under 17 abstract classes such as AntiDebugging, Cryptography, InterProcessCommunication); Files (role-based hierarchy: ApplicationFile, SystemFile, UserFile, with access properties specialized to createdFile, openedFile, writtenToFile); Registry (intent-based key classification: PersistenceRegistryKey, CredentialAccessRegistryKey, DefenseEvasionRegistryKey, etc.); Techniques (691 ATT&CK technique subclasses + 14 tactic abstractions). Critical design decisions: (1) Treating actions as UFO perdurants separate from dispositions — well-justified by the distinction between statically inferred action_refs and dynamically observed initialed_action in MAEC. (2) Classifying registry keys and files by semantic role rather than structural path — plausible but the mapping procedure from raw sandbox output to semantic class is not specified. (3) Omitting individual Technique occurrence modeling — explicitly pragmatic, justified by the intended DL learning use case. Evaluation uses DL-Learner with OCEL, CELOE, PARCEL, SPACEL in 5-fold cross-validation (80/20 split, 5 minutes per fold per algorithm) on 1,000 balanced samples. Key Results On MAECO-Lite: OCEL achieves accuracy 0.79±0.03, F1 0.84±0.03; PARCEL achieves accuracy 0.80±0.01, F1 0.79±0.01; CELOE achieves accuracy 0.74±0.01, F1 0.77±0.01; SPACEL achieves accuracy 0.71±0.03, F1 0.62±0.06. On the naive MAEC flat baseline: OCEL and CELOE both return ∃triggeredSignature.⊤ (classify every sample with any triggered signature as malware; recall 1.00, precision 0.49, F1 0.66); PARCEL and SPACEL predict all instances as negative (precision, recall, F1 all 0.00). Learned class expressions are semantically interpretable: expression (1) characterizes malware with ≥2 defense evasion techniques; expression (2) characterizes malware combining process hollowing with inter-process communication. Dataset scale is 1,000 samples; source and family distribution not disclosed. No comparison to non-ontological classifiers. Two qualitatively interesting examples of learned DL expressions are provided but no statistical significance tests are reported. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the specific UFO-grounded ontological analysis of MAEC 5.0 and STIX 2.1 constructs (identifying concrete category mismatches, not just vague critique) and the resulting MAECO-Lite modular ontology. The application of DL concept learning to malware classification is not new (the same research group has prior work on static analysis); UFO is an established framework; the novelty is the operationalization of foundational ontology principles as a design constraint for malware knowledge representation, and the empirical demonstration that ontological correctness has measurable downstream effects on automated learning. Rigor & Reproducibility 5-fold cross-validation with ±standard deviations is reported, appropriate for 1,000 samples. No ontology repository URL, dataset release, or code is provided, making independent replication impossible. The dataset source, malware family distribution, obfuscation coverage, and sandbox configuration (beyond a reference to Cuckoo) are not described. No error bars from multiple random seeds are needed given fixed cross-validation splits, but the 5-minute per-fold cap is an external constraint that may have truncated learning (SPACEL's 0.48 recall vs. 0.83 for OCEL raises this concern). The baseline ontology is explicitly the weakest possible comparator — a flat mirroring of the MAEC JSON schema — not an optimized competing representation, which makes the contrast compelling but the absolute F1 scores uninterpretable relative to detection practice. No comparison to Random Forest, LightGBM, or LSTM on raw API call sequences is provided. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a designed OWL ontology with a DL reasoning evaluation. - Practical: DL-Learner runs on an 18-core i9 / 256 GB RAM Debian workstation; the 14 MB RDF instance dataset is manageable, but 256 GB RAM is not commodity. - Efficient: Concept learning completes within a 5-minute per-fold budget for 800 training instances; scaling behavior to 10k+ samples is not reported, and DL concept learning is known to be superlinear in dataset size. - Effective: F1 up to 0.84 on the binary malware/benign task is demonstrated, but without comparison to simpler classifiers the actual effectiveness relative to deployment alternatives is unknown. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 1,000 samples; the paper does not claim or test scalability, and the authors explicitly flag "larger dynamic malware datasets" as future work. The paper describes the ontology schema and module structure in detail but releases no public artifact (no repository URL cited). Strengths - The UFO analysis is technically precise: it identifies specific, named mismatches (Behavior conflating disposition and complex event; ProcessTreeNode holding three incompatible UFO roles; Dynamic Features bundling entities and events without separation) rather than vague expressiveness complaints. - The empirical demonstration that reformulation from flat-JSON to ontologically grounded structure shifts DL-Learner from degenerate predictions to F1 0.84 is a non-obvious finding that provides concrete motivation for the ontology engineering effort. - Modular design with five independently selectable modules enables systematic feature-ablation experiments in downstream work — a genuine practical contribution. - Classifying registry keys by adversarial intent (PersistenceRegistryKey, CredentialAccessRegistryKey, DefenseEvasionRegistryKey) rather than structural path directly aligns with ATT&CK tactic semantics and produces more interpretable learned expressions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors describe MAECO-Lite as "a first step" toward a larger ontology. They explicitly note the Techniques module does not model individual Technique occurrences or their composition from observed Actions, calling this a potential future extension module. They acknowledge the current scope excludes network artifacts, static features, detailed API call parameters, and dynamic features beyond process/file/registry. These limitations are stated honestly but briefly, with no discussion of how the omission of network traffic (a major malware behavioral signal) affects classification performance. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The naive MAEC baseline is not a fair comparator: it is constructed by mechanically mirroring the flat JSON hierarchy, not by any attempt to represent MAEC semantics well. A fair ablation would compare MAECO-Lite to a hand-tuned feature ontology or to the PE Malware Ontology adapted for dynamic features. - The preprocessing pipeline — specifically how raw sandbox JSON output gets mapped to MAECO-Lite OWL instances, and how file paths and registry key paths get assigned to semantic role classes (e.g., which paths count as SystemConfigFile vs. ApplicationDataOrConfigFile) — is completely undescribed, leaving the most implementation-sensitive step opaque. - No comparison to non-ontological malware classifiers means the absolute F1 of 0.84 is uninterpretable: a naïve TF-IDF bag-of-API-calls with LightGBM routinely achieves 0.97+ on standard benchmarks at comparable scale. - The dataset source, family composition, and evasion behavior of the 1,000 samples are not disclosed; the results cannot be assessed for ecological validity or bias toward easily classified malware families. - SPACEL's recall of 0.48 under the 5-minute cap vs. OCEL's 0.83 may reflect time budget allocation rather than algorithmic properties; no sensitivity analysis on the time cap is provided. - The evaluation covers Windows PE malware exclusively (Cuckoo sandbox); generalization to Linux ELF, Android APK, or cross-platform malware is unstated. Bottom Line A researcher building explainable ontology-based malware analysis systems will find the UFO-grounded analysis of MAEC/STIX and the MAECO-Lite design principled and reusable, but the absence of a public artifact, undisclosed dataset, and no comparison to standard ML baselines make this a position-and-prototype paper that requires substantial follow-on work before its practical claims can be validated.
Search Results 2605.31199 PDF
#167 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Amortising Bayesian Experimental Design for Sequential Information Gathering in LLMs

J Hartmann, J Harvey, J Navott, EY Wang, LC Melo… · openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ASIG (Amortised Sequential Information Gathering), a LoRA fine-tuning method that trains LLMs to ask maximally informative questions by using Expected Information Gain as a dense per-turn reward within a multi-turn GRPO framework. - Why it's important: LLMs deployed in clinical, scientific, and interactive settings often fail to gather information efficiently across multi-turn conversations, and existing Bayesian inference-time approaches that solve this problem are 25–36× too slow for practical deployment. - How it does it: The Proposer LLM is fine-tuned via online RL where EIG is estimated each turn using the model's own sampled beliefs and likelihood outputs (Rao-Blackwellised estimator over binary outcomes), combined with a sparse outcome reward, using a curriculum that introduces EIG only after the model first learns to make explicit guesses. - The good: On 7B Qwen2.5, ASIG raises 20 Questions success rate from 7.6% to 21.0% on animals (2.8×) and matches or exceeds BED-LLM in 3 of 4 evaluation settings at over 25× lower inference cost. - The missing: All evaluation uses only 5 episodes per condition — standard deviations of ±1.6–4.8 percentage points on means of 7–39% make most comparisons statistically underpowered — and there is no ablation of EIG versus outcome-only reward. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on active information gathering, Bayesian RL, or multi-turn LLM fine-tuning, but treat the quantitative claims cautiously given the workshop-paper evaluation scale and missing ablations.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (30) drives the composite down hard: only 5 episodes per condition with ±1.6-4.8 pp standard deviations on 7-39% means renders most pairwise comparisons statistically underpowered, the central EIG-vs-outcome-only ablation is absent, the closest prior work (ΔBelief-RL) is not compared, the model-under-optimization EIG reward is a non-stationary/reward-hacking risk that goes unanalyzed, and no code is released (workshop preliminary). Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (60) are credible — moving BED's per-turn search into a dense EIG training reward is principled. Resilience (40) is weak given mixed/null 14B and plants results. The 25-36x inference savings are real, but the evaluation cannot yet substantiate the core claims. Composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*30 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*40 ≈ 45.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as interactive agents in domains requiring sequential information gathering — medical diagnosis, scientific experiment design, interactive search — where they must uncover a hidden target through a series of questions or experiments. Despite strong world knowledge, LLMs exhibit a "lost in conversation" failure mode: they fail to recognize when they need more information and choose informationally suboptimal follow-up questions. Inference-time Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) methods address this by explicitly maximizing Expected Information Gain over candidate designs at each turn, but require repeated belief sampling and EIG estimation, incurring 25–36× the inference cost of a base LLM. Weight-space alternatives either need external retrieval infrastructure or use static preference datasets that cannot adapt to the evolving model distribution. The gap is a self-contained online RL approach that amortises BED behavior into model weights without a per-turn search overhead. Key Idea: The core insight is that the expensive per-turn computation in BED (belief sampling + EIG maximisation over candidate questions) can be moved from inference time into the training signal: by using the LLM's own belief generation and likelihood estimation as the substrate for computing EIG as a dense RL reward, the trained model internalises information-maximising question strategies without running explicit search at deployment time. Methodology: ASIG operates within a two-LLM interaction loop. The Proposer LLM (LoRA fine-tuned, 7B or 14B Qwen2.5-Instruct) generates questions or guesses conditioned on task context and conversation history. A frozen Oracle LLM (Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct) evaluates each question against the hidden target and returns a binary answer. At each turn, EIG is estimated in three steps: (1) sample N=15 plausible target entities from the Proposer LLM conditioned on history, filter for consistency with prior answers; (2) query the Proposer for per-belief likelihoods p(y|θ,ξ) for each belief-question pair; (3) apply the Rao-Blackwellised estimator, which is tractable because the outcome space is binary. The per-turn EIG reward (normalized to [0,1]) is combined with a sparse outcome reward (correct guess: +5; each non-terminal step: −1) via a weighted sum with discount factors. Multi-turn GRPO generates G=8 rollouts per prompt state, with the EIG horizon set to E=1 (only the first turn in each rollout) and outcome horizon O=3. LoRA adapters (rank=32) are updated with KL penalty=0. A learning curriculum trains outcome-only (α=0) for the first 20 epochs before introducing EIG (α=1.0), because without it the model rarely makes specific guesses. Critical design decisions: (a) using the same Proposer LLM for both belief generation and likelihood estimation — well-motivated for efficiency but creates a non-stationary reward signal as the model evolves (not discussed); (b) binary outcome space enabling the Rao-Blackwellised EIG estimator — limits the framework to settings with finite, discrete observations; (c) curriculum ordering — empirically motivated but not ablated. Key Results: On the 20 Questions task (100 entities per held-out category, averaged over 5 episodes): 7B model achieves 21.0% ± 4.8% (animals) and 16.6% ± 2.1% (plants), versus baselines of 7.6% ± 1.8% and 6.8% ± 1.6%, and BED-LLM at 19.2% ± 4.0% and 22.8% ± 2.2% — ASIG wins on animals, BED-LLM wins on plants. 14B model: ASIG 39.2% ± 3.6% (animals) and 13.6% ± 0.9% (plants) versus baseline 35.0% / 11.8% and BED-LLM 26.4% / 21.0% — ASIG wins on animals, BED-LLM wins on plants. ASIG matches base LLM inference latency, while BED-LLM is 25–36× slower. On MediQ (out-of-distribution medical diagnosis), the 7B model improves accuracy by 0.9 pp (46.7% → 47.6%), voluntary accuracy by 1.7 pp, and reduces overconfidence from 36.1% to 33.4%; 14B results are mixed (voluntary accuracy decreases slightly). General LLM benchmarks (ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, MMLU) show no meaningful degradation. Surprising finding: BED-LLM, despite 25–36× more compute, degrades below both ASIG and the baseline on the 14B animals category, suggesting poor robustness across settings. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The combination of multi-turn GRPO with per-turn EIG computed from the LLM's own belief distributions as a training reward — enabling amortisation of BED without external retrieval infrastructure — is genuinely new. The components individually (GRPO, EIG, LoRA, 20 Questions) are established. The closest prior work, ΔBelief-RL (Auzina et al., 2026), uses the same online self-contained RL paradigm but tracks change in probability assigned to the correct target rather than EIG; the distinction matters theoretically but is not verified empirically here since ΔBelief-RL is not included as a baseline. Rigor & Reproducibility: No code or model weights are released. Evaluation uses only 5 episodes per condition; with standard deviations of ±1.6–4.8 pp on means of 7–39%, the confidence intervals are wide enough to render most pairwise comparisons statistically underpowered — for example, the 7B animals comparison (ASIG 21.0 ± 4.8 vs. BED-LLM 19.2 ± 4.0) is not clearly significant. No ablation is presented for the EIG reward versus outcome-only GRPO, which is the central claimed contribution relative to prior work. The curriculum (outcome-first then EIG) is discussed qualitatively but not ablated. The belief set size N=15 is not varied. The EIG reward is computed from the model being optimized, creating a non-stationary reward signal (reward hacking risk) that is not analyzed. Hyperparameters are fully reported in Table 3. This is a workshop paper (FoGen Workshop at ICML 2026, "preliminary work") and has not undergone peer review. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: LoRA fine-tuning on 7–14B models runs on commercially available multi-GPU hardware; inference cost matches the base LLM forward pass. - Efficient: Deployment inference cost is equivalent to the base LLM. Training cost is substantially higher than standard RL due to belief generation and likelihood queries for EIG computation at each step, but training compute is not reported. - Effective: The evidence supports effectiveness at 7B on animals and MediQ overconfidence reduction, but is inconsistent: ASIG trails BED-LLM on both model sizes for plants, and 14B MediQ results are essentially null. The 5-episode evaluation limits confidence in all numbers. - Scalable: Tested only at 7B and 14B parameter scales, on a binary-outcome game with a 20-entity search space per episode. Transfer to MediQ is partial and weakens at 14B. No test at larger entity spaces, longer horizons, or continuous outcome spaces. The work delivers a trained LoRA adapter and a training procedure, not a reusable software library. Strengths: - The EIG-as-RL-reward formulation is theoretically principled: unlike pure outcome-based RL, it provides dense, turn-level signal tied to information-theoretic optimality, not just final task success. - The efficiency gain is unambiguous: amortising BED eliminates 25–36× inference overhead versus BED-LLM, making the approach viable for interactive deployment where BED-LLM is not. - Partial OOD transfer to MediQ at 7B — a structurally different task unseen during training — provides evidence that the learned strategy has some domain-agnostic character rather than purely task-specific memorization. - Capability preservation on ARC/HellaSwag/MMLU rules out catastrophic forgetting, a common failure mode of aggressive RL fine-tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge mixed results for the 14B model, attributing them to stronger prior capabilities leaving less room for behavioral adaptation. They flag the need to disentangle EIG and outcome reward contributions as future work, and note that BED-LLM's high hypothesis-seeking rate is partly driven by hard-coded guessing rules rather than emergent strategy. The paper characterizes itself as preliminary. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Five episodes per condition is insufficient for the claims made: with ±4.8 pp standard deviation on a 21% mean, the 95% CI spans roughly 12 percentage points, making the central comparisons unreliable. The most important missing ablation is EIG versus outcome-only GRPO — without it, the paper cannot distinguish ASIG's gains from what outcome-based online RL alone (as in ΔBelief-RL) would achieve. ΔBelief-RL is identified as the closest prior work but is absent from the experimental comparison, which is the single largest gap in the evaluation design. The circular EIG reward (computed from the model under optimization) introduces a non-stationary reward signal that could incentivize reward hacking (e.g., the model learns to generate beliefs that make its questions appear informative without actually being so); this is unacknowledged and unanalyzed. The consistent plants underperformance across both model sizes is unexplained and may reveal a structural limitation of binary-EIG estimation when entity properties are highly correlated. Training compute cost is not reported, which matters for the practical efficiency comparison. Bottom Line: The amortisation idea is principled and the inference efficiency gains are real, but the 5-episode evaluation, absent EIG ablation, and missing ΔBelief-RL comparison leave the core empirical claims undersubstantiated at this workshop-paper stage — practitioners should await a full-paper version with proper statistical power before drawing strong conclusions.
Search Results
#168 — Moderately Innovative (45)

GDP: Graph-Based Dynamic Personalization for Multimodal Large Language Models

C Ray, X Deng, F Huang, ZX Wu, S Jiang, P Jiao, Z Liu… · openaccess.thecvf.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GDP (Graph-based Dynamic Personalization), a framework that stores evolving user preferences in a confidence-weighted graph and reranks retrieved preference nodes by combining semantic similarity, extraction confidence, and graph-structural importance to generate personalized MLLM responses. - Why it's important: Personalized AI assistants that rely on static user profiles and face-recognition-only identity resolution fail when preferences change mid-interaction or when users appear in occluded or non-frontal views — both common in real deployment. - How it does it: A supervised fine-tuned Personalization Enhancement (PE) Agent extracts typed preference nodes with confidence scores; those scores drive edge weights that decay over time and amplify high-frequency co-occurrences; graph-based relational cues provide fallback identity inference when face recognition fails. - The good: On a 835-pair PQA benchmark, GDP achieves 0.834 weighted accuracy versus 0.624 for the next-best method (RAP+Prompt) — a 21-point absolute gap — alongside 0.956 weighted visual recognition accuracy. - The missing: The benchmark is generated by DeepSeek-R1 from the same TV drama source data used to train the PE Agent, collapsing train and evaluation distributions; the dataset covers only 42 user subgroups with simulated dialogues, making the gains unverifiable on real users. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing graph-based memory systems for personalized dialogue agents, but the self-generated micro-benchmark means the empirical claims need independent replication before being taken at face value.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (35) is the dominant drag: the PQA evaluation pairs and the PE-Agent SFT data are both generated by DeepSeek-R1 from the same 42-subgroup TV-drama corpus, conflating train and eval distributions (a Goodhart-style flaw), with no error bars, no significance tests, no code/weights/dataset release, and structurally unequal baselines (fine-tuning baselines get 5 reference images, GDP gets 1). Resilience (35) collapses under scrutiny for the same reasons — the 21-point gap is not credibly attributable to the method. Novelty (65) is the redeeming dimension (NEW PROBLEM/METHOD/DATASET, confidence-weighted graph memory with identity fallback), but the tiny self-generated micro-benchmark pulls the composite well below the novelty signal.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal personal assistants must both reliably identify a user from images and maintain a model of that user's evolving preferences. Two failure modes motivate this work. First, identity resolution that depends solely on face recognition degrades under occlusion or non-frontal viewing angles, which are common in casual video interaction. Second, existing personalization methods store fixed, predefined user profiles that cannot incorporate preference updates expressed during conversation (e.g., a user saying mid-session that they dislike a food because it is too sour). Fine-tuning approaches require retraining to add new users or update preferences; retrieval-augmented methods retrieve by semantic similarity, which favors topical relevance over actual preference strength. No prior benchmark captures preference evolution over sequential interactions. Key Idea The core insight is that preference importance is not the same as semantic relevance: a frequently confirmed, recently expressed, high-confidence preference should outrank a topically similar but weakly attested one. GDP operationalizes this by assigning each graph edge a weight that multiplies extraction confidence, temporal recency (exponential decay e^{−λΔt}), and co-occurrence frequency, divided by a semantic distance penalty. Retrieval reranks top-k semantically similar nodes by a composite score that adds node confidence and average neighbor edge weight, so structurally central, high-confidence preferences float to the top. Methodology The framework has three sequential modules. (1) Personalized Extraction: A LLaVA-1.5-13B model fine-tuned via SFT serves as the PE Agent. Given an image I, conversation C, face-recognition output Rec, and top-k retrieved graph nodes N, it produces typed nodes — User, Likeness, place, scene, emotion, activity, object — each with a confidence score conf. When face recognition fails, the agent performs identity inference using previously stored nodes for clothing, accessories, and location. (2) Graph Update: New nodes are merged with or appended to a per-user graph. Edge weight w_ij = (conf_ij · e^{−λΔt} · β · freq_ij) / (1 + α · dist_sem) where α=0.05 controls semantic penalty, λ=0.05 controls temporal decay, and β=0.5 weights co-occurrence frequency. (3) Personalized Retrieval: Query q selects top-k candidates by cosine similarity; Graph Rerank scores each candidate as R_i = γ1·S_i + γ2·conf_i + γ3·w̄_i (semantic similarity + node confidence + mean neighbor edge weight). Top reranked nodes are injected into the LLM prompt. Critical design decisions: (a) confidence as a dynamic weight is justified over pure semantic ranking because it captures user-expressed preference strength; (b) temporal decay with small λ is justified to prioritize recency without discarding long-term state; (c) SFT on the PE Agent rather than prompt-only is justified by the need for structured node output, confirmed by the ablation. The GDP dataset is constructed from TV dramas and online videos: ffmpeg extracts keyframes, DownSub collects subtitles, LLM post-processing clusters character IDs, and DeepSeek-R1 generates QA pairs and SFT output nodes. The PQA dataset contains 835 question-answer pairs split into Consistency PQA (does the model retain existing information) and Update PQA (does it absorb new information). The SFT dataset trains the PE Agent end-to-end with a standard cross-entropy loss over structured node outputs. Key Results All experiments use LLaVA-1.5-13B as the backbone and run on a single A100 GPU. The benchmark comprises 42 subgroups of 5–10 images each with simulated dialogues. Visual Recognition (Table 2): GDP achieves positive accuracy 0.970, negative accuracy 0.943, weighted 0.956. The second-best method, Yo'LLaVA+Prompt (fine-tuned, 5 images), achieves 0.833 weighted. RAP+Prompt achieves 0.809. PQA (Table 2): GDP achieves consistency 0.832, update 0.835, weighted 0.834. RAP+Prompt achieves 0.624 weighted; all other baselines fall below 0.620. Ablation (Table 3): removing the PE Agent drops weighted PQA to 0.628; removing graph storage drops to 0.568; removing SFT drops to 0.592; removing prompt input drops to 0.557. Hyperparameter sensitivity (Figure 5): all three parameters (α, λ, β) show sharp inverted-U response curves, with accuracy collapsing to ~0.55–0.60 at extreme values. No error bars or significance tests are reported. Baselines are relevant (RAP, Yo'LLaVA, Per. Toolkit, LLaVA-LoRA, base LLaVA) but some are tested under conditions different from their native setups (e.g., fine-tuning baselines receive 5 reference images; GDP uses 1). Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM (dynamic multimodal preference evolution as a formal task), NEW METHOD (confidence-weighted graph memory with PE Agent for MLLM personalization), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (GDP dataset and PQA metrics). The graph mechanism — weighted edges with temporal decay and frequency boosting — is a standard combination of ideas from MemoryBank, GraphRAG, and RAP applied to a new setting; the genuine novelty lies in integrating preference confidence as a first-class dynamic retrieval signal and the graph-enhanced identity fallback. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative, isolating PE Agent, graph storage, SFT, and prompt input as individually necessary components. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The most serious methodological concern is that both the SFT training data and the PQA evaluation pairs are generated by DeepSeek-R1 from the same 42-subgroup TV drama corpus — this conflates training and evaluation distributions and could inflate reported accuracy relative to baselines that were not trained on LLM-generated data from the same source. The dataset is also tiny (42 users, 835 QA pairs) and domain-specific (TV drama characters). Code, model weights, and dataset release are not mentioned. The paper is published at CVPR 2026 Findings (peer-reviewed). Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The system runs on a single A100 GPU using LLaVA-1.5-13B, FAISS, and a standard face-recognition library — all commercially available components. - Efficient: Latency, memory footprint, and graph retrieval cost at inference time are not reported; SFT of a 13B-parameter model adds training overhead compared to prompt-only baselines, but the magnitude is unstated. - Effective: Shows large gains on its own PQA benchmark, but that benchmark is generated from the same TV drama data used for training, so it does not cleanly demonstrate effectiveness on real or out-of-distribution user interactions. - Scalable: Tested only on 42 users with 5–10 images each; graph scaling behavior with hundreds of users, thousands of interaction turns, or long-term preference drift is not discussed or evaluated. The work delivers a trained model and dataset pipeline but does not release code or weights publicly. Strengths - The preference-confidence edge-weight formulation is a principled and well-motivated design: it addresses a genuine gap in semantic-similarity-only RAG by encoding both preference strength and temporal relevance into the graph structure. - The graph-enhanced identity fallback (using clothing, accessory, and location nodes when face recognition fails) is a practical and underexplored technique for robust user identification in unconstrained settings. - The ablation study is unusually thorough for a short paper: every major component (PE Agent, graph storage, SFT, prompt) is individually knocked out, and each removal degrades performance substantially. - The Consistency vs. Update PQA split is a conceptually useful evaluation decomposition that could benefit future benchmarks in this space. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge only one limitation: reliance on personalized visual and interaction data raises privacy concerns, and they propose future work on differential privacy or federated learning. They do not acknowledge the small dataset size, the TV drama domain gap, the self-generated evaluation, or the absence of latency/scalability analysis. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The PQA evaluation data is generated by DeepSeek-R1 from the same TV drama corpus used to train the PE Agent; the reported 21-point advantage over RAP may partly reflect that baselines were not optimized for this evaluation format rather than genuine preference modeling superiority. - 42 subgroups with 5–10 images each and simulated dialogues provides almost no basis for claims about real-world generalization; there is no held-out user set, no real-user interaction data, and no domain transfer experiment. - The comparison is structurally unequal: fine-tuning baselines (Yo'LLaVA, LLaVA-LoRA) receive 5 reference images per user while GDP uses 1; no ablation shows GDP given 5 images, nor any baseline given 1 image. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is high (accuracy drops to ~0.55 at suboptimal α, λ, β values per Figure 5), but no guidance is given on how to set these parameters for a new domain without access to labeled QA pairs. - Inference latency and graph retrieval cost are not reported — critical omissions for a system claiming real-time preference modeling. - No statistical significance testing on any reported metric. - Neither code nor dataset is released, precluding independent replication. Bottom Line GDP introduces a sensible graph-based memory design with a meaningful conceptual contribution to dynamic preference modeling, but the evaluation rests on a self-generated, 42-user dataset drawn from TV dramas with simulated dialogues — too small, too narrow, and too entangled with the training pipeline to support the broad performance claims; practitioners should wait for independent replication on real-user data before adopting this framework.
Search Results
#169 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Do LLMs and VLMs Share Reasoning Neurons? Evidence and Mechanisms of Cross-Modal Transfer

C Cui, A Zhang, Y Chen, G Deng, J Zheng, Z Liang… · openaccess.thecvf.com · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper shows that more than 70% of top-activated neurons during reasoning are shared between LLMs and LVLMs, and introduces SNRF (Shared Neuron Low-Rank Fusion), a training-free method that selectively injects a low-rank LLM weight update into a LVLM's shared-neuron subspace to improve multimodal reasoning. - Why it's important: LVLMs consistently lag text-only LLMs on mathematical and scientific reasoning, yet paired image-rationale training data is scarce — a mechanism to transfer mature text-model reasoning circuitry at near-zero cost directly addresses this bottleneck. - How it does it: SNRF identifies shared neurons via activation-ablation profiling, computes the SVD-truncated weight difference between the LLM and LVLM, and applies that update only within the shared-neuron mask, leaving perceptual pathways untouched. - The good: Deactivating only the shared neurons (a small subset) collapses MathVista accuracy to exactly 0.0 on all three tested LVLMs, while random ablations of equal neuron count cause only partial drops (32.6–56.2), establishing strong causal necessity. - The missing: The primary model pairs (Qwen2.5-Math-7B vs Qwen2.5-VL-7B) share the same pre-trained language backbone, so the 74.5% neuron overlap may reflect shared initialization rather than convergent cross-modal reasoning — this confounder is not disentangled. - Read it or not: Read if you work on mechanistic interpretability, model merging, or LVLM efficiency — the causal ablation result is striking and the method beats several training-based baselines without any fine-tuning; skip if you need rigorous mechanistic evidence rather than ablation-based correlation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (35), Completeness (35), and Resilience (38) all drag the composite down. The shared-neuron deactivation result (MathVista collapses to 0.0 vs. partial drops for random ablation) is a striking causal finding (Novelty 60, Non-Obviousness 55), but the headline 74.5% overlap is confounded by shared backbone initialization — an alternative explanation the paper never tests — and the causal ablation is shown only for shared-backbone pairs. There is no limitations section, no ablation separating SNRF's three components, no error bars, no released code, an unreported activation threshold, and a self-generated probe corpus that overlaps the eval benchmarks (Goodhart concern). Gains are modest and scale-inconsistent (+22.6 at 3B vs. +0.9 at 7B on ScienceQA). Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*35 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*38 + 0.10*45 = 44.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 35 Resilience 38 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LVLMs built on pretrained LLM backbones consistently trail text-only LLMs on compositional and multi-step reasoning tasks such as MathVista and MMMU-Pro. The dominant remedy — collecting more multimodal reasoning data and scaling model size — is bottlenecked by the scarcity of paired image-rationale corpora relative to pure-text corpora. A parallel, open question in interpretability is whether the reasoning computations performed by LLMs are structurally preserved in their VL extensions or overwritten by multimodal training. If shared circuits exist and are causally necessary, they could serve as a targeted channel for transferring reasoning ability without any additional multimodal training. No prior work had investigated whether reasoning-specific neurons are shared across LLMs and LVLMs, or whether such overlap could be leveraged for cross-modal transfer. Key Idea The neurons an LLM activates most strongly during reasoning are, to a surprising degree (>70% overlap in tested pairs), the same neurons the corresponding LVLM activates — despite different training signals and, in some cases, different architectures. These shared neurons are causally necessary for reasoning: remove them and accuracy collapses to zero, while removing an equal number of random neurons causes only a partial drop. A low-rank update restricted to exactly those shared neurons can therefore inject reasoning capacity from a mature text model into a multimodal model without corrupting perceptual pathways. Methodology The pipeline has two sequential stages. Stage 1 builds a neuron detection corpus by running each model on reasoning problems (GSM8K for text-only, Geo3K for multimodal) and concatenating problem with the model's own generated rationale. A neuron N (defined as a single row or column of a weight matrix across five types: attn.q, attn.k, attn.v, fwd.up, fwd.down) is considered activated if zeroing its associated parameters changes the output embedding by at least threshold σ in L2 norm. Neurons activated consistently across the full context set form that model's reasoning basis; shared neurons are the intersection across the LLM and LVLM bases restricted to the shared language backbone. Stage 2 applies SNRF: for each layer ℓ and projection type p, compute Δ_{ℓ,p} = W^{src} − W^{tgt}, take the rank-r SVD approximation Δ^{(r)}_{ℓ,p}, then update W^{tgt}_{ℓ,p} += β · M_{S}(Δ^{(r)}_{ℓ,p}), where M_{S} zeroes all entries outside the shared-neuron index set S. Three critical design decisions: (1) ablation-based rather than activation-magnitude-based neuron identification, motivated by prior interpretability work but introducing circularity when the probe corpus is model-generated; (2) restriction of the weight update to the shared-neuron subspace, justified in Section 5.3 by a curvature argument showing linear merging incurs avoidable loss from the complement subspace — though the required curvature conditions (μ_⊥, μ_S) are never empirically measured; (3) low-rank approximation via SVD, which reduces parameter count but whose rank r is not ablated in the main paper. The amplification analysis (multiplying a neuron's activation by λ and measuring resulting token-frequency shifts) provides qualitative concept-level labeling of individual neurons. Key Results Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B share 4,703 of 6,312 union neurons (74.5%); Intern2.5-VL-4B vs Qwen2.5-GRPO-3B share 71.3%; Idefics3-8B vs Deepseek-LLaMA3-8B (architecturally distinct backbones) share 71.5%. Deactivating shared neurons collapses MathVista to 0.0 across all three tested LVLMs (baselines: 37.5, 50.8, 61.6) while random ablation of equal count produces 32.6, 42.1, 56.2. SNRF over base LVLM on MathVista Solution: +4.7 (Qwen2.5-VL-3B), +4.6 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B); ScienceQA: +22.6 (3B), +14.6 (Idefics3-8B); MMMU-Pro (V): 0.000→0.186 on Intern2.5-VL-4B; best MMMU-Pro (V) at 7B is 0.179 vs. prior best training-based 0.1162. Against training baselines at 3B, SNRF leads ScienceQA (75.1 vs GRIT's 67.1) while avoiding the MME collapse seen in Open-R1-Distill (857 vs base 1535). Against merging baselines at 7B, SNRF achieves MMMU-Pro (V) 0.179 vs. Linear 0.165, DARE 0.125, FRANK 0.107. Shared neurons concentrate in attn.k matrices, with secondary attn.v presence, clustering in early (<layer 6) and late (>layer 20) layers. Counter-intuitively, format accuracy regresses on Qwen2.5-VL-3B (−3.7), and MathVista Solution gains at 7B (+4.6) do not translate to closing the gap with training-based VLAA-thinker (42.1 vs 59.7). Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The finding that >70% of reasoning-critical neurons are shared across LLM/LVLM pairs is genuinely novel — no prior interpretability work has measured this cross-modal overlap or established its causal necessity via deactivation experiments. SNRF is a new composition of established techniques (model merging, SVD/LoRA approximation, ablation-based neuron identification) that conditions weight updates on an empirically derived neuron mask; the individual components are standard, but their integration for cross-modal reasoning transfer is new. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation isolates the contribution of individual SNRF components — the mask, the low-rank approximation, and β scaling are always applied together; comparisons against Linear/DARE/FRANK in Table 4 conflate all design choices simultaneously. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported for any benchmark. The activation threshold σ (Eq. 4-5) is not stated anywhere in the main paper, and the rank r and mixing coefficient β appear without sensitivity analysis. No code, model weights, or detection corpus are released. The neuron detection corpus is built from model-generated rationales on reasoning benchmarks that overlap with the evaluation suite (GSM8K → MathVista; Geo3K → ScienceQA), raising a Goodhart's-Law concern: identified neurons may reflect the model's own output-formatting patterns rather than abstract reasoning circuits. The paper is peer-reviewed (CVPR 2026 Findings track, not main track). Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: SNRF requires simultaneous access to both source LLM and target LVLM weights for a one-time offline merge; inference runs the merged model alone at standard LVLM memory cost on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: The merge is a one-time offline computation (SVD of weight differences + masking); inference cost is identical to the base LVLM with zero additional parameters or latency. - Effective: MathVista Solution improves 4–5 points on Qwen2.5-VL models and ScienceQA improves substantially on some backbones, but gains are uneven — Idefics3-8B and LLaVA-Next-8B show <1-point MathVista Solution gains, and format accuracy regresses on Qwen2.5-VL-3B (−3.7), limiting end-to-end solution quality. - Scalable: Tested only at 3B–8B. No experiments at 13B, 34B, or 70B scale; the paper makes no claim and provides no evidence about whether neuron overlap percentages or SNRF gain magnitudes hold at larger scale. The work delivers a merged-weight artifact but releases no code, tooling, checkpoints, or detection corpus. Strengths - The deactivation experiment in Table 1 is the paper's strongest contribution: a targeted removal of only shared neurons (not all neurons, not random equal-count neurons) collapses MathVista to 0.0 while random ablation causes only modest damage — this is a clean, falsifiable causal result that would be difficult to dismiss as coincidence. - The amplification-based neuron interpretation (Finding 3) provides concept-level semantics for individual neurons (algebra vs. geometry specialization in adjacent FFN units), moving beyond overlap statistics toward mechanistic understanding, even if the method is qualitative. - SNRF requires no multimodal training data and outperforms several training-based baselines (Open-R1-Distill, VLAA-thinker) on key reasoning metrics while avoiding the severe perception degradation those methods exhibit (e.g., Open-R1-Distill MME 857 vs. base 1535). - Testing multiple architecturally distinct pairs (Idefics3 + Deepseek-LLaMA3, where the backbone is not shared) and still finding >71% neuron overlap suggests the phenomenon has some generality beyond shared initialization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that MathVista Format score decreases slightly on average (−0.98) and suggest a lightweight formatting adapter as a fix. No dedicated limitations section exists. No other failure modes, scope boundaries, or methodological concerns — including the shared-initialization confounder, the self-generated probe corpus, or the absence of ablations — are explicitly acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The headline 74.5% overlap is measured on Qwen2.5-Math-7B vs Qwen2.5-VL-7B, which share the same Qwen2.5 pre-trained language backbone; the overlap likely reflects shared initialization rather than convergent cross-modal reasoning, a critical alternative explanation the paper does not test (e.g., by measuring overlap before any fine-tuning from the shared base). - The causal deactivation experiment (Table 1) is reported only for Qwen2.5-VL and Intern2.5-VL pairs sharing Qwen backbones — the cross-architecture pair (Idefics3 + Deepseek-LLaMA3) is shown only in the Venn diagram (Figure 3), not in the ablation table, leaving the causal claim unverified for the architecturally distinct case. - The activation threshold σ and the number of top-activated neurons selected are unreported, making the 74.5% overlap figure uninterpretable and unreproducible without the appendix or code. - No ablation separates the three SNRF components (shared-neuron mask, low-rank approximation, β magnitude); it is unknown whether the mask alone (equivalent to masked linear merging) accounts for most of the gain. - The theoretical improvement guarantee (Section 5.3, Eq. 12-13) conditions on curvature terms μ_⊥ and μ_S that are never empirically estimated, so the guarantee is vacuous in practice without knowing whether its premise holds. - MathVista Solution gains at 7B (+4.6) still leave SNRF at 42.1 versus VLAA-thinker's 59.7, and ScienceQA gains are 0.9 at 7B vs 22.6 at 3B — the inconsistency across scale is unexplained and limits confidence in the method's reliability. Bottom Line The causal ablation showing that shared-neuron deactivation crashes reasoning accuracy to zero is a genuinely interesting mechanistic result worth reading for interpretability researchers, but SNRF's practical gains are modest and scale-inconsistent, the primary overlap claim is confounded by shared backbone initialization, and the absence of ablations, error bars, and code makes the work difficult to build on.
Citations
#170 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Evaluating and Enhancing Generative Model Unlearning with LLM World Knowledge

S Mahan, E Yeats, D Hannan, H Kvinge, T Doster… · openaccess.thecvf.com · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces autoeval-unlearning, a framework that uses an off-the-shelf LLM to automatically generate fine-grained concept evaluation plans across 7 semantic relation types (synonyms, hypernyms, unique/non-unique attributes, siblings, instances, coincidental) for diagnosing and improving machine unlearning in generative models. - Why it's important: Fixed forget/retain set benchmarks cannot reveal whether unique-attribute prompts still elicit an unlearned concept (under-forgetting) or whether collateral damage has spread to semantically related concepts (over-forgetting), leaving practitioners blind to the real robustness of any deployed unlearning procedure. - How it does it: An LLM iteratively brainstorms and ranks concepts related to the unlearning target via high-temperature generation followed by low-temperature verification, then uses those concepts to generate adversarial evaluation probes and synthetic supplementary training data that sharpens the unlearning boundary. - The good: Unique-attribute prompts function as zero-shot adversarial attacks after unlearning — ESD achieves attack success rates of 0.316 ± 0.223 on unique attributes vs. 0.094 ± 0.115 on non-unique attributes — and LLM-ranked concept similarity visually correlates with measured KID damage to sibling concepts. - The missing: The assistant LLM is never named, only 3 T2I unlearning targets are examined by name, no correlation coefficient is reported for the LLM-rank vs. KID relationship, and no comparison to automated baselines (embedding-based neighbor selection, jailbreak generators) is provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML safety and unlearning researchers who need a practical red-teaming and data-augmentation toolkit, but the workshop-scale evaluation (few targets, one T2I architecture, no code release) means results should be treated as a proof of concept rather than a validated system.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The unique-attribute zero-shot adversarial finding and the 7-way relation taxonomy give modest Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (58), but Rigor (35) and Completeness (38) collapse the composite: the assistant LLM is never named (every result is unreproducible), the 'strong' KID-vs-rank correlation has no reported coefficient, there are no ablations on the brainstorming procedure, no code release, only 3 named targets on a 2022-vintage SD v1.4, and no automated-baseline comparison — compounded by the absence of any limitations section, a credibility negative.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 38 Resilience 38 Impact 42
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Machine unlearning in generative AI must simultaneously remove a target concept (efficacy) while preserving all other knowledge (locality). Existing benchmarks use fixed, pre-specified forget and retain sets that are coarse-grained and static: they do not reveal whether residual concept knowledge remains accessible through semantically related prompts, nor do they expose collateral damage to neighboring concepts. The gap is particularly acute for broad concept targets — unlearning "Star Wars" is not fully captured by testing prompts that explicitly say "Star Wars." GDPR compliance, copyright enforcement, and safety filtering all require unlearning that is robust to rephrasing and that does not inadvertently degrade related legitimate content. No existing tool provides automated, concept-aware evaluation that generalizes to arbitrary targets without manual annotation. Key Idea An LLM's world knowledge implicitly encodes graded semantic proximity to any concept: concepts the LLM ranks as most similar to the unlearning target suffer the most collateral damage during unlearning (measurable by KID), and concepts the LLM classifies as "unique attributes" of the target simultaneously serve as the most effective zero-shot adversarial prompts and the most useful additions to the forget set. The same semantic structure that reveals vulnerabilities also prescribes the fix. Methodology The framework has two phases. In the planning phase, given a user-specified target concept, an LLM executes a `brainstorm` function: it generates candidate related concepts at high temperature over multiple iterations, deduplicates, re-verifies each candidate at low temperature, adds disambiguation context (e.g., Pluto the cartoon character vs. the planet), and ranks survivors via iterative shuffling with position averaging. Synonyms are drawn from WordNet rather than the LLM. For siblings, candidates generated from target + hypernyms are filtered to exclude subtypes. Attributes are classified unique vs. non-unique via a structured multi-question LLM probe. Instances undergo a confirmatory LLM filtering step. Top-K concepts per relation type constitute the evaluation plan. In the metric recording phase, T2I evaluation uses generated captions passed through the unlearned model; images are scored by CLIP caption-image alignment, CLIP classification accuracy against sibling concepts, and KID between the original and unlearned model's output distributions. LLM evaluation generates QA pairs from the original model and measures ROUGE-score change after unlearning. For data augmentation, unique-attribute caption-image pairs are appended to the forget set D_f, and sibling concept pairs are appended to the retain set D_r, then standard fine-tuning unlearning (min_θ L_f(D_f;θ) + λL_r(D_r;θ)) is applied. T2I experiments use Stable Diffusion v1.4 with ESD, REC (Receler), and UCE at default hyperparameters. LLM experiments use Llama-3.2-3B with gradient ascent, gradient difference, and NPO. Three critical design decisions: (1) LLM-ranked semantic similarity as a proxy for unlearning damage — empirically motivated but with only visual correlation evidence; (2) unique/non-unique attribute classification via structured questioning — well-justified by the downstream attack-success differential; (3) generating retain data from the original (not unlearned) model — sound, prevents retain-set drift. Key Results After ESD unlearning on 10 T2I targets, CLIP attack success rate on unique-attribute prompts is 0.316 ± 0.223 vs. 0.094 ± 0.115 on non-unique attributes and 0.207 ± 0.328 on the target itself. Supplementing the forget set with unique attributes (ESD+) reduces the unique-attribute attack rate to 0.060 ± 0.051. UCE drops from 0.371 to 0.135. On the HUB erasure benchmark, REC+ improves over REC on Star Wars (13.8 vs. 18.4) and Harry Potter (8.4 vs. 20.6). KID damage to sibling concepts shows a visual downward trend as LLM similarity rank decreases (rank 1 siblings show KID ~0.20 for ESD vs. ~0.02 for rank 10); supplementing the retain set with siblings reduces this damage substantially. For Llama-3.2-3B LLM unlearning averaged across 5-8 concept plans, unique attributes and siblings show the largest ROUGE score degradation (up to ~100% relative drop for some methods); adding sibling retain data reduces collateral damage across all relation types. Evaluation targets by name are Mickey Mouse, Star Wars, and Harry Potter for T2I; LLM targets are not individually named. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The tool is a composition of established LLM prompting techniques (iterative generation, temperature-based diversity, structured verification) applied to a new problem context. The genuinely novel empirical finding is the demonstrated correlation between LLM-assigned semantic similarity rank and measured KID damage, and the identification of unique attributes as both the primary under-forgetting vulnerability and the primary data-augmentation target — a finding that is not obvious from prior unlearning or jailbreak literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Standard errors are reported for Table 1 and Figure 4a; no statistical significance tests or p-values are provided. There are no ablations on the brainstorming procedure (temperature, iteration count, top-K, LLM choice). The assistant LLM is described only as "off-the-shelf" and "LLM-agnostic" — the actual model used in all experiments is never disclosed in the main text, making replication impossible. No code or model weights are released. The KID-vs-rank correlation is presented only visually; no correlation coefficient is reported despite the claim of a "strong" correlation. Table 1 averages over 10 unnamed unlearning targets, which prevents external verification. The paper is a peer-reviewed CVPR workshop paper, not a main-track submission, implying lighter scrutiny. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Yes — Stable Diffusion v1.4 and Llama-3.2-3B run on a single consumer or workstation GPU; the LLM planning step requires API access or a local model. - Efficient: The framework adds an LLM preprocessing pass and synthetic data generation; incremental compute overhead is proportional to the number of brainstormed concepts but not characterized quantitatively. - Effective: Evidence is positive but limited — unique-attribute attack success rates drop substantially with supplementation, and sibling retention improves visibly, but results span only 3 named targets on one T2I model vintage. - Scalable: Not tested beyond Stable Diffusion v1.4 and Llama-3.2-3B; whether the approach transfers to SDXL, FLUX, or 70B LLMs is unexamined and merely claimed. No software artifact is released; the contribution exists in paper form only. Strengths - The unique-attribute adversarial attack finding is immediately actionable: it provides a training-free, zero-shot method to probe whether unlearning is complete, with concrete attack success numbers across multiple methods. - The 7-way relation taxonomy is more semantically principled than prior coarse-grained neighbor sets and maps cleanly to distinct failure modes (unique attributes → under-forgetting; siblings → over-forgetting), enabling targeted remediation. - The dual-use insight — the same evaluation plan that diagnoses weaknesses also prescribes the data augmentation that fixes them — gives the framework a self-contained, practical structure. - Cross-modal applicability (T2I diffusion and LLM) without architectural modification demonstrates genuine generality of the concept-based approach. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper does not include an explicit limitations section. The conclusion acknowledges that "some unique attributes are not fully unlearned" and that the supplementation experiments "begin to explore" the benefits of data augmentation — hedging language that implicitly acknowledges limited scope. The authors do not directly address LLM dependence, scale limits, or evaluation narrowness, which is a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The assistant LLM is never identified, making every result in the paper unreproducible; the "LLM-agnostic" claim is also untested — plan quality almost certainly varies substantially across LLMs. - Only 3 named T2I targets (Mickey Mouse, Star Wars, Harry Potter) appear in the detailed results; the 10-target Table 1 aggregate masks target-specific variance and the full target list is not disclosed. - No correlation coefficient for the LLM rank vs. KID relationship is reported, and the x-axis in Figure 4a mixes concepts of very different semantic distances; the "strong correlation" claim is not statistically substantiated. - No comparison to simpler automated neighbor-selection baselines (e.g., embedding cosine similarity, clip-score-ranked neighbors, or generic jailbreak generators) — it is unknown whether an LLM adds measurable value over embedding-space proximity. - All T2I experiments use Stable Diffusion v1.4 (released 2022); applicability to contemporary architectures with stronger RLHF-style safety training is unstated. - The unique-attribute attack success rates have very high standard errors (e.g., 0.316 ± 0.223 for ESD), suggesting high variance across targets; some targets may show no vulnerability at all, which would significantly qualify the headline finding. Bottom Line Practitioners red-teaming unlearned generative models will find the unique-attribute attack result and the augmentation recipe concretely useful, but the absent code release, unnamed LLM, and small-scale evaluation make this a workshop-grade proof of concept that needs a follow-up with broader targets, identified tools, and statistical validation before it can be treated as a reliable methodology.
Recommended
#171 — Moderately Innovative (44)

FM Meets AI: Equipping AI Agents with Formal-Methods Tools

D Beyer, T Lemberger, H Wachowitz · sosy-lab.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
