#187 — Incremental (37)

DriftSched: Adaptive QoS-Aware Scheduling under Runtime Token Drift for Multi-Tenant GPU Inference

K Palaniappan · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DriftSched, a scheduling framework for multi-tenant LLM inference that applies exponential-moving-average (EMA) bias correction to per-workload-category token-budget estimates, then feeds corrected estimates into five classical scheduling policies evaluated on a single NVIDIA L4 GPU. - Why it's important: LLM schedulers that rely on static max_tokens limits consistently overestimate actual generation length, causing workload misclassification, queue imbalance, and inflated tail latency under GPU contention — a real operational cost in shared inference infrastructure. - How it does it: After each completed request, the framework updates a per-category bias factor as B_new = (1−α)×B_old + α×(T_actual/T_base) using whitespace-delimited word counts as the observed length proxy, then scales future budget estimates by the learned bias. - The good: Across 3 runs and ~1180 unique prompts, EMA adaptation reduced token estimation MAE by ~39% and RMSE by ~40% vs. static budgets, and SJF with corrected estimates cut P50 end-to-end latency 42% relative to FIFO (107s vs. 185s). - The missing: All experiments use Qwen1.5-1.8B-Chat on a single L4 GPU; whether bias-convergence values, drift magnitudes, or the SJF latency advantage transfer to production-scale models (7B–70B+) or multi-GPU deployments is entirely untested and not claimed with evidence. - Read it or not: Researchers designing LLM inference schedulers from scratch may find the scheduler policy comparison instructive, but the core mechanism is trivially simple, the evaluation scale is toy, and practitioners already running vLLM or Orca will find no deployable upgrade here.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (30) and Non-Obviousness (28) are low — per-category EMA bias correction is a decades-old technique applied to a new observable, and the 'token drift' phenomenon is implicit in existing vLLM/Orca behavior. Rigor (38) and Resilience (30) are weak: a toy 1.8B model on a single L4 under artificial extreme overload, a whitespace word-count proxy that can deviate 2-5× from real token counts and invalidate the 128/512 classification thresholds, an unstated EMA learning rate, and no comparison to any real adaptive scheduler (Orca, Sarathi, vLLM's own). This resembles the low-rigor negative-calibration set more than the positive set; the public code and reported per-run std on tail latency are the only meaningful credibility supports.
systems Novelty 30 Rigor 38 Non-Obv 28 Completeness 48 Resilience 30 Impact 42
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-tenant LLM inference services on shared GPU infrastructure must schedule heterogeneous requests that compete for memory and compute. A fundamental difficulty is that schedulers must estimate job cost before execution: most production systems use user-specified max_tokens as a proxy. Because models routinely stop generating well before the token ceiling — due to EOS tokens, prompt-length variation, and model behavior — the static estimate systematically overestimates actual cost. The overestimation propagates into workload classification (short requests labeled as long), queue ordering for policies like SJF that are cost-sensitive, and admission decisions under contention. The paper's target setting is a single-GPU enterprise inference node serving premium, standard, and batch tenants simultaneously under GPU saturation. Key Idea The core insight is that the ratio of actual output length to baseline budget estimate is stable per workload category and can be tracked online using an EMA. Once the category-level bias stabilizes, multiplying future estimates by the learned bias shifts them toward the realized distribution, reducing classification error for cost-sensitive schedulers. This insight is real but modest: EMA-based ratio tracking is a decades-old technique applied to a new observable (LLM output length vs. a token ceiling). Methodology DriftSched consists of five layers: (1) workload generation — 1180 unique prompts across four semantic categories (short_qa, summary, technical, report) submitted by 50 concurrent Python threads; (2) workload analysis — an adaptive token estimator computes T_estimated_output = T_base × B_runtime × S_tenant × F_input, and a workload classifier maps total budget to {short ≤128, medium 128–512, long >512}; (3) tenant queue management — three Redis-backed queues (Premium, Standard, Batch); (4) scheduling engine — five policies: FIFO, Priority, Weighted (50/30/20 ratio), SJF, and Aging Priority; (5) adaptive feedback — an EMA updates B_runtime after each inference completion using B_measured = T_actual/T_base, where T_actual is whitespace-delimited word count, not actual tokenizer token count. The EMA learning rate α is used but its value is not stated in the paper. Experiments run 3000 requests per trial: 1000 calibration + 2000 stress, on a single NVIDIA L4 (24 GB) running vLLM with Qwen1.5-1.8B-Chat in FP16. Critical design decisions: using word count instead of tokenizer tokens (acknowledged as a known approximation), per-category rather than per-request bias (coarse but tractable), and a fixed 1:2 calibration/evaluation split (not ablated). Key Results All results are from a single L4 GPU running a 1.8B model under artificial saturation (3 runs, 3000 requests each, 50 concurrent clients). Adaptive bias correction vs. static baseline: average MAE reduction 38.8%, RMSE reduction 40.5%, consistent across all five schedulers (FIFO: 39.5%/41.4%, SJF: 36.8%/37.2%). Learned bias values converged to 0.79–0.84, confirming that static max_tokens estimates overestimate actual output by 16–21%. Scheduler comparison (BIAS=ON): SJF achieves P50=107s, P95=491s, P99=526s; FIFO achieves P50=185s, P95=593s, P99=630s — SJF is ~42% lower at median, ~16% lower at P99. Aging Priority performs worst on tail latency (P99=645s) due to fairness-driven promotions. Tenant-level: Priority scheduling reduces Premium latency to 77s but inflates Batch to 427s; SJF inverts this (Batch=95s, Premium=227s), demonstrating a fundamental tradeoff between global latency optimization and tenant QoS enforcement. GPU utilization: 85–92% across all schedulers (independent of policy), and GPU memory stable at ~14.5 GB — confirming that observed end-to-end latency differences are attributable to queue management rather than GPU execution cost. The absolute latency values (P50 ~100–250s, P99 ~526–645s) reflect deliberate extreme overload conditions and are not representative of realistic serving deployments. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The token-drift phenomenon and its systematic overestimation direction are described as newly named, though the underlying observation (LLMs often generate fewer tokens than max_tokens) is implicit in the vLLM and Orca literature. The adaptive EMA bias correction is a straightforward application of standard online estimation to this specific signal. The scheduler comparison study is an empirical contribution for the LLM inference scheduling domain, though it applies entirely classical algorithms (FIFO, SJF, aging priority) without modification. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available at https://github.com/kpalania1/driftsched. Three independent runs are reported with standard deviations for tail latency (σ95 and σ99 in Table III), which is appropriate but minimal — no seed reporting, no statistical significance tests. No ablation on the EMA learning rate α (value never stated). No ablation on the 1:2 calibration/evaluation split. The workload corpus of 1180 prompts across 4 categories is synthetic, not from production traces. The primary proxy metric — whitespace-delimited word count — is explicitly not equivalent to tokenizer token count and is acknowledged as future work to replace. The paper self-cites two other arXiv preprints by the same single author [1][2] as the main prior work context. No peer review is indicated; this is an arXiv preprint. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: Runs on a single commercially available NVIDIA L4 GPU using vLLM; the overlay scheduler adds only lightweight Redis queuing and EMA computation with negligible overhead. - Efficient: The adaptive estimator adds no GPU overhead — all computation is CPU-side post-inference bookkeeping; the EMA update is O(1) per request per workload category. - Effective: The evidence shows it reduces estimation error and improves SJF latency under deliberate overload on a 1.8B model; whether it solves the problem for production serving workloads with larger models, real prompt distributions, and realistic load levels is not demonstrated. - Scalable: Not tested beyond a single GPU and a single 1.8B model; multi-GPU scheduling, larger models (where KV-cache growth and prefill/decode dynamics change substantially), and real traffic patterns are left as future work with no empirical basis for extrapolation. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo) plus a benchmarking framework, but stops at single-GPU, single-model, synthetic-workload validation. Strengths - The empirical confirmation that static max_tokens estimates consistently overestimate actual output by ~20% is quantified cleanly across five schedulers, providing a concrete number useful for practitioners. - The tenant-level latency decomposition (Table V) clearly exposes the SJF-vs-priority tradeoff: SJF minimizes global latency by ignoring tenant class, while Priority inverts Batch and Premium latency — a practically important observation for operators choosing a scheduling policy. - The isolation of queue-side vs. GPU-execution-side latency contributions (GPU inference P99 ~11s vs. end-to-end P99 ~526–645s) is a useful diagnostic demonstrating that scheduling dominates under saturation. - Code and workload corpus are released publicly, enabling reproduction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) word-count proxy rather than exact tokenizer token counts, with plans to fix in future work; (2) single GPU platform and single LLM model (Qwen1.5-1.8B-Chat), limiting generalizability to different architectures and scales; (3) synthetic workload corpus that may not capture continuously evolving production prompt distributions. These limitations are disclosed directly and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The EMA learning rate α is never stated — the most sensitive hyperparameter in the entire system is unreported, making reproduction and comparison impossible without the code. - No comparison against any existing adaptive scheduler or production inference system (Orca, Sarathi, vLLM's own scheduler). The baselines are purely classical OS scheduling algorithms. This omits the most relevant related work. - Qwen1.5-1.8B-Chat generates very short responses by design; production LLMs (7B–70B+) have different generation dynamics, KV-cache pressure, and decode/prefill ratios. The 0.79–0.84 bias range and convergence speed may not transfer. - The word-count proxy is not just an approximation — for code generation, multilingual output, or structured data, whitespace-delimited counts can deviate from token counts by 2–5×, invalidating the classification thresholds (128/512 tokens) used for short/medium/long labeling. - The experiment runs at deliberate extreme overload (P50 latency ~107–185 seconds for a model that generates responses in ~10 seconds of GPU time). This regime, where queue wait dominates by 10–20×, is a stress test, not a realistic serving scenario; policy tradeoffs may differ substantially at moderate utilization. - Jain's fairness index or other formal fairness metrics are not computed — the fairness discussion is qualitative. - The paper cites only its own prior arXiv preprints [1][2] as the primary related work for GPU architectural context; no engagement with concurrent LLM scheduling literature (e.g., Clockwork, AlpaServe, Shepherd, SpotServe) that directly addresses multi-tenant LLM serving. Bottom Line DriftSched documents a real and practically relevant phenomenon (systematic token overestimation) and applies a correct but trivial fix (per-category EMA bias), but the single-author arXiv preprint evaluates only on a toy 1.8B model with a word-count proxy for tokens, omits any comparison to existing adaptive schedulers, and cannot support conclusions about production-scale deployments — making it a starting point for exploration rather than a deployable or publishable contribution in its current form.
Search Results 2606.02982 PDF
#188 — Incremental (37)

Development and verification of an orchestration architecture of AI agents for automated API testing with a unified representation of requirements

M Moskalenko · journals.uran.ua · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a four-level AI agent orchestration architecture formalized as T = G(O(A(S))) that generates deterministic API test suites by confining LLM non-determinism to a requirements-extraction layer and feeding a fixed-schema intermediate representation R to a fully deterministic test-generation algorithm G. - Why it's important: LLM-based test generation exhibits ±12% coverage standard deviation between runs, which breaks CI/CD pipelines that require reproducible regression suites; meanwhile, specification-only tools miss roughly 25% of requirements embedded in textual documentation — the proposed architecture targets both failure modes simultaneously. - How it does it: Specialized agents parse each requirements source (OpenAPI spec via openapi-spec-validator, Confluence-style docs via GPT-4 API with JSON-mode output) into structured fragments; an orchestration layer deduplicates them (Jaccard 0.8 + TF-IDF cosine 0.85) and resolves conflicts by "strictest constraint wins"; a pure-Python algorithm G then applies ISTQB equivalence partitioning and boundary-value analysis to R, producing pytest files with zero further LLM calls. - The good: The 3.7× reduction in test maintenance effort (65.7% → 17.7% manual updates across three requirement-change types) is the most practically credible result, because it reflects the value of decoupling requirement sources from test scripts through the R abstraction. - The missing: The entire evaluation runs on a 5-endpoint synthetic FastAPI service built by the author for the experiment; RESTler, EvoMaster, and RESTTestGen — discussed in the literature review — are not run as baselines, making the performance gains impossible to contextualize against the actual state of practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for software engineers evaluating LLM-augmented CI/CD pipelines who want a concrete architectural pattern; insufficient as a research contribution for ISSTA/FSE/TSE audiences due to the toy-scale self-designed benchmark and absent statistical significance testing.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (30) and Novelty (40) cap the composite. The architectural separation of stochastic extraction from deterministic generation (Property 1) is a sound principle, but the entire evaluation runs on a self-built 5-endpoint synthetic API deliberately seeded with three text-only rules — a best-case design — with no established baselines (RESTler/EvoMaster/RESTTestGen are reviewed but never run), no significance tests despite 20 trials, and no public artifact (data 'on request'). Resilience (35) suffers from the unjustified temperature=0.1 choice that reintroduces the very non-determinism the architecture claims to eliminate. The 3.7× maintenance reduction is the most credible result, but the narrow synthetic basis leaves the quantitative claims unconvincing.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Software teams practicing Agile/DevOps must regenerate API test suites continuously. Two incumbent approaches fail in complementary ways: specification-driven tools (RESTler, EvoMaster, RESTTestGen) achieve 70–80% coverage because OpenAPI specs omit business rules that exist only in textual documentation (e.g., a Confluence page stating "age > 18" when the spec declares age as an unconstrained integer); direct LLM invocation reaches higher potential coverage but yields test sets differing by 20–40% between identical runs (σ = ±12%), making them unsuitable for reproducible CI/CD regression. RAG-based approaches inherit the same non-determinism because the LLM still both interprets requirements and generates tests in a single stochastic pass. The gap targeted is the absence of a formalized mechanism that simultaneously integrates heterogeneous requirement sources and guarantees reproducible test output. Key Idea The central insight is to decouple requirements interpretation (inherently stochastic when done by an LLM) from test generation (which can be fully deterministic) by inserting a fixed-schema intermediate representation R between the two stages. All non-determinism is quarantined in agents A at extraction time; once R is formed, the generation algorithm G is a pure function of R. The formal Property 1 states: if G is deterministic with respect to R, then semantically equivalent source sets S and S′ always yield the same T — because the orchestration layer normalizes both to the same R. Methodology The architecture has four functional levels. Level 1 — source-specific agents run in parallel: a spec agent (Python, openapi-spec-validator, pydantic) processes OpenAPI 3.0 syntactically; a doc agent calls GPT-4 API (gpt-4-0125-preview, temperature=0.1, seed=42, JSON-mode, confidence threshold 0.7) to extract business rules as structured {entity, endpoint, constraint_type, value, confidence, source_sentence} objects; an issue-tracker agent is architecturally specified but not evaluated in the experiment. Level 2 — orchestration layer aggregates fragment sets by (entity, endpoint), deduplicates via two-pass filter (Jaccard token similarity > 0.8, then TF-IDF cosine similarity > 0.85), and resolves conflicts by "strictest constraint wins." Level 3 — unified representation R is a fixed-schema JSON document with five components: endpoint list E, parameter descriptions P (type, mandatory, min/max, format), response schemes RS, business rules BR, and edge-cases. Level 4 — generation algorithm G is a pure Python script that applies equivalence partitioning EC(p), boundary value analysis BV(p) = {min−1, min, min+1, max−1, max, max+1}, and invalid value sets IV(p) per ISTQB, producing parameterized pytest files. End-to-end latency is 47–63 seconds for 5 endpoints, of which ~40 seconds is GPT-4 network latency. Three critical design decisions: (a) temperature=0.1 rather than 0, justified by claimed instability of some GPT-4 API versions at zero — no citation provided; (b) two-pass deduplication to avoid the cost of full TF-IDF on all fragment pairs; (c) strictest-constraint-wins conflict resolution — unjustified for cases where a looser constraint is intentionally correct. Key Results The experiment runs 20 independent trials on a synthetic CRUD REST API (5 endpoints, 18 parameters, 12 business rules — 3 of which exist only in text documentation) built in Python/FastAPI and deployed in Docker by the author. Three approaches compared, all using the author's own implementations: specification-only (single-source OpenAPI generator), LLM-direct (raw GPT-4 context, identical prompt per run), and proposed. Coverage C(T): proposed 88–92% (avg 90.2%, σ=±2%) vs. spec 72–78% (avg 75.1%, σ=±3%) vs. LLM 55–82% (avg 68.3%, σ=±12%). Reproducibility R_rep: proposed 0.97 vs. spec 0.95 vs. LLM 0.62. Maintenance M_maint after three controlled requirement changes (new constraint, changed business rule, new endpoint): proposed 17.7% vs. spec 65.7% vs. LLM 46.7%. Total test counts: proposed 64–69, spec 47–52, LLM 31–58. The ±2% residual variance in the proposed approach is traced to three ambiguously worded conditional business rules in the documentation, not to algorithm G. Baselines are weak: established tools (RESTler, EvoMaster, RESTTestGen) are cited in the literature review but not evaluated; no significance tests accompany the numerical comparisons. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (prototype). The specific contribution is the compositional architecture T = G(O(A(S))) and its Property 1 formalization. The individual components — multi-agent orchestration, intermediate schema representations, LLM-based NLP extraction, equivalence-partitioning test generation — are all established; the novelty is their specific composition as a means to achieve reproducibility by boundary-isolating the stochastic component. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are performed; the contribution of each individual component (spec agent alone + G, doc agent alone + G, orchestration deduplication vs. none) is not isolated. No statistical significance tests (t-test, Mann-Whitney) despite 20-run data that would support them. R_rep = 0.97 measures syntactic test identity (string matching), not semantic equivalence — the paper acknowledges this gap and defers a semantic metric to future work. No code, dataset, or trained artifact is publicly released; the data availability statement says "upon reasonable request." The benchmark API was designed by the author specifically for the experiment with business rules deliberately split between sources — a best-case scenario for the proposed architecture. The spec and LLM baselines are the author's own implementations, not canonical tools. Published in Technology Audit and Production Reserves (Ukrainian journal), not a top-tier SE venue; peer-reviewed but with unknown rigor. Single author. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (prototype system evaluated by comparative experiment). - Practical: Requires GPT-4 API access (OpenAI subscription) and Python 3.11 runtime — both commercially available; no specialized hardware needed. - Efficient: 47–63 seconds per full cycle for 5 endpoints, of which ~40 seconds is GPT-4 network latency; cost per run in API tokens is not reported, a meaningful omission for CI/CD integration at scale. - Effective: Demonstrates 90.2% average coverage and 17.7% maintenance effort on a synthetic API designed to reward the proposed approach; whether these numbers hold on real, inconsistent industrial documentation is not tested. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 5 endpoints; GPT-4 latency and cost scale with documentation volume; conflict resolution and deduplication thresholds were empirically calibrated for one documentation corpus and require recalibration per project. The paper acknowledges 50+ endpoint behavior is future work. The work delivers a Python prototype (not a released library or CLI) — it stops essentially at paper form with reproducible-on-request artifacts. Strengths - The architectural separation of stochastic requirements extraction from deterministic test generation is a sound engineering principle, and Property 1 provides a formal guarantee (given its assumptions) rather than an empirical claim of reproducibility alone. - The residual ±2% variance is honestly traced to three specific ambiguous documentation phrases rather than attributed to measurement noise — and the engineering path to σ=0% (prompt standardization for conditional constructs) is clearly identified. - The maintenance metric (17.7% vs. 65.7% manual updates) is the most practically meaningful measure in the paper because it directly quantifies CI/CD workflow cost, not just a coverage proxy. - The limitations section is unusually candid for a solo-author paper: scale, documentation-style sensitivity, single-LLM dependency, and the semantic-vs-syntactic reproducibility distinction are all explicitly acknowledged. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) scale — 5 endpoints is far below industrial; 50+ endpoint behavior is hypothesized but untested; (2) documentation style — the NLP agent degrades when documentation is descriptive rather than structured with explicit if-then conditionals; the approach is inapplicable when documentation lags the codebase by years; (3) single LLM model — confidence threshold 0.7 is GPT-4-specific; other models require separate calibration; (4) the issue tracker agent S_issues is in the architecture but not validated, deferred to future work; (5) R_rep is syntactic, not semantic — a semantic equivalence metric is future work; (6) Jaccard/TF-IDF thresholds are corpus-specific and need recalibration per project. Analyst-Identified Gaps - RESTler, EvoMaster, and RESTTestGen are reviewed in the paper but not evaluated as baselines. Omitting them means the 13–17 percentage-point coverage gain cannot be compared against the actual state of practice in REST API test automation. - The synthetic benchmark was designed by the author to have exactly three business rules absent from the spec — the scenario that most advantages the proposed approach. There is no evaluation on an existing open-source API with pre-existing documentation of varying quality. - With 20 runs per approach, paired significance tests are computationally trivial; their absence means the coverage and reproducibility differences between proposed and spec-only (90.2% vs. 75.1%, R_rep 0.97 vs. 0.95) cannot be distinguished from sampling variation. - The "strictest constraint wins" conflict resolution policy is stated without any validation that it produces semantically correct tests. Cases where the specification intentionally relaxes a documentation rule (e.g., a beta endpoint with looser validation) would produce incorrect tests silently. - The claim that temperature=0 causes unpredictable GPT-4 API behavior is stated as fact without citation; using temperature=0.1 deliberately introduces the non-determinism the architecture is designed to eliminate, and the resulting ±2% variance is an architectural consequence of this choice, not of ambiguous documentation alone. - GPT-4 API call cost (tokens consumed per run, total experiment cost) is not reported — a material omission for teams evaluating whether 47-second, API-cost-incurring test regeneration on every commit is economically viable. Bottom Line The architectural separation principle is sound and clearly demonstrated, but the self-designed 5-endpoint benchmark, the absence of established tool baselines (RESTler, EvoMaster), and the lack of significance testing leave the quantitative claims on too narrow an empirical foundation to justify adoption without independent replication on real industrial APIs.
Search Results
#189 — Incremental (37)

Agentic Video Summarization via Self-Reflecting Multimodal Understanding

M Guo, S Dou, Y Li, A Men, D Jiang · openaccess.thecvf.com · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AgenticVS, a three-agent pipeline (Summarizer, Verifier, Reflector) for video summarization that uses a multimodal LLM to iteratively detect and recalibrate missed keyframes through a closed-loop summarize–verify–reflect cycle. - Why it's important: One-shot frame-importance regression systems cannot self-correct when they assign low scores to genuinely important keyframes, and missed keyframes degrade summarization quality with no automatic recovery mechanism. - How it does it: A dual-encoder V2I Alignment module fuses image- and video-level features for initial scoring, a 5-round MLLM dialogue assigns per-frame confidence values, and a training-free Reflector replaces low-confidence scores with CLIP cosine similarities against an MLLM-generated video caption. - The good: AgenticVS outperforms all compared baselines on SumMe (ρ=0.308) and TVSum (ρ=0.290), and a concrete ablation finding—that MLLM direct scoring is inferior to MLLM-for-understanding plus CLIP-for-scoring—is a useful, generalizable insight. - The missing: Evaluation covers only 25 and 50 videos respectively with no error bars or significance tests; the Verifier uses ground-truth annotated training videos at inference time, undermining its "training-free" characterization; and computational cost versus single-pass baselines is never reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building MLLM-augmented video pipelines specifically to understand the V2I alignment ablations and the MLLM+CLIP scoring insight, but the headline "agentic" claims and benchmark-scale results should not be taken at face value.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The MLLM-as-captioner + CLIP-as-scorer decomposition and V2I alignment ablations yield a transferable insight (Non-Obviousness 50, Novelty 45), but the evidence base is thin. Rigor (30) and Resilience (30) are the drag: benchmarks of only 25 and 50 videos with no error bars where 0.012 ρ gains are plausibly noise, a 'training-free' Verifier that actually consumes ground-truth annotations at inference, an overstated 'agentic' label for a fixed three-stage pipeline, unreported compute cost, and above-human correlation scores that invite skepticism. No artifact released (Impact 35). Weighted: 0.20*45+0.25*30+0.15*50+0.15*35+0.15*30+0.10*35 = 37.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 35 Resilience 30 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Extractive video summarization requires assigning frame-level importance scores to select a compact keyframe set. CNN- and LSTM-based regressors lack holistic semantic understanding; MLLM-augmented methods like LLMVS improve semantic grounding but still operate in a single forward pass, producing scores that cannot be revised once a key event is missed. When a frame's initial score is erroneously low—often because the model lacks global context about event transitions—no subsequent correction occurs. This leaves a systematic gap: the ability to detect and recover missed keyframes through autonomous self-correction. Key Idea: The core insight is that a multimodal LLM can serve two distinct roles in a pipeline: first as a confidence auditor (using multi-round dialogue to identify which low importance scores are unreliable), and then as a semantic descriptor (generating a caption that CLIP can use to compute more discriminative frame similarity scores). Separating these roles across three sequential agents avoids the problem of asking the MLLM to directly output precise numerical scores—something it does poorly due to limited inter-frame score variation. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) The Summarizer extracts image-level embeddings via GoogLeNet and video-level embeddings via VideoMAEv2 (8-frame sliding window, space-time cube tokens). Temporal attention pooling collapses the temporal dimension; an AdaptMLP with LayerNorm and GELU maps video features into the image embedding space. Training loss combines MSE alignment with a residual regularization term (λ=10⁻³, Eq. 3). The resulting aligned embeddings feed CSTA to produce initial importance scores S. (2) The Verifier, using Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct in a 5-round structured dialogue, first learns scoring patterns from ground-truth annotated training videos, then assigns per-frame confidence values (0–1), computes an adaptive threshold θs = mean(S) − 0.5·std(S) to flag low-score frames, and decides: if score is low and confidence is also low, trigger the Reflector; if score is low but confidence is high, accept the frame as a non-keyframe. (3) The Reflector (also training-free) prompts Qwen2.5-VL to generate a holistic video caption, then computes cosine similarity between each flagged frame and the caption using CLIP-ViT-B/32 (Eq. 2). These calibrated scores are normalized and rescaled to match the initial score distribution, replacing the flagged scores. Critical design decisions: V2I over I2V direction (empirically justified, ρ 0.296 vs. 0.265), temporal attention pooling over mean pooling (ρ 0.296 vs. 0.270), and CLIP scoring over MLLM direct scoring (τ 0.116/ρ 0.128 vs. τ 0.073/ρ 0.081 for direct Qwen2.5-VL). Key Results: On SumMe (25 videos): AgenticVS achieves τ=0.274, ρ=0.308, vs. best prior visual method CSTA at τ=0.246, ρ=0.274 and best visual+text method LLMVS at τ=0.253, ρ=0.282. On TVSum (50 videos): τ=0.220, ρ=0.290, vs. LLMVS τ=0.211, ρ=0.275. The V2I Alignment alone (Summarizer without agentic loop) reaches ρ=0.296 on SumMe; adding Verifier+Reflector adds 0.012 ρ. Notably, AgenticVS exceeds the paper's stated human performance (SumMe ρ=0.213, TVSum ρ=0.204) on both benchmarks—a result that warrants skepticism about what these correlation metrics actually capture rather than indicating superhuman performance. The train-free Reflector alone achieves τ=0.116, ρ=0.128, roughly matching supervised method A2Summ (τ=0.108, ρ=0.129). Baselines include both older methods (VASNet, DSNet) and recent ones (CSTA 2024, LLMVS 2025). F1-score is deliberately excluded, limiting comparability with the majority of the video summarization literature. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The genuinely novel elements are: (a) the V2I alignment module combining VideoMAEv2 temporal attention pooling with AdaptMLP projection, and (b) the two-role MLLM usage pattern—confidence auditing via multi-round dialogue and caption-driven CLIP recalibration. The individual components (CLIP, Qwen2.5-VL, VideoMAEv2, CSTA, multi-round prompting) are all established; the contribution is their specific composition into a corrective pipeline. The "agentic" label overstates the autonomy—the three components execute a fixed sequential workflow, not a flexible planning loop with tool use or dynamic replanning. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation tables (Tables 2, 3, 4) systematically test component contributions, pooling strategies, alignment directions, and scoring mechanisms, which is a genuine strength. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported on benchmarks of 25 and 50 videos—at this scale, 0.01 differences in τ/ρ are plausibly within noise. No code or model weights are released (not mentioned). A significant methodological concern: the Verifier's Round 2 prompt explicitly instructs the MLLM to study ground-truth annotated training videos to learn scoring patterns, which constitutes supervised signal used at inference time; calling the Verifier and Reflector "training-free" is misleading. Evaluation avoids F1 (citing known limitations) but this choice conveniently sidesteps the dominant metric in the community. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA V100 GPUs with batch size 1; all components are commercially available models. - Efficient: Inference cost is not reported; the pipeline requires at least two Qwen2.5-VL-7B calls plus CLIP scoring per video, on top of the base CSTA model, implying substantial latency overhead versus single-pass methods—this gap is never quantified. - Effective: Improves Spearman ρ by 0.012–0.015 over the strongest prior baseline on the two evaluated benchmarks, with consistent direction across metrics and datasets. - Scalable: Tested only on videos up to a few hundred frames from SumMe/TVSum; the paper lists long videos and multi-view data as future work, providing no evidence at larger scale. The work delivers a paper-form pipeline description; no software artifact or trained model is released. Strengths: - The V2I alignment ablations (Table 3) are well-controlled and reveal a genuine direction-of-alignment asymmetry (V2I > I2V) and pooling-strategy effect (temporal attention > mean), providing reproducible design guidance. - The finding that MLLM direct importance scoring is inferior to MLLM-as-captioner + CLIP-as-scorer (Table 4) is a concrete, transferable insight applicable beyond this specific system. - Replacing hand-crafted, video-specific prompts with a unified instructional prompt reduces subjective bias and makes the system more practically deployable across domains. - The paper provides genuine motivation for why holistic video understanding (global event context) is needed to catch missed keyframes that local-window models miss—backed by the qualitative examples in Figure 5. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that the Summarizer lacks holistic video understanding (framed as motivation for the Verifier/Reflector, not stated as a residual limitation). The conclusion identifies long videos, multi-view data, and additional modalities as future extensions, implicitly acknowledging the current scope is limited to standard-length single-view YouTube clips. No limitations on benchmark scale, computational cost, or the supervised signal in the Verifier are explicitly stated. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The Verifier uses ground-truth annotated training video examples at inference time to learn scoring patterns; this is unlabeled at training time but labeled at inference time—a form of in-context supervision that the paper describes as "training-free," which is misleading. (2) At SumMe's 25-video scale, the difference between AgenticVS (ρ=0.308) and LLMVS (ρ=0.282) is 0.026 ρ with no statistical test; this could easily be noise. (3) Inference time per video is unreported, making the compute-quality tradeoff unassessable. (4) The F1 exclusion, while cited to one paper, prevents comparison with the dominant metric used in >80% of video summarization literature. (5) The "agentic" framing implies autonomy and tool use; the actual system is a fixed three-stage pipeline with no dynamic control flow, replanning, or tool invocation—closer to a multi-module system than an agent. (6) No out-of-domain or cross-dataset generalization is tested; both training and evaluation use SumMe/TVSum. Bottom Line: The V2I alignment module and the MLLM-for-captioning + CLIP-for-scoring decomposition are the paper's genuinely useful contributions, but the results rest on implausibly small benchmarks without significance testing, the "agentic" and "training-free" claims are substantially overstated, and a practitioner will learn more from Tables 3 and 4 than from the headline numbers.
Citations
#190 — Incremental (34)

Evaluating the Accuracy of GUI Testing Using Multi-Modal Large Language Models

A Lindeborg, K Ödquist · diva-portal.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis implements GUIOracle, a three-stage (Specification → Interaction → Verification) pipeline for screenshot-based GUI test automation using locally deployed multi-modal LLMs, and empirically evaluates five Qwen 3.5 model sizes (0.8B–27B) across two GUI environments. - Why it's important: Industrial GUI testing still depends heavily on brittle record-and-playback scripts or manual execution that must be repeated after every interface change; a screenshot-and-natural-language approach could reduce that maintenance burden without requiring access to internal application APIs. - How it does it: OmniParser V2 parses each screenshot into labeled bounding boxes; the M-LLM selects one element-level action per step from a fixed action set; a separate verification model judges the final GUI state against a pre-generated oracle specification. - The good: The 9B model achieves 95.2% action accuracy and 100% scenario success in Tacsi and 90.9% / 100% in OpenScope under reference conditions, demonstrating that mid-size locally deployable models can reliably execute predefined GUI test scenarios. - The missing: The evaluation covers only 3 manually constructed scenarios per environment, all scenarios are short (≤5 intended actions, ≤10 step limit), and there is no comparison against any traditional automated GUI testing baseline (record-and-replay, Selenium, VGT tools), making it impossible to judge absolute value. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners evaluating local M-LLM deployment for GUI testing in confidentiality-constrained industrial settings; can be skipped by researchers seeking peer-reviewed evidence or comparisons against established GUI automation methods.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is a competently-executed master's thesis but sits in the Incremental band because Rigor (30) and Non-Obviousness (30) are weak: the pipeline is a standard OmniParser+Ollama+stage-separation engineering combination, evaluated on only 3 manually-constructed scenarios per environment with N=5 runs, no error bars or significance tests, no released code, and no peer review. The decisive gap is the complete absence of any comparison against traditional GUI-testing baselines (Selenium, record-and-replay), making it impossible to judge whether the approach delivers real value rather than merely beating smaller models.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 45 Resilience 35 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Industrial GUI testing — especially acceptance-level and regression testing of complex desktop simulators — relies heavily on manual execution because record-and-playback scripts break whenever the interface changes. Software QA teams in safety-relevant industries (e.g., defense simulation at Saab) face increasing interface complexity and CI/CD cadence, making manual GUI testing a scalability bottleneck. Prior M-LLM-based GUI agent work focuses on web navigation, grounding accuracy, or open-ended exploration rather than measuring step-level accuracy on predefined, sequential test scripts in controlled industrial environments. Confidentiality constraints also prevent use of cloud-based frontier models for interfaces containing sensitive operational data, motivating evaluation of locally deployable models. Key Idea: Use OmniParser V2's element-based parsed screenshot representation — each interactive region assigned a transient numeric ID — to turn coordinate-free element selection into the M-LLM's output, then separate specification (oracle generation before interaction), interaction (one grounded action per step), and verification (post-hoc semantic and deterministic verdict) into three independent pipeline stages. This element-centric, stage-separated design makes each action traceable and avoids the pixel-regression fragility of direct coordinate prediction. Methodology: GUIOracle proceeds in three stages. (1) Specification Stage: given the natural-language test instruction and an optional initial screenshot, a 27B Qwen model generates a JSON oracle (required typed values, action types, milestones) and a natural-language outcome description; this is frozen for the run. (2) Interaction Stage: at each step, PyAutoGUI captures a screenshot, OmniParser V2 detects interactable elements with bounding boxes and IDs, and the interaction model receives the instruction, current parsed state, and action history and returns a single JSON-structured action (left_click, type, double_click, key, scroll, wait, or None); the action is executed via PyAutoGUI; loop terminates at task completion, None output, or 10-step timeout. (3) Verification Stage: combines deterministic checks (oracle JSON vs. action history) with a semantic judge (LLM evaluates final parsed state against expected_outcome_text). Five Qwen 3.5 model variants (0.8B Q8, 2B Q8, 4B Q4_K_M, 9B Q4_K_M, 27B Q4_K_M) are evaluated via Ollama on local consumer hardware. All stages use zero-shot prompting. Specification stage always uses 27B; interaction and verification stages vary by model size condition. Each of 3 scenarios per environment is repeated 5 times per model size, yielding 75 Tacsi runs (4 models × 3 scenarios × 5 repeats) and 75 OpenScope runs (5 models × 3 scenarios × 5 repeats). Two controlled variations — GUI layout complexity increase and simplified instruction wording — are also evaluated. Critical design decisions: (a) element-level rather than pixel-coordinate action selection (justified by precision concerns; adopted from REPEEK/Wu et al.); (b) zero-shot prompting (justified as keeping prompts compact and model-size comparisons clean; unjustified that few-shot might have closed much of the small-model gap); (c) 10-step hard limit (limits scope to short-horizon tasks; not explicitly justified against longer realistic workflows). Key Results: In Tacsi (4 model sizes, Linux, RTX 2000 Ada 8GB): action accuracy rises from 25.0% (0.8B) → 33.3% (2B) → 72.7% (4B) → 95.2% (9B); scenario success 13.3% → 20.0% → 73.3% → 100%. Avg. execution time per step: 13.2s / 13.8s / 19.3s / 26.5s. Verification-stage accuracy: 33.3% / 33.3% / 80.0% / 100%. Smallest models are dominated by timeouts (11/15 runs for 0.8B and 2B). In OpenScope (5 model sizes, Windows, RTX 4070 12GB): accuracy 29.9% → 31.0% → 76.0% → 90.9% → 100%; success 6.7% → 13.3% → 73.3% → 100% → 100%. Per-step time: 9.3s / 11.4s / 11.4s / 15.0s / 62.5s. Verification accuracy: 46.7% / 33.3% / 86.7% / 93.3% / 100%. GUI layout variation reduces performance more than instruction wording simplification for mid-size models (4B, 9B); 27B is unaffected across both conditions in OpenScope. 9B provides the best accuracy-efficiency balance in both environments; 27B achieves perfect accuracy but at 4× the per-step cost of 9B. No statistical significance tests or error bars are reported. Baselines against traditional GUI automation methods are absent. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The pipeline itself is a novel engineering combination of OmniParser V2 + Ollama + stage separation, but each individual component is a standard technique from prior work. The genuine novelty is the controlled empirical measurement: step-level action accuracy, sensitivity to GUI layout vs. instruction wording, and accuracy-efficiency trade-off across local M-LLM sizes, specifically in an industrially-relevant desktop simulation environment under confidentiality constraints. Comparison of layout sensitivity vs. wording sensitivity as distinct variation axes is a useful methodological contribution absent from prior GUI agent evaluation. Rigor & Reproducibility: No error bars, standard deviations, or statistical significance tests are reported; with N=5 per condition, variance estimates are unreliable. Manual labeling of action correctness and run success is performed by the thesis authors without inter-rater reliability measurement, introducing potential subjectivity. The Tacsi environment is not publicly available (access requires Saab affiliation), limiting full replicability. OpenScope portions are reproducible in principle but hardware and Ollama version differences could affect results. No code is released. All results are based on 5-run samples, making scenario-level success rate estimates (e.g., 40% = 2/5) coarse. The action accuracy denominator includes recovery and repeated actions, which conflates model capability with scenario difficulty. Ablations are absent: OmniParser vs. raw screenshot, zero-shot vs. few-shot, structured vs. unstructured output are not compared. The 27B model is not tested in Tacsi (hardware constraint), creating an asymmetry in the model-size coverage across environments. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL / SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs entirely on consumer hardware (RTX 2000 Ada 8GB for 0.8B–9B; RTX 4070 12GB for up to 27B); the 9B model requires approximately 6.6GB VRAM, available on mid-range GPUs. - Efficient: The 9B model takes 14–26 seconds per interaction step; a 5-step scenario takes roughly 75–130 seconds of Interaction Stage time, not counting Specification and Verification stages; the 27B model is 4× slower per step and thus impractical for frequent regression runs. - Effective: 9B achieves 100% scenario success on 3-scenario suites (3–5 intended actions each) in controlled environments with predefined initial states; effectiveness on longer, noisier, or more dynamic GUIs is untested and likely lower. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 5-action scenarios or 10-step limits; the thesis explicitly acknowledges that highly dynamic GUIs and long interaction sequences are out of scope; no evidence that results transfer to larger test suites. The work delivers a proof-of-concept Python pipeline using OmniParser V2, Ollama, PyAutoGUI, and Gradio; code is not publicly released. Strengths: - The controlled variation design (layout vs. wording, held separately) is methodologically clean and produces an actionable finding: GUI layout sensitivity dominates over instruction wording sensitivity, which informs prompt engineering priorities. - Evaluating five model sizes from the same family on the same tasks produces a clean scaling curve that separates model-size effects from architecture differences. - Including an industrially-relevant, non-public environment (Tacsi) alongside a reproducible open environment (OpenScope) strengthens external validity beyond purely academic benchmarks. - Verification-stage accuracy is measured separately from interaction accuracy, revealing that small models produce many false positives (incorrectly accepting failed runs), a practically important safety concern for any deployment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: small scenario suite size limits generalization; Tacsi and OpenScope use different hardware and OS, making cross-environment runtime comparison invalid; simulator state is not fully reproducible (aircraft positions vary); parsing errors conflate with model reasoning errors; manual correctness labeling requires subjective interpretation; the 27B model was not evaluated in Tacsi due to GPU memory constraints; only minor controlled variations (not major structural changes or highly dynamic GUIs) were tested. These are stated forthrightly and reflect genuine self-awareness. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious gap is the complete absence of any comparison against existing GUI testing tools (Selenium, Sikuli, record-and-replay, or even a scripted baseline), making it impossible to assess whether 130 seconds per test scenario represents a useful improvement over alternatives or is strictly slower and less reliable. Second, with only 5 runs per condition, the 20-percentage-point differences at the 4B–9B boundary could be partly noise; a 20% success rate (1/5 runs) is statistically indistinguishable from 40% (2/5) at conventional significance levels. Third, the decision to always use the 27B model for the Specification Stage conflates specification quality with interaction quality: if the oracle specification is imperfect for smaller interaction models, the verification-stage false positives may be oracle errors rather than interaction errors. Fourth, using zero-shot prompting throughout is not ablated against few-shot, which prior work (cited in the thesis) shows substantially improves performance for in-context grounding tasks — the finding that small models fail frequently may be an artifact of the zero-shot constraint rather than an intrinsic model capability limit. Fifth, the 10-step hard limit may disproportionately penalize mid-size models that require one or two recovery steps, biasing the timeout metric. Bottom Line: This is a competently executed master's thesis that delivers actionable empirical evidence — 9B local M-LLM as a practical sweet spot for screenshot-based GUI testing under confidentiality constraints — but the absence of any traditional baseline comparison and the very small sample sizes mean practitioners cannot yet assess whether this approach is competitive with existing automation tools rather than merely better than smaller M-LLMs.

#191 — Incremental (33)

Tourism Waste Management Governance in Denpasar City After the Closure of Suwung Landfill

IW Suwitantra, NPT Widanti · jurnaldialektika.com · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper analyzes the governance of tourism waste management in Denpasar City, Indonesia, following the permanent closure of the Suwung regional landfill, using collaborative governance and network governance frameworks applied to qualitative field data. - Why it's important: The closure of a decades-old landfill serving 1.2 million-ton/year Bali waste output exposes a structural governance vacuum in one of Southeast Asia's highest-density tourism economies, where no adequate decentralized alternative yet exists. - How it does it: Qualitative descriptive analysis combining semi-structured interviews with purposively sampled stakeholders, field observation of waste facilities, and document review of national and local regulations, interpreted through collaborative and network governance analytical lenses. - The good: The paper correctly identifies that historical landfill dependence created perverse institutional incentives that suppressed upstream segregation and area-based processing — a structural explanation for the current governance gap, not merely a compliance story. - The missing: No primary data (interview excerpts, observation records, or document matrices) are presented; the "findings" section reads as interpretive narrative without traceable empirical grounding, making the analytical claims unverifiable. - Read it or not: Skip unless you are a public administration student or practitioner specifically researching Indonesian municipal waste governance — the paper's contribution is contextual description, not transferable theory or replicable method.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (qualitative field study). Non-Obviousness (40) is the relative strength — the structural argument that landfill dependence created institutional moral hazard suppressing upstream governance investment is analytically coherent and under-explored. Rigor (30) and Completeness (35) pull the composite down: despite a described interview/observation/document methodology, no excerpts, field notes, or document matrices are presented, so it is impossible to verify the method was executed, and no quantitative anchors (diversion rates, capacity figures, baselines) exist. Novelty (32) is a weak context-specific application of established Ansell & Gash / Provan & Kenis frameworks. Weighted: 0.20*32 + 0.25*30 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*32 + 0.10*28 ≈ 33.
empirical Novelty 32 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 32 Impact 28
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Denpasar City is Bali's administrative and tourism hub, generating the province's largest share of the approximately 1.2 million tons of annual waste (Bali-wide figure per SIPSN). Waste peaks at 900–1,000 tons per day during major events. The Suwung Landfill, the regional disposal site for the Sarbagita metropolitan area (covering Denpasar and surrounds), reached overcapacity after three decades of operation and was slated for permanent closure with a transition period extending to 2026. The closure forces a shift from a collect–transport–dispose model to decentralized, source-based processing — but existing TPS 3R facilities, waste banks, and urban-ward processing units were built to complement, not replace, the landfill. The governance gap: inter-agency coordination between environmental management, sanitation, and tourism offices is sectoral rather than integrated; tourism businesses (major waste producers) are treated as compliance targets rather than governance co-actors; and monitoring and enforcement of existing regulations are inconsistently applied. Key Idea: The paper's central analytical claim is that landfill closure is primarily a governance transformation problem, not a technical one: the landfill's existence suppressed institutional incentives to invest in upstream infrastructure and multi-actor coordination, so its removal forces a structural reset. Applying collaborative governance (Ansell & Gash) and network governance (Rhodes; Provan & Kenis) frameworks, the authors argue the post-closure period requires institutionalized multi-actor forums, formalized role distribution (MoUs, SLAs), and horizontal accountability mechanisms — moving from government-centric hierarchical control toward cross-sectoral network governance. This framing is not novel in the public administration literature but represents its first formal application to the Suwung post-closure context. Methodology: The study uses qualitative descriptive design. Informant selection is purposive, targeting: (1) officials from city environmental management and sanitation agencies; (2) Tourism Office staff; (3) TPS 3R and area-based unit managers; (4) hotel/restaurant/destination managers; (5) waste bank and environmental community managers; (6) academics and experts in waste management/sustainable tourism. Data collection combines semi-structured interviews, direct observation of waste management practices in tourism zones and processing facilities, and documentary analysis of national laws (Law No. 18/2008, Government Regulation No. 81/2012), ministerial regulations, and city-level policies including Bali Governor Regulation No. 97/2018 on single-use plastics. Analysis follows Miles, Huberman & Saldaña's qualitative stages: data reduction, display (narratives, tables, matrices), and conclusion drawing with source and method triangulation. The analytical framework operationalizes five governance dimensions: regulation, institutions, coordination, participation, and accountability — each assessed against indicator sets drawn from collaborative and network governance theory. Critical design gap: the paper describes this methodology but presents no excerpts from interviews, no observation field notes, no documentary matrices, and no illustrative cases. The results section is entirely narrative synthesis, making it impossible to evaluate whether the method was actually executed as described. Key Results: The paper reports four principal findings, all stated as qualitative assessments without supporting data excerpts: (1) Denpasar's regulatory framework is formally adequate (national law + local ordinances mandate source segregation, 3R infrastructure, and tourism business responsibility) but on-the-ground compliance is predominantly administrative rather than behavioral. (2) Institutional roles are formally defined but inter-agency coordination remains sectoral; the Environmental Agency dominates while the Tourism Office plays an advisory role and ward governments manage facility operations without integration into a city-level collaborative framework. (3) Coordination between government and tourism businesses is persuasive/voluntary rather than contractual; large hotels independently engage third-party waste contractors while SMEs lack resources or access; environmental communities and waste banks operate sporadically. (4) Monitoring, enforcement, and financing mechanisms for waste management are identified as the weakest governance elements. No quantitative indicators are provided for any of these findings — no compliance rates, facility capacity figures, waste diversion percentages, or baseline comparisons against pre-closure performance. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (weak) applied to a specific geographic and policy context. The governance frameworks applied (Ansell & Gash collaborative governance; Rhodes/Provan & Kenis network governance) are established and widely applied. The contribution is their first systematic application to Denpasar's post-Suwung context, making this primarily a context-specific descriptive study. There is no new theory, method, dataset, or formal result. The paper's value lies in identifying this specific institutional transition moment as a research site, not in methodological or theoretical innovation. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations, no error bars, no significance tests — these are not applicable to the qualitative design. However, by qualitative standards the paper also falls short: no interview excerpts are provided to substantiate interpretive claims; no observation vignettes are offered; the documentary analysis yields no specific policy inconsistencies traced to named documents. Triangulation is claimed but not demonstrated. The study's self-declared scope limitation — explicitly excluding quantitative waste generation data and technical processing detail — removes the empirical anchors that would allow the governance claims to be evaluated against measurable outcomes. No code, dataset, or supplementary materials are released. The paper is peer-reviewed (accepted February 2026, published April 2026 in Jurnal DIALEKTIKA: Jurnal Ilmu Sosial — an Indonesian social science journal, not a top-tier international governance outlet). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (qualitative field study with policy document analysis). - Practical: The governance recommendations (multi-actor forums, MoUs, joint monitoring units) are implementable by Denpasar city government using standard administrative tools already referenced in Indonesian national law. - Efficient: No cost-benefit analysis or resource estimation is provided for the recommended governance interventions; implementation burden on city agencies is not assessed. - Effective: The evidence does not demonstrate that the recommended collaborative governance arrangements would achieve measurable waste diversion outcomes; the causal chain from governance reform to environmental impact is asserted, not tested. - Scalable: The paper makes no claims about scalability, and the context (a specific Indonesian tourist city in a particular regulatory environment) limits direct transferability to other settings without further empirical testing. The work delivers no software artifact, dataset, or reusable analytical tool — it stops at paper form. Strengths: - The structural argument — that landfill dependence created institutional moral hazard suppressing upstream governance investment — is analytically coherent and under-explored in the waste governance literature. - The five-dimension governance framework (regulation, institutions, coordination, participation, accountability) provides a replicable analytical scaffold that could guide future comparative studies of post-landfill transitions in other cities. - The paper situates local waste governance within Indonesia's NDC climate commitments, connecting a municipal operational problem to national methane reduction targets — a linkage rarely made explicit in Indonesian municipal governance studies. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state the study excludes quantitative waste generation data and detailed technical analysis of processing technologies. They also note the study is bounded to Denpasar City's specific context. These are stated clearly as scope boundaries and represent a mild acknowledgment, though the absence of quantitative grounding is treated as a design choice rather than a limitation that weakens the findings. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The results section presents no verifiable empirical evidence: no interview quotes, no observation records, no document analysis matrices. Without these, the "findings" are indistinguishable from informed expert opinion or secondary literature synthesis. - The paper conflates the research design (describing who will be interviewed and what documents will be reviewed) with executed methods — it is unclear whether primary data collection actually occurred or whether this is a policy review article. - No baseline is established: governance quality before versus after the landfill closure is described qualitatively without reference points, making the claim that governance "has not yet been fully effective" empirically unanchored. - Key questions are left unaddressed: How much of Denpasar's waste is currently being processed by TPS 3R versus transported to alternative landfills? What fraction of tourism businesses have formal waste management agreements? What enforcement actions have been taken under existing regulations? - The literature review cites foundational governance theory (Ansell & Gash 2008; Rhodes 1996) correctly but does not engage with more recent empirical studies of waste governance transitions in comparable Asian tourism cities (e.g., Bali-specific waste studies, Lombok, Thai resort municipalities), leaving the comparative contribution unestablished. Bottom Line: This paper offers a plausible governance diagnosis of a real and consequential urban waste transition, but presents no traceable primary evidence, making it unsuitable as a basis for policy reform decisions or academic citation beyond its contextual framing; practitioners managing the Suwung transition would benefit more from reading the source regulatory documents and World Bank solid waste management reports than from this article.
